### [1] [Lightweight Multimodal LLM-Enabled Cost-Effective Defect Grading of Power Transmission Equipment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28822)
*Tao Wang,Lipeng Zhu,Jiayong Li,Feng Gao,Siwen Liang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a defect grading framework for power transmission equipment using a multimodal large language model (MLLM), leveraging in-context learning and small-scale chain-of-thought Q&A data to fine-tune a lightweight open-source model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing machine learning methods can detect defects in power transmission equipment but struggle with two key issues: (1) they do not adequately integrate domain experts’ knowledge and reasoning, and (2) they perform poorly under class imbalance when more fine-grained defect grading is required. There is a need for a method that can embed expert experience, remain interpretable, and handle multiple fine-grained grading tasks efficiently.

Method: The authors first use a commercial multimodal large language model (MLLM) with in-context learning to perform defect grading of power transmission equipment and obtain a strong baseline (SOTA) grader. They then send secondary prompts to this strong model to automatically generate a small set of chain-of-thought question–answer (Q&A) pairs, reducing the need for manual annotation. These high-quality, interpretable Q&As are then used to fine-tune an open-source MLLM, Qwen3-VL-8B, using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)-based supervised fine-tuning, with fine-tuning restricted mainly to the language model layers. They also perform multi-task joint fine-tuning to allow a single lightweight MLLM to handle multiple grading tasks.

Result: On three different defect grading tasks for power transmission equipment, the fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B model, with only its language model layers adapted via LoRA, achieves state-of-the-art performance, surpassing existing methods. The generated chain-of-thought Q&As are sufficient for effective training, confirming that a modest amount of automatically generated expert-like reasoning data can significantly enhance performance. Multi-task joint fine-tuning shows that the same lightweight model can effectively learn and handle multiple grading tasks simultaneously.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that a commercially available MLLM can be exploited via in-context learning to generate high-quality chain-of-thought Q&A data, which in turn can be used to efficiently fine-tune a smaller open-source MLLM for defect grading of power transmission equipment. Fine-tuning only the language model layers with LoRA is enough to reach or surpass SOTA performance, and a single lightweight MLLM can address multiple grading tasks after joint training. This framework effectively integrates expert-like reasoning, alleviates data imbalance issues, reduces annotation costs, and provides a practical, scalable solution for fine-grained defect grading in power systems.

Abstract: Defect grading of power transmission equipment (DGPTE) is crucial to the stability of electric energy transmission. Although existing machine learning methods exhibit strong capabilities in defect detection, they are plagued by difficulties in integrating expert experience and facing class imbalance in more refined defect grading field. To address this issue, this paper introduces a novel defect grading framework based on multimodal large language model (MLLM). Specifically, this approach maximizes the commercial MLLMs' potential of DGPTE through in-context learning and obtains the state-of-te-art (SOTA) model. By sending a secondary request to this model, a small number of chain of thought-based question-answer pairs (Q\&As) are generated, which effectively reduces the cost of manual annotation. In this way, these high-quality interpretable Q\&As are used to train Qwen3-VL-8B via Low-Rank Adaption-based supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Experimental results on three DGPTE tasks demonstrate that fine-tuning only the language model layer yields the SOTA performance. Furthermore, multi-task joint fine-tuning verifies the feasibility of handling multiple grading tasks within only a single lightweight MLLM.

</details>


### [2] [What are They Thinking? Delineation, Probing and Tracking of Concepts in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28823)
*Mohamed Abdelwahab,Michelle Yu Collins,Sihan Chen,Yi Cheng Zhao,Zafarullah Mahmood,Jiading Zhu,Soliman Ali,Jonathan Rose*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a scalable way to build many cheap, linear 'concept probes' for LLMs, so we can see which concepts their internal embeddings encode during normal operation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are more widely used, we need practical tools to understand and monitor what they are "thinking" about. Existing interpretability methods can be expensive or narrowly targeted. The authors want a low-cost, model-agnostic way to detect a wide range of concepts directly from intermediate embeddings so that many concepts can be monitored in real-time for any LLM.

Method: 1) Define concepts precisely by constructing labeled datasets where each concept is explicitly present or absent. 2) For each concept, train linear classification probes on embeddings from different layers of several LLMs to detect whether the concept is encoded, and analyze how complex the linear probe must be. 3) Evaluate these probes on extended contexts to test whether they can still track the concept reliably over longer sequences. The study uses four different concepts and three different LLMs as a proof-of-concept.

Result: The authors successfully train linear probes that can reliably distinguish the presence vs. absence of each of the four concepts across multiple layers of three LLMs. They find that relatively simple linear probes often suffice and that these probes can still track concepts in longer contexts, indicating stable representational structure over context length.

Conclusion: Simple, low-cost linear probes can be systematically constructed to detect many semantic concepts within LLM embeddings, and these probes generalize across layers, models, and longer contexts. This provides a concrete first step toward scalable, concept-level monitoring of LLM behavior that could be extended to many more concepts and future models.

Abstract: As the influence of LLMs expands, it is imperative to gain insight into their decisions. One way to do that is to develop probes that detect the presence or absence of a broad set of concepts within the embeddings computed in an LLM - which is what we might say a model is "thinking" about. Such probes should be low-cost and easily applicable to any LLM, so that monitoring for many concepts is possible during normal operation.
  In this paper, we take the first steps towards developing the capability of creating many such probes by defining and executing examples of the key tasks needed: first, the careful delineation of a concept through the creation of a dataset with the concept both present and then absent. Then, the training and testing of a set of linear probes to detect the concept on any layer of an LLM, including an exploration of the complexity of the probe needed. Finally, we show that such probes can track concepts across larger contexts. This is done with four separate concepts and three different LLMs. When this process is scaled to many more concepts, it will create the ability to easily monitor new models.

</details>


### [3] [A Modular Architecture for Typologically Controlled Lexicon Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28824)
*Sankalp Tattwadarshi Swain,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build a modular system for generating artificial languages that sound realistic and have structured meanings, and show probabilistic phonological grammars work best.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automatic construction of artificial lexicons (conlangs) that are pronounceable, typologically realistic, and semantically organized is hard; existing tools either lack formal phonotactic guarantees or rely on opaque LLM pipelines, limiting reproducibility and linguistic control.

Method: They design a modular pipeline that (1) samples phoneme inventories from the PHOIBLE database, (2) generates word forms through pluggable phonological grammars—deterministic, Optimality Theory (OT), and Maximum Entropy (MaxEnt) models—and (3) assigns word meanings using a Swadesh–Leipzig–Jakarta based ontology that enforces explicit form–meaning alignment. They then compare grammar types and baselines using quantitative metrics.

Result: Across lexicons of 100 to 5,000 word forms, probabilistic phonological grammars (OT and especially MaxEnt) yield lower character n-gram perplexity, higher log-likelihood, and smaller KL divergence from PHOIBLE-derived distributions than deterministic and random baselines, indicating more coherent phonotactics and typological realism.

Conclusion: A modular, data-grounded approach with probabilistic phonological grammars can reliably generate artificial lexicons that are both phonotactically coherent and typologically plausible, outperforming deterministic and random methods while remaining transparent and reproducible.

Abstract: Constructing artificial lexicons that are pronounceable, typologically plausible, and semantically structured remains an open challenge in computational linguistics. Existing conlang generators either lack formal phonotactic guarantees or delegate generation to opaque, non-reproducible LLM-based pipelines. We propose a modular framework that samples phoneme inventories from PHOIBLE, generates word forms under interchangeable phonological grammars (deterministic, OT, and MaxEnt), and assigns meanings via a Swadesh--Leipzig--Jakarta ontology with explicit form--meaning alignment. Evaluation on character $n$-gram perplexity, log-likelihood, and KL divergence against PHOIBLE across lexicon sizes of 100-5,000 forms shows that probabilistic grammars consistently outperform deterministic and random baselines on both phonotactic coherence and typological realism.

</details>


### [4] [MechELK: A Mechanistic Interpretability Framework for Eliciting Latent Knowledge in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28825)
*Ji-jun Park,Soo-joon Choi,Jiwon Jeong,Taeyang Yoon,Ju-Wan Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduce MechELK, a mechanistic-interpretability-based framework to elicit latent knowledge from LLMs more reliably than prior methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs often internally represent correct facts or reasoning that they fail to express in their outputs (latent knowledge), posing challenges for reliability and AI safety, especially in deceptive or multi-step reasoning settings; existing methods like CCS and linear probing are limited in robustness and complexity handling.

Method: Propose MechELK, a three-stage framework: (1) Locate knowledge-bearing representations via Sparse Autoencoder feature analysis and activation patching; (2) Verify them using causal probing to separate true latent knowledge from spurious correlations; (3) Elicit the knowledge by representation engineering interventions that change activations to surface the hidden knowledge without changing model weights.

Result: On TruthfulQA, a Deceptive Alignment benchmark, and Quirky LM, MechELK attains 84.7% average elicitation accuracy, outperforming CCS by 6.2 percentage points and linear probing by 9.1 points, and manages to recover latent knowledge in 78.3% of cases where the model’s explicit answer is wrong or evasive.

Conclusion: Mechanistic-interpretability-based MechELK can reliably locate, validate, and elicit latent knowledge from LLMs, significantly improving over prior elicitation methods and making it a promising tool for AI safety use cases such as detecting deceptive alignment.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) frequently encode factual and reasoning knowledge in their internal representations that is not faithfully reflected in their surface-level outputs -- a phenomenon known as \emph{latent knowledge}. Existing approaches to eliciting latent knowledge, such as Contrastive Consistency Search (CCS), rely on contrastive activation patterns and struggle with complex multi-step reasoning tasks, while mechanistic interpretability tools have primarily been used to \emph{understand} model behavior rather than to \emph{extract} hidden knowledge. We present \textbf{MechELK}, a unified three-stage framework that bridges mechanistic interpretability and latent knowledge elicitation. MechELK operates through: (1) \textbf{Locate} -- using Sparse Autoencoder (SAE) feature analysis and activation patching to identify knowledge-bearing representations; (2) \textbf{Verify} -- employing causal probing to distinguish genuine latent knowledge from spurious correlations; and (3) \textbf{Elicit} -- applying representation engineering to surface hidden knowledge without modifying model weights. Evaluated on TruthfulQA, a curated Deceptive Alignment benchmark, and the Quirky LM dataset, MechELK achieves an average elicitation accuracy of 84.7\%, outperforming CCS by 6.2\% and direct linear probing by 9.1\%. Crucially, MechELK successfully identifies latent knowledge in 78.3\% of cases where the model's surface output is incorrect or evasive, demonstrating its utility for AI safety applications including deceptive alignment detection.

</details>


### [5] [From Context Shift to Stylistic Collapse: Why Training Objectives Matter More Than Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28826)
*Rohan Mahapatra*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that modern LLM alignment pipelines drastically and systematically distort linguistic feature distributions, concentrating probability mass and reducing entropy in discourse/structure, and that only strong, explicit control can partially undo this collapse.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work largely treats linguistic features as style markers to distinguish human vs. AI text. This paper instead investigates how the training and alignment pipeline itself reshapes the underlying language distribution, which is critical for understanding model behavior, AI detection, data contamination, and long-term effects on language use.

Method: They evaluate 17 language models ranging from 410M to over 100B parameters using 24 linguistically grounded distributional probes. These probes measure entropy, amplification, and frequency changes across discourse structure, syntax, punctuation, and lexical diversity, comparing base vs. instruction-tuned vs. RLHF-aligned systems. They then experiment with different strengths (lambda values) of a regularization/control mechanism to see how varying intervention strength affects collapse and diversity metrics.

Result: Instruction-tuned models show severe entropy collapse in discourse and structural features, with amplification of certain patterns by 1,949–16,853% on average and up to over 200,000% in peaks. Complex punctuation is heavily suppressed to 3.2–23.2% of baseline frequencies. RLHF does not significantly worsen this relative to instruction tuning alone (p > 0.25). Weak intervention (lambda=1.0) makes collapse 240% worse, whereas strong control (lambda=5.0) reduces collapse by 40.5%, beats much larger frontier models on these probes by ~97%, and yields substantially higher n-gram diversity, vocabulary diversity, and lower repetition.

Conclusion: Modern alignment pipelines reallocate probability mass from richer, higher-entropy linguistic structures into narrower, lower-entropy regions, in a way that standard quality metrics overlook. The key is not just smoothing the distribution but applying sufficiently strong, explicit control to maintain linguistic diversity. This has important implications for building aligned LLMs that do not degrade language richness, for AI text detection and guarding against training data contamination, and for understanding how widespread LLM deployment may influence the future evolution of language.

Abstract: In modern LLMs, linguistic features function not as stylistic artifacts but as probes of probability mass, allocated under training alignment objectives. Language models trained with contemporary pipelines exhibit severe reshaping of linguistic features, leading to extreme language re-distribution. While previous stylometric analyses explored linguistic differences between AI-generated and human texts, we focus on the reshaping plaguing the LLM training pipeline itself. We analyze 17 models (410M-100B+ parameters) across 24 linguistically-motivated probes, documenting that instruction-tuned systems systematically collapse language entropy along discourse and structural dimensions (mean amplification: 1,949-16,853%, peaks: 5,181-209,675%), while selectively suppressing complex punctuation to 3.2-23.2% of baseline frequencies. These effects do not worsen under RLHF, as divergence patterns are statistically indistinguishable (p > 0.25) across matched base and instruction-tuned model pairs. Weak intervention (lambda=1.0) exacerbates collapse by 240%, while strong control (lambda=5.0) achieves 40.5% improvement and outperforms frontier models by 96.7-98.2% despite 200-1000x scale disadvantage. Additionally, lambda=5.0 delivers 15% higher distinct-4, 27% higher vocabulary diversity, and 78% lower repetition than moderate regularization, establishing that alignment requires sufficient control strength, not merely distributional smoothing. Our findings underscore how modern LLMs reallocate stylistic probability mass, despite RLHF and scale. More broadly, our work reveals a structural limitation of current alignment pipelines: preference optimization reshapes language distributions invisible to standard quality metrics yet detectable through distributional probes, with implications for AI detection, training data contamination, and long-term linguistic evolution.

</details>


### [6] [RightNow-Arabic-0.5B-Turbo: An Open Sub-1B Arabic Language Model via Vocabulary Injection and Edge-First Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28827)
*Jaber Jaber,Osama Jaber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces RightNow-Arabic-0.5B-Turbo, a 518M-parameter Arabic-specialized LLM that fills the gap between tiny multilingual and large Arabic-only models, achieving strong benchmark performance while being small and fully open-source.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing open Arabic LLMs are either very small multilingual models that do not prioritize Arabic or large (7B–70B) Arabic-specialized models that require server-class hardware. There is virtually no openly released Arabic-specialized model in the 0.5–2B range, limiting practical on-device and low-resource deployment for Arabic users. The authors aim to build a strong, efficient, and fully open Arabic-focused model that can run on commodity or edge hardware.

Method: They start from Qwen2.5-0.5B and specialize it for Arabic through a multi-stage pipeline: (1) expand the tokenizer with 27,032 new Arabic tokens using mean-subtoken initialization; (2) continue pretraining on 504M Arabic tokens using an optimized training stack (FSDP, FlashAttention with variable-length packing, and Liger fused kernels) on 8×H100 GPUs; (3) apply supervised fine-tuning on 129,116 Arabic instruction–response pairs with response-only loss masking; (4) apply direct preference optimization (DPO) on 6,750 Arabic preference pairs; and (5) perform weight soup merging across three checkpoints to get a final merged model. They also produce multiple quantized versions and measure inference speed with llama.cpp.

Result: On three Arabic benchmarks from lm-evaluation-harness (COPA-ar, Arabic HellaSwag, ArabicMMLU), the merged model achieves 35.9% mean accuracy, outperforming all other open models of similar size. It matches Falcon-H1-1.5B on COPA-ar (58.4%) despite being about one-third the size and recovers roughly 67% of the average performance of SILMA-9B while using only 1/18 of its parameters. The edge-optimized quantized build is 398 MB (q4_k_m) and runs at 635 tokens/s at batch size 1 on a single H100 using llama.cpp.

Conclusion: RightNow-Arabic-0.5B-Turbo demonstrates that a carefully specialized 0.5B-parameter model can deliver competitive Arabic performance compared to much larger systems, while being small enough for edge and low-resource deployment. The full release of code, weights (bf16, int8, GGUF), and benchmarks provides a practical, open-source Arabic LLM foundation for further research and applications in the Arabic-speaking world.

Abstract: Open Arabic large language models split into two classes: sub-1B multilingual models that treat Arabic as an afterthought (Qwen2.5-0.5B, Falcon-H1-0.5B), and 7B-70B Arabic-specialized models that require a server to run (Jais, AceGPT, ALLaM, SILMA). The one published attempt at a sub-2B Arabic-specialized model, Kuwain-1.5B, never released its weights. We present RightNow-Arabic-0.5B-Turbo, a 518M-parameter Arabic-specialized decoder LLM built on Qwen2.5-0.5B. The pipeline adds 27,032 Arabic tokens via mean-subtoken initialization, continues pretraining on 504M Arabic tokens on 8xH100 with FSDP, FlashAttention varlen packing, and Liger fused kernels, then applies supervised fine-tuning on 129,116 Arabic instruction pairs with response-only loss masking, direct preference optimization on 6,750 Arabic preference pairs, and weight soup merging across three checkpoints. On three lm-evaluation-harness Arabic benchmarks (COPA-ar, Arabic HellaSwag, ArabicMMLU) the merged model reaches 35.9% mean accuracy, beats every same-class open model, ties Falcon-H1-1.5B on COPA-ar (58.4%) at one-third the size, and recovers 67% of SILMA-9B's mean at 1/18 the parameters. The edge build quantizes to 398 MB (q4_k_m) and delivers 635 tokens/s at batch size 1 on a single H100 via llama.cpp. All code (5,555 lines across 25 scripts), weights (bf16, int8, and four GGUF quantizations), and benchmark scripts are released at https://huggingface.co/RightNowAI/RightNow-Arabic-0.5B-Turbo.

</details>


### [7] [Micro-Macro Retrieval: Reducing Long-Form Hallucination in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28828)
*Yujie Feng,Jian Li,Zhihan Zhou,Pengfei Xu,Yujia Zhang,Xiaoyu Li,Xiaohui Zhou,Alan Zhao,Xi Chen,Xiao-Ming Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Micro-Macro Retrieval (M2R), a retrieve-while-generate framework that brings key retrieved information closer to the model's outputs to reduce hallucinations in long-form generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs hallucinate frequently in long-form generation, where redundant context and long reasoning chains increase factual errors. Prior work shows that factual accuracy improves when key information is closer to the generated text, but existing retrieval-augmented language models lack mechanisms to guarantee this proximity. The paper is motivated by the need to systematically control how and where external evidence influences generation to curb hallucinations.

Method: The authors propose Micro-Macro Retrieval (M2R), which operates at two levels: (1) a macro level that performs coarse-grained retrieval from external sources during generation, and (2) a micro level that constructs and maintains a key information repository during reasoning, extracting and reusing essential results so they appear near the generated outputs. M2R is trained via curriculum learning-based reinforcement learning, using customized rule-based rewards to encourage effective retrieval and factual grounding while maintaining training stability.

Result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks, with a focus on tasks involving long contexts, show that M2R reduces hallucinations and improves factual accuracy compared to existing RALMs. The improvements are particularly notable in settings where reasoning chains are long or input documents are lengthy.

Conclusion: Ensuring the proximity of key evidence to generated outputs is a critical factor in mitigating hallucinations in LLMs. M2R’s dual-level (micro and macro) retrieval and its retrieve-while-generate design effectively address this by keeping essential information close to the answer text, leading to more reliable long-form generation. The curriculum RL training further enables stable learning of retrieval and grounding behavior, making M2R a promising direction for robust RALMs.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve impressive performance across many tasks but remain prone to hallucination, especially in long-form generation where redundant retrieved contexts and lengthy reasoning chains amplify factual errors. Recent studies highlight a critical phenomenon: the closer key information appears to the model outputs, the higher the factual accuracy. However, existing retrieval-augmented language models (RALMs) lack effective mechanisms to ensure this proximity - external evidence is injected into reasoning via multi-turn retrieval, but this cannot ensure key information stays close to the outputs. We propose Micro-Macro Retrieval (M2R), a novel retrieve-while-generate framework to fill this gap. At the macro level, M2R retrieves coarse-grained evidence from external sources; at the micro level, it extracts essential results from a key information repository built during reasoning and reuses them while generating answers. This design directly addresses the key-information-to-output proximity bottleneck, effectively reducing hallucination in long-form tasks. M2R is trained with a curriculum learning-based reinforcement learning strategy using customized rule-based rewards, enabling stable acquisition of retrieval and grounding skills. Extensive experiments across different benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of M2R, especially in lengthy-context settings.

</details>


### [8] [Aryabhata 2: Scaling Reinforcement Learning for Advanced STEM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28829)
*Ritvik Rastogi,Vishal Singh,Tejas Chaudhari,Sandeep Varma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A specialized RL-tuned 20B language model, Aryabhata 2, is built and evaluated for Indian competitive STEM exams, delivering better reasoning accuracy with shorter solutions than its base model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LLMs, while good on generic reasoning benchmarks, are hard to deploy at scale for competitive STEM exams where millions of student doubts require multi-step symbolic reasoning, accurate calculation, and domain-specific, well-structured solutions. There is a need for a model tailored to this setting that can reliably solve such problems efficiently.

Method: The authors start from GPT-OSS-20B and perform reinforcement-learning-based post-training using verifiable rewards. They construct a high-quality, domain-specific curriculum from PhysicsWallah’s proprietary question banks for JEE and NEET. The RL procedure uses prolonged training and encourages broader exploration by progressively increasing the rollout group size during policy optimization, all focused on reasoning quality and efficiency.

Result: Aryabhata 2 is evaluated on JEE Main, JEE Advanced, NEET, and several out-of-distribution reasoning benchmarks, including AIME, HMMT, MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux 2.0, and GPQA. Across these evaluations, Aryabhata 2 outperforms the base GPT-OSS-20B model in competitive STEM reasoning tasks while also generating substantially fewer tokens in its solutions, with reductions in output length of up to 64%.

Conclusion: Reinforcement-learning post-training on a carefully curated, verifiable STEM curriculum can transform a general 20B LLM into a more accurate and more efficient solver for competitive exam-style problems. Aryabhata 2 demonstrates that targeted RL with verifiable rewards and exploration-aware training yields both higher performance and shorter, more scalable solutions for large-scale educational deployments.

Abstract: Competitive STEM examinations such as JEE and NEET require multi-step symbolic reasoning, precise numerical computation, and deep conceptual understanding across physics, chemistry, and mathematics. Recent large language models perform strongly on common reasoning benchmarks, yet they remain difficult to deploy at scale, where millions of student doubts demand domain-specific, consistently structured problem solving.
  We introduce Aryabhata 2, a reasoning-focused language model for competitive STEM examinations, trained via reinforcement-learning post-training. Using PhysicsWallah's internal question banks, we construct a high-quality training curriculum and post-train GPT-OSS-20B through reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards. Training combines prolonged reinforcement learning with broadened exploration via progressively larger rollout group sizes.
  We evaluate Aryabhata 2 on competitive examination benchmarks, including JEE Main, JEE Advanced, and NEET, as well as out-of-distribution reasoning datasets such as AIME, HMMT, MMLU-Pro, MMLU-Redux 2.0, and GPQA. Results show that Aryabhata 2 outperforms its base model GPT-OSS-20B on competitive STEM reasoning while requiring substantially fewer output tokens (up to 64\% fewer).

</details>


### [9] [Benchmarking Open-Source Safety Guard Models: A Comprehensive Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28830)
*Reetu Raj Harsh,Bhaskarjit Sarmah,Stefano Pasquali*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates 14 open-source safety guard models for LLM content moderation on a large, safety-focused benchmark, finding that recall is crucial and that smaller, general-purpose guards can outperform larger or specialized ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are deployed in safety-critical contexts, it is vital to reliably detect and moderate harmful content (e.g., violence, hate, self-harm). Existing guard models vary widely, and practitioners lack clear, comparative evidence about which models work best and how factors like size or specialization affect safety performance.

Method: The authors construct a large benchmark of 79,331 safety-relevant samples by aggregating and filtering four datasets (HarmBench, StrongREJECT, RealToxicityPrompts, BeaverTails) into 8 NIST-aligned safety categories. They then systematically evaluate 14 open-source safety guard models on this dataset, measuring especially recall and comparing performance across model sizes, architectures, and specialization levels (general-purpose vs. task-specific).

Result: Qwen Guard (4B) attains the highest recall at about 84%, while much larger models such as Llama Guard (12B) and GPT-OSS Safeguard (20B) are overly conservative and miss up to 75% of unsafe content. The study finds no positive correlation between parameter count and safety detection effectiveness, and observes that general-purpose guard models tend to outperform specialized safety models on this benchmark.

Conclusion: For safety-critical deployments, high recall should be prioritized over precision, and practitioners should not assume larger or more specialized models are safer. Smaller, well-designed, general-purpose guard models like Qwen Guard can offer superior harmful-content detection. The benchmark and results provide actionable guidance for choosing guard models in production content moderation pipelines.

Abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in safety-critical applications, robust content moderation becomes essential. We present a comprehensive evaluation of 14 open-source safety guard models on a curated benchmark of 79,331 samples spanning 8 NIST AI Risk Framework safety categories. Our benchmark aggregates four diverse datasets (HarmBench, StrongREJECT, RealToxicityPrompts, and BeaverTails), filtered to focus exclusively on safety-relevant content (violence, hate speech, harassment, sexual content, suicide/self-harm, profanity, threats, and health misinformation). We find that recall is the critical metric for safety applications, as missing harmful content poses greater risk than false positives. Our evaluation reveals surprising results: Qwen Guard (4B parameters) achieves the highest recall (83.97%) while larger models like Llama Guard (12B) and GPT-OSS Safeguard (20B) exhibit conservative behavior, missing up to 75% of unsafe content. We demonstrate that model size does not correlate with safety detection performance and that general-purpose guard models outperform specialized ones. These findings provide practical guidance for selecting safety guard models in production deployments.

</details>


### [10] [S3Mem: Structured Spatiotemporal Scene-Event Memory for Long-Horizon Interactive Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28831)
*Encheng Su,Jinouwen Zhang,Jianyu Wu,Qiucheng Yu,Chen Tang,Pengze Li,Lintao Wang,Yizhou Wang,Xinzhu Ma,Shixiang Tang,Aoran Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes S3MEM, a structured episodic memory framework that improves long-horizon interactive QA over Vanilla RAG and other memory baselines, with better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon interactive agents accumulate long histories but struggle to answer questions about earlier events, not just due to context length limits but because plain-text RAG retrieves locally relevant yet chain-incomplete evidence, especially for complex spatial, temporal, and multi-hop queries.

Method: Introduce S3MEM, which writes trajectories into structured scene-event episodic memory units, uses anchor-sensitive retrieval tailored to the query, and presents a compact, token-efficient evidence interface for answer-time inference. Evaluate under a frozen answer-time protocol across four environments against Vanilla RAG and three adapted structured-memory baselines.

Result: Across Crafter, Jericho, SciWorld, and ALFWorld, S3MEM consistently outperforms Vanilla RAG, beats Graph-NoReader on three of four environments and matches it on SciWorld, while using far fewer evidence tokens. Other adapted baselines improve over Vanilla RAG in some settings but never match S3MEM’s overall accuracy-efficiency balance.

Conclusion: Within the tested frozen answer-time setup, structuring memory and using anchor-sensitive evidence routing yields a better accuracy-efficiency frontier for long-horizon interactive QA than generic plain-text or less specialized memory interfaces.

Abstract: Long-horizon interactive agents often accumulate large trajectory histories yet still fail to answer questions about earlier events reliably. We argue that the main bottleneck is not context length alone, but the trajectory-to-answer interface of long-term memory. When histories are stored as plain-text chunks and queried with standard retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), systems often retrieve locally relevant but chain-incomplete evidence, especially for spatial, temporal, repeated-event, and multi-hop state questions. We propose S3MEM, a structured scene-event episodic memory framework for long-horizon interactive question answering (QA). S3MEM writes trajectories into structured memory units, retrieves evidence through anchor-sensitive retrieval, and exposes a compact token-budget-aware evidence interface for answer-time inference. In this sense, S3MEM is a structured evidence harness that converts agent trajectories into query-aligned support. We evaluate S3MEM on two internal headline environments (Crafter, Jericho) and two out-of-family environments (SciWorld, ALFWorld). Under a shared frozen answer-time protocol, S3MEM consistently outperforms Vanilla RAG across all four environments, surpasses Graph-NoReader on Crafter, Jericho, and ALFWorld, and matches it on SciWorld while using dramatically fewer evidence tokens. Three adapted recent baselines -- A-MEM-inspired, MemoryOS-adapted, and LightMem-adapted -- improve over Vanilla RAG in several settings, but none matches S3MEM's overall accuracy-efficiency frontier. Overall, the evidence supports a bounded conclusion: under the current frozen answer-time protocol, structured writing and anchor-sensitive evidence routing provide a stronger accuracy-efficiency frontier for long-horizon interactive QA than more generic memory interfaces.

</details>


### [11] [A comparative study of transformer-based embeddings for topic coherence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28832)
*Alex Ding,Tarun Rapaka,Willy Rodriguez,Jason Yang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies whether increasing transformer language model size improves topic quality in BERTopic and finds model size has negligible effect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While transformer-based language models provide strong document embeddings for topic modeling, it is unclear whether using much larger models (with many more parameters) actually yields better topic quality, despite the higher computational cost. This gap is important because practitioners may overinvest in large models assuming they will produce significantly better topics.

Method: The authors use a BERTopic pipeline with seven transformer-based language models of varying sizes, from small models like MiniLM (tens of millions of parameters) to large models like LLaMA-2 (up to 13 billion parameters). They apply these models across multiple text corpora to generate topics, then evaluate topic quality quantitatively using coherence and divergence metrics as proposed by Röder et al. (2015). The analysis systematically compares how topic quality changes as model size increases.

Result: Across model sizes ranging from 22M to 13B parameters, the measured topic coherence and divergence scores change very little. Larger models do not consistently outperform smaller ones in the topic quality metrics considered.

Conclusion: Model size has negligible impact on topic quality within a BERTopic framework over the tested range. Consequently, smaller transformer models can be used to obtain topic quality comparable to much larger models, offering computational efficiency without sacrificing performance for this task.

Abstract: Topic modeling is a branch of Natural Language Processing (NLP) that aims to organize large collections of texts into coherent groups according to word co-occurrence patterns, with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) remaining one of the most widely used and interpretable probabilistic approaches. Recent advances in NLP, particularly transformer-based language models, offer improved document representations. It is also known that the size of the model (in terms of number of parameters) has a significant impact in the performance of the language models on different pre-defined tasks. In this study, we systematically examine the effect of model size on topic quality by analyzing the performances of seven transformer-based language models (from small models such as MiniLM to large ones such as LLaMA-2) in a BERTopic pipeline on a variety of corpora. Topic quality is evaluated using coherence and divergence metrics following R{ö}der et al. (2015). Our results indicate that model size, ranging from 22 million to 13 billion parameters, has a negligible impact on the quality of the topic, suggesting that smaller models can achieve comparable performance to larger models.

</details>


### [12] [Assessing Dutch Syllabification Algorithms and Improving Accuracy by Combining Phonetic and Orthographic Information through Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28834)
*Gus Lathouwers,Wieke Harmsen,Catia Cucchiarini,Helmer Strik*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Paper compares several Dutch syllabification algorithms (including a new deep-learning model) across multiple datasets and shows that data-driven and phonetic-plus-orthographic approaches achieve state-of-the-art accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Syllabifying Dutch words is difficult because of many rules, exceptions, and differences between spelling and pronunciation. Existing Dutch syllabification algorithms have not been evaluated comparatively, modern deep learning has not yet been applied to Dutch orthographic syllabification, and phonetic vs. orthographic methods have been studied separately rather than in an integrated way.

Method: The authors systematically compare four algorithms – Brandt Corstius, Liang, Trogkanis-Elkan (CRF-based), and a newly designed deep-learning model – on three datasets: dictionary words, loanwords, and pseudowords. They also design deep-learning architectures that can incorporate both orthographic and phonetic information into a single model, and analyze where phonetic information yields gains.

Result: Data-driven algorithms (CRF and deep learning) outperform the knowledge-based Brandt Corstius algorithm in all but one setting. The new deep-learning approach achieves state-of-the-art performance with 99.65% word-level accuracy, improving the previous best by 0.14%. Cases where phonetic information helps are mainly words whose spelling is ambiguous but whose pronunciation disambiguates the syllable boundaries.

Conclusion: Combining phonetic and orthographic information in a unified, data-driven (deep-learning) framework yields top-level performance for Dutch syllabification and can successfully resolve orthographic ambiguities. The proposed frameworks are promising not only for Dutch but are likely applicable to other languages and to other orthographic tasks where phonetic cues are informative, warranting further investigation.

Abstract: Syllabification describes the task of dividing words into syllables. Due to many rules and exceptions, training an algorithm to perform syllabification with high accuracy remains a challenge. Throughout the last decades, different algorithms have been put forth for Dutch syllabification, yet a comprehensive comparative assessment has not been done. Additionally, deep learning has gained significant popularity within NLP in recent years, yet no modern deep-learning based framework has been developed for Dutch orthographic syllabification. Finally, phonetic and orthographic syllabification algorithms have been examined separately, but not in combination. The aim of the current research was twofold: (a) to examine the performance of existing Dutch syllabification algorithms, and (b) to investigate whether combining phonetic and orthographic information into a single model can increase syllabification performance. To compare the performance of algorithms, four algorithms (Brandt Corstius, Liang, Trogkanis-Elkan (CRF), and a newly conceived deep-learning model) were applied to three different datasets (dictionary words, loanwords, pseudowords). The algorithms show varying performance across datasets, with the data-driven algorithms outperforming a knowledge-based algorithm in all but one condition. The new deep-learning methods developed led to increased performance compared to the best found in the literature (99.65% word accuracy, a 0.14% improvement). An analysis of the words for which adding phonetic information improved syllabification performance indicates that these were words in which the orthographic ambiguity could be resolved by information on pronunciation. Future research could examine other areas where phonetic information can benefit orthographic processing. In addition, the newly developed deep learning frameworks can be applied to other languages than Dutch.

</details>


### [13] [GenesisFunc: Multi-Agent Data Generation for Accurate and Generalizable Function-Calling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28835)
*Hao-Xiang Xu,Chong Deng,Jiaqing Liu,Wen Wang,Qian Chen,Lujia Bao,Xiangang Li,Zhen-Hua Ling*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GenesisFunc is an automated multi-agent pipeline that generates high-quality, diverse synthetic function-calling (FC) training data for LLMs, leading to strong in-domain and out-of-domain FC performance comparable to latest API-based models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Function-calling extends LLM capabilities but requires high-quality, diverse, and broadly covered training data. Real FC data is hard to obtain and annotate, and existing synthetic data pipelines struggle with unreliable APIs, limited scalability, low diversity, and weak quality control. A better, scalable way to generate reliable FC data is needed.

Method: GenesisFunc builds on reliable tools from public benchmarks and uses a multi-agent framework to drive a dialogue generation system that creates diverse FC conversations. It maintains diversity and quality through designed generation processes and reinforces accuracy via a multi-stage evaluation system. The resulting synthetic data is used to fine-tune an 8B LLM, and its performance is evaluated against other open-source and API-based models on in-domain and out-of-domain FC tasks.

Result: An 8B LLM fine-tuned on GenesisFunc’s synthetic data outperforms similarly sized open-source models on in-domain function-calling tasks and shows better out-of-domain generalization. Its FC capabilities are comparable to some recent API-based models. The pipeline also shows strong potential to scale effectively to new downstream tools.

Conclusion: Automated, benchmark-grounded, multi-agent synthetic data generation via GenesisFunc can reliably produce high-quality, diverse FC training data. This enables relatively small LLMs to achieve strong and generalizable FC performance, offering a scalable, practical solution for enhancing tool-use capabilities across a wide range of downstream applications.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) extend their capabilities through function-calling (FC), which relies on training data with high quality, diversity, and broad coverage of scenario. However, obtaining and annotating real function-calling data is challenging, while synthetic data from existing pipelines often suffers from unreliable APIs, limited tool scalability, insufficient diversity, and weak quality control. To address these, we present GenesisFunc, an automated pipeline for generating FC training data. Starting from reliable tools in widely used public benchmarks, our GenesisFunc employs a multi-agent framework to support a dialogue generation system that produces conversations spanning diverse scenarios, while maintaining both diversity and quality throughout the process. The accuracy of the data is further reinforced through a multi-stage evaluation system. We fine-tune an 8B LLM on the synthetic dataset and show through extensive experiments that it outperforms similarly sized open-source models in in-domain FC performance and out-of-domain generalization, while reaching FC capabilities comparable to some of the latest API-based models. In addition, our method demonstrates strong potential to scale effectively across downstream tools, underscoring its real-world applicability.

</details>


### [14] [No Reader Left Behind: Multi-Agent Summaries Everyone Can Understand](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28836)
*Jimin Jung,MyoungJin Kim,Jaehyung Seo,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NRLB is a multi-agent system that creates plain-language summaries tailored to readers with different linguistic and cognitive needs, improving readability without sacrificing factual accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing summarization systems do not adequately account for diverse reader groups, such as children, non-native speakers, and people with attention deficits, which is problematic in contexts like U.S. government communication where clear public access is legally required.

Method: The authors design NRLB, a multi-agent framework that simulates three reader profiles (elementary students, non-native readers, and readers with attention deficits). It uses template-based planning to structure summaries and iterative, reader-focused refinement steps to identify and fix difficult terms, missing context, and confusing sentences.

Result: Across multiple datasets, NRLB consistently improves the readability of summaries while maintaining factual accuracy. Human annotators prefer NRLB’s outputs 55–76% of the time over baselines, indicating better accessibility and quality.

Conclusion: NRLB effectively generates plain-language summaries that meet diverse reader needs while remaining faithful to the original text, showing promise for applications like government communication where accessibility for the general public is essential.

Abstract: The Plain Writing Act in the United States requires government documents to be accessible in clear and simple language that the general public can easily understand, yet existing summarization systems struggle to address diverse linguistic and cognitive barriers among general readers. We present NRLB (No Reader Left Behind), a multi-agent framework for plain language summarization that simulates three representative reader groups: elementary school student readers, non-native readers, and readers with attention deficits. NRLB combines template-based planning with iterative, reader-oriented refinement, enabling systematic detection and resolution of difficult terms, missing contexts, and confusing sentences. Evaluations across multiple datasets demonstrate consistent improvements in readability while preserving factual accuracy. Human evaluation further validates NRLB's impact, with annotator preference rates ranging from 55% to 76%, highlighting NRLB's potential to produce plain language summaries that are both faithful to the source and broadly accessible to the general public.

</details>


### [15] [SERC: LDPC-Inspired Semantic Error Correction for Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28837)
*Gyumin Kim,Juhwan Park,Jaeha Kim,Seunggyun Han,Kyungrak Son,Ikbeom Jang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes SERC, an LDPC-inspired semantic error-correction framework for retrieval-augmented generation that reduces LLM hallucinations with sparse, training-free verification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs hallucinate, and existing intrinsic self-correction struggles with self-bias and high verification cost. The authors seek a principled, efficient way to detect and correct factual errors without retraining or exhaustive checking, especially in resource-constrained settings.

Method: They model text generation as a semantic noisy channel, where outputs are noise-corrupted codewords. Inspired by LDPC codes, they design SERC, which issues a small number of low-density verification queries about key facts to an external retriever, uses retrieved evidence to identify inconsistencies, and then corrects the answer, all in a model-agnostic, training-free pipeline.

Result: On LongForm Bio and TruthfulQA with Llama-3-8B and Qwen2.5-14B, SERC outperforms intrinsic self-correction and strong RAG baselines, with notably higher factual precision (FactScore) and better hallucination reduction, even enabling smaller models to beat larger baselines.

Conclusion: SERC offers a principled, LDPC-inspired framework for semantic error correction in RAG that is training-free, model-agnostic, and uses sparse verification to minimize cost while improving fidelity, providing an effective hallucination-mitigation strategy for smaller, resource-limited LLM deployments.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities, their reliability is significantly compromised by hallucinations. Existing intrinsic self-correction methods attempt to address this, but often fail due to self-bias, where models struggle to identify errors in their own outputs without external verification. To overcome these limitations, we propose the LDPC-inspired semantic error correction for retrieval-augmented generation (SERC), providing a theoretical framework to interpret and mitigate LLM hallucinations. We reformulate the text generation process as a semantic noisy channel, treating generated responses as noise-corrupted codewords. Inspired by low-density parity-check (LDPC) codes, SERC employs a sparse verification strategy: instead of exhaustively checking all facts, it generates low-density verification queries and validates them against external evidence to efficiently detect and correct errors. We evaluate SERC on LongForm Bio and TruthfulQA benchmarks using Llama-3-8B and Qwen2.5-14B. Experimental results demonstrate that SERC outperforms both intrinsic self-correction methods and strong retrieval-augmented baselines, demonstrating significant gains especially in factual precision (FactScore). Notably, SERC enables small language models (SLMs) to surpass the performance of larger baselines in hallucination reduction and information preservation. Our findings demonstrate that SERC provides a training-free, model-agnostic solution that significantly reduces verification overhead compared to dense methods, achieving an optimal trade-off between cost and fidelity in resource-constrained environments.

</details>


### [16] [Specialty-Specific Medical Language Model for Immune-Mediated Diseases](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28838)
*Veysel Kocaman,Gursev Pirge,Yigit Gul,Ace Vo,Zhenya Nargizyan,David Talby*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They built a specialized NER model for immunology and infectious disease case reports, achieving high F1 (0.89) by combining clinical-domain transformers with expert-labeled data, outperforming baselines and LLM prompting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: General NLP/NER systems perform poorly on immune-mediated and infectious disease narratives because terminology is inconsistent and very fine-grained, so researchers lack reliable tools to extract structured disease information from free-text case reports for downstream clinical and research tasks.

Method: The authors curated 371 immunology and infectious disease case reports and worked with two clinical experts to manually annotate them with 12 entity types spanning diseases, symptoms, and clinical descriptors. They then trained and compared several NER approaches: a MedicalNER architecture using multiple healthcare-specific embeddings, a BERT-style transformer token classification model with clinical-domain embeddings, and zero-shot NER/LLM-based baselines prompted to tag entities. Models were evaluated on span-level performance (e.g., F1) over the labeled entities.

Result: The best-performing approach was a transformer-based NER model using clinical-domain embeddings, which achieved an F1 score of 0.89 and consistently beat both the MedicalNER setup and zero-shot/LLM baselines. LLM prompting in a zero-shot setting produced notably worse results, especially due to inconsistencies in predicted entity spans and difficulty with fine-grained boundaries.

Conclusion: A domain-specific, transformer-based NER model trained on expert-annotated immunology and infectious disease case reports can reliably capture nuanced disease-related entities and generalize across heterogeneous biomedical text. Specialized embeddings plus expert annotation are crucial for performance, and current prompted LLMs are less suitable for precise span-level NER in this domain. The model enables structured analysis of case reports to support applications like cohort identification, disease surveillance, and decision support.

Abstract: Extracting detailed clinical information from free-text medical narratives remains a practical challenge for researchers and healthcare systems. Terminology for immune-mediated and infectious diseases is especially inconsistent across sources, which often limits the ability of general-purpose Natural Language Processing (NLP) systems to capture the relevant biomedical concepts with sufficient granularity. We developed a domain-specific Named Entity Recognition (NER) model tailored to identify disease-related entities occurring in immunology and infectious disease contexts. We assembled and manually annotated a dataset of 371 case reports in collaboration with two clinical specialists, defining twelve entity classes covering immune-mediated and infectious conditions as well as related symptoms and clinical descriptors. We evaluated several modeling strategies, including the MedicalNER architecture with multiple healthcare-specific embeddings, a BERT-based token classification model, and zero-shot NER systems. The strongest performance was obtained with a transformer-based model trained on clinical-domain embeddings, which reached an F1 score of 0.89, consistently outperforming baseline and zero-shot approaches. The combination of specialized embeddings and expert annotation proved particularly valuable for capturing nuanced disease terminology and improving generalization across heterogeneous biomedical text. The prompted LLM baseline achieved substantially lower performance under the same evaluation protocol, reflecting difficulties in producing span-consistent outputs for fine-grained entity boundaries despite detailed prompting. The resulting model provides a structured way to analyze case reports and can support downstream tasks such as cohort identification, disease monitoring, and clinical decision support.

</details>


### [17] [How Consistent Are LLM Agents? Measuring Behavioral Reproducibility in Multi-Step Tool-Calling Pipelines](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28840)
*Abel Yagubyan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper empirically studies how consistently multi-step, tool-using LLM agents repeat their behavior under identical conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM agents with tool-calling are deployed in real applications, practitioners need to know if an agent will behave deterministically enough for reliability, debugging, and safety. Prior consistency work mostly focused on simpler ReAct-style agents using unstructured text actions rather than structured tools with typed parameters and real side effects. There is a gap in understanding how stable tool choices and arguments are across repeated runs.

Method: The authors run a systematic empirical study: they repeatedly invoke multi-step tool-calling agents on identical inputs and log their behaviors. They then measure consistency at several levels—whether the same tools are selected, in the same order, and with the same arguments. The study contrasts these structured tool-calling agents with prior ReAct-style (search-only, free-text) settings, emphasizing typed interfaces and consequential side effects.

Result: They obtain quantitative measurements of how often agents reproduce the same tool sequences and parameter settings across runs, revealing the extent and patterns of behavioral variability in multi-step tool use. The results show that even under identical prompts and settings, agents can diverge in which tools they call, the order of calls, and the specific arguments passed.

Conclusion: Behavioral consistency in tool-calling LLM agents cannot be taken for granted: repeated identical invocations can yield different tool sequences and parameters. This underscores the need for reliability-oriented evaluation and possibly new design or training methods to improve consistency in production tool-using agents, going beyond what has been studied in simpler ReAct-style scenarios.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents with tool-calling capabilities are increasingly deployed in production systems, yet a fundamental reliability question remains under-explored: does the same agent behave the same way twice? We present a systematic empirical study of behavioral consistency in multi-step tool-calling agents, measuring whether agents select the same tools, in the same order, with the same arguments, across repeated identical invocations. Unlike prior work on consistency in ReAct-style agents(search-only, free-text actions), we study the richer setting of structured tool-calling interfaces with typed parameters and consequential side effects.

</details>


### [18] [Thoughts-as-Planning: Latent World Models for Chain-of-Thoughts Optimization via Reinforcement Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28842)
*Dong Liu,Yanxuan Yu,Ying Nian Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Thoughts-as-Planning, a framework that treats reasoning chain optimization in LLMs as a sequential decision-making and planning problem in a latent semantic space, achieving better efficiency, robustness, generalization, and interpretability than prior tuning methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for optimizing reasoning chains in large language models often depend on black-box heuristics or gradient-free search, which are hard to interpret, sample-inefficient, and do not generalize well. There is a need for a principled, model-based approach that can systematically edit and optimize reasoning steps while aligning them with downstream task objectives, and that provides interpretable structure over the reasoning process.

Method: The authors formalize reasoning chain optimization as planning in a partially observable environment where the LLM is the environment. They learn a latent world model that predicts how edits to the reasoning chain affect the final outputs. They construct a proximity-preserving embedding space that captures reasoning chain–response dynamics and use it as the state space for planning. Optimization over this space can be done with gradient descent or reinforcement learning. The framework supports multi-scale abstraction, allowing edits at token, segment, and instruction levels to be handled within a single unified planner, and it generates structured planning trajectories for reasoning chain modifications.

Result: Across a range of language understanding and generation benchmarks, the proposed Thoughts-as-Planning framework surpasses state-of-the-art baselines for reasoning chain tuning. It is reported to be more sample-efficient, robust to variations, and better at generalizing across tasks, while also yielding interpretable plans that expose the intermediate reasoning edits.

Conclusion: Treating reasoning chain optimization as a planning problem in a learned latent semantic space enables more effective and interpretable control over LLM reasoning. The Thoughts-as-Planning framework provides a unified, multi-scale planner that improves performance, efficiency, robustness, and generalization over existing black-box or gradient-free tuning methods, suggesting that model-based planning over latent reasoning representations is a promising direction for aligning LLM behavior with task objectives.

Abstract: The success of large language models (LLMs) across diverse NLP tasks has elevated the importance of reasoning chain optimization as a critical step in aligning model behavior with task objectives. Existing reasoning chain tuning methods often rely on black-box heuristics or gradient-free search, which lack interpretability, generalization, and sample efficiency. In this work, we introduce \textbf{Thoughts-as-Planning}, a novel framework that formalizes reasoning chain optimization as a sequential decision-making process over a latent semantic space. We model the LLM as a partially observable environment and learn a latent world model that simulates the effect of reasoning chain edits on downstream outputs. A proximity-preserving embedding space is constructed to encode reasoning chain-response dynamics, enabling planning via gradient descent or reinforcement learning. Our method supports multi-scale abstraction, allowing reasoning chain edits at token, segment, and instruction levels to be integrated into a unified planner. Through extensive experiments on language understanding and generation tasks, we demonstrate that Thoughts-as-Planning outperforms state-of-the-art reasoning chain tuning baselines in efficiency, robustness, and generalization, while offering interpretability through its structured planning trajectory. Our code is available at https://github.com/FastLM/Thoughts-as-Planning.

</details>


### [19] [GPF-LiveNews: A Streaming Evaluation Protocol for Group-Conditioned Framing in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28848)
*Mohd Ariful Haque,Fahad Rahman,Kishor Datta Gupta,Roy George*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents GPF-LIVENEWS, a streaming benchmark and protocol to audit how language models frame fresh news events for different identity groups and prompt types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing bias and fairness benchmarks for language models are static and cannot capture how models behave in dynamic, real-world settings where news, inputs, and systems continuously change. The authors want a way to monitor how models frame emerging events for different audiences and detect group-conditioned disparities as they appear, rather than relying only on fixed datasets.

Method: They build a streaming evaluation protocol that continuously pulls new BBC and Reuters news articles, then generates prompts that vary along 42 identity labels and seven prompt families (e.g., different use cases such as policy/action prompts). Models are asked to respond to these open-ended prompts, producing ‘response bundles’ per article-identity-prompt combination. The outputs are then analyzed using two main kinds of signals: semantic-sensitivity (how much the content changes with the group label) and sentiment-disparity (differences in sentiment across groups and prompts). The authors run 12 monitoring rounds over 23 hosted models, and provide a benchmark snapshot plus artifacts for replication.

Result: Across their pilot runs, they find that prompts focused on Policy/Action induce the largest semantic changes across identity conditions—indicating that how models talk about what should be done varies more by group in these scenarios. Sentiment scores, however, vary less across identities and prompt types, suggesting relatively flatter sentiment disparities. They also successfully produce a reusable benchmark snapshot with all the associated metadata and tools.

Conclusion: GPF-LIVENEWS provides a practical way to audit group-conditioned framing of live news in LLM outputs over time. The authors stress that the resulting metrics should be treated as time-bounded audit signals that guide human review, not as definitive or permanent fairness rankings, and not as direct evidence of harmful bias. The artifact is positioned as infrastructure for ongoing monitoring rather than one-shot model certification.

Abstract: Deployed language models are evaluated in a non-stationary environment: model versions, retrieval layers, safety systems, and real-world inputs all change over time. Static bias benchmarks remain useful, but they do not show how models frame newly emerging events for different prompted audiences. We introduce GPF-LIVENEWS, a streaming evaluation protocol and benchmark snapshot for auditing group-conditioned framing in open-ended LLM outputs. The protocol expands fresh BBC/Reuters news anchors across 42 identity labels and seven prompt families, then evaluates response bundles using semantic-sensitivity and sentiment-disparity signals. In a pilot over 12 monitoring runs and 23 hosted models, Policy/Action prompts produce the strongest semantic movement, while sentiment variation is flatter across dimensions and prompt families. The released artifact includes article metadata, prompt templates, instantiated prompts, model-output metadata, score tables, documentation, and reproduction scripts. We interpret all scores as observed-window audit signals for human review, not as permanent fairness rankings or direct proof of harmful bias.

</details>


### [20] [Large language models reorganize representational geometry during in-context learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28854)
*Hua-Dong Xiong,Li Ji-An,Robert C. Wilson,Kwonjoon Lee,Xue-Xin Wei*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies in-context learning (ICL) in large language models from a geometric / representational viewpoint.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While we know that LLMs can do in-context learning and some circuits are known, it is not well understood how the geometry of internal representations constrains the effectiveness of ICL.

Method: Define synthetic classification tasks whose labels are determined by the model’s own internal representations with controllable structure; then measure ICL performance, analyze representational geometry (separability, untangling, reorganization) during in-context learning, and fit algorithmic models (e.g., prototype-like classifiers) to LLM behavior.

Result: ICL performance correlates with the structure of the underlying representation space; successful ICL is associated with geometric reorganization that increases separability of task-relevant representations; and the observed behavior matches a prototype-like algorithm that both aggregates evidence and reshapes representations.

Conclusion: In-context learning in pretrained LLMs can be explained geometrically as online untangling and separation of task-relevant representations, with representational geometry acting as a mechanistic constraint and revealing a gap between what static pretrained representations provide and what ICL can dynamically leverage.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit remarkable flexibility: they can adapt to novel tasks from in-context examples without any parameter updates, a capability known as in-context learning (ICL). Prior work on synthetic tasks has shown that ICL can implement specific algorithms, demonstrating architectural competence, and mechanistic analyses have identified key circuits that support this behavior. However, because in-context computation -- regardless of its algorithmic form -- relies on transformations in high-dimensional representation space, it remains unclear how the geometry of that space shapes ICL effectiveness. Motivated by the neuroscience view of classification as the untangling of neural representations, we hypothesize that ICL depends on the successful online untangling of task-relevant representations. To test this idea, we study how LLMs classify in-context examples whose labels are defined by the model's own internal representations with known structure. We show that ICL performance correlates systematically with the representational structure of the underlying classification task and that successful ICL is accompanied by geometric reorganization that increases online separability. We further find that LLM behavior is well described by a prototype-like algorithm that integrates evidence while reshaping representations to support classification. These findings offer a geometric account of ICL in pretrained LLMs, establish representational geometry as a mechanistic constraint on ICL, and quantify the gap between what pretrained representations afford and what in-context learning can exploit.

</details>


### [21] [From Data to Insights: Exploring Program-of-Thoughts Prompting for Chart Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28874)
*Yutong Qu,Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a lightweight, zero-shot framework that uses Python-based Program-of-Thought reasoning and a chart-to-dictionary representation to improve factual accuracy and efficiency in chart summarization, achieving performance comparable to existing heavier methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing chart summarization with visual language models struggles with two key problems: (1) semantic visual understanding and numerical reasoning over chart data is difficult, leading to errors in the descriptions; and (2) current methods lack explicit mechanisms to verify the correctness of statistical facts and are computationally expensive. The authors aim to design a more efficient approach that can still perform reliable computational reasoning and factual verification for charts.

Method: The paper proposes a zero-shot learning strategy for chart summarization that leverages lightweight visual language models guided to perform computational reasoning using Python programs. The core idea is to insert Python as an intermediate reasoning layer: the VLM first maps the chart into a flexible dictionary-like structured representation (a novel chart-to-dictionary auxiliary task), instead of a rigid chart-to-table format. This dictionary representation is then used within a Program-of-Thought (PoT) framework, where Python code computes valid summary statistics and supports step-by-step reasoning to generate accurate natural language summaries of charts.

Result: Experiments show that the proposed strategy matches the performance of state-of-the-art chart summarization approaches on both semantic quality metrics and factual correctness metrics. Despite using lightweight VLMs and a zero-shot setting, their method achieves comparable results to more computationally heavy models, demonstrating its effectiveness and efficiency.

Conclusion: The study concludes that integrating a chart-to-dictionary auxiliary task with Program-of-Thought reasoning via Python enables lightweight visual language models to perform reliable computational reasoning for chart summarization. This zero-shot strategy effectively addresses factual verification and efficiency issues, offering a flexible and accurate alternative to traditional chart-to-table-based approaches and heavy VLM architectures.

Abstract: Charts play a critical role in conveying numerical data insights through structured visual representations. However, semantic visual understanding and numerical reasoning requirements hinder the accurate description of charts, interpreting a challenging task in chart summarization. Despite recent advancements in visual language models (VLMs), approaches lack robust mechanisms for verifying statistical fact correctness and are computationally heavy. To address this gap, this paper explores a strategy of using zero-shot learning to motivate the lightweight VLMs to perform computational reasoning, via Python programs as intermediaries to derive valid summary statistics for chart understanding. Specifically, we introduce a novel chart-to-dictionary auxiliary task, offering a more flexible representation compared to traditional chart-to-table methods, making it particularly well-suited for integration with the Program-of-Thought (PoT) strategy. Experimental results demonstrate our strategy performs on par with existing chart summarization methods across semantic and factual metrics. Code is available on https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ZeroShot-PoT-C2T-5A6B.

</details>


### [22] [GrowLoop: Self-Evolving Conversation Evaluation Seeded by Human](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28882)
*Yihang Lin,Yunze Gao,Zeyang Lin,Dongbo Li,Kun Peng,Chenglong Song,Yue Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces GrowLoop, a self-evolving system for evaluating how human-like large language model conversations are, using minimal human input and iterative LLM-driven rubric and case refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating human-likeness in open-ended dialogue is crucial as LLMs advance, but current methods struggle because human-likeness is tacit, human judgments can legitimately disagree, criteria are implicit, and what counts as human-like shifts over time with model capability and expectations. Existing approaches like static expert benchmarks, reward models, and previous self-evolving methods do not tackle all these issues together.

Method: GrowLoop starts from a small set of human-annotated seed examples and uses LLM agents to iteratively derive and refine evaluation rubrics via a Heuristic Learning process. It explicitly models regions of strong annotator agreement versus disagreement: where humans agree, the system enforces high human–AI agreement; where humans diverge, it only requires plausibility. A Rubric–Case co-evolution mechanism continuously updates both the evaluation criteria and the test cases as models change, allowing the system to be extended with new seeds when evaluation needs shift.

Result: When applied to assessing human-likeness in open-ended conversations, the rubrics produced by GrowLoop align much better with human judgments than existing evaluation methods and can also surface conversational issues that human annotators fail to notice. The resulting benchmark can clearly distinguish models of different capability levels, identify specific weaknesses, and still generalize to new conversational scenarios while adapting as model capabilities improve.

Conclusion: GrowLoop demonstrates that conversation benchmarks can be made self-evolving: starting from minimal human input, the system can continuously refine and expand both evaluation rubrics and test cases in step with advancing models and changing scenarios. This represents a shift away from manually updated or difficulty-scaled benchmarks toward dynamic, comprehensive, and adaptive evaluation of human-likeness in open-ended dialogue.

Abstract: With the rapid advancement of large language models, evaluating human-likeness in open-ended conversation has become increasingly important. However, human-likeness is a form of tacit knowledge that humans perceive intuitively, yet the underlying criteria resist explicit formulation. Human judgments vary widely, with strong agreement on some cases and legitimate disagreement on others. Meanwhile, the criteria behind human judgments remain implicit, leaving no clear basis for constructing cases. Further, what counts as human-like is not static, but evolving with model capability and human expectations. Despite progress in evaluation methods such as expert-authored benchmarks, Reward Models, and self-evolving benchmarks, none addresses all three challenges simultaneously. Therefore, we propose GrowLoop, a self-evolving conversation evaluation system that continuously adapts as models advance and scenarios shift. With minimal human seed annotations as the first mover, LLM agents iteratively extract and refine evaluation rubrics through Heuristic Learning. Human-AI agreement is required where annotators converge, while only plausibility is expected where they diverge. Moreover, the Rubric-Case co-evolution mechanism enables continuous evolution, expanded through new seeds when the evaluation target moves. Applied to human-likeness evaluation in open-ended conversation, the generated rubrics not only substantially outperform existing methods in alignment with human judgments, but also uncover issues that annotators overlook. The resulting benchmark effectively discriminates models across capability tiers and reveals where they fall short, while generalizing to new scenarios and adapting as models advance. Our work shifts the benchmarking paradigm from manual updates or difficulty scaling to comprehensive, continuous self-evolution.

</details>


### [23] [Hallucination Detection-Guided Preference Optimization for Clinical Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28910)
*Shamanth Kuthpadi Seethakantha,Dung Ngoc Thai,Vara Prasad Gudi,Simran Tiwari,Rami Matar,Avijit Mitra,Wenlong Zhao,Wael Salloum,Andrew McCallum*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes detection-guided iterative refinement and preference-learning-based finetuning to reduce hallucinations in LLM-based clinical summarization while preserving summary quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs work well for summarization but frequently hallucinate, which is especially problematic and unsafe in specialized healthcare settings like clinical note summarization.
