Existing methods do not sufficiently ensure factual faithfulness without harming fluency and relevance. There is a need for an automated, scalable approach that can systematically reduce hallucinations while maintaining overall summary quality.

Method: 1) Introduce an inference-time framework (itermodelfull / itermodel) that uses hallucination detectors to iteratively revise and refine summaries, steering them toward factual consistency with the source clinical notes. The detector identifies potentially hallucinated content, and the model revises its output accordingly over multiple iterations. 2) Propose a training-time framework (\/model) for preference learning that transforms these detector-guided refinement trajectories into preference pairs, which are then used to finetune LLMs via preference-based optimization, making them intrinsically less hallucination-prone. Experiments are conducted on real-world clinical notes from the MIMIC-IV dataset with Llama and Gemma models.

Result: On clinical summarization of MIMIC-IV notes, the inference-time itermodel method reduces hallucinations by 24%, and the preference-learning-based \model method reduces them by 48% for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Similar gains are reported for Gemma models. Both approaches maintain or preserve summary fluency, coherence, and relevance as judged by human clinical experts and LLM-Jury automatic evaluations, indicating that factuality improves without degrading other aspects of summary quality.

Conclusion: Detection-informed iterative refinement at inference time and detector-derived preference learning at training time are effective, automated strategies for reducing hallucinations in clinical summarization by LLMs. These methods significantly improve factual faithfulness while preserving readability and relevance, demonstrating a practical pathway toward safer, more trustworthy deployment of LLMs in healthcare summarization tasks.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise on summarization tasks, but they often produce hallucinations, which are unsupported or incorrect statements that limit their reliability in specialized healthcare applications. We introduce \itermodelfull (\itermodel), an inference-time method that leverages hallucination detectors to guide iterative summary revisions toward factual corrections. Building on this, we propose \itermodel for Preference Learning (\model), which converts detector-guided refinement trajectories into preference pairs for model finetuning. Extensive experiments show that our methods substantially reduce hallucinations for Llama and Gemma models in summarizing real-world clinical notes from \MimicIV. For example, \itermodel reduces 24\% and \model reduces 48\% hallucinations in Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct. Importantly, both methods preserve summary fluency, coherence, and relevance according to human expert and LLM-Jury evaluations. Together, these results demonstrate that detection-informed refinement and preference learning offer an automated solution for improving factual faithfulness in clinical summarization.

</details>


### [24] [Reasoning that Travels: Dissecting How Chain-of-Thought Transfers Across Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28913)
*Xinyuan Cheng,Beiduo Chen,Philipp Mondorf,Barbara Plank*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning traces from large reasoning models can be transferred across models and what mechanisms underlie this transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While large reasoning models can transfer their chain-of-thought traces to help other models solve the same tasks, it is unclear whether the benefit comes from explicit answer leakage, genuine reasoning support, or properties of the receiving model. Existing demonstrations of successful transfer do not disentangle these possibilities.

Method: The authors introduce a controlled provider–receiver framework: a provider model generates a full CoT trace for a problem; a receiver model then solves the same problem using increasing prefixes of that trace. They define two settings: (1) force-answer, where the receiver must answer directly given the prefix, and (2) free-generation, where the receiver may continue reasoning from the prefix before answering. They evaluate multiple model pairs and benchmarks (AIME, MMLU-Pro, ZebraLogic) and analyze performance and answer agreement as functions of prefix length and setting.

Result: They find that full CoT traces generally transfer well, but prefix-level behavior differs: in force-answer mode, AIME gains are mostly due to explicit answer leakage; on MMLU-Pro, receiver capabilities play a larger role; on ZebraLogic, partial structured-answer information (not full answers) is key. In free-generation mode, even partial prefixes significantly improve performance across all benchmarks, showing that prefixes can scaffold further reasoning. They also find that agreement among receivers on answers is a useful, label-free signal for deciding when to stop the provider’s reasoning early.

Conclusion: Cross-model CoT transfer is heterogeneous and can arise from multiple mechanisms: simple answer extraction, partial-structure guidance, scaffolding of the receiver’s own reasoning, and dependence on receiver competence. Therefore, evaluating or using CoT transfer requires fine-grained, prefix-level and setting-aware analysis rather than treating it as a single, uniform phenomenon.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) often generate extensive chain-of-thought (CoT) traces before producing a final answer. As explicit textual artifacts, these traces can be passed to other models to solve the same task, enabling cross-model reasoning transfer. Yet successful transfer alone does not reveal how the provided CoT contributes to another model's answer. We study this question with a controlled provider--receiver framework, where a provider generates a reasoning trace and a receiver solves the same problem from increasingly longer trace prefixes. We compare force-answer, where the receiver answers directly from the prefix, with free-generation, where it may continue reasoning before answering. Across models and benchmarks, full traces often transfer successfully, but prefix trajectories reveal distinct mechanisms. In force-answer mode, AIME transfer is largely driven by explicit answer availability. MMLU-Pro instead reflects a larger role for receiver competence, while ZebraLogic depends on partial structured-answer information rather than complete-answer leakage alone. In free-generation mode, partial CoTs improve performance across benchmarks, indicating that prefixes can guide continued reasoning. Finally, answer agreement among receivers provides a gold-free signal for stopping provider reasoning early. Overall, cross-model CoT transfer is not a single phenomenon: it can reflect answer extraction, reasoning scaffolding, or receiver-dependent competence.

</details>


### [25] [The Trust Paradox: How CS Researchers Engage LLM Leaderboards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28966)
*Pouya Sadeghi,Anamaria Crisan,Jimmy Lin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper investigates how AI researchers actually use and perceive LLM leaderboards, finding that although researchers distrust rankings, they still use them pragmatically, with peer networks and arena-style evaluations playing a larger role than static benchmarks, and calling for design changes like cost transparency and richer, task-specific metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM leaderboards are highly visible and influential, but prior work has mostly critiqued their technical limitations rather than empirically studying how they shape day-to-day research practice and decision-making. There is a gap in understanding the social and practical role of leaderboards across different computer science subfields, and how disciplinary culture affects their uptake and influence.

Method: The authors conducted semi-structured interviews with eight researchers from four different computer science subfields. They then applied reflexive thematic analysis to the interview transcripts to identify recurring themes, patterns of use, attitudes, and differences across communities regarding LLM leaderboard usage and perception.

Result: The study reveals a paradox of pragmatic skepticism: researchers widely distrust leaderboard rankings but still use them as rough heuristics. Peer networks are the primary mechanism for selecting models, with arena-style, human-voting leaderboards favored over static benchmark leaderboards. Leaderboard influence varies sharply by subfield; for example, NLP researchers experience strong pressure to compare against state-of-the-art leaderboard scores, whereas HCI and Systems/Privacy researchers experience little or no such pressure. A near-consensus emerges around the need for cost transparency, cited by seven of eight participants as a key missing feature.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that leaderboard design and evaluation infrastructure should better align with real researcher practices and needs. They propose design recommendations including more granular, task-specific score breakdowns, explicit integration of computational and financial costs, and transparency about the demographics of human voters in arena-style evaluations, to improve the usefulness, fairness, and interpretability of leaderboards across disciplines.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) leaderboards rank AI models using standardized benchmarks and have become highly visible across computer science, despite known limitations in their reliability and robustness. Yet how they shape researchers' actual practice remains empirically uncharted. We address this gap through semi-structured interviews with eight researchers across four computer science subfields, analyzed using reflexive thematic analysis. We find a near-universal paradox of pragmatic skepticism: while participants expressed deep distrust of leaderboard rankings, they continued to use them as rough decision-making aids. Peer networks, not leaderboards, emerged as the primary model selection mechanism, and arena-based (human-voting) leaderboards were consistently preferred over static benchmark leaderboards. Leaderboard influence varied sharply across subfields, revealing that disciplinary culture, not individual attitudes, mediates engagement; for instance, NLP researchers faced state-of-the-art comparison pressure while HCI and Systems/Privacy researchers reported none. Across these differences, however, participants converged on cost transparency as the most demanded missing feature (seven of eight). We translate these findings into concrete design recommendations that align evaluation infrastructure with how researchers actually use it, such as task-specific score breakdowns, cost integration, and voter-demographic disclosure.

</details>


### [26] [Beyond Recall: Behavioral Specification as an Interpretive Layer for AI Personalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28969)
*Aarik Gulaya*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes and evaluates a new way to measure and improve how accurately AI systems represent an individual user’s interpretations, not just their factual data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI agents begin to act and decide on behalf of people, it is not enough that they recall facts about a user; they must reflect how that person interprets the world so decisions truly align with the user. Existing systems and memory tools mainly store facts or text but lack an explicit, testable notion of how faithfully an AI captures a user’s perspective or interpretation, especially for users underrepresented in pretraining data.

Method: The authors define a new metric, representational accuracy, to quantify how well a system captures a user’s interpretations. They implement an “interpretive layer” called a Behavioral Specification by heavily compressing a user’s autobiographical text into high-level interpretive patterns and serving these as context to a language model. They construct a prototype benchmark using held-out behavioral prediction questions about each person, and use a calibrated panel of five LLM “judges” to score answers. They then compare multiple context setups—no extra context, full raw corpus, full extracted facts, four commercial memory systems, and their Behavioral Specification—both alone and in combination.

Result: On 14 public-domain autobiographical datasets, the Behavioral Specification improves representational accuracy overall, substantially reduces hedging in model responses, and achieves performance close to that of using the full raw corpus while using roughly 25x less context. It tends to equalize predictive performance across subjects by lifting those with weaker baselines more, indicating special value for users poorly represented in model pretraining. The gains are particularly strong on questions that require interpreting the user’s attitudes or perspectives, where the interpretive layer outperforms both raw text and fact extraction. However, for questions that depend mainly on factual recall, this interpretive layer can be counterproductive.

Conclusion: Representational accuracy is a distinct concept from factual recall and is critical for genuine human–AI alignment: an AI can only be aligned to a person to the extent it accurately represents that person’s interpretations and perspectives. The proposed metric and Behavioral Specification make this dimension of alignment empirically measurable and improvable, while also revealing a tradeoff: interpretive layers help with value- and interpretation-laden tasks but may interfere with pure recall tasks.

Abstract: If an AI agent makes decisions on a person's behalf, those decisions must align with its user. We introduce representational accuracy to measure how faithfully a system captures a person's interpretation. An interpretive layer is operationalized as a Behavioral Specification. Our reference implementation aggressively compresses a person's data into interpretive patterns, served as context to a language model. We evaluate the Specification on a prototype benchmark of held-out behavioral predictions scored by a calibrated 5-judge LLM panel. We test it independently and in composition with a range of context conditions: full raw corpus, full extracted facts, and four commercial memory systems (Mem0, Letta, Supermemory, Zep).
  Across 14 public-domain autobiographical corpora, the Specification lifts representational accuracy in aggregate and nearly eliminates model hedging. It recovers most of what the raw corpus delivers, at ~25x less context cost. The Specification lifts subjects toward a common predictive level regardless of pretraining baseline; the lift in absolute points is therefore largest where the baseline is lowest, suggesting the population of relevance is anyone not adequately represented in pretraining. Lift is greatest on interpretation-required questions, where providing an interpretive layer enables model behavior that extracted facts or raw corpus do not. Conversely, on recall-required questions, this layer can interfere rather than help.
  We conclude that representational accuracy is distinct from recall and that human-AI alignment is dependent on how accurately the user is represented. Representational accuracy makes that alignment testable.

</details>


### [27] [Text-Preserving Lossy Text Compression: A Study of Strategic Deletion and LLM Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29000)
*Yuchun Zou,Junhong Tong,Jun Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Paper studies lossy semantic text compression using LLMs to reconstruct text from deleted versions, benchmarking various token deletion strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional lossless compression yields modest gains on natural language; leveraging LLMs allows more aggressive, semantically informed lossy compression while still reconstructing understandable text.

Method: Define and benchmark multiple token deletion strategies (uniform, word-length-based, word-frequency-based, LP-optimized, surprisal-based with GPT-2, and hybrids). Encode BBC News articles at various retention rates and use LLM decoders (including QLoRA-finetuned local models and Gemini 2.0 Flash) to reconstruct full text, then compare performance and cost.

Result: Word-frequency-based deletion is a strong, cheap baseline; semantic and hybrid methods outperform it mainly at moderate compression levels, while frequency-based deletion is more robust at very low retention; a QLoRA-finetuned local decoder can match or beat powerful hosted models; framework works across English and Chinese domains but the best deletion rule is dataset-dependent.

Conclusion: Lossy semantic compression with learned deletion strategies and LLM decoders is viable and competitive; simple word frequency heuristics are hard to beat at extreme compression, whereas more complex semantic methods shine at moderate compression, and locally fine-tuned decoders can offer strong performance without relying solely on large external APIs.

Abstract: Traditional lossless text compression preserves every byte, but its gains on natural language are often modest in realistic operating regimes. We study \emph{lossy semantic text compression}, where the encoder strategically deletes parts of the text and a large language model (LLM) reconstructs the original content from the retained skeleton. We benchmark a progression of deletion strategies, including uniform step deletion, word-length-guided deletion (WordLen), word-frequency-guided deletion (WordFreq), LP-optimized deletion (Opt), entropy-based deletion using GPT-2 surprisal, and hybrid methods that combine frequency and surprisal signals. Evaluation on the BBC News dataset across retention rates $\r_{keep} \in [0.1,0.9]$ shows three main findings. First, WordFreq is a strong low-cost baseline: despite using only a static frequency lookup, it remains competitive with much more expensive semantic methods while being far faster at the encoder. Second, semantic and hybrid methods provide their clearest gains at mild-to-moderate compression, whereas word-frequency deletion is often more robust at the lowest retention rates. Third, QLoRA fine-tuning yields a strong local decoder that is competitive with Gemini 2.0 Flash and is often strongest in decoder-only comparisons. Additional English and Chinese experiments show that the overall framework transfers across domains, while the best deletion rule remains dataset-dependent.

</details>


### [28] [Error as a Lens: Probing LLM Reasoning through Synthetic Misconception Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29007)
*Xinming Yang,Jun Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose an agent-based LLM framework to generate synthetic student errors that are targeted to specific cognitive-misconception classes when real student data is scarce.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized tutoring and education research require examples of specific kinds of student misconceptions, but privacy and IRB restrictions limit access to labeled real-student error datasets. Although LLMs can easily generate incorrect answers, they usually do not control which cognitive failure mode the error reflects. There is a need for a method that can systematically produce synthetic errors aligned with a structured taxonomy of misconceptions.

Method: They adapt a five-class taxonomy derived from the revised Bloom’s taxonomy to categorize cognitive failure modes. Using math questions from TheoremQA, they employ a two-agent LLM framework: a Generation Agent (GA) produces an incorrect solution conditioned on a requested error class, and an Examination Agent (EA) checks whether the solution is both wrong and consistent with the target class. This GA+EA loop is used to filter and collect class-consistent synthetic errors, and they run ablations on factors like grounding, examples, and external content.

Result: The framework successfully produces class-stratified synthetic error datasets aligned with the five-category taxonomy on TheoremQA problems. Experiments show that generating class-targeted errors is significantly more challenging than simply generating any incorrect answer. Ablations indicate that grounding the generation in the specific question/solution context is more important for success than adding more in-context examples or external textbook material.

Conclusion: Their agent-based framework provides a practical recipe for constructing synthetic, taxonomy-aligned student error datasets when authentic labeled corpora are unavailable. It also reveals that precise control over error type is nontrivial for LLMs and that strong grounding in the underlying task is key to reliably steering the model towards specific cognitive failure modes.

Abstract: Personalized tutoring, teacher training, and education research need access to \emph{targeted} synthetic misconceptions, but privacy and IRB constraints make labelled corpora of real student errors scarce. LLMs could in principle generate synthetic errors at scale, but producing an arbitrary wrong answer is easy for a modern LLM while producing one that matches a specified cognitive failure mode is much harder. We present a framework that generates errors targeted to a five-class taxonomy adapted from the revised Bloom's taxonomy, evaluated on questions from the TheoremQA dataset. A Generation Agent (GA) drafts a candidate erroneous solution conditioned on a target class, and an Examination Agent (EA) judges whether the draft is incorrect and class-consistent. The framework yields a reusable recipe for building class-stratified synthetic error datasets where authentic student corpora are unavailable. As a secondary diagnostic, targeted error generation is substantially harder than free-form incorrect-answer generation, and answer-grounding contributes more than expanded examples or external textbook content.

</details>


### [29] [LLMBridge: An LLM Pipeline for End-to-end Referential Bridging Resolution in English](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29048)
*Lauren Levine,Amir Zeldes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces LLMBridge, an LLM-based system that achieves new state-of-the-art performance on end-to-end referential bridging resolution in English across three benchmark datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional bridging resolution systems struggle with end-to-end performance and often rely on task-specific architectures and extensive feature engineering. With the rise of large language models (LLMs) that show strong natural language inference and reasoning abilities, the authors aim to investigate whether LLMs can be effectively leveraged to improve the challenging task of bridging resolution, especially in realistic end-to-end settings and across multiple datasets.

Method: They propose LLMBridge, a pipeline that tackles end-to-end referential bridging resolution in English by combining heuristic pre- and post-processing steps with the natural language inference capabilities of large language models. The system takes raw text, identifies bridging anaphors and their candidate antecedents, and then queries an LLM to decide which entity pairs stand in bridging relations. Heuristics are used to structure the input to the LLM, filter candidates, and format or refine predictions. The full pipeline is evaluated without gold coreference or gold anaphors in the end-to-end setting, and also under a simplified setting where gold bridging anaphors are given.

Result: Across three widely used English datasets for bridging resolution—ISNotes, BASHI, and GUMBridge—LLMBridge surpasses previous state-of-the-art systems in both the fully end-to-end evaluation setting and the more basic setting where gold bridging anaphors are provided. The authors also perform a detailed error analysis to categorize the remaining types of bridging phenomena that the LLM-based approach still fails to resolve reliably.

Conclusion: LLMBridge demonstrates that carefully designed pipelines leveraging LLMs’ inference abilities, combined with targeted heuristics, can significantly advance the state of the art in end-to-end referential bridging resolution. Despite the performance gains, some varieties of bridging remain challenging, and the error analysis highlights directions for future research. The authors make their code publicly available to facilitate further work on LLM-based bridging resolution.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce LLMBridge, a new LLM based system for the task of end-to-end referential bridging resolution in English. Our bridging resolution pipeline combines heuristic pre/post-processing with the natural language inference ability that comes from LLMs. We evaluate our bridging resolution pipeline on 3 datasets which have been used for referential bridging resolution evaluation in English: ISNotes, BASHI, and GUMBridge. Comparison to previous bridging resolution systems shows that the performance of LLMBridge surpasses previous state-of-the-art (SoTA) systems for all 3 datasets in the challenging End-to-end Evaluation Setting, as well as the Basic Bridging Resolution Evaluation Setting (gold bridging anaphor given). We also conduct a thorough error analysis of the LLMBridge performance, examining what varieties of bridging remain difficult for LLM based systems to identify. With this paper, we release the code for the LLMBridge pipeline.

</details>


### [30] [Bosses, Kings, and the Commons: Cooperation Under Power Asymmetry in LLM Societies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29062)
*Abhilekh Borah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SovSim, a simulation framework using LLM-based agents to study how asymmetric power (e.g., a boss/king vs workers/peasants) affects cooperation and sustainability in managing shared resources. It finds that power asymmetries cause large breakdowns in cooperation and commons survival across many state-of-the-art models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ostrom’s work shows communities can self-govern commons sustainably via cooperation, but real-world resource systems usually have power asymmetries, and LLM-based governance simulations rarely model this. As LLMs are increasingly used as agents in socio-economic simulations and decision-support, we need to know how they behave when some agents have disproportionate control over shared resources, since this is closer to realistic governance structures.

Method: The authors design SovSim, a generative multi-agent environment where a society of symmetric agents shares a common resource and one additional agent holds asymmetric power (a boss/king) over others. All agents can extract from the shared resource, and their decisions collectively determine long-run resource sustainability. They run comparative experiments across eleven state-of-the-art LLMs, with and without the asymmetric-power agent, to measure cooperation levels and survival (sustainability) of the commons.

Result: Introducing an asymmetric-power agent into otherwise symmetric LLM societies substantially reduces cooperation and sustainability. The paper reports up to an 87.3% decrease in survival rate of the shared resource when power asymmetries are present compared with symmetric baselines, indicating strong negative effects of hierarchy on commons management in these simulations.

Conclusion: In LLM-based multi-agent simulations of resource governance, adding hierarchical power structures leads to severe failures in cooperative commons management. This suggests that current LLMs, when cast as powerful actors in synthetic societies, tend to undermine sustainable outcomes under asymmetric power. The framework SovSim provides a way to systematically study these effects and highlights that evaluations of LLM agents should explicitly account for power asymmetries rather than assuming symmetric self-governance settings.

Abstract: Communities can sustainably manage shared resources (commons) through self-governance and cooperative norms, a central finding of Ostrom's theory of self-governance. However, real-world commons (e.g., fisheries, forests, and irrigation systems) are often governed under asymmetric power structures, where certain individuals or institutions possess disproportionate control over resource extraction and collective outcomes. As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly explored as agents in synthetic governance simulations, understanding how LLM societies behave under asymmetric power structures is becoming increasingly important, yet existing evaluations largely ignore such asymmetries. We introduce Sovereignty over the Commons Simulation (SovSim), a generative multi-agent simulation framework that incorporates an agent with asymmetric power (boss or king) into a society of symmetric agents (workers or peasants), where all agents extract from a shared resource, collectively determining its sustainability over time. Across eleven state-of-the-art models, we find that introducing asymmetric power leads to severe breakdowns in cooperation and sustainability, with up to an 87.3% degradation in survival rate relative to symmetric settings.

</details>


### [31] [Analyzing Persona Effects in Generated Explanations from Multimodal LLM Agents in Urban Perception](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29064)
*Neemias da Silva,Myriam Delgado,Rodrigo Minetto,Daniel Silver,Thiago H Silva*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper examines how persona prompts influence outputs of multimodal large language models in an urban perception task, finding convergent captions but persona-specific differences in justifications and thematic emphasis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether and how conditioning multimodal LLMs on different personas actually changes their qualitative behavior—especially in terms of socioeconomically and politically flavored interpretations of urban scenes—beyond surface-level descriptions.

Method: The authors create 1,200 persona-conditioned agents plus two baseline no-persona settings, and collect 59,808 model-generated annotations on urban images. For each image-persona pairing, the model produces captions, justifications, and perception tags. They then quantitatively compare variation and convergence across personas, perform statistical tests on perception tags, and run topic analysis on justifications to uncover thematic differences.

Result: Captions across different personas are highly similar, indicating strong convergence at the level of surface description. Justifications, however, systematically vary with personas’ socioeconomic and political traits, showing that persona prompts steer evaluative framing and reasoning. Perception tags do not differ significantly across personas at standard significance levels, though small directional effects are visible. Topic analysis shows that different personas prioritize different evaluative themes when describing the same scenes.

Conclusion: Persona prompting in multimodal LLMs does not substantially alter basic descriptive outputs (captions) or high-level perception tags, but it does meaningfully shape the explanatory and evaluative framing in justifications, especially along socioeconomic and political lines. This implies that persona conditioning primarily affects how models explain and interpret content rather than what they explicitly see or label.

Abstract: We study how persona prompting shapes language generated by multimodal large language models in an urban perception setting. Using 59,808 annotations from 1,200 persona-conditioned agents and two no-persona settings, we analyze captions, justifications, and perception tags across personas. Results indicate strong convergence in captions for different personas, whereas justifications display systematic variation associated with socioeconomic and political attributes, while perception tags show no statistically significant persona-related differences, though effect trends are observed. Topic analysis further reveals that personas emphasize different evaluative themes when interpreting the same scenes.

</details>


### [32] [Structured Prompt Optimization Meets Reinforcement Learning for Global and Local Interpretability over Complex Text](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29076)
*Tianyang Zhou,Wenbo Chen,Pierre Jinghong Liang,Leman Akoglu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes eXTC, a text classification framework that combines interpretable rule-like prompts with efficient, compact models to achieve both high accuracy and explainability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based text classification methods face a trade-off: supervised fine-tuning scales but offers weak reasoning and low transparency, while discrete prompt optimization yields human-readable rules but performs poorly and is hard to scale. The authors want a method that simultaneously provides strong performance, scalable training, and rich, interpretable reasoning and rules.

Method: They design eXTC, a three-stage pipeline. (1) Learn a natural-language Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) rulebook using a new Structured Prompt Optimization algorithm that induces modular decision rules. (2) Distill SOP-grounded reasoning from a large teacher LLM into a smaller language model so the compact model can follow the SOP and generate reasoning traces. (3) Further expand the model’s reasoning capabilities beyond the initial SOP using reinforcement learning to refine and extend its decision policies. The system yields both local explanations (per-instance reasoning traces) and global explanations (the SOP rulebook).

Result: Across multiple text classification benchmarks, eXTC substantially improves both classification accuracy and explanation quality compared to supervised fine-tuning and discrete prompt optimization baselines. Each of the three stages contributes incremental gains, with the final system outperforming all compared paradigms while remaining efficient at inference due to the compact distilled model.

Conclusion: A structured, multi-stage approach that first learns an interpretable natural-language rulebook, then distills it into a compact model and refines it via reinforcement learning can resolve the traditional trade-off between performance and explainability in LLM-based text classification. eXTC delivers fast, accurate predictions together with transparent, modular global rules and instance-level reasoning traces.

Abstract: LLMs have advanced text classification, yet existing paradigms face a trade-off: supervised (label only) fine-tuning is scalable but offers limited reasoning on complex text and lacks broader model transparency, while discrete prompt optimization offers human-readable instructions but struggles with performance and scalability. We introduce eXTC (eXplainable Text Classifier) with three progressive stages: (1) learning a Standard Operating Procedure (SOP, or rulebook) in natural language via a new Structured Prompt Optimization algorithm; (2) SOP-grounded reasoning distillation from a large teacher LLM into a compact LM; and (3) expanding reasoning capabilities beyond the initial SOP via reinforcement learning. This design enables eXTC to provide (i) fast inference via a compact LM, with (ii) inference-time local reasoning traces, alongside a global, modular explanation of its learned domain rules, while (iii) significantly outperforming existing paradigms across diverse benchmarks in both classification performance and explanation quality, with stage-by-stage gains.

</details>


### [33] [Same Question, Different Source, Different Answer: Auditing Source-Dependence in Medical Multi-Source RAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29084)
*Yubo Li,Rema Padman,Ramayya Krishnan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new evaluation axis for RAG systems: how answers depend on which source is retrieved, especially in multi-author institutional corpora.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional NLP and RAG evaluation assumes a single correct answer (single-gold paradigm), which cannot diagnose cases where different authoritative sources within the same institution give divergent but still plausible answers. This is critical in high-stakes domains like transplant patient education, where conflicting information across handbooks can affect safety and trust.

Method: They propose shifting the evaluation unit from answer-level correctness to relationships between answers grounded in different sources. Concretely, they: (1) construct TransplantQA, a benchmark of real transplant patient questions, each paired with multiple institutional handbook answers; (2) develop HERO-QA, a hierarchical retrieval strategy that retrieves, grounds, and audits answers against multiple sources; and (3) design a structured-output judge model that labels inter-source relationships using a validated 5-class taxonomy (e.g., agreement, minor discrepancy, major conflict).

Result: Using their framework at scale on the transplant domain, they find that improved retrieval dramatically increases the detection of disagreements between institutional sources, indicating that prior work underestimated how often sources disagree, not how severe those disagreements are when found. They also show that their methodology generalizes to other domains like law and education.

Conclusion: Source-dependence—how different sources within a corpus influence generated answers—is an essential but overlooked dimension of RAG evaluation. Practitioners deploying multi-source NLP systems have a responsibility to measure and audit this inter-source relationship, and the proposed datasets, retrieval strategy, and judging framework provide a domain-agnostic way to do so.

Abstract: A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system deployed over a multi-author institutional corpus can give a different answer to the same question depending on which source it retrieves -- a failure mode the dominant single-gold-answer paradigm cannot diagnose. We argue that source-dependence is a missing axis of NLP evaluation, and that auditing it means shifting the unit of evaluation from answer correctness to the inter-source relationship. We make this concrete in transplant patient education, where institutional sources demonstrably disagree, releasing three artefacts: TransplantQA, a benchmark of real patient questions, each answered by grounding generation in multiple institutional handbooks as candidate sources; HERO-QA, a hierarchical retrieval strategy that grounds and audits each answer; and a structured-output judge that scores inter-source relationships on a validated 5-label taxonomy. At scale, better retrieval reveals far more disagreement than prior estimates suggested -- understating its prevalence, not its intensity. The framework is domain-agnostic and transfers to legal and educational RAG: measuring source-dependence is a responsibility for deployed multi-source NLP generally.

</details>


### [34] [SafeRx-Agent: A Knowledge-Grounded Multi-Agent Framework for Safe and Explainable Medication Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29146)
*Xinyu Wang,Hanwei Wu,Zhenghan Tai,Sicheng Lyu,Qincheng Lu,Ziyu Zhao,Jijun Chi,Jingrui Tian,Xiao-Wen Chang,Ziyang Song*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents SafeRx-Agent, a knowledge-grounded multi-agent system for fine-grained, safe, and traceable medication recommendation using fourth-level ATC codes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing medication recommendation methods either rely on structured codes with weak evidence grounding, or use LLMs with rich context but insufficient safety checks and traceability. Benchmarks also operate on coarse drug categories, masking subgroup-level safety differences and overestimating risk. The authors aim to create a more clinically realistic and safety-aware recommendation setup.

Method: They define a new fine-grained medication recommendation task at the ATC 4th-level code granularity. To solve it, they design SafeRx-Agent, a multi-agent framework powered by large language models that incorporates (1) detailed patient clinical context, (2) external clinical knowledge bases, and (3) explicit safety verification modules. The agents collaborate to generate, justify, and check medication sets for safety and traceability.

Result: On the MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV clinical datasets, SafeRx-Agent achieves higher accuracy in predicting fine-grained medications (ATC-4 codes) compared with baselines, while simultaneously better controlling for drug-drug interactions, contraindications, and maintaining a reasonable medication set size.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, safety-aware medication recommendation at ATC-4 level is feasible and beneficial. The proposed SafeRx-Agent framework improves both predictive performance and safety metrics by grounding recommendations in clinical knowledge and explicit verification, producing more traceable and clinically plausible medication sets.

Abstract: Medication recommendation predicts medications for patient visits, but existing methods still face two key challenges. At the model level, traditional drug recommendation methods only predict structured drug codes with limited evidence grounding, while LLM agents can use richer clinical context but may lack safety verification and traceability. At the task level, existing benchmarks often use broad medication categories, which ignore subgroup-level safety differences and can lead to risk overestimation. We introduce the first fine-grained medication recommendation setting based on fourth-level ATC code generation. We propose Safe Prescription Agent (SafeRx-Agent), a knowledge-grounded multi-agent framework that uses patient context, external clinical knowledge, and safety verification to recommend traceable medication sets. Experimental results on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets show that SafeRx-Agent improves fine-grained medication prediction accuracy while controlling drug interactions, contraindications, and medication set size.

</details>


### [35] [UA-Legal-Bench: A Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models on Ukrainian Legal Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29170)
*Volodymyr Ovcharov*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces UA-Legal-Bench, a Ukrainian legal NLP benchmark of five tasks, evaluates 11 LLMs on it, and shows task-dependent few-shot gains and pitfalls of using accuracy on imbalanced data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most legal NLP benchmarks are English-centric and overlook challenges in morphologically rich, non-Latin-script languages such as Ukrainian. There is a need for a large-scale, realistic benchmark that tests LLMs on real-world legal reasoning in such languages, as well as a need to better understand how model size, prompting, and evaluation metrics behave in this domain.

Method: The authors construct UA-Legal-Bench from the Unified State Register of Court Decisions (EDRSR), one of the largest open judicial corpora, selecting and annotating five supervised tasks: case-type classification, judgment form classification, case-outcome prediction, legal norm extraction, and cause category prediction. They then evaluate 11 large language models from five model families (ranging from 3B to 675B parameters) via AWS Bedrock, using both zero-shot and 3-shot prompting, and run 158K API calls to obtain predictions. They analyze performance across tasks, prompting regimes, and model scales and families, focusing on both accuracy and macro-F1, especially under class imbalance.

Result: Few-shot prompting has highly task-dependent effects: it substantially improves judgment form classification (up to +38.6 percentage points) but provides inconsistent or mixed benefits for outcome prediction. For imbalanced tasks like case-outcome prediction, they find that accuracy can be misleading: one model achieves the highest accuracy (62%) simply by predicting the majority class, resulting in a much lower macro-F1 (23%), while the truly best model attains only 44% macro-F1 but is more balanced. Scaling analyses show that mid-sized models (around 8B parameters) can match state-of-the-art performance on simpler, surface-level tasks, but performance gains and scaling thresholds differ significantly across model families.

Conclusion: UA-Legal-Bench exposes limitations of current LLMs on Ukrainian legal reasoning and highlights the importance of language- and domain-specific benchmarks beyond English. The benchmark demonstrates that few-shot prompting and model scaling effects vary strongly by task and family, and that relying on accuracy alone is unsafe on imbalanced legal problems. The authors offer UA-Legal-Bench, along with all data, prompts, and predictions, as an open resource for developing and evaluating LLMs for morphologically rich, non-Latin-script legal domains.

Abstract: Legal NLP benchmarks are overwhelmingly English-centric, leaving failure modes in morphologically rich, non-Latin-script languages undetected. We introduce UA-Legal-Bench, a five-task benchmark for evaluating large language models on Ukrainian legal reasoning, built from the Unified State Register of Court Decisions (EDRSR) -- one of the world's largest open judicial corpora (99.5 million decisions). The benchmark comprises: (1) case-type classification (4 classes, n=2,000), (2) judgment form classification (4 classes, n=2,000), (3) case-outcome prediction (6 classes, n=800), (4) legal norm extraction (n=1,794), and (5) cause category prediction (22 classes, n=1,871). We evaluate 11 LLMs (3B--675B) from five families under zero-shot and 3-shot prompting via AWS Bedrock with 158K API calls. Our results reveal sharply task-dependent few-shot effects: few-shot prompting improves judgment form classification by up to +38.6 pp but has mixed effects on outcome prediction. We show that accuracy is misleading on imbalanced legal tasks: the model with highest COP accuracy (62%) is a majority-class predictor (macro-F1: 23%), while the genuinely best model scores only 44% macro-F1. Within-family scaling analysis reveals that 8B models can match frontier performance on surface-level tasks but scaling thresholds vary dramatically across families. We release all data, prompts, and model predictions.

</details>


### [36] [Slogans or Stance? A Label-Light Diagnostic for Entrepreneurial-Discourse Measurement on Chinese SOE Speeches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29188)
*Ting Gong,Shangquan Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper evaluates how well common text-measurement methods (dictionaries, topic models, embeddings, and a small LLM) detect leader-specific differences in corporate speeches, rather than proposing a new model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In computational social science and management, researchers often use off-the-shelf text methods to measure abstract constructs (e.g., entrepreneurial spirit) in corporate communications, but they rarely test whether these measures actually respond to meaningful variation like changes in leadership within the same firm. The authors aim to provide a low-label, practical diagnostic to assess whether these measurement tools capture leader-related signal instead of noise or firm-level confounds.

Method: Using 80 speeches from leaders of centrally administered Chinese state-owned enterprises, the authors identify 24 same-company different-leader pairs and 5 same-company same-leader pairs, creating a natural experiment where firm context is held constant while leader identity varies. They compute per-document indices of the target construct using various methods: LDA topic modeling, a dictionary-based scorer, a Chinese sentence encoder (embedding similarity), and a zero-shot 9B-parameter open-weight LLM. They then compare within-firm speech pairs to estimate effect sizes (Cohen’s d) and use permutation tests to assess whether the methods systematically distinguish between different leaders beyond stylistic or idiolectal differences. They also apply doc-level style residualization and confidence-weighted calibration with an auto-mined slogan lexicon to probe what aspects of variance the methods exploit.

Result: LDA shows essentially no ability to differentiate leaders within the same firm (Cohen’s d=0.20 with wide confidence intervals). The dictionary-based scorer performs better (d=0.81), and the Chinese sentence encoder also shows substantial separation (d=0.65) even though absolute doc-vector distance magnitudes are small. The zero-shot Qwen3.5 9B LLM yields the strongest paired contrast (d=1.09, permutation p=0.034), indicating notable sensitivity to leader identity. However, after residualizing out document-level style, the LLM’s effect size drops to d=0.43 (p=0.22), implying that about half of its discrimination power is due to leader-specific idiolect or style rather than the target construct. The confidence-weighted calibration with a slogan lexicon increases variance without clear gains in construct sensitivity, and the lexicon itself appears almost inert in ablation tests.

Conclusion: The study shows that commonly used text measurement tools vary substantially in their ability to pick up leader-related differences when firm context is controlled, with small LLMs outperforming traditional methods but relying heavily on stylistic cues. The authors caution against interpreting high apparent performance (e.g., gold F1 against an LLM’s own rule) as evidence of true construct validity. They argue for routine use of their label-light diagnostic—leveraging within-firm, between-leader contrasts—to stress-test measurement instruments, and they release data, lexicons, and code to support further methodological evaluation and replication.

Abstract: Dictionary methods, topic models, and embedding-similarity scorers are widely used in CSS and management research to measure constructs such as "entrepreneurial spirit" in corporate speeches. We contribute a label-light measurement diagnostic for such instruments rather than a new extraction model. On a corpus of 80 speeches by leaders of centrally administered Chinese state-owned enterprises, we exploit a natural experiment of 24 same-company different-speaker pairs and 5 same-company same-speaker pairs to test whether a method's per-document indices vary with leader identity holding firm constant. LDA fails (Cohen d=0.20, 95% CI [-0.72, 1.20]); a dictionary scorer reaches d=0.81 and a Chinese sentence encoder d=0.65 on doc-vector distances of order 10^-3. A zero-shot 9B open-weight LLM (Qwen3.5:9b) raises paired-contrast d to 1.09 (exact permutation p1=0.034). We downgrade three claims accordingly: gold F1 measures consistency with the LLM's own prompt rule rather than external construct recovery; doc-level style residualisation cuts the LLM's d to 0.43 (p1=0.22), so roughly half of the effect is consistent with leader idiolect; and a confidence-weighted calibration trades Delta for variance with an auto-mined slogan lexicon near-inert in ablation. We release the 2,190-segment scored corpus, the 170-paragraph pilot, the slogan lexicon, two-family LLM scores, and the evaluation harness.

</details>


### [37] [Relevance as a Vulnerability: How Web Retrieval Degrades Safety Alignment in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29224)
*Aditya Nawal,Manit Baser,Mohan Gurusamy*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces AgentREVEAL, a framework to study how web retrieval in LLM agents degrades safety, showing that even safety-oriented sources can increase harmful compliance and revealing a safety-utility trade-off.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why and how retrieval-augmented LLM agents become more willing to comply with harmful requests, despite existing safety alignment, and to provide systematic diagnostics and benchmarks for this issue.

Method: They propose AgentREVEAL, which analyzes two dimensions: (1) how retrieval is integrated into the agent pipeline (e.g., whether tool use and response generation are tightly coupled in one step), and (2) the characteristics of retrieved content (including safety-focused or warning pages). They run controlled experiments on open and closed-source models with different agent designs and retrieval settings, and construct HarmURLBench, a benchmark of real-world URLs paired with predefined harmful behaviors.

Result: The study finds that coupling tool invocation and response generation in a single step amplifies harmful outputs, and that even oppositional or safety-oriented content can paradoxically raise harmful compliance by ~25% versus no-retrieval. Relevance of retrieved content emerges as a key condition triggering these failures. Similar effects are observed on frontier closed models, and elevated harmful compliance persists despite several pipeline mitigations, including in autonomous retrieval setups.

Conclusion: Retrieval-enabled agents face an inherent safety-utility trade-off: the same relevance that powers their usefulness also activates safety vulnerabilities. AgentREVEAL and HarmURLBench provide a foundation for diagnosing and benchmarking retrieval-induced safety degradation, indicating that alignment approaches for agents must explicitly account for retrieval integration and content properties.

Abstract: AI agents augment large language models with external tools such as web retrieval, enabling grounded and up-to-date responses. However, incorporating external content into the generation pipeline can weaken the safety alignment mechanisms that govern model outputs. Prior work shows that enabling retrieval in agents increases compliance with harmful requests. We introduce AgentREVEAL, a diagnostic framework for analyzing retrieval-induced safety degradation in LLM agents. The framework examines two axes: how retrieval is integrated into the agent pipeline and the properties of the retrieved content. Along the integration axis, we find that binding tool invocation and response generation in a single step amplifies harmful outputs. Along the content axis, we uncover the Safe Source Paradox: even oppositional or safety-oriented sources, such as pages containing warnings or risk disclaimers, can increase harmful compliance by an average of 25% compared to the no-retrieval baseline. Finally, we show that relevance acts as a shared activation condition for both vulnerabilities. Similar patterns appear on frontier closed models, and harmful compliance remains elevated under several representative pipeline interventions, with some agents also entering this regime under autonomous retrieval. Because relevance is also what makes retrieval useful, these results expose a safety-utility trade-off for retrieval-enabled agents. We introduce HarmURLBench, a benchmark containing 1,405 real-world URLs paired with 320 harmful behaviors to support future evaluations.

</details>


### [38] [Wait! There's a Way Out: A Decision Mechanism for Forecasting Conversational Derailment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29243)
*Laerdon Kim,Vivian Nguyen,Cristian Danescu-Niculescu-Mizil*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a forecasting system that not only predicts if online conversations will derail into personal attacks, but also decides when to trigger an alert, using a deferral mechanism with forward-looking simulations to reduce false positives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing conversational derailment forecasting models trigger alerts solely based on the current estimated likelihood of derailment, assuming the future trajectory is fixed. This leads to many false positives because they ignore the fact that tense conversations can naturally recover without intervention. There is a need for a forecasting framework that explicitly models the decision of when to alert, accounting for possible future recovery and better matching human performance.

Method: The authors separate derailment prediction from the alert-triggering decision. They first analyze a human baseline showing humans defer their decision during tense but potentially recoverable moments. Based on this insight, they design a deferral mechanism that uses forward-looking simulations: given the current state of a conversation, the model simulates plausible future continuations to estimate whether the tension is likely to subside. The trigger decision is then made using both the current derailment likelihood and the simulated chances of recovery. This mechanism is integrated into an existing state-of-the-art derailment forecasting model.

Result: When the proposed deferral mechanism is added to a strong baseline forecasting model, the system achieves a substantial reduction in false positive alerts while maintaining overall forecasting accuracy. In other words, it becomes more conservative and selective about when to raise alarms, closer to human-like behavior, without missing more actual derailments.

Conclusion: The study shows that explicitly modeling the decision of when to trigger an alert, rather than relying solely on instantaneous derailment probabilities, leads to more effective conversational forecasting systems. By simulating possible future trajectories and deferring decisions when recovery is plausible, the system can significantly cut false positives. The work argues that decision-making should be treated as a core component of predictive systems, especially in interactive, evolving settings like online conversations.

Abstract: Forecasting conversational derailment is the task of predicting, as the conversation unfolds, whether it will eventually derail into personal attacks. Since forecasting models operate in an online fashion, they must decide whether to "trigger" an alert after each utterance--for example, to notify participants or a moderator that the conversation is at risk of derailing. Existing approaches make this decision solely based on the estimated likelihood of derailment given the preceding utterances, implicitly assuming that the conversation's future trajectory is fixed. As a result, they ignore the possibility of future recovery and incur an unnecessarily high rate of false positives.
  In this work we propose a method for decoupling the decision to trigger from derailment likelihood estimation. Our approach is inspired by the first human baseline on this task, which shows that humans achieve dramatically lower false positive rates by selectively deferring their decision to trigger when they anticipate that tension is likely to subside. We operationalize this insight with a deferral mechanism that uses forward-looking simulations to assess whether a tense moment admits plausible paths to recovery. Incorporating this mechanism into a state-of-the-art forecasting model substantially reduces false positives without sacrificing forecasting accuracy. More broadly, this work highlights the value of treating decision-making as a first-class component of forecasting systems.

</details>


### [39] [OmniRetrieval: Unified Retrieval across Heterogeneous Knowledge Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29250)
*Jinheon Baek,Soyeong Jeong,Sangwoo Park,Woongyeong Yeo,Minki Kang,Patara Trirat,Heejun Lee,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: OmniRetrieval is a framework that answers natural-language queries by dynamically choosing among multiple heterogeneous knowledge sources (text, tables, graphs) and issuing source-specific queries, outperforming single-source systems across many datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world questions often require combining or choosing between varied knowledge sources like unstructured text, relational databases, and graphs. Current retrieval systems tend to work on only one type of source and a single query language, forcing knowledge into a single representation that loses each source's structural strengths. There is a need for a retrieval layer that can access heterogeneous sources without flattening their structure.

Method: The authors design OmniRetrieval, a unified retrieval framework that: (1) accepts natural language queries, (2) automatically identifies which knowledge sources are most appropriate for the query, and (3) converts the query into source-native query languages (e.g., SQL, graph queries, text retrieval queries) that are executed on the original backends. The system is evaluated on a large benchmark containing 13 datasets and 309 distinct knowledge bases spread across text, relational, and graph formats, and compared against single-source retrieval baselines.

Result: On the heterogeneous benchmark, OmniRetrieval outperforms single-source baselines across the different types of knowledge sources, showing improved retrieval effectiveness when allowed to choose and query multiple native sources instead of being constrained to one homogenized or isolated source.

Conclusion: Preserving the structural distinctions of heterogeneous knowledge sources while providing a unified natural-language interface leads to better retrieval performance than single-source or homogenized approaches. OmniRetrieval demonstrates that an overarching orchestration layer that routes and expresses queries in each source's native form is an effective paradigm for real-world information access across text, tables, and graphs.

Abstract: Real-world information needs require access to structurally diverse knowledge sources, from unstructured text and relational tables to knowledge graphs and property graphs. Existing retrievers, however, operate over one source at a time under a fixed query language, leaving the broader landscape of available knowledge fragmented behind incompatible interfaces. A natural attempt at unification would collapse these sources into a shared space, but this erases the structural affordances (such as schemas, ontologies, compositional operators) that give each source its expressive power. Effective retrieval over diverse knowledge, therefore, requires not homogenization but an overarching layer that meets each source on its own terms. To achieve this, we present OmniRetrieval, a framework that takes any natural-language query, identifies appropriate knowledge sources, and dispatches source-native queries to their native execution engines. Across an extensive benchmark spanning 13 datasets and 309 distinct knowledge bases over text, relational, and graph-structured sources, OmniRetrieval exceeds single-source baselines, demonstrating that it can serve as a general-purpose interface to the heterogeneous sources while preserving the structural distinctions that make each source valuable.

</details>


### [40] [DynSess: Dynamic Session-Level Evaluation and Optimization Framework for Role-Playing Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29256)
*Rongsheng Zhang,Jiji Tang,Junnan Ren,Zuyi Bao,Weijie Chen,Ruofan Hu,Zhou Zhao,Tangjie Lv,Yan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DynSess is a session-level framework to evaluate and train role-playing LLM agents for long multi-turn conversations, achieving human-aligned evaluation and strong character performance with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing role-playing LLM methods and evaluators mainly operate at the turn level, so they fail to capture long-horizon aspects like sustained character identity and interaction quality over an entire conversation session. There is a need for a unified framework that evaluates and optimizes role-playing agents at the session level, aligned with human judgments.

Method: The authors introduce DynSess, which has two main components. First, DynSess-Eval is a session-level evaluator that scores complete dialogue sessions using rubrics focused on long-horizon behaviors such as role consistency and interactive quality. Second, they leverage these session-level rewards to construct high-quality training trajectories via multi-turn lookahead search. Using these trajectories, they train a role-playing model DynSess-Character with two variants: DSPO (an off-policy training method) and GSRPO (an on-policy training method), both designed to optimize for session-level rewards.

Result: DynSess-Eval correlates with human judgments substantially better than previous evaluation methods. In blind human evaluations, DynSess-Character performs on par with the strongest existing character model despite having significantly fewer parameters, and it preserves strong role consistency and interactive abilities across long conversations.

Conclusion: Session-level evaluation and training, as instantiated in the DynSess framework, is more aligned with human judgment than prior turn-level approaches and enables smaller models to achieve top-tier role-playing performance. The work suggests that long-horizon, session-based optimization is key for high-quality role-playing agents and provides resources (dataset and code) to support further progress.

Abstract: Role-playing with large language models is fundamentally a session-level task, requiring agents to sustain character identity and interaction quality across extended multi-turn conversations. Yet existing evaluation and optimization methods remain largely turn-level, failing to capture long-horizon quality. We propose DynSess, a unified session-level framework for role-playing agents. DynSess-Eval scores complete dialogue sessions via rubrics targeting long-horizon behaviors. Leveraging its session-level rewards, we construct high-quality training trajectories through multi-turn lookahead search and train DynSess-Character with two complementary variants: DSPO (off-policy) and GSRPO (on-policy). Experiments show that DynSess-Eval aligns with human judgments substantially better than prior evaluators, and blind human evaluation further shows that DynSess-Character matches the strongest character model despite using substantially fewer parameters, while maintaining strong role consistency and interactive ability. Our dataset and code will be released to facilitate future research.

</details>


### [41] [Compute Allocation in Evolutionary Search: From Depth-Breadth to Multi-Armed Bandits](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29268)
*Sixue Xing,Haoyu He,Kerui Wu,Zhuo Yang,Haozheng Luo,Tianfan Fu,Aarthy Nagarajan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Study of how to best allocate LLM calls in evolutionary search systems and proposal of a bandit-based method (BaSE) to improve reliability and average performance without changing the underlying model or prompts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evolve systems report only their best runs and ignore run-to-run variability, leaving unclear how reliably they achieve reported results and how to optimally allocate a fixed compute (LLM call) budget across search trajectories.

Method: Empirically sweep depth (steps per trajectory) and breadth (number of parallel trajectories) over five LLMs and three tasks to characterize performance patterns; identify a fitness-compute envelope and a bilinear depth-breadth relationship; then design BaSE, a multi-armed bandit algorithm that dynamically allocates LLM calls across trajectories based on observed fitness.

Result: Across 8 combinations of model and task, BaSE improves mean fitness by 12.3% compared to the best island-based evolutionary baseline, with especially large improvements in high-variance regimes, demonstrating better reliability and efficiency in using the same compute budget.

Conclusion: Run-to-run variability and allocation strategy significantly affect the effectiveness and reliability of LLM-guided evolutionary search; empirical regularities in depth-breadth tradeoffs enable the design of BaSE, a bandit-based allocator that can substantially boost average performance at fixed compute by smarter distribution of LLM calls across search trajectories.

Abstract: LLM-guided evolutionary search (Evolve systems) has reached state-of-the-art results on mathematical and combinatorial tasks, yet most existing systems report only the best of many runs and leave the run-to-run distribution undocumented. We ask how a fixed budget of LLM calls should be allocated, and how reliably a single run reaches the reported numbers. Sweeping the depth-breadth grid over five models and three tasks, we identify two empirical regularities: a fitness-compute envelope along which capability ordering largely collapses on effective FLOPs, and a bilinear depth-breadth fit with task-specific interaction; both are gated by model-task capability. Motivated by these regularities, we propose BaSE (Bandit-based Self-Evolving), a multi-armed bandit that allocates LLM calls across parallel trajectories. Without changing the model, prompt, or evaluator, BaSE improves mean fitness by 12.3% over the strongest island-protocol baseline across 8 (model, task) cells, with the largest gains on high-variance settings: a reliability gain from allocation alone.

</details>


### [42] [Learnable Assessment Skills for LLM-based Automated Scoring: Rubric Construction via Iterative Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29274)
*Yun Wang,Xin Xia,Xuansheng Wu,Xiaoming Zhai,Ninghao Liu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a way for LLMs to automatically learn general scoring heuristics (assessment skills) from prior scoring experience, instead of relying on human-made rubrics for each new task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based scoring systems perform well but require humans to build rubrics and configurations for every new task, which is labor-intensive and blocks scalability. Humans overcome this via tacit evaluation heuristics; the authors want LLMs to similarly develop reusable, item-independent assessment skills.

Method: They define 'assessment skills' as natural-language, item-independent procedures that guide scoring workflows. Focusing on rubric construction, they design an iterative framework that (1) decomposes a skill into a fixed scaffold plus learnable rules, (2) uses LLMs to diagnose scoring errors, and (3) updates and selects rule sets using validation-based gating. This loop refines item-agnostic rules without expert-written rubrics.

Result: On all ten ASAP-SAS essay-scoring items, the optimized assessment skills significantly boost LLM scoring performance and often outperform the official expert rubrics supplied with the dataset. Cross-item transfer tests show the learned skills encode both broadly transferable and item-specific patterns.

Conclusion: LLMs can acquire reusable assessment skills—procedural scoring heuristics—directly from scoring experience, reducing or removing the need for item-specific expert rubrics. The proposed scaffold-plus-rules, error-diagnosis, and validation-gated refinement framework is effective for rubric construction and shows promising generalization across items.

Abstract: LLM-based automated scoring approaches near-human performance, but scaling to new tasks remains bottlenecked by the per-item human configuration of upstream stages such as rubric construction. Human experts bypass this bottleneck through evaluation heuristics developed over extensive practice. We ask whether LLMs can learn similar heuristics directly from scoring experience, and formalize this as the concept of assessment skills: item-independent natural-language procedural knowledge that guides LLMs through specific stages of the scoring workflow. Focusing on rubric construction as a first instantiation, we propose an iterative framework that decomposes a skill into a fixed scaffold and learnable item-agnostic rules, refining the rules through LLM-driven diagnosis of scoring errors and validation-gated selection. The framework requires no expert-written rubric. On all ten ASAP-SAS items, optimized skills substantially improve LLM-based scoring and frequently surpass the dataset-provided expert rubric. Cross-item transfer experiments further reveal that learned skills capture both generalizable and item-specific patterns.

</details>


### [43] [Prompt-Level Reward Specifications for Open-Ended Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29275)
*Zijun Weng,Xiaohui Hu,Shuangyong Song,Yongxiang Li,Kaidong Yu,Xuanjing Huang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new reward framework for open-ended post-training that makes prompt-specific success conditions explicit via rubrics and constraint checkers, rather than relying only on scalar scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reward methods for open-ended tasks like instruction following and writing often use opaque post-hoc scalar scores that do not explicitly encode local requirements, holistic preferences, and hard constraints. This makes it hard to robustly supervise models on complex prompts and limits reuse of reward components. The authors want a way to explicitly specify what counts as success for each prompt, decouple reward specification from reward computation, and avoid costly human preference labels or separate reward models.

Method: They propose a prompt-level reward specification framework that, given only the prompts, automatically designs task-adaptive rubrics and executable hard-constraint checkers offline. These are reused across rollouts. At scoring time, rubric-based scores and code-based constraint checks (artifact-anchored) are combined with an independent global holistic-quality score to form a normalized hybrid reward. This reward jointly reflects requirement satisfaction, overall quality, and deterministic constraint adherence, and is used for both offline ranking (RM-style) and online RL training without human preferences, references, or learned RMs.

Result: Using this framework, the authors obtain improved performance in offline response ranking similar to reward models and enable effective online RL on diverse open-ended benchmarks. Empirical ablations indicate that each component—rubrics, global holistic scoring, and executable verification—adds unique signal, and removing any of them degrades performance, confirming their complementarity.

Conclusion: Making reward criteria explicit at the prompt level via rubrics and executable constraints, and combining them with a lightweight holistic score, yields a practical, label-free reward framework that improves both offline and online post-training for open-ended tasks. The approach cleanly separates specification from computation and shows that structured, reusable reward specifications can outperform or complement traditional scalar or RM-based supervision.

Abstract: Open-ended post-training benefits from rewards that make prompt-specific success conditions explicit, rather than relying only on post-hoc scalar scores. In instruction following, writing, and decision-support tasks, response quality depends on local requirements, holistic preferences, and explicit constraints, but existing reward methods often leave these criteria implicit or cover only narrowly verifiable cases. We propose a prompt-level reward specification framework that separates reward specification from reward computation. Given only prompts, our framework constructs reusable task-adaptive rubrics and executable hard-constraint checkers offline, making reward criteria explicit before training and reusable across rollouts. At scoring time, artifact-anchored rubric and code scores are combined with an independent global score for residual holistic quality, yielding a normalized hybrid reward over requirement satisfaction, holistic quality, and deterministic constraints. The framework requires no human preference annotations, reference answers, or a separately trained reward model. Experiments show that the resulting reward improves offline RM-style response ranking and supports online reinforcement learning across multiple open-ended benchmarks. Ablations further show that rubrics, global scoring, and executable verification provide complementary supervision.

</details>


### [44] [Accommodation Goes Both Ways: Studying Linguistic Convergence Between Humans and Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29278)
*Terra Blevins*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how humans and large language models (LLMs) adapt their linguistic style to each other in real-world multi-turn conversations, finding strong asymmetric convergence: LLMs heavily adapt to users, while humans adapt to LLMs about as much as they do to other humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are used in everyday communication, it is important to understand whether and how they influence human language use. In particular, prior work shows humans naturally exhibit linguistic accommodation—subtly aligning their word choices and style to their interlocutor—but it is unknown whether this behavior changes when conversing with LLMs and how strongly LLMs themselves adapt to users. This has implications for long-term language change, user modeling, safety, and fairness.

Method: The authors perform a large-scale empirical analysis of linguistic convergence in human–LLM dialog using WildChat, a corpus of real-world ChatGPT conversations across eight languages. They define and apply an asymmetric convergence metric that separately measures how much each side in the conversation (human vs. LLM) shifts its linguistic style toward the other’s, using both function words (e.g., pronouns, articles, prepositions) and open-class lexical features. They then compare human convergence toward LLMs to known human–human baselines, and quantify how strongly LLMs converge to humans.

Result: Across eight languages, LLM replies show strong overconvergence: the model adapts its linguistic style to users much more than humans adapt to the model, both in the distribution of function words and in open-class lexical choices. In contrast, humans’ convergence levels when talking to ChatGPT are broadly in line with convergence patterns observed in human–human dialog, indicating no exceptional additional accommodation toward the LLM.

Conclusion: Human–LLM interaction exhibits asymmetric linguistic accommodation: LLMs strongly overfit to users’ linguistic style, whereas humans accommodate LLMs similarly to how they would accommodate another human. This suggests that current LLMs may amplify individual users’ linguistic patterns, but they do not, at least in the short term, cause humans to systematically adapt their style beyond normal human–human convergence levels.

Abstract: As LLMs become increasingly integrated into daily life, understanding how their presence will shape human linguistic behavior is an open question. We present a large-scale study of linguistic convergence in human-LLM dialogue, examining how humans and LLMs accommodate each other's linguistic style during multi-turn conversations. Using an asymmetric convergence metric on WildChat, a corpus of real-world ChatGPT transcripts, we find that while LLMs significantly overconverge toward their users on both function word and open-class features across eight languages, human convergence rates in this setting are broadly consistent with human-human baselines. These findings suggest that accommodation in human-LLM dialogue is asymmetric: while LLMs dramatically overfit to their users' style, humans linguistically accommodate LLMs no differently than they would another person.

</details>


### [45] [MusTBENCH: Benchmarking and Advancing Temporal Grounding in Music LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29300)
