These findings underscore the effectiveness of multi-modal reasoning for image-to-text translation and provide a solid foundation for future research on integrating visual understanding and language generation in multilingual settings.

</details>


### [66] [PhoneWorld: Scaling Phone-Use Agent Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29486)
*Zhengyang Tang,Yuxuan Liu,Xin Lai,Junyi Li,Pengyuan Lyu,Jason,Yiduo Guo,Zhengyao Fang,Yang Ding,Yi Zhang,Weinong Wang,Huawen Shen,Xingran Zhou,Liang Wu,Fei Tang,Sunqi Fan,Shangpin Peng,Zheng Ruan,Anran Zhang,Benyou Wang,Rui Yan,Ji-Rong Wen,Chengquan Zhang,Han Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PhoneWorld is a pipeline that turns real mobile GUI trajectories into reusable, controllable phone-use environments, tasks, verifiers, and training data, enabling scalable training and evaluation of phone-use agents across many apps and domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current mobile-agent research lacks a scalable way to build many realistic, controllable phone-use environments. Existing benchmarks help evaluate agents but are hand-crafted, limited in coverage, and do not easily generalize to new apps or behaviors. This limits both training data diversity and systematic progress in mobile agents.

Method: PhoneWorld takes real user GUI trajectories and screenshots and automatically infers important screens, their transitions, and which interactions affect environment state. It then constructs runnable mock Android apps with read-only content plus mutable state, and from these builds executable tasks, rule-based automatic verifiers, and training rollouts. The system is instantiated over 34 apps across 16 domains, and its supervision is used to train phone-use agents, including ablations varying the amount of PhoneWorld data and app coverage.

Result: Substituting 10K training steps from an auxiliary AndroidWorld corpus with PhoneWorld supervision in an AndroidWorld-based baseline improves performance on four benchmarks simultaneously: +17.7 on HYMobileBench, +6.0 on AndroidControl, +14.7 on AndroidWorld, and +52.5 on the PhoneWorld benchmark. Further experiments show that more PhoneWorld supervision yields higher PhoneWorld performance, and that enlarging app coverage under a fixed supervision budget leads to even larger gains.

Conclusion: PhoneWorld enables scalable construction of realistic, controllable phone-use environments from real trajectories, shifting the research focus from manually designing individual benchmarks to systematically scaling the supply and diversity of mobile environments. Its supervision substantially boosts performance across multiple mobile-agent benchmarks, and performance continues to improve as supervision amount and app coverage scale up.

Abstract: A central bottleneck for phone-use agents is that controllable, reproducible environments covering real mobile behavior are hard to build at scale. Existing mobile-agent benchmarks have made important progress on evaluation, but they do not by themselves provide a scalable way to construct many new phone-use environments. We present PhoneWorld, a reusable pipeline that converts real GUI trajectories and screenshots into controllable phone-use environments, executable tasks, automatic verifiers, and training rollouts. Rather than hand-building one mobile benchmark at a time, PhoneWorld uses real trajectories to recover which screens matter, how screens connect, which interactions must change environment state, and which user goals admit automatic verification. From these signals, it builds runnable mock Android apps backed by read-only app content and mutable state, then derives executable tasks, rule-based verifiers, and training rollouts from the same environments. In its current instantiation, PhoneWorld covers 34 apps across 16 domains, spanning common consumer mobile behaviors such as search, browsing, shopping, booking, media, and social interaction. Under a fixed training budget, replacing 10K steps from an auxiliary AndroidWorld corpus in an AndroidWorld-based baseline with broad PhoneWorld supervision improves all four evaluation benchmarks at once, raising HYMobileBench by 17.7 points, AndroidControl by 6.0 points, AndroidWorld by 14.7 points, and PhoneWorld by 52.5 points. We then study two additional scaling questions: increasing the amount of PhoneWorld supervision strongly improves PhoneWorld performance, and under a fixed PhoneWorld budget, expanding app coverage yields even larger gains. Overall, PhoneWorld shifts the focus from building one mobile benchmark at a time to scaling the supply of phone-use environments themselves.

</details>


### [67] [On Asymmetric Optimization of Reasoning and Perception in Vision-Language Model Post-Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29496)
*Xueqing Wu,Yu-Chi Lin,Kai-Wei Chang,Nanyun Peng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper finds that current post-training methods improve reasoning in vision-language models much more than perception, diagnoses why this happens in both SFT and RL settings, and proposes training fixes that significantly boost end-to-end visual reasoning accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end visual reasoning requires both strong perception (extracting visual facts) and reasoning (operating on those facts). Recent post-training methods like SFT and RLHF greatly improve reasoning but leave perception relatively weak, creating a bottleneck. The authors want a controlled way to measure and understand this imbalance, and then identify mechanisms that cause it and potential remedies.

Method: They design two synthetic diagnostic tasks that explicitly separate perception from reasoning, allowing controlled measurement of each capability. They then analyze the effect of two common post-training paradigms—supervised fine-tuning with chain-of-thought (CoT) supervision and reinforcement learning with outcome rewards—on perception vs reasoning performance. For SFT, they study how token-level supervision is distributed between perception and reasoning tokens and introduce dynamic loss reweighting to correct token imbalance. For RL, they examine how scalar rewards couple more strongly to reasoning outcomes than to perception quality and propose adding a perception-aware reward term, including a version based on a learned surrogate when ground-truth perception labels are unavailable.

Result: In SFT, they find that CoT annotations devote many more tokens to reasoning than to perception, so perception receives a weaker gradient signal. Dynamically reweighting loss toward perception tokens leads to large end-to-end gains, up to 18.2 points. In RL settings, they show that standard outcome rewards are more strongly correlated with reasoning success than with perception correctness, leading to under-optimization of perception. Augmenting RL with an explicit perception-aware reward improves accuracy by up to 6.0 points; even when only a surrogate perception reward is available, it still yields improvements of about 3.2 points.

Conclusion: There is a robust perception–reasoning asymmetry in post-trained vision-language models: current SFT and RL procedures preferentially optimize reasoning. This asymmetry arises from different mechanisms in each paradigm—token imbalance in SFT and reward coupling in RL. By explicitly rebalancing the training signals (via loss reweighting or perception-aware rewards), one can substantially enhance end-to-end visual reasoning. The work provides both a diagnostic framework and concrete training interventions for more balanced optimization of perception and reasoning in multimodal models.

Abstract: Post-training has greatly improved reasoning in frontier vision-language models, yet its gains for perception remain comparatively limited, creating a bottleneck for end-to-end visual reasoning. To investigate this gap, we introduce a controlled diagnostic framework with two synthetic tasks that disentangle perception from reasoning. Our analysis reveals a consistent perception-reasoning asymmetry: posttraining improves reasoning more substantially than perception, though the underlying mechanism differs by training paradigm. For supervised fine-tuning (SFT), this asymmetry stems from token imbalance in chain-of-thought supervision, where perception occupies fewer tokens and thus receives a weaker training signal. Dynamically reweighting the loss mitigates this imbalance and boosts end-to-end performance by up to 18.2. For reinforcement learning (RL), the asymmetry instead arises from reward coupling: outcome rewards correlate more strongly with reasoning than with perception, weakening the signal for perception learning. Adding a perception-aware reward alleviates the imbalance and improves end-to-end accuracy by up to 6.0; even without groundtruth perception rewards, a reliable surrogate reward provide useful signal, yielding gains of 3.2 points. Together, our results comprehensively diagnose asymmetric optimization and suggest concrete interventions to balance perception and reasoning.

</details>


### [68] [Mask the Target: A Plug-and-Play Regularizer Against LoRA Forgetting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29498)
*Runze Xu,Arpit Garg,Hemanth Saratchandran,Simon Lucey*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how LoRA fine-tuning can cause catastrophic forgetting of prior capabilities and proposes a simple loss-level regularizer that improves the trade-off between learning new tasks and preserving existing skills without using replay data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA is widely used to adapt large language models, but standard LoRA fine-tuning can significantly degrade the model’s previously acquired abilities, especially when the new task’s data distribution is very different from the original pretraining or alignment data. In practice, the original training data is unavailable, limiting standard continual-learning or replay-based solutions. The authors are motivated to understand and control this learn–forget trade-off in a realistic, replay-free setting for LoRA-based updating.

Method: They empirically study forgetting behavior under LoRA-based adaptation when the adaptation distribution is substantially shifted from the base model’s training/alignment distributions. To mitigate forgetting, they introduce an output-space regularizer that works entirely at the loss level: for each training example, they remove the ground-truth token from both the base and adapted model output distributions, renormalize the remaining probabilities, and apply a KL divergence penalty only over this non-target vocabulary. This preserves the base model’s relative preferences over alternative tokens while letting cross-entropy drive learning on the target token. The method requires no replay data, architectural changes, adapter redesign, or inference-time modifications and is compatible with existing LoRA variants.

Result: Across multiple LoRA variants and model backbones, the proposed regularizer consistently improves the trade-off between adaptation performance on the new distribution and retention of prior capabilities, particularly when the adaptation data differs strongly from the original training/alignment distributions. Empirical results show reduced forgetting at comparable or improved performance on the new tasks, effectively pushing out the frontier between new learning and forgetting.

Conclusion: LoRA-based adaptation can lead to substantial forgetting, particularly under distribution shift, even when adaptation performance appears strong. A simple KL-based output regularizer that excludes the ground-truth token can effectively stabilize LoRA updates, preserving prior behavior without hindering new learning. Because it is replay-free, architecture-agnostic, and imposes no inference cost, this approach offers a practical, broadly applicable technique for more reliable and conservative updating of large language models.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has become one of the most widely used fine-tuning mechanisms for adapting large language models to new domains, tasks, and users. Yet adaptation performance alone can obscure an important failure mode: LoRA updates may improve performance on the target distribution while degrading prior capabilities learned during pretraining and alignment. We show that this forgetting becomes especially severe when the adaptation distribution differs substantially from the models original training or alignment distributions. The challenge is amplified in practical settings, where the original training and alignment data are typically unavailable. Motivated by this constraint, we study how LoRA based adaptation balances new learning against forgetting in a replay-free setting, and introduce a simple output space regularizer that can be added directly to existing training pipelines. Our method removes the ground-truth token from both the base and adapted model distributions, renormalizes the remaining probabilities, and applies KL regularization only over the non-target vocabulary. This preserves the base models relative preferences among alternative tokens without directly opposing the cross-entropy signal required for adaptation. As the regularizer acts only at the loss level, it requires no replay data, architectural changes, adapter redesign, or inference-time overhead, and can be applied directly to existing LoRA variants. Across all LoRA variants tested and across various backbones, our method improves the frontier between new learning and forgetting when the adaptation distribution differs substantially from the base models original training or alignment distributions, suggesting a broadly applicable route toward more reliable LLM updating.

</details>


### [69] [Source-Grounded Semantic Reinforcement Learning for Low-Resource Target-Language Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29502)
*Zeli Su,Ziyin Zhang,Zewei Pan,Zhou Liu,Dingcheng Huang,Dehan Li,Zhankai Xu,Longfei Zheng,Xiaolu Zhang,Jun Zhou,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose an RL-based framework that uses abundant source-language monolingual data plus a cross-lingual semantic reward to improve low-resource target-language generation, then fix verbosity and format with a small parallel corpus.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Low-resource target languages lack parallel data, which limits supervised fine-tuning for generation tasks. Meanwhile, there is plenty of monolingual data in a related high-resource source language that is not directly usable with standard supervised methods. The authors want a way to exploit this cheap, abundant source-language data to improve target-language generation while preserving semantic grounding and factual coverage.

Method: They introduce Source-Grounded Semantic Reinforcement Learning (SG-SRL). First, they train or use a cross-lingual semantic reward model implemented as a source–target reranker that scores how semantically relevant a target-language output is to a source-language input. Then they run reference-free reinforcement learning on monolingual source-language data, optimizing the target-language generator using this reward instead of parallel references. This RL step tends to cause verbosity-based reward hacking. To counter this, they add a lightweight recovery stage using a small parallel corpus to fine-tune the model back toward fluent, concise, and correctly formatted outputs, while trying to retain semantic improvements.

Result: On Chinese-to-Thai generation tasks, models trained with SG-SRL show better semantic grounding and factual coverage than models trained with only cold-start supervised fine-tuning. Further analyses on long-form knowledge transfer and experiments using Tibetan with embedding-based (encoder) rewards show that the method generalizes beyond the initial setting and that a simpler encoder-based semantic reward can effectively replace a large reranker LLM in realistic low-resource language scenarios.

Conclusion: SG-SRL is an effective framework for leveraging high-resource source-language monolingual data to improve low-resource target-language generation through cross-lingual semantic rewards and RL, with a small parallel corpus used to fix reward hacking side effects. It improves semantic grounding and factual coverage, generalizes to other languages and setups, and can use lightweight encoder-based rewards instead of large LLM rerankers, making it more practical for low-resource settings.

Abstract: Low-resource target-language generation is often limited by scarce parallel data, while high-resource source-language monolingual data is abundant but difficult to use with standard supervised fine-tuning. We propose Source-Grounded Semantic Reinforcement Learning (SG-SRL), a resource-utilization framework that converts source-language monolingual data into cross-lingual semantic supervision for target-language generation. SG-SRL performs reference-free reinforcement learning (RL) on source-language data using a cross-lingual semantic reward model, instantiated by a cross-lingual reranker that scores the semantic relevance between the source input and the target-language generation. While this induces severe verbosity-based reward hacking, a lightweight recovery stage using a small parallel corpus restores fluency, conciseness, and task format while preserving the semantic gains. Experiments on Chinese-to-Thai generation show that SG-SRL improves semantic grounding and factual coverage over cold-start SFT. Additional analyses on long-form transfer and Tibetan embedding-based rewards clarify the generalization behavior of SG-SRL and show that an encoder-based semantic reward can substitute for an LLM-based reranker in a realistic low-resource language setting.

</details>


### [70] [From Blind Guess to Informed Judgment: Teaching LLMs to Evaluate Materials by Building Knowledge-Augmented Preference Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29555)
*Yeyong Yu,Wenya Hu,Xing Wu,Quan Qian*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Paper introduces MaterEval, a framework that converts expert materials-evaluation rules into preference signals to fine-tune LLMs for more reliable candidate assessment in materials discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In modern materials discovery, candidate generation and property prediction are no longer the main bottlenecks. Instead, the challenge is reliably evaluating and ranking huge numbers of candidate materials. General-purpose LLMs have intuitive reasoning but lack domain-specific evaluation criteria, and rule-based expert systems are often closed-source, costly, or hard to deploy widely. There is a need for a low-cost, deployable approach that encodes expert evaluation rules into a form that LLMs can learn, improving their reliability without heavy external infrastructure.

Method: The authors propose MaterEval, a Knowledge-Augmented Preference Signals Framework. For each candidate material, MaterEval generates two kinds of evaluations: (1) an informed judgment that explicitly follows codified expert rules and includes supporting evidence, and (2) a rule-removed blind guess based only on intuitive judgment. The pair of evaluations is treated as preference data, where the informed, evidence-backed evaluation is preferred over the blind guess. This preference data is then used to guide or fine-tune general-purpose LLMs so they shift from purely intuitive reasoning toward rule-consistent, evidence-based evaluation. Additionally, the framework adopts a fast-slow reasoning scheme: a fast, lightweight reasoning mode for high-throughput initial screening, and a slow, more in-depth reasoning mode for detailed evaluation of a smaller subset of candidates. A case study on high-entropy alloy (HEA) assessment is used to validate the approach.

Result: In the HEA assessment case study, small open-source LLMs, trained using MaterEval’s preference signals and without relying on external retrieval tools, substantially improve along several dimensions: overall evaluation accuracy, consistency of conclusions across reasoning passes, and the ability to distinguish relevant from irrelevant evidence. Their performance approaches that of rule-based, closed-source LLMs, indicating that the expert rules have been successfully embedded into the models’ internal behavior via preference learning.

Conclusion: The study shows that expert evaluation rules in materials science can be systematically converted into preference signals that steer LLMs toward reliable, evidence-based judgments. This produces a low-cost, deployable evaluation module suitable for autonomous materials discovery loops, helping to overcome the current bottleneck of reliable candidate evaluation in high-throughput materials research.

Abstract: As candidate generation and high-throughput experimentation advance, the primary bottleneck in materials discovery is shifting from property prediction to making reliable evaluations among massive candidate sets. We propose a Knowledge-Augmented Preference Signals Framework, MaterEval, that automatically produces, for the same candidate, two evaluations: an informed judgment that follows expert rules and provides supporting evidence, and a rule-removed blind guess. By pairing the two evaluations as preference data, we guide general-purpose large language models (LLMs), originally lacking materials-specific criteria, from intuitive judgment toward reliable evaluation supported by explicit evidence. To balance throughput, cost, and reliability, we further introduce a fast-slow reasoning scheme that decouples large-scale rapid screening from in-depth review on a small subset. Using high-entropy alloy (HEA) assessment as a case study, we show that, without external retrieval and relying solely on internalized capabilities, small open-source LLMs achieve substantial gains in accuracy, conclusion consistency, and evidence discrimination, approaching the performance of rule-based closed-source LLMs. These results demonstrate that expert rules can be systematically transformed into learnable preference signals, enabling a low-cost and deployable evaluation module for autonomous materials discovery loops.

</details>


### [71] [LiteCoder-Terminal: Scaling Long-Horizon Terminal Environments for Learning Language Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29559)
*Xiaoxuan Peng,Kaiqi Zhang,Xinyu Lu,Boxi Cao,Yaojie Lu,Hongyu Lin,Xianpei Han,Le Sun*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents LiteCoder-Terminal-Gen, a synthetic pipeline to generate executable terminal training environments and associated datasets that significantly improve language agents’ performance on terminal benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training for terminal-capable language agents depends on scraped external repositories, which restricts domain diversity, controllability, and the ability to focus on specific weaknesses. The authors want a scalable, controllable, and verifiable way to train agents to handle complex, multi-step command-line workflows.

Method: They design LiteCoder-Terminal-Gen, a zero-dependency synthesis pipeline that, given high-level domain specifications, automatically generates executable and verifiable terminal environments. Using this pipeline, they build two resources: (1) LiteCoder-Terminal-SFT with 11,255 expert trajectories over 10 domains for supervised fine-tuning, and (2) LiteCoder-Terminal-RL with 602 verifiable environments for trajectory-level preference optimization. They then fine-tune Qwen-family models on SFT data and further apply Direct Multi-turn Preference Optimization (DMPO) using the RL environments.

Result: Qwen models fine-tuned on LiteCoder-Terminal-SFT substantially outperform their base versions on standard terminal benchmarks. The 32B model variant reaches 29.06% pass@1 on Terminal Bench 1.0, 18.54% on 2.0, and 34.00% on Pro. Applying DMPO on the RL environments yields further performance improvements beyond SFT alone.

Conclusion: Fully synthetic, executable terminal environments generated by LiteCoder-Terminal-Gen provide a scalable, controllable, and verifiable supervision signal. This enables more effective training of language agents for complex, real-world command-line tasks, surpassing models trained only on conventional scraped data.

Abstract: Mastering terminal environments requires language agents capable of multi-step planning, feedback-grounded execution, and dynamic state adaptation. However, training such agents is currently bottlenecked by a reliance on scraped external repositories, which limits domain diversity, environment controllability, and the targeting of specific capability deficits. We introduce LiteCoder-Terminal-Gen, a zero-dependency synthesis pipeline that autonomously generates executable and verifiable terminal training environments directly from domain specifications. Using this framework, we construct two large-scale resources: LiteCoder-Terminal-SFT, comprising 11,255 expert trajectories across 10 domains, and LiteCoder-Terminal-RL, featuring 602 verifiable environments for trajectory-level preference optimization. Supervised fine-tuning of Qwen-family models on our SFT dataset yields agents that significantly outperform their base counterparts. Notably, our 32B variant achieves 29.06%, 18.54%, and 34.00% pass@1 on Terminal Bench 1.0, 2.0, and Pro, respectively. Furthermore, applying Direct Multi-turn Preference Optimization (DMPO) on our RL environments yields additional performance gains. These results systematically demonstrate that fully synthetic, executable environments offer a scalable and verifiable supervision signal for mastering complex, real-world command-line workflows.

</details>


### [72] [GAPD: Gold-Action Policy Distillation for Agentic Reinforcement Learning in Knowledge Base Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29584)
*Xin Sun,Jianan Xie,Zhongqi Chen,Qiang Liu,Shu Wu,Bowen Song,Weiqiang Wang,Zilei Wang,Liang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: GAPD improves reinforcement learning for knowledge base question answering by distilling supervision from gold action sequences into the RL policy via mid-anchor matching between student and gold trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL-based KBQA systems rely on sparse end-of-episode rewards, which provide little supervision for intermediate actions, even when datasets contain gold logical forms that could be converted to detailed action sequences. This underutilization of rich supervision limits learning efficiency and accuracy.

Method: GAPD (Gold-Action Policy Distillation) augments outcome-based RL with dense token-level guidance derived from gold action sequences. It introduces MID-ANCHOR MATCHING, which aligns intermediate states in the student’s exploratory trajectory with states in the gold trajectory using reached entities as anchors. Conditioned on the aligned gold actions, the current policy is used as a stop-gradient teacher; its token-level action distribution is then distilled back into the standard student policy over the corresponding action-token spans.

Result: GAPD achieves consistent performance improvements and surpasses the previous state of the art on three logical-form annotated KBQA benchmarks: WebQSP, GrailQA, and GraphQ.

Conclusion: Incorporating dense supervision from gold action sequences into RL training for KBQA, via mid-anchor matching and policy distillation, addresses the limitations of sparse reward signals and yields state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a natural fit for agentic knowledge base question answering (KBQA), where a model must issue executable actions, observe knowledge-base feedback, and eventually return an answer. However, current RL-based KBQA systems mainly optimize sparse rewards from the final answer, leaving intermediate action errors weakly supervised. This is especially limiting for logical-form annotated KBQA benchmarks: gold logical forms can be converted into executable action sequences, but existing pipelines use them mainly for warm-start data construction rather than for on-policy RL updates. We propose GAPD, a training-time Gold-Action Policy Distillation framework that adds dense token-level guidance to outcome-based RL. To align gold actions with on-policy student rollouts, GAPD uses MID-ANCHOR MATCHING: it treats the intermediate entities reached during student exploration and gold execution as state anchors, and matches student states to gold states through these explored entity sets. The current policy conditioned on this aligned gold action serves as a stop-gradient teacher, whose token distribution is distilled back to the ordinary student policy over generated action-token spans. GAPD consistently surpasses the current state of the art on WebQSP, GrailQA, and GraphQ.

</details>


### [73] [World Models in Words: Auditing Physical State-Transition Commitments in Vision-Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29585)
*Emmanuelle Bourigault*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new evaluation framework that inspects the full chain of a vision-language model’s physical reasoning rather than only its final answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of vision-language models on physical reasoning tasks typically judge only whether the final answer is correct. This obscures whether the model correctly perceived objects, represented physical states, or predicted plausible transitions, and allows models to get answers correct for the wrong reasons. There is a need for a method that can systematically audit the internal, language-expressed physical commitments underlying a model’s answers.

Method: The authors propose \wmw, a framework that requires VLMs to output a structured reasoning trace for each question about an image: an initial physical state s0, a state change Δs, a resulting state s1, and an answer a. They design a hybrid verifier that checks the trace for schema validity, grounding in the image and question, physical consistency of the transition, and compatibility between the trace and final answer. The verifier assigns fine-grained error types (e.g., object, relation, force, transition, temporal, unit/scale, faithfulness errors). They also construct TraceBank, a synthetic, schema-validated dataset of physics scenarios with contrastive pairs, along with verifier code, audit guidelines, and model outputs, and then evaluate multiple VLMs on both this controlled dataset and external physical reasoning examples.

Result: The framework exposes substantial hidden errors: for mid-tier models, 35% of instances with correct final answers are supported by physically invalid reasoning traces. Using verifier feedback to rerank candidate traces improves trace validity by up to 7 percentage points without lowering answer accuracy. Furthermore, training models with preferences at the trace level reduces hidden inconsistency in reasoning by 41% relatively.

Conclusion: The work provides not just another benchmark of final-answer accuracy, but a general, reusable protocol to evaluate whether a VLM’s internally stated physical world and its answer can be jointly true. This enables more transparent, fine-grained auditing of physical reasoning and offers tools (verification, reranking, and trace-based preference tuning) to improve the faithfulness and consistency of VLMs’ physical reasoning processes.

Abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) are increasingly used to answer questions about physical scenes, yet most evaluations reduce performance to a final answer. This hides whether the model perceived the right objects, represented the right physical state, predicted a plausible transition, or merely selected the right option for the wrong reasons. We introduce \wmw, an evaluation framework for auditing the \emph{language-expressed physical commitments} of VLMs. Instead of scoring only $I,q\mapsto a$, we ask models to produce a typed trace $I,q\mapsto(s_0,Δs,s_1,a)$: an initial state, a state transition, a resulting state, and an answer. A hybrid verifier then checks schema validity, state grounding, transition consistency, and answer-trace compatibility, yielding typed error labels such as object, relation, force, transition, temporal, unit/scale, and faithfulness errors. We release \tracebank, a controlled trace resource with \nSeed schema- and recomputation-validated synthetic scenarios across \nFamilies physics families, \nPairs minimally perturbed contrastive preference pairs, verifier code, audit guidelines, and model outputs. We evaluate \nModels VLMs on both controlled and external physical-reasoning examples. \wmw reveals failures that answer-only evaluation misses: 35\% of correct answers from mid-tier models are backed by physically invalid traces. Verifier-guided reranking recovers up to 7 percentage points of trace validity without sacrificing answer accuracy, and trace-level preference tuning reduces hidden inconsistency by 41\% relative. The contribution is not another final-answer physics benchmark, but a reusable protocol for measuring whether a VLM's stated physical world can be true at the same time as its answer.

</details>


### [74] [Training Deliberative Monitors for Black-Box Scheming Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29601)
*Aditya Sinha,Akshat Naik,Victor Gillioz,Simon Storf,Kilian Merkelbach,Rich Barton-Cooper,Axel Højmark,Marius Hobbhahn*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a low-cost, open-weight monitoring approach that detects scheming or sabotaging behavior in autonomous agents using only their observed actions, and shows these monitors can rival or beat cheaper frontier-model-based monitors at much lower inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As autonomous AI agents gain the ability to perform complex real-world tasks, they may engage in scheming or sabotage that is hard to detect, especially when access to their internal reasoning (chain-of-thought) or activations is unavailable or too expensive. Existing safety monitors often require such internal access or rely on expensive frontier models as prompted overseers, limiting scalability and practical deployment in real-world systems. There is a need for effective, inexpensive, and deployable monitors that work from observable behavior alone and that can generalize across different misalignment scenarios.

Method: The authors introduce action-only deliberative monitors: smaller open-weight models trained to classify whether an agent’s trajectory indicates scheming or sabotage, using only the sequence of observed actions. They start from a formal scheming specification and use a powerful frontier model as a teacher to generate structured rationales about trajectories. A separate judge model filters these rationales to keep only the highest-quality explanations. These filtered rationales are then used to supervise smaller open-weight monitors via supervised fine-tuning, and further improved with reinforcement learning, effectively distilling the teacher’s deliberative safety reasoning into compact, deployable monitors.

Result: Trained on five datasets and evaluated on six out-of-distribution benchmarks of agentic misalignment, the monitors derived from Qwen3.5-27B outperform all tested low-cost frontier models when those frontier models are used as prompted monitors (including Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite, GPT-5.4 Nano, and Claude Haiku 4.5), and even surpass Gemini 2.5 Pro. These open-weight monitors achieve this at lower marginal inference cost per 1,000 evaluations. Although the strongest prompted frontier monitors (Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, Claude Opus 4.6) still obtain better raw detection performance, they do so at approximately 16–34 times the inference cost. Several of the proposed monitors lie on the empirical cost–performance Pareto frontier of all evaluated monitors, with competitive detection accuracy and low false positive rates.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that deliberatively trained, action-only open-weight monitors can provide practical, low-cost, and effective detection of scheming and sabotage in autonomous agents without needing internal access to the monitored models. By distilling high-quality deliberative rationales from a frontier teacher into smaller models, the approach offers a competitive alternative to directly using prompted frontier models, particularly in settings where inference cost and deployment constraints matter. These monitors occupy favorable positions on the cost–performance Pareto frontier, suggesting they are viable building blocks for scalable AI control and oversight systems.

Abstract: As autonomous agents become more capable of performing real-world tasks, distinguishing scheming behavior from benign task pursuit may become a central AI control problem. Existing monitors often rely on chain-of-thought access or internal activations, or use prompted frontier models, all of which can be unavailable, unreliable or expensive in deployment. In this work, we study action-only deliberative monitors: smaller open-weight models trained to detect scheming and sabotage from agentic trajectories without accessing the monitored agent's reasoning or model internals. Our method, inspired by deliberative alignment, uses a scheming specification to elicit structured rationales from a frontier teacher, filters them with a separate judge, and distills the highest-quality rationales into open-weight monitors with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning. We train on five datasets, and evaluate across six out-of-distribution agentic misalignment benchmarks. We show that applying our method to Qwen3.5-27B yields higher performance than all low-cost frontier models as prompted monitors (Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite, GPT-5.4 Nano, and Claude Haiku 4.5) and than Gemini 2.5 Pro, while also achieving lower marginal inference cost (token-metered USD per 1,000 evaluations). Stronger prompted frontier monitors (Gemini 3.1 Pro, GPT-5.4, Claude Sonnet 4.6, and Claude Opus 4.6) achieve higher performance but at roughly $16$--$34\times$ higher marginal inference cost. Several of our trained monitors are positioned on the empirical cost--performance Pareto frontier among the monitors we evaluate, providing practical low-cost, low-FPR alternatives to prompted frontier models.

</details>


### [75] [DLM-SWAI: Steering Diffusion Language Models Before They Unmask](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29626)
*Hyeseon An,Yo-Sub Han*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces DLM-SWAI, a simple, training-free method for steering diffusion language models (DLMs) toward desired textual properties using pre-computed token-level style scores, achieving effective style and safety control with minimal computational overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Controlling generated text for style, safety, and other attributes is crucial in real-world applications. Existing inference-time steering methods are mainly designed for autoregressive models or require auxiliary models, and thus are not directly applicable or efficient for diffusion language models, which generate text via iterative denoising of masked sequences. There is a need for a lightweight, training-free steering approach tailored to DLMs.

Method: The authors propose DLM-SWAI, which, during each denoising step of a diffusion language model, biases the token distribution using pre-computed token-level style scores. This modifies the probability of each candidate token so that tokens more aligned with the target style or attribute are favored throughout the denoising process, without retraining the base model.

Result: On both style control and safety control benchmarks, DLM-SWAI successfully steers diffusion language models toward desired attributes while preserving overall generation quality. It achieves these gains with minimal extra computation compared to standard DLM decoding. Ablation studies demonstrate how varying the strength of the bias trades off steerability versus fluency.

Conclusion: DLM-SWAI is an effective, training-free steering technique for diffusion language models that enables practical control over style and safety. The method is computationally light, offers a tunable balance between control strength and fluency, and the authors’ analysis suggests that the effectiveness of steering varies by attribute class depending on how strongly the attribute is reflected in token-level cues.

Abstract: Steering language model generation toward desired textual properties is essential for practical deployment, and inference-time methods are particularly appealing because they enable controllable generation without retraining. Recent work has also highlighted diffusion language models as an emerging generation paradigm with distinct decoding properties. However, most existing steering approaches either rely on auxiliary models or are designed for autoregressive next-token decoding, making them difficult to apply to diffusion language models DLMs, which generate text through iterative denoising of partially masked sequences. Therefore, we propose DLM-SWAI, a simple training-free steering method that biases the token distribution at each denoising step using pre-computed token-level style scores. Experiments on style and safety control tasks show that DLM-SWAI effectively steers diffusion language models while preserving generation quality and requiring minimal computational overhead. Ablations further reveal a controllable trade-off between steering strength and fluency, and our analysis links class-wise steerability to the strength of token-level attribute cues.

</details>


### [76] [Entity-Collision: A Stratified Protocol for Attributing Retrieval Lift in Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29630)
*Youwang Deng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce a stricter, system-agnostic benchmark protocol for agent-memory retrieval that isolates true embedding gains from confounds and show that model capacity alone doesn’t explain performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing end-to-end agent-memory benchmarks report only a single hit@k per retriever, which mixes together different effects: (1) lexical leakage, where overlap between query, gold answers, and distractors lets even simple lexical retrievers appear strong; and (2) tag-mixing, where very different query types (preferences, tools, services, intents, etc.) are averaged, obscuring where specific retrievers actually help. This makes it hard to tell when neural embedders are genuinely improving over a strong lexical baseline like BM25 and along which dimensions.

Method: They propose an evaluation protocol called entity-collision: (a) construct distractors that all share the answer’s entity tokens, which fixes a strong BM25 “floor” by design and removes lexical leakage as a confound; (b) stratify and evaluate queries by discriminator tags (e.g., lexical vs intent/preference vs service/tool), avoiding tag-mixing; (c) apply this protocol on an open-source agent-memory testbed across a grid of conditions: 5 query tags × 3 embedding models × 5 degrees of entity collision; (d) estimate reliability with paired-bootstrap 95% confidence intervals. They also test a synthetic intent-tag null condition on LongMemEval and evaluate adaptive vector-weight routing on LoCoMo to see if routing signals can close the observed performance gap. All experiments are run on a deterministically governed, event-sourced memory testbed with strict version control and public registry checks for full byte-level reproducibility.

Result: The experiments show a clear two-dimensional performance pattern: (1) a 256-dimensional hashed trigram model only helps on closed-vocabulary, lexical-style tags, and only when entity collision is very strong; (2) MiniLM-384 consistently dominates on both lexical and intent-like dimensions; (3) a larger encoder, BGE-large (2.7× parameters), does not universally outperform MiniLM— it does better on intent-style queries but actually worse on lexical queries. This indicates that sheer encoder capacity is not the limiting factor. The synthetic intent-tag effect reappears on LongMemEval as a sharp drop in recall for single-session preference memories. On LoCoMo, adaptive vector-weight routing shows measurable oracle headroom (11.7 percentage points possible gain) but none of the tested routing signals realize this gain, effectively a null result.

Conclusion: Their entity-collision protocol cleanly isolates the contribution of embedding models over a fixed lexical baseline and reveals that improvements depend on query type and collision depth rather than simply on model size. MiniLM-384 provides a better tradeoff than a much larger BGE-large for many agent-memory tasks, and capacity alone is not the main bottleneck for retrieval. The reproducible, deterministically governed testbed plus comprehensive scripts and tables establish a reliable foundation for future work on agent-memory retrieval, including more effective use of routing or other system-level signals to exploit the remaining oracle headroom.

Abstract: End-to-end agent-memory benchmarks report a single hit@k per retriever, confounding lexical leakage (uncontrolled query/gold/distractor entity overlap) with tag-mixing (preferences, services, tools averaged together). We propose entity-collision, a system-agnostic protocol that pins the BM25 floor by construction -- every distractor shares the answer's entity tokens -- and stratifies queries by discriminator tag, so any lift over BM25 is attributable to the embedder. Applied to an open-source agent-memory testbed across 5 tags x 3 embedders x 5 collision degrees with paired-bootstrap 95% CIs, the protocol reveals a two-axis pattern: a 256-d hash trigram helps only on closed-vocabulary lexical tags at deep collision; MiniLM-384 dominates both axes; and a 2.7x-parameter BGE-large does not uniformly improve on MiniLM -- it wins on intent-style queries but loses on lexical ones. Encoder capacity alone is not the binding constraint. The synthetic intent-tag null replicates on LongMemEval (n=500) as a single-session-preference recall cliff. Adaptive vector-weight routing on LoCoMo is a measured null: 11.7pp of oracle headroom exists, but no signal we tested recovers it. All 26 result tables and 37 reproduce scripts are version-controlled and verified by a public registry; the protocol is exercised on a deterministically governed memory testbed (event-sourced decision log, DAG-state-machine schema lifecycle) so every reported CI is reproducible byte-for-byte from the ingest stream.

</details>


### [77] [Predicting Causal Effects from Natural Language Queries using Structured Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29631)
*Giuliano Martinelli,Piriyakorn Piriyatamwong,Abelardo Carlos Martinez Lorenzo,Jasmin Baier,Riccardo Orlando,Satvik Garg,Sharif Kazemi,Linxi Wang,Arianna Legovini,Samuel Fraiberger*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build a benchmark and a two-step LLM-based framework to predict causal effect sizes from natural language queries about randomized controlled trials, showing fine-tuning and structured intermediate representations greatly improve accuracy and generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Randomized controlled trials provide gold-standard causal evidence but are expensive and slow, so there is interest in forecasting causal effect sizes from existing experiments. With LLMs performing well on knowledge-intensive tasks, the authors want to know if such models can forecast causal effect sizes for realistic information-seeking queries about interventions and outcomes.

Method: They construct Query2Effect, a large benchmark of 72,000+ natural language questions linked to RCT descriptions, systematically varying query specificity (implicitness, abstraction, ambiguity) to mimic real user queries. They then design a two-step method: first, an LLM generates a synthetic structured representation of the query; second, a supervised encoder model takes this representation to predict the numeric effect size. They compare prompted, out-of-the-box LLMs with fine-tuned models and evaluate in-domain and out-of-domain generalization.

Result: Fine-tuning substantially improves performance over zero-shot/prompted LLMs, reducing absolute error by 27–71%. The proposed two-step framework, which separates semantic interpretation from numerical prediction, yields better out-of-domain generalization than direct end-to-end prediction from text alone.

Conclusion: LLMs, when properly fine-tuned and coupled with a structured intermediate representation, can be effective tools for forecasting causal effect sizes from natural language queries grounded in experimental evidence. Decomposing the task into semantic parsing and numerical effect estimation improves both accuracy and robustness across domains.

Abstract: Randomized controlled trials are a cornerstone of medicine and the social sciences as they enable reliable estimates of causal effects. However, they are costly and time-consuming to conduct, motivating interest in predicting causal effects from existing experimental evidence. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong performance on knowledge-intensive tasks, raising the question of whether these models can be used for forecasting causal effect sizes. To investigate this, we introduce Query2Effect, a new large-scale benchmark consisting of more than 72,000 natural language questions aligned with experiment descriptions, created to simulate realistic information-seeking scenarios by varying query specificity along dimensions of implicitness, abstraction, and ambiguity. We then propose a two-step framework that first generates a synthetic structured representation of a query before predicting effect size using a supervised encoder model. Experiments show that finetuning plays a crucial role in improving prediction performance, with absolute error reducing by -27% up to -71% compared to prompted out-of-the-box LLMs, and that our two-step framework is beneficial for out-of-domain generalization, highlighting the benefits of separating semantic interpretation from numerical effect estimation.

</details>


### [78] [Evaluating Cross-lingual Knowledge Consistency in Code-Mixed vis-a-vis Indian Languages using IndicKLAR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29637)
*Debajyoti Mazumder,Divyansh Pathak,Prashant Kodali,Aditya Joshi,Akshay Agarwal,Jasabanta Patro*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces IndiKLAR, a benchmark to study how reliably large language models recall knowledge across English, Indian languages, and their code-mixed forms, showing that code-mixing substantially narrows the accuracy gap to English and exploring prompting strategies that leverage internal translation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While large language models recall factual knowledge well in English, they often fail on semantically equivalent queries posed in low-resource languages. This crosslingual inconsistency is particularly underexplored for Indian languages and their common code-mixed variants with English, despite their massive user base. The authors aim to systematically measure and understand this gap, and to investigate whether code-mixing or prompt-based language conversion can recover English-level performance without retraining models.

Method: The authors construct IndiKLAR, an Indic extension of the KLAR-CLC benchmark, covering 18 scheduled Indian languages and 11 English–Indic code-mixed pairs. For 11 languages, they provide a three-way aligned set: English, native Indic, and code-mixed variants, all verified by native speakers. They then evaluate nine open-weight LLMs on this benchmark to measure knowledge recall accuracy across the three input types. Beyond raw evaluation, they test several prompting strategies for handling non-English inputs: (1) a two-stage translate-then-answer pipeline, (2) a single-prompt joint translation-and-answer approach, and (3) Translate-in-Thought (TinT), where the model internally converts the query but outputs only the final answer. They analyze where along the trajectory from native → code-mixed → English the model’s predictions flip from incorrect to correct, both for explicit input changes and for internally induced conversions via prompts.

Result: Across models, native Indian language queries exhibit large accuracy gaps relative to English, with performance deficits as high as about 0.50. Code-mixed inputs, however, dramatically reduce this disparity, bringing accuracy to within roughly 0.05 of English without altering model parameters. Evaluations of the different prompting strategies show that guiding the model to internally translate or operate in English can replicate similar gains: both explicit code-mixed inputs and prompt-induced internal conversions shift many examples from incorrect to correct answers. The authors consistently observe a “flip point” between native and code-mixed representations where the model transitions from failure to success in knowledge recall, regardless of whether this transition is driven by surface-form changes or by internal translation prompted in the model.

Conclusion: IndiKLAR reveals a substantial crosslingual consistency gap in knowledge recall for Indian languages in LLMs, but also shows that code-mixing and prompt-level language conversion can nearly close this gap without model retraining. The consistent flip point between native and code-mixed/English inputs suggests that models possess the underlying knowledge but struggle to access it directly in lower-resourced Indic languages. This has practical implications: simple interface-level strategies—encouraging code-mixed usage or using prompts that induce internal translation—can significantly improve performance for Indic language users, while highlighting the need for better multilingual training to avoid such disparities in the first place.

Abstract: Large language models recall knowledge reliably in English but often fail on the same query posed in a lower-resourced language -- a crosslingual consistency gap that remains underexplored for Indian languages and their code-mixed counterparts. To study this gap, we introduce IndiKLAR, an Indic extension of the KLAR-CLC benchmark covering 18 of the 22 scheduled Indian languages and pairing them with code-mixed variants for 11 widely used language pairs, with native-speaker verification of both monolingual and code-mixed variants for these 11 settings. This three-way alignment offers a unique opportunity to examine how knowledge recall consistency varies across the spectrum of English, code-mixed, and native Indian language inputs. Evaluating across nine open-weight models, we find that the native-language accuracy gap to English can reach $\sim$0.50, while code-mixed inputs close most of it -- bringing performance within $\sim$0.05 of English without any model-level intervention. Motivated by this, we evaluate several prompting strategies that vary in how language conversion is exposed, including a two-stage translate-then-answer setup, a one-stage joint translation-and-answer prompt, and Translate-in-Thought (TinT) -- a single-step strategy in which the model converts the input internally and emits only the final answer. Across the performance trajectory native $\rightarrow$ code-mixed $\rightarrow$ English, we identify a consistent flip point -- the boundary between incorrect and correct prediction -- that lies between the native and code-mixed settings. Interestingly, this holds whether the trajectory is induced by the input surface form or by the model's internal conversion process.

</details>


### [79] [Classification of non-analyzable word types in web documents to implement an effective Korean e-learning system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29638)
*Sang-Taek Park,Ae-Lim Ahn,Eric Laporte,Jee-Sun Nam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper compares formal and informal Korean language corpora and proposes Local Grammar Graphs to better handle informal expressions in e-learning systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: E-learning systems for Korean often use only formal language, which does not reflect how Korean is used in real-world contexts such as web documents, mobile texts, or social media. High-level learners need exposure to these informal expressions to achieve real communicative competence.

Method: The authors construct two corpora: a formal corpus from online news articles and an informal corpus from customer product reviews in web blogs. They then compare the linguistic expressions found in the two corpora and conduct a survey of the main characteristics of the informal corpus. Based on this analysis, they evaluate modeling approaches and introduce Local Grammar Graphs (LGG) as a suitable model.

Result: The comparison reveals clear differences between formal and informal Korean expressions, and the analysis highlights distinctive characteristics of the informal corpus. These findings support the view that standard formal-language models are insufficient for handling informal text in e-learning contexts.

Conclusion: Since a large portion of real-world Korean text is informal, e-learning systems that rely solely on formal language are incomplete. Local Grammar Graphs are proposed as an effective model for processing informal Korean expressions and should be incorporated into Korean e-learning systems to better reflect actual language use.

Abstract: E-learning systems should deliver contents that reflect various phenomena of the language as it is used. In addition to formal Korean, e-learning systems that would include real-world Korean expressions such as those in web documents, mobile text messages, or twitter posts, would be useful to high-level learners. We construct two types of corpora: one is made of formal documents like online news articles; the other is made of informal documents like customer reviews about new products in web blogs. By comparing these corpora, we show how expressions differ in these two types of corpora. We survey the main characteristics of the informal corpus. Given that a significant proportion of text is informal, we propose Local Grammar Graphs (LGG) as an appropriate model to treat them effectively in Korean e-learning systems.

</details>


### [80] [Verifiable Rewards Beyond Math and Code: Lightweight Corpus-Grounded Process Supervision for Factual Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29648)
*Shicheng Fan,Haochang Hao,Dehai Min,Weihao Liu,Philip S. Yu,Lu Cheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They introduce CorVer, a lightweight corpus-based reward for reinforcement learning in knowledge-intensive QA that uses Wikipedia co-occurrence statistics for sentence-level rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reward designs for RL in knowledge-intensive QA are problematic: response-level rewards are too coarse and cannot distinguish correct from incorrect parts of an answer, while sentence-level rewards typically rely on neural verifiers (NLI models, LLM judges, knowledge pipelines) that are costly and unreliable, especially for rare-entity facts where precise rewards are most needed.

Method: They propose CorVer, a process-level reward mechanism that replaces neural verifiers with a corpus-grounded signal derived from Wikipedia co-occurrence statistics. CorVer assigns sentence-level credit by measuring how well answer sentences are supported by co-occurrence patterns in Wikipedia, then maps these sentence scores to token-level advantages via a straightforward alignment. Implementation uses only a 0.5B-parameter extractor model and a single corpus lookup per sentence, making it lightweight and scalable.

Result: Across 30 (model, benchmark) combinations involving six instruction-tuned models (ranging from 3B to 14B parameters) and five QA datasets, CorVer consistently improves performance over an RL-free baseline, including an average gain of +4.1 percentage points on TriviaQA. Compared to four strong neural-verifier-based reward baselines, CorVer achieves better performance in 18 out of 20 comparable settings, while being 4.8–8.4 times faster to train.

Conclusion: Corpus-based, co-occurrence-driven process rewards like CorVer can provide fine-grained, scalable, and relatively reliable supervision for RL in knowledge-intensive QA, improving factual accuracy while avoiding the cost and brittleness of neural verifiers, particularly for rare-entity questions.

Abstract: Applying reinforcement learning to improve factual accuracy in knowledge-intensive question answering faces a reward design dilemma. Response-level rewards provide only coarse supervision and cannot distinguish correct from incorrect statements within a reasoning trace. Sentence-level alternatives offer finer-grained feedback, but typically rely on NLI verifiers, LLM judges, or knowledge-verification pipelines that are expensive to deploy at RL scale and often unreliable for rare-entity facts, where accurate reward signals are especially important. We propose CorVer (Corpus Verify), a lightweight, plug-in-ready process reward that replaces neural verifiers with a corpus-grounded signal derived from Wikipedia co-occurrence statistics. CorVer assigns sentence-level credit and maps it to token-level advantages via a simple alignment, requiring only a 0.5B extractor and a single corpus lookup per sentence. Across 30 (model, benchmark) cells spanning six instruction-tuned models (3B to 14B) and five QA benchmarks, CorVer improves over the raw baseline for every cell, with an average TriviaQA gain of +4.1 pp. It also outperforms four neural-verifier baselines in 18 of 20 cells under their feasible configurations, while training 4.8 to 8.4x faster.

</details>


### [81] [Beyond English and Evasion: A Human-Annotated Multi-Domain Benchmark for High-Stakes LLM Safety Evaluation in Chinese](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29667)
*Wajdi Zaghouani,Kholoud K. Aldous,Yicheng Gao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces ChiSafe-PAS, a human-annotated benchmark dataset of adversarial Chinese prompts for testing and improving LLM safety systems, which often fail when transferred from English to Chinese.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety filters and alignment methods tuned on English do not transfer reliably to Chinese contexts, where users employ language-specific obfuscation strategies (Pinyin, character decomposition, slang, hedging). This creates serious safety blindspots in high-risk domains.

Method: The authors construct ChiSafe-PAS, a dataset of 1,897 adversarial Chinese prompts in four high-stakes domains. For 1,544 prompts, they provide detailed human annotations: a 3-way label for desired model behavior, a nine-type obfuscation taxonomy, risk levels, and free-text rationales. They also carefully document dataset design and annotation procedures.

Result: They obtain a culturally grounded, fine-grained benchmark covering common Chinese adversarial techniques and risk levels, with rich labels suitable for evaluating and training LLM safety mechanisms in Chinese. The dataset explicitly captures multiple forms of linguistic obfuscation encountered in the wild.

Conclusion: ChiSafe-PAS fills a key gap in LLM safety research by providing a specialized, human-annotated benchmark for Chinese adversarial prompts. It supports more realistic evaluation of safety alignment, highlights tensions around training/eval leakage and domain coverage, and argues that cultural expertise, not just larger scale, is essential for safe deployment.

Abstract: When Large Language Models (LLMs) are deployed in Chinese-language settings, a troubling pattern emerges: safety systems that work well in English break down. These systems struggle to cross linguistic and cultural bound-aries, leaving models exposed to adversarial prompts that exploit Chinese-specific evasion techniques, including Pinyin romanization, character decomposition, internet slang, and hedging tone. To address this gap, we introduce ChiSafe-PAS (Chinese Safety Pilot Annotation Set), a human-annotated benchmark of 1,897 adversarial Chinese prompts spanning four high-stakes domains: self-harm and violence, drug and illicit trade, fraud, and satire. Of these, 1,544 entries carry complete gold-standard annotations: a 3-class response label (REFUSE, SAFE-REDIRECT, RESPOND), a nine-category obfuscation taxonomy, a risk-level rating, and annotator rationale. We describe the dataset design, annotation process, and obfuscation taxonomy in detail. Our primary goal is practical: to give the research community a high-quality, culturally grounded resource for benchmarking LLM safety alignment. In doing so, we engage three broader tensions in the field: the blurring boundary between training and evaluation data, the need for domain coverage grounded in real-world risk, and the limits of scale as a substitute for cultural expertise.

</details>


### [82] [EviLink: Multi-Path Schema Linking with Uncertainty-Guided Evidence Acquisition for Large-Scale Text-to-SQL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29670)
*Huawei Zheng,Sen Yang,Zhaorui Yang,Yuhui Zhang,Haozhe Feng,Haoxuan Li,Xuan Yi,Chao Hu,Defeng Xie,Chen Hou,Danqing Huang,Wei Chen,Yingcai Wu,Peng Chen,Dazhen Deng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new, uncertainty-aware way to perform schema linking for large-scale Text-to-SQL, improving how systems pick relevant database schema elements while controlling token cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In large-scale Text-to-SQL, databases are big and ambiguous, so models must select a small but sufficient portion of the schema to answer a question. Existing schema linking approaches usually assume a single deterministic SQL path per question and select schema based on that, which fails when questions have multiple valid SQL interpretations that require different schema elements. This leads to either missing necessary schema items or including too many irrelevant ones, hurting both performance and efficiency.

Method: The authors reframe schema linking as uncertainty-aware schema-need inference over multiple plausible SQL paths. Instead of committing to one SQL interpretation, their approach identifies which schema items are required across paths and which are path-dependent and uncertain, and then selectively seeks additional evidence only for the uncertain parts. They implement this idea in a system called EviLink, which combines multi-hypothesis schema grounding (considering several plausible SQL paths/hypotheses) with uncertainty-guided evidence acquisition (actively gathering more information when the need for a schema item is uncertain).

Result: On the BIRD-Dev and Spider2-Snow benchmarks, the proposed approach outperforms previous methods in balancing three factors: schema completeness (not missing needed schema), schema relevance (avoiding irrelevant schema items), and token cost (keeping the input compact). On Spider2-Snow, EviLink reaches a 90.15% strict recall at the field level, uses on average 123.30K tokens, and leads to better downstream SQL generation performance when the SQL generator model is kept fixed.

Conclusion: Treating schema linking as an uncertainty-aware schema-need inference problem over multiple plausible SQL paths leads to better schema selection for Text-to-SQL, improving both efficiency and effectiveness. The EviLink system demonstrates that multi-hypothesis grounding with targeted evidence acquisition can significantly enhance schema recall and downstream SQL generation while controlling token usage.

Abstract: Schema linking is a difficult and important step in large-scale Text-to-SQL, where systems must identify a compact yet sufficient schema context from large and ambiguous databases. Existing methods often treat schema linking as deterministic selection around a single SQL path, but complex questions may admit multiple valid realizations with different schema needs. We reframe schema linking as uncertainty-aware schema-need inference over multiple plausible SQL paths, where the system distinguishes required schema items from path-dependent uncertain ones and acquires evidence only where needed. We instantiate this reframing with EviLink, which combines multi-hypothesis schema grounding with uncertainty-guided evidence acquisition. Experiments on BIRD-Dev and Spider2-Snow show that this perspective improves the balance among schema completeness, schema relevance, and token cost. On Spider2-Snow, EviLink achieves 90.15% field-level strict recall rate, uses 123.30K average tokens, and improves downstream SQL generation under a fixed generator.

</details>


### [83] [Spurious Prompts: Can Irrelevant Prompts Steer Large Language Models?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29678)
*Pawel Batorski,Abtin Pourhadi,Jerzy Sarosiek,Przemyslaw Spurek,Paul Swoboda*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper shows that large language models can be strongly influenced by “spurious prompts” that are unrelated to the task, which can both boost accuracy and cause systematic but unintended behaviors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While prompt sensitivity has mainly been studied with meaningful task instructions and examples, it is unknown whether task-irrelevant text can also steer model behavior in systematic ways. Understanding this is important for both getting better performance and ensuring safe, predictable model behavior.

Method: The authors define “spurious prompts” as task-irrelevant text added to the prompt. They then design a black-box search procedure to automatically discover such prompts that improve or systematically change model outputs. They evaluate these prompts on multiple reasoning and QA benchmarks, using several LLM families (0.8B–27B parameters), comparing spurious prompts against standard prompting and prompt-optimization baselines.

Result: Spurious prompts are surprisingly effective: they often match or outperform standard task-aware prompts on reasoning and QA tasks. Moreover, carefully found spurious prompts can reliably bias models toward specific unintended behaviors like always choosing the first option or producing numbers with particular properties, without explicitly asking for that behavior.

Conclusion: LLMs exhibit a new kind of prompt sensitivity: they can be consistently and strongly steered by text that is semantically unrelated to the task. This has implications for prompt design, performance optimization, and safety, since innocuous-looking or irrelevant text in prompts may substantially alter model behavior in hard-to-predict ways.

Abstract: Large language models are highly sensitive to prompts, but this sensitivity is usually studied through task-relevant instructions, demonstrations, or reasoning cues. In this paper, we study a different form of prompt sensitivity: whether prompts that are semantically unrelated to the task can nevertheless steer model behavior. We call them spurious prompts and show their surprising efficacy. We also propose a simple black-box search procedure for discovering them. Across reasoning and question-answering benchmarks, using models ranging from 0.8B to 27B parameters and spanning three model families, we show that spurious prompts can improve performance, often matching or outperforming standard prompting baselines and task-aware prompt optimization. We further show that they can steer models toward unintended behaviors, such as repeatedly selecting the first answer option, producing incorrect answers, returning an even, prime or small number without explicitly instructing the model to do so. These findings reveal a new kind of prompt sensitivity: LLMs can be systematically steered by prompts that are unrelated to the task they are asked to solve. Our code is available at https://github.com/Batorskq/spurious

</details>


### [84] [Scaling Laws for Agent Harnesses via Effective Feedback Compute](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29682)
*Xuanliang Zhang,Dingzirui Wang,Keyan Xu,Qingfu Zhu,Wanxiang Che*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes Effective Feedback Compute (EFC), a new way to measure and predict the performance of agentic LLM systems by focusing on the quality and usefulness of feedback rather than raw computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing test-time scaling analyses for LLM agents mostly track raw resources (tokens, tool calls, time, money), which fail to distinguish between productive feedback and wasteful or unstable interactions. This makes it hard to understand or optimize how agent harnesses should be scaled. The authors want a metric that captures how effectively an agent converts compute into actionable, lasting feedback that actually drives task success across diverse tasks with different feedback needs.

Method: They define Effective Feedback Compute (EFC) as a trace-level metric that only counts feedback when it is informative, valid, non-redundant, and used in later decisions. They also normalize EFC by a task-demand term so that tasks with inherently different feedback requirements can be compared fairly. The authors then empirically validate EFC on synthetic controllable tasks, executable code tasks, real benchmark traces, held-out splits, and a prospective validation batch. They compare EFC-based coordinates to baselines based on raw tokens, tool calls, overall cost/operations, and a strong multivariate SAS model. They analyze R^2 fits, conduct controlled scaling experiments, and run matched-budget interventions where feedback quality is improved while raw compute is held constant.

Result: EFC-based coordinates consistently predict failure rates more accurately than raw compute baselines and the strong SAS baseline. In controlled scaling, raw tokens and tool calls explain only limited variance in performance (R^2 of 0.33 and 0.42). The SAS baseline reaches R^2 = 0.88, while Oracle-EFC and Estimated-EFC reach 0.94, and Oracle-EFC normalized by task demand (D_task) reaches 0.99. Matched-budget interventions show that increasing feedback quality can raise success rates from 0.27 to 0.90 without increasing raw tokens or tool calls. On mixed real traces, NRS-EFC/D_task achieves R^2 = 0.92, whereas raw compute has near-zero or even negative predictive fit, and EFC remains the best predictor in a prospective holdout with R^2 = 0.85.

Conclusion: The dominant factor in harness scaling for LLM agents is not the total amount of computation but how effectively that computation is transformed into durable, task-adequate feedback. Effective Feedback Compute (especially when normalized by task demand) serves as a superior scaling coordinate and predictor of success across a variety of tasks and datasets, suggesting that future work should prioritize improving feedback quality and its retention rather than merely increasing compute or tool usage.

Abstract: Agent harnesses increasingly determine the performance of language-model systems by deciding how models call tools, receive feedback, verify intermediate states, store memory, and revise solutions. Yet current test-time scaling analyses often parameterize this process by raw expenditure -- tokens, tool calls, operations, wall time, or cost -- which does not distinguish useful feedback from redundant or unstable interaction. We introduce \emph{Effective Feedback Compute} (EFC), a trace-level scaling coordinate that credits feedback only when it is informative, valid, non-redundant, and retained for subsequent decisions, and we normalize it by task demand when comparing tasks with different feedback requirements. Across synthetic controllable tasks, executable code tasks, real benchmark traces, held-out splits, and a prospective validation batch, EFC-based coordinates consistently predict failure rates better than raw-compute baselines and a strong multivariate SAS baseline. In controlled scaling, raw tokens and tool calls explain limited variation ($R^2=0.33$ and $0.42$), SAS reaches $0.88$, while Oracle-EFC and Estimated-EFC reach $0.94$ and Oracle-EFC/$D_{\mathrm{task}}$ reaches $0.99$. Matched-budget interventions show that improving feedback quality raises success from $0.27$ to $0.90$ while raw cost and tool calls are fixed. On mixed real traces, NRS-EFC/$D_{\mathrm{task}}$ reaches $R^2=0.92$ while raw compute has near-zero or negative fit, and it remains the best predictor in a prospective holdout ($R^2=0.85$). These results suggest that harness scaling is governed less by how much computation is spent than by how efficiently raw budget is converted into durable, task-sufficient feedback.

</details>


### [85] [Domino: Decoupling Causal Modeling from Autoregressive Drafting in Speculative Decoding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29707)
*Jianuo Huang,Yaojie Zhang,Qituan Zhang,Hao Lin,Hanlin Xu,Linfeng Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Domino is a speculative decoding framework that accelerates LLM inference by combining a parallel draft backbone with a lightweight causal refinement head, achieving higher speedups without sacrificing draft quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing speculative decoding methods face a trade-off between draft quality and drafting cost. Autoregressive drafters capture causal dependencies but are slow due to sequential execution, while parallel drafters are fast but fail to model strong intra-block dependencies, limiting verification acceptance and overall speedup. A new method is needed that preserves causal dependency modeling while retaining the efficiency of parallel drafting.

Method: Domino uses a two-stage drafting architecture. First, a parallel draft backbone generates preliminary draft token distributions for an entire block in parallel. Then, a lightweight Domino head refines these distributions using prefix-dependent causal information, effectively injecting causal structure without full autoregressive execution. Training uses a base-anchored curriculum: it initially focuses on strengthening the parallel backbone’s predictions, and progressively shifts optimization toward the Domino head’s causally corrected final distributions to stabilize learning under teacher-forced causal encoding.

Result: When applied to Qwen3 models, Domino delivers up to 5.49x end-to-end speedup with the standard Transformers backend and up to 5.8x throughput speedup under the SGLang serving stack, suggesting that it achieves both efficient drafting and high verification acceptance in practical deployment settings.

Conclusion: By decoupling causal dependency modeling from expensive autoregressive drafting and introducing a tailored training curriculum, Domino improves the efficiency of speculative decoding, leading to substantial real-world speedups in LLM inference while preserving modeling quality.

Abstract: Speculative decoding accelerates LLM inference by drafting multiple tokens and verifying them in parallel with the target model. However, its practical speedup is constrained by the trade-off between draft quality and drafting cost: autoregressive drafters model causal dependencies among draft tokens but incur sequential overhead, while parallel drafters reduce drafting cost but weaken intra-block dependency modeling.
