Verb bias has also previously been linked to in-context learning; in further analyses, we find that steering vectors encode error signals that could drive the error-driven update behavior seen in in-context learning but that these aspects of the steering vectors are not causally used in downstream production. Overall, these results show causal interventions can be applied to continuous variables, though connecting continuous variables to in-context learning remains a challenge.

</details>


### [107] [Adapting Multilingual Embedding Models to Turkish via Cross-Lingual Tokenizer Surgery and Offline Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29992)
*M. Ali Bayram,Banu Diri,Savaş Yıldırım*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a new, efficient Turkish sentence embedding model (embeddingmagibu-200m) with long context, trained via a three-stage adaptation/distillation pipeline that outperforms its larger teacher while being cheaper and smaller.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing Turkish sentence encoders are BERT-based with short context windows (around 512 tokens) and are often expensive to pretrain from scratch. There is a need for a Turkish-focused embedding model that supports long context, is parameter-efficient and cheap to train, yet remains competitive or better in quality, and that can be reproduced and reused by others.

Method: They design a 3-step adaptation and distillation pipeline instead of full pretraining: (1) build a Turkish-optimized multilingual tokenizer (vocab 131k) by pruning redundant tokens from the teacher tokenizer and adding multilingual tokens based on frequency analysis on a 40-language corpus; (2) clone the teacher embedding model by keeping transformer backbone weights and creating a new embedding matrix aligned to the new vocabulary through mean-composition token mapping; (3) perform offline embedding distillation using precomputed teacher sentence embeddings and a cosine-similarity objective over a balanced 40-language Wikipedia corpus, thus avoiding online teacher inference and significantly reducing training cost. The student model has about 200M parameters and supports an 8,192-token context window.

Result: The student model, embeddingmagibu-200m, yields 768-dim L2-normalized sentence embeddings, supports an 8,192-token context window, and trains in about four hours on a single GPU at an estimated cost of $5–$20. On the Turkish STSbTR benchmark, it achieves Pearson/Spearman correlations of 77.55%/77.45%, beating its 300M-parameter teacher model (73.84%/72.92%). On the Turkish subset of MTEB (TR-MTEB, 26 tasks), it scores 63.9% on average, ranking 7th of 26 models and offering a good cost-quality trade-off with 33% fewer parameters than the teacher.

Conclusion: A Turkish-focused sentence embedding model can be efficiently obtained via tokenizer adaptation, model cloning, and offline embedding distillation, without costly full pretraining. The proposed embeddingmagibu-200m achieves better or comparable performance to a larger teacher while being cheaper, smaller, and supporting much longer context. By releasing all models, tokenizers, and datasets, the work enables reproducibility and practical adoption for Turkish-language semantic NLP tasks.

Abstract: Sentence embeddings are a foundational component for semantic search, clustering, classification, and retrieval-augmented generation. This paper presents embeddingmagibu-200m, a Turkish-focused sentence embedding model that produces 768-dimensional L2-normalized vectors and supports an 8,192-token context window, far exceeding the 512-token limit of earlier BERT-based Turkish encoders. Instead of full pretraining, an efficient three-stage adaptation pipeline is introduced: (1) construct a Turkish-optimized multilingual tokenizer with a 131,072 vocabulary by pruning redundant tokens from the teacher's vocabulary and incorporating multilingual tokens via frequency analysis on a 40-language corpus, (2) clone a teacher embedding model while preserving transformer backbone weights and initializing a compatible embedding table for the new vocabulary via mean-composition token mapping, and (3) perform offline embedding distillation from precomputed teacher vectors using a cosine similarity objective over a balanced 40-language Wikipedia corpus. The resulting student model contains approximately 200M parameters and trains in roughly four hours on a single GPU by avoiding online teacher inference during training, at a total cost of $5-$20. Empirically, Pearson/Spearman correlations of 77.55%/77.45% are obtained on STSbTR, surpassing the 300M-parameter teacher model (73.84%/72.92%). On TR-MTEB (26 tasks), a mean score of 63.9% is achieved (7th out of 26 models), providing a competitive cost-quality trade-off with 33% fewer parameters than the teacher. To facilitate reproducibility and downstream use, all artifacts are released including model weights, tokenizer files, precomputed embedding datasets, and open-source cloning and distillation tooling.

</details>


### [108] [Latent Performance Profiling of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30018)
*Tanmoy Chakraborty,Ayan Sengupta,Suparna Bhattacharya,Partha Pratim Chakrabarti,Amlan Chakrabarti,Supratik Chakraborty,Partha Pratim Das,Lipika Dey,Richa Singh,Mayank Vatsa*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper argues that benchmark accuracy alone is insufficient to evaluate LLMs and proposes Latent Performance Profiling (LPP), a framework that uses hidden activations and output distributions to derive intrinsic, task-agnostic metrics for deeper, more reliable model assessment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard leaderboard-style benchmarks for LLMs suffer from contamination, narrow scope, and weak alignment with real-world reliability, and they only measure what models output, not how they internally process information or represent uncertainty. There is a need for evaluation methods that look inside models to capture scale-independent, architecture-sensitive properties and uncover vulnerabilities that benchmarks miss.

Method: The authors introduce Latent Performance Profiling (LPP), which defines a set of scalar, task-agnostic metrics computed from LLMs’ hidden activations and output distributions. These metrics capture properties like entropy, adaptability, and other aspects of latent representations and dynamics, producing model signatures that are independent of task benchmarks and allow interpretable comparison across models of similar size. They then apply LPP empirically to eight LLMs ranging from 0.5B to 14B parameters.

Result: The empirical analysis shows that models with similar traditional benchmark scores can differ substantially in their latent profiles, including in entropy and adaptability. LPP yields stable, architecture-sensitive signatures that highlight hidden vulnerabilities and differences not visible from accuracy alone. Using insights from LPP, the authors design synthetic probes for uncertainty and symbolic reasoning that correlate with intrinsic metrics but are less tied to leaderboard biases.

Conclusion: Benchmark-centric evaluation is incomplete; combining traditional benchmarks with Latent Performance Profiling gives a deeper and more interpretable understanding of LLM behavior. LPP exposes intrinsic, scale-independent traits, reveals hidden vulnerabilities, and supports more reliable model selection and safety assessment by focusing on how models internally represent and process information rather than only their output accuracy.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) frequently achieve impressive scores on standardized benchmarks, yet accuracy alone offers a limited view of their capabilities. Evaluating open-source LLMs through leaderboards faces persistent issues like data contamination, narrow task scope, and weak alignment with real-world reliability. Benchmark-based evaluations such as MMLU PRO, BBH, or IFEval primarily capture \textit{what} a model outputs on fixed test sets, not \textit{how} it processes information, calibrates uncertainty, or structures internal knowledge. In this article, we advocate for a shift from benchmark-centric evaluation toward a complementary, \textit{state-centered intrinsic assessment} of LLMs. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Latent Performance Profiling (LPP)} -- a framework that derives task-agnostic diagnostics from hidden activations and output distributions. LPP defines a set of scalar metrics on a model's latent representations and dynamics, revealing scale-independent traits that enable interpretable comparisons and uncover hidden vulnerabilities. Unlike static accuracy scores, LPP provides stable, architecture-sensitive signatures across models of similar size. With extensive empirical analyses across eight LLMs, spanning a size range of 0.5B-14B, we demonstrate that models with similar benchmark scores can exhibit contrasting latent profiles, such as differences in entropy or adaptability. Guided by these insights, we design synthetic probes for uncertainty and symbolic reasoning that align with intrinsic metrics while decoupling from leaderboard bias. We recommend that reporting LPP alongside benchmarks provides a deeper, interpretable understanding of model behavior, enabling more reliable model selection, safety assessment, and evaluation beyond surface-level accuracy.

</details>


### [109] [Recovering Diversity Without Losing Alignment: A DPO Recipe for Post-Trained LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30021)
*Vinay Samuel,Yapei Chang,Mohit Iyyer*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes REDIPO, a data-construction pipeline for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) that restores answer diversity in instruction-tuned LLMs without sacrificing alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Instruction tuning often collapses the range of valid responses an LLM can produce, favoring a narrow, canonical style and reducing the diversity of useful answers. Existing diversity-enhancing approaches, such as DivPO, either fail to significantly increase diversity or hurt alignment and safety. The authors want a way to systematically reintroduce diverse but still safe and instruction-following answer modes from the base model back into the post-trained model.

Method: REDIPO is an offline preference data construction pipeline. For each prompt, it: (1) samples responses from both a base model and its corresponding instruction-tuned model; (2) rewrites base-model generations using the instruct model to better match its style while keeping semantic diversity; (3) filters all candidate responses using safety and instruction-following quality criteria; and (4) constructs preference pairs that explicitly favor marginally more diverse responses among answers with similar instruction-following scores. These preference pairs are then used to train the model via DPO, shifting its behavior toward multiple valid response modes while keeping alignment constraints.

Result: Applied to Qwen3-4B, OLMo-3-7B, and LLaMA-3.1-8B, REDIPO substantially increases response diversity as measured by NoveltyBench distinct_k: +134%, +33%, and +44% over the instruct baselines, whereas DivPO yields 0%, -6%, and -4% change, respectively. At the same time, REDIPO mostly preserves standard alignment and capability benchmarks (MTBench, IFEval, Arena-Hard) and improves safety by lowering direct-category HarmBench attack success rates. Ablation studies show that the marginal-diversity–based pair selection and the rewriting of base responses are primarily responsible for diversity gains, while filtering and enforcing quality-bounded pairing are key to maintaining alignment and safety.

Conclusion: Carefully engineered preference datasets can reintroduce diverse valid answer modes from base LLMs into instruction-tuned models without undoing their alignment benefits. REDIPO demonstrates that combining base/instruct sampling, semantic-preserving rewriting, safety and quality filtering, and marginal-diversity preference construction yields models that are both more diverse and comparably aligned to standard instruct checkpoints. The released code and data provide a practical recipe for improving answer diversity in post-trained LLMs.

Abstract: Many open-ended instructions have multiple valid answers that users can benefit from seeing, but post-training often narrows an LLM's output space toward a small set of canonical responses. We introduce REDIPO, an offline DPO data-construction pipeline for recovering distinct valid answer modes while preserving the alignment benefits of the instruct model. For each prompt, REDIPO samples responses from both base and instruct models, rewrites base-model responses with the instruct model, filters candidates for safety and instruction-following quality, and builds preference pairs that favor marginally diverse responses among candidates with similar instruction-following reward. Across Qwen3-4B, OLMo-3-7B, and LLaMA-3.1-8B, REDIPO improves NoveltyBench distinct_k by 134%, 33%, and 44% relative to the instruct checkpoints, while DivPO changes diversity by 0%, -6%, and -4% on the same models. These gains largely maintain MTBench, IFEval, and Arena-Hard performance, and reduce direct-category HarmBench attack success rate. Ablations show that marginal-diversity pair selection and base-response rewriting drive the diversity gains, while filtering and quality-bounded pairing help maintain alignment. Overall, our results show that diverse valid answers from base-model generations can be reintroduced through carefully constructed preference data while retaining the alignment benefits of post-training. We release our code and data at https://github.com/vsamuel2003/RiDiPO.

</details>


### [110] [Give it Space! Explicit Disentangling of Positional and Semantic Representations in Encoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30022)
*Pierre-Antoine Lequeu,Camille Barboule,Benjamin Piwowarski*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Study disentangles semantic and positional information streams in Transformers to analyze and improve positional encodings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Positional encoding is crucial for Transformers to represent sequence order, yet its internal processing and storage mechanisms are not well understood, and current methods struggle with long-context tasks. A deeper mechanistic understanding could lead to better positional encoding designs.

Method: Modify an encoder-only Transformer into three disentangled streams—semantic, absolute positional (AP), and relative positional (RP). Constrain the masked language modeling objective to the semantic stream, leaving AP and RP to develop without direct task pressure. Analyze the learned representations, attention head specializations, and compare with standard positional encodings like RoPE and entangled AP.

Result: (1) The AP subspace collapses into a low-frequency 2D manifold capturing document structure; (2) attention heads separate into structure- vs semantic-oriented groups, with RP used only for semantic heads; (3) standard positional encodings fail to robustly retain macroscopic structure—RoPE and RP encode it only weakly, and entangled AP loses it in later layers under MLM pressure—whereas the disentangled design preserves positional information and improves performance on 49/65 Flash-Holmes probing tasks.

Conclusion: Explicitly disentangling semantic and positional streams in Transformers reveals how positional information is internally organized and shows that conventional, entangled positional encodings are fragile under training objectives like MLM. The proposed disentangled architecture preserves macroscopic structure and yields better linguistic representations, suggesting a promising direction for designing more effective positional encoding schemes, especially for long-context understanding.

Abstract: Positional encoding (PE) underpins how permutation-invariant Transformers represent sequence order, yet how positional information is processed and stored remains poorly understood. Modern PE methods such as RoPE still struggle on tasks such as long-context understanding or retrieval \cite{chen-etal-2025-hope}. Hence, a better understanding of the internal positional mechanism could help design better PE. Building on evidence that positional and semantic signals occupy nearly orthogonal subspaces in trained Transformers, we modify an encoder Transformer to process three explicitly disentangled streams: semantic, absolute positional (AP) and relative positional (RP), and confine the masked-language-modeling (MLM) objective to the semantic stream. This decoupling enables a clean mechanistic study and yields three take-aways. (1) The isolated AP subspace spontaneously collapses into a low-frequency two-dimensional manifold that captures the structure of the document; (2) Attention heads specialize into structure and semantic-oriented groups, with RP exclusively supporting the latter; (3) Standard positional encodings do not robustly retain macroscopic structure: RoPE and RP only weakly encode it, and entangled AP loses it in the final layers under MLM pressure. The disentangled approach preserves positional encoding, which improves linguistic representation on 49 of the 65 linguistic phenomena of the Flash-Holmes probing benchmark.

</details>


### [111] [Who Am I? History-Aware Profiles for Student Simulation in Tutoring Dialogues](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30051)
*Zhangqi Duan,Shuyan Huang,Alexander Scarlatos,Jaewook Lee,Simon Woodhead,Andrew Lan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build an RL-trained framework that simulates student dialogue turns in tutoring systems using each student's prior interaction history.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated tutoring systems need realistic student simulators for safe, scalable evaluation and training, but most current simulators ignore students’ prior learning history, so they fail to capture realistic knowledge states and behavior.

Method: Define a new task, history-conditioned student simulation. Collect a real-world dataset of math learning dialogues and question responses. Propose a two-part framework: (1) a profile generator that summarizes each learner’s past interactions into a profile; and (2) a student simulator that predicts the learner’s next dialogue turn using the profile. Train both components jointly with reinforcement learning so that generated profiles are optimized for accurate, faithful simulation rather than just superficial summary quality. Compare against baselines that lack history, profiles, or RL training.

Result: On the collected real-world math learning dataset, their RL-trained, history-conditioned framework significantly outperforms baselines on predicting student dialogue turns. Ablation studies show that incorporating history, explicit profiles, and RL-based training each contribute meaningfully to performance gains.

Conclusion: Conditioning LLM-based student simulators on learned profiles derived from students’ actual learning histories substantially improves the fidelity of simulated student behavior. Reinforcement learning is an effective way to train both the profile generator and simulator jointly, and realistic history-conditioned simulation is feasible and beneficial for developing and evaluating LLM-powered tutoring systems.

Abstract: A key part of developing large language model (LLM)-powered, automated tutoring tools is student simulation, i.e., using LLMs to role-play as students, which can facilitate tutor model evaluation and training. Existing work mostly focuses on within-dialogue simulation, which lacks context on student knowledge and behavior, partly due to not grounding in past student question-answering or dialogue interactions. In this work, we introduce the task of history-conditioned student simulation, where the goal is to accurately predict student dialogue turns by leveraging information in the student's learning history. We propose a two-component framework in which a profile generator summarizes a student's history and a simulator predicts student turns conditioned on the resulting profile. We train both components with reinforcement learning (RL), yielding profiles optimized for faithful student simulation. We evaluate our method and baselines on the first-of-its-kind real-world dataset of student dialogues and question responses that we collect from a math learning platform. Extensive experiments show that our method significantly outperforms baselines, and demonstrate the importance of history, profiles, and RL training.

</details>


### [112] [HEART-Bench: Do LLM Agents Exhibit Human-like Psychology?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30058)
*Weihan Peng,Chenxu Zhang,Qianao Wang,Yuling Shi,Heng Lian,Qihong Mao,Jiahao Pang,Chunliang Feng,Bowen Li,Xiaodong Gu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Benchmark to evaluate if LLM agents can act as coherent human-like personalities using Big Five–based characters, rich episodic memories, and psychologically-grounded decision scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM research largely evaluates task-oriented skills like reasoning and planning, but overlooks whether LLMs can express stable, coherent human-like psychology, especially emotional and personality consistency across situations. The authors aim to fill this gap with a rigorous, scalable benchmark grounded in established psychological theory.

Method: They construct 11 diverse synthetic human characters, each defined by orthogonal Big Five personality traits and linked to 1,000 structured autobiographical episodic memories spanning different developmental life stages. They then design 64 decision-making situations informed by the DIAMONDS taxonomy (Duty, Intellect, Adversity, Mating, pOsitivity, Negativity, Deception, Sociality). For each scenario, they generate MCQs that probe how an LLM agent, instantiated as a given character, makes decisions. Human annotators validate and filter the items, resulting in 673 final MCQs. The benchmark tests whether agents integrate personality traits and memories to make personality-consistent behavioral choices across scenarios.

Result: The outcome is a validated benchmark dataset: 11 psychologically grounded character profiles, each with 1,000 life-episode memories, and 673 human-checked MCQs built from 64 DIAMONDS-based decision scenarios. While the abstract does not report quantitative model scores, it establishes that the benchmark is feasible, systematically designed, and human-validated for assessing personality- and memory-consistent behavior in LLMs.

Conclusion: The authors conclude that their benchmark offers a principled and scalable way to assess whether LLM agents can exhibit coherent, human-like psychological profiles—integrating personality traits, autobiographical memories, and situational context to make value-consistent decisions. This resource can support future research on emotions, personality stability, and psychologically plausible behavior in LLM-based agents.

Abstract: While LLM agents have demonstrated remarkable task-oriented abilities such as planning, reasoning, and action, few works have treated them as complete human personalities where emotional dimensions hold equal importance. In this paper, we introduce a novel benchmark to systematically assess whether LLM agents can simulate coherent, human-like psychology. Specifically, our benchmark constructs 11 diverse human characters grounded in orthogonal Big Five personality traits, with each profile deeply integrated with 1,000 structured autobiographical-style episodic memories distributed across theory-grounded developmental life stages. To rigorously evaluate the psychological manifestations of LLMs, we designed a curated suite of 64 decision-making scenarios, guided by the DIAMONDS taxonomy, a psychological framework that characterizes situations along eight dimensions: Duty, Intellect, Adversity, Mating, pOsitivity, Negativity, Deception, and Sociality. By subjecting agents to varying scenarios, the benchmark evaluates whether they can consolidate their innate personality traits and autobiographical memories to make behavioral decisions that are consistent with their specific psychological profiles. After systematic human validation and filtering, we obtained a benchmark consisting of 673 multiple-choice questions (MCQs). We believe this benchmark provides a principled and scalable testbed for studying human-like emotions, personality consistency, and value-consistent behavioural decision-making in LLM-based agents.

</details>


### [113] [UniSteer: Text-Guided Flow Matching in Activation Space for Versatile LLM Steering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30076)
*Yingdong Shi,Ruiming Zhang,Changming Li,Zhiyu Yang,Kaixing Zhang,Jingyi Yu,Kan Ren*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: UniSteer is a universal, text-guided activation-based controller for LLMs that learns a conditional flow in activation space, enabling flexible behavior control and activation-space classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing activation-based control methods for LLMs either use fixed steering vectors or train separate, task-specific intervention modules. These approaches don’t scale well to fine-grained concepts, compositional constraints, or multiple behaviors, because each new behavior often requires a new intervention. The authors want a single, general mechanism that can flexibly steer diverse behaviors and concepts using natural-language descriptions, while also supporting tasks like classification directly in activation space.

Method: The authors introduce UniSteer, a text-guided activation flow matching model over the residual stream of an LLM. UniSteer learns a universal conditional velocity field that maps activations under a source condition toward those consistent with a target natural-language condition. At inference, it performs partial flow inversion: it transports the current activation toward a latent state conditioned on the target text, regenerates a compatible activation, and then injects this modified activation back into the frozen base LLM. The same conditional flow model defines an energy for reconstruction under each textual label, enabling activation-space classification by picking the label with minimal reconstruction energy.

Result: Across three different target LLMs, UniSteer successfully controls diverse behaviors using the same learned conditional model. It is able to steer persona/style-like behaviors, increase truthfulness, manipulate fine-grained concepts, enforce multiple constraints in instruction following, and perform classification in activation space. The results indicate that a single universal flow-based controller can match or surpass task-specific steering baselines while being more flexible and compositional.

Conclusion: UniSteer demonstrates that a universal, text-conditioned flow in activation space can provide a unified interface for controlling and interpreting LLM behaviors. By learning a conditional distribution over residual-stream activations, it replaces many separate, hand-designed or task-specific steering mechanisms with a single model that supports fine-grained, compositional control and activation-space classification, all while keeping the base LLM frozen.

Abstract: Activation-based control steers large language models (LLMs) by intervening on their internal representations during inference, and has emerged as an effective paradigm for controlling behaviors such as persona and style. However, existing methods often rely on fixed steering directions or task-specific intervention modules, making them difficult to adapt to fine-grained concepts and compositional constraints. We propose UniSteer, a text-guided activation flow matching model that learns a conditional distribution over residual-stream activations from natural-language conditions. Instead of fitting a separate intervention for each target behavior, UniSteer learns a universal conditional velocity field in activation space. At inference time, UniSteer performs flow inversion by partially transporting a source activation toward a latent state and regenerating it under a target textual condition before injecting it back into the frozen LLM. The same conditional model supports activation-space classification by selecting the textual label with the lowest reconstruction energy. Experiments on three target LLMs show that UniSteer provides a unified interface across behavioral control, truthfulness steering, fine-grained concept steering, multi-constraint instruction following, and activation-space classification.

</details>


### [114] [Adaptive Targeted Dynamic Chunking for Tokenization-Free Hierarchical Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30080)
*Thang Dang,Akira Nakagawa,Kenichi Kobayashi,Koichi Shirahata*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Adaptive Targeted Dynamic Chunking (ATDC), a curriculum-based byte-compression control mechanism for tokenization-free hierarchical language models, improving compression control, training stability, and downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tokenization-free, byte-level hierarchical language models avoid vocabulary and OOV issues but struggle to choose and control the compression ratio for chunking bytes, which critically affects performance and stability. Existing methods typically use fixed compression ratios, leading to suboptimal compression, unstable training, and difficulty balancing efficiency with modeling power. A method is needed to dynamically and adaptively control compression during training while preserving the advantages of byte-level processing.

Method: The authors propose Adaptive Targeted Dynamic Chunking (ATDC), which controls byte-level compression in hierarchical models via curriculum learning. Training starts with low compression (smaller chunks, easier learning) and progressively increases to higher compression (larger chunks, better efficiency). ATDC explicitly targets a desired compression ratio and uses an analysis linking this target to Bytes-Per-Innermost-Chunk (BPIC), enabling monitoring and steering of chunk-size evolution over training. This mechanism is integrated into tokenization-free hierarchical architectures that operate directly on bytes, replacing fixed-ratio dynamic chunking with an adaptive schedule.

Result: On the FineWeb-Edu 100B dataset, hierarchical models equipped with ATDC achieve Bits-Per-Byte (BPB) performance competitive with strong byte- and token-level baselines. Compared to hierarchical models that use fixed compression ratios, ATDC-based models show smoother, more stable training curves and obtain better final metrics on a range of downstream tasks, while maintaining byte-level robustness across languages and inputs.

Conclusion: Controlling byte-level compression via an adaptive curriculum is key for effective tokenization-free hierarchical language models. ATDC provides a principled way to relate target compression to actual chunk structure (via BPIC), yielding stable training, competitive compression efficiency, and improved downstream performance over fixed-ratio baselines, without sacrificing the flexibility and robustness of byte-level modeling.

Abstract: Tokenization-free hierarchical models are emerging as a promising alternative to traditional Large Language Models (LLMs), addressing inherent preprocessing issues such as vocabulary design complexity, out-of-vocabulary (OOV) errors, and language-specific constraints. However, a significant challenge in these byte-level methods is the optimization of the compression ratio, a critical factor that dictates model performance for processing bytes data via chunks. In this paper, we propose Adaptive Targeted Dynamic Chunking (ATDC), a novel byte-compression control mechanism designed to enhance the effectiveness of dynamic chunking within hierarchical architectures. Our approach utilizes curriculum learning to progressively adjust the compression ratio during training, transitioning from low to high compression to stabilize the learning process. We provide an analysis establishing the relationship between the target compression ratio and Bytes-Per-Innermost-Chunk (BPIC), allowing for tracking of chunk-size evolution throughout the training phase. Evaluations conducted on the FineWeb-Edu 100B dataset demonstrate that hierarchical models equipped with ATDC achieve competitive Bits-Per-Byte (BPB) performance compared to conventional baselines operating at both byte and token levels. Furthermore, the proposed method exhibits more stable training dynamics and superior final performance across diverse downstream tasks compared to models using fixed compression ratios, while maintaining the inherent robustness and flexibility of byte-level processing.

</details>


### [115] [DirectorBench: Diagnosing Long-Form Video Generation with Personalized Multi-Agent Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30090)
*Jiamin Chen,Qianben Chen,Jiawen Zhang,Yidi Wu,Yuchen Li,Xiaokun Zhang,Wangchunshu Zhou,Chen Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: DirectorBench is a diagnostic benchmark for long-form video generation that evaluates workflows across script, visuals, audio, cross-modal sync, and stability using rich metadata, user profiles, and checkpoint-based criteria instead of a single score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video generation benchmarks mostly assess short clips and aggregate quality into single scores, failing to capture narrative structure, multi-shot transitions, workflow bottlenecks, and user-specific preferences in long-form video. There is a need for a detailed, profile-aware diagnostic tool that can reveal where generation workflows fail, especially in transitions and multi-modal alignment.

Method: The authors design DirectorBench, a personalized multi-agent diagnostic benchmark with 80 structured metadata entries, 7 distinct user profiles, and 40 checkpoint criteria spanning five dimensions: script, visual, audio, cross-modal, and stability. They apply it to evaluate 4 long-form video generation workflows and 6 base LLMs, with checkpoint-level scoring and profile-aware analysis instead of a single aggregate metric. They also run a human study with 14 annotators to compare DirectorBench outcomes to human judgments.

Result: DirectorBench identifies a key between-unit bottleneck in current workflows: transition quality between shots is low (average score 0.256, best workflow 0.356), even though prompt-level user demand fulfillment is relatively high (average 0.71). The benchmark reveals workflow-specific and user-profile-dependent failure modes that conventional aggregate scores obscure. The human evaluation shows that DirectorBench’s assessments correlate with human-perceived quality differences.

Conclusion: Diagnostic, profile-aware benchmarking like DirectorBench is better suited than traditional aggregate metrics for assessing long-form video generation. It can localize bottlenecks, expose nuanced workflow and user-profile-specific failure modes, and aligns well with human judgments, underscoring the need for richer evaluation tools as video generation systems become more complex.

Abstract: Long-form video generation is rapidly moving from short, single-scene synthesis toward minute-long, multi-shot creation with narrative structure, cinematic control, audio, and cross-modal synchronization. However, evaluating such videos remains challenging, since existing benchmarks largely focus on local visual quality, short-horizon temporal consistency, or generic prompt alignment, and provide limited diagnosis of workflow failures and user-dependent preferences. We introduce DirectorBench, a personalized multi-agent diagnostic benchmark for long-form video generation. DirectorBench evaluates generated videos with respect to 80 structured metadata entries, 7 user profiles, and 40 checkpoint criteria across 5 dimensions: script, visual, audio, cross-modal, and stability. Instead of reducing quality to a single aggregate score, DirectorBench localizes checkpoint-level bottlenecks and supports profile-aware evaluation. We evaluate 4 long-form video generation workflows, 6 base LLMs, and 7 user profiles. Across workflows, DirectorBench reveals a between-unit bottleneck: transition quality averages only 0.256 and reaches 0.356 for the best workflow, while prompt-level user demand fulfillment averages 0.71. We further conduct human evaluation with 14 annotators to validate the alignment between DirectorBench and human judgment. The results show that DirectorBench captures human-perceptible quality differences and reveals workflow- and profile-dependent failure modes that are hidden by aggregate scoring. These findings highlight the importance of diagnostic and profile-aware benchmarking for long-form video generation.

</details>


### [116] [SEAL: Can Saturated Benchmarks Be Revived by LLM-as-a-Meta-Judge?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30104)
*Jiamin Chen,Yidi Wu,Qiexiang Wang,Qianben Chen,Yuchen Li,Yansen Zhang,Xiaokun Zhang,Wangchunshu Zhou,Chen Ma*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes SEAL, a new evaluation protocol that makes saturated language-model benchmarks informative again by extracting better ranking signals from existing candidate outputs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Common language-model benchmarks are becoming saturated, with state-of-the-art systems achieving nearly tied scores that standard metrics cannot distinguish. Building entirely new and harder benchmarks is costly and slow. The authors want to know whether more nuanced and efficient evaluation methods can recover meaningful performance differences from the same benchmark tasks and outputs.

Method: They introduce Seeded Elimination with Adaptive LLM-as-a-Meta-Judge (SEAL). SEAL organizes candidate model outputs into a single-elimination tournament, seeding them and having an LLM-based judge compare outputs in each match. Judging uses task-specific principles plus a dynamically refined checklist of criteria that improves over time. This meta-judge adaptively refines its evaluation to extract latent ranking signals on saturated benchmarks, while reducing the number of pairwise comparisons needed compared to full pairwise evaluation.

Result: On several saturated benchmarks spanning code generation, mathematical reasoning, knowledge-intensive QA, and tool-use agent tasks, SEAL achieves high agreement with exhaustive full pairwise judging. It reaches Spearman rank correlations between 0.83 and 1.00 and perfect top-1 agreement (4/4 cases), while cutting evaluation cost to about 11.89 LLM calls per task versus 28.00 for full pairwise comparison.

Conclusion: SEAL demonstrates that existing saturated benchmarks can still yield informative rankings of advanced language models if evaluated with a more sophisticated, adaptive protocol. By using an LLM meta-judge in a seeded elimination structure with self-improving criteria, SEAL enhances ranking accuracy at significantly lower evaluation cost, offering a practical alternative to continually creating harder benchmarks.

Abstract: Widely used language-model benchmarks are increasingly saturated, with frontier systems often receiving near-tied scores that standard metrics cannot resolve. Rather than constructing harder alternatives, we ask whether existing tasks can be made informative again through improved evaluation over the same candidate outputs. Therefore, we present Seeded Elimination with Adaptive LLM-as-a-Meta-Judge, a self-improving evaluation protocol for extracting latent ranking signal from saturated benchmarks. SEAL seeds candidate outputs into a single elimination and evaluates each match with task-level principles plus self-improving checklist criteria. We evaluate SEAL on multiple saturated benchmarks covering code generation, mathematical reasoning, knowledge-intensive question answering, and tool-use agent task completion. Across these settings, SEAL improves the ranking-accuracy--latency trade-off over competing protocols, attaining 0.83--1.00 Spearman agreement with full pairwise judging and 4/4 top-1 agreement, while requiring only 11.89 calls per task compared with 28.00 for full pairwise evaluation.

</details>


### [117] [Dial HEALTHDIAL for Advice: A Multilingual and Multi-Parallel Spoken Dialogue Dataset for Knowledge-Grounded Information Seeking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30107)
*Songbo Hu,Yinhong Liu,Ej Zhou,Evgeniia Razumovskaia,Xiaobin Wang,Alexander Fraser,Ivan Vulić,Anna Korhonen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents HEALTHDIAL, a large multilingual, multi-parallel spoken dialogue dataset for RAG-based systems in four WHO languages, with detailed speaker annotations and benchmark results showing cross-language performance gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Building robust multilingual spoken dialogue systems, especially retrieval-augmented ones, requires large, high-quality, parallel datasets across languages, but collecting such data is methodologically difficult, particularly for speech and at scale. There is also a need to understand and quantify performance disparities across languages, even for high-resource ones, and to provide shared benchmarks, tools, and resources to enable reproducible research in this space, especially for health information grounded in trusted content like WHO resources.

Method: The authors design and collect HEALTHDIAL: 6,000 information-seeking spoken dialogues (1,500 per language) grounded in WHO content across Arabic, Chinese, English, and Spanish. Native speakers of diverse dialects record 163 hours of user speech, and each speaker is annotated with demographic and sociolinguistic variables such as gender, age, primary language, and region of origin. Using this dataset, they build RAG-based spoken dialogue benchmarks and evaluate baseline systems on key dialogue tasks, comparing performance across languages. They also develop and release a prototype system and a general-purpose toolkit for data collection and system evaluation.

Result: HEALTHDIAL offers a substantial multilingual, multi-parallel dataset of spoken, information-seeking health dialogues with rich speaker metadata. Benchmark evaluations on key dialogue tasks show consistent and notable performance gaps between languages, including among those considered high-resource, highlighting cross-lingual inequities in current models. The authors also successfully produce a prototype RAG-based dialogue system and an accompanying toolkit that can be reused by the community.

Conclusion: HEALTHDIAL fills an important gap in resources for multilingual, RAG-based spoken dialogue research by providing a large, health-focused, multi-parallel dataset with detailed annotations, along with a prototype system and toolkit. The observed cross-language performance disparities underscore the need for targeted methods and evaluation practices to improve equity and robustness across languages. The released resources are intended to catalyze further work on multilingual spoken dialogue, especially in health information access.

Abstract: Creating spoken dialogue datasets is methodologically challenging, and these challenges are amplified when the goal is to build multilingual, multi-parallel datasets at scale. This work introduces HEALTHDIAL, a large-scale, multilingual, and multi-parallel dataset for developing and evaluating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)-based spoken dialogue systems. The dataset comprises 6,000 information-seeking dialogues (1,500 per language) grounded in trusted content from the World Health Organization (WHO) and 163 hours of user speech recorded from native speakers of diverse dialects across four official WHO languages: Arabic, Chinese, English, and Spanish. Each speaker is annotated with demographic (e.g., gender, age) and sociolinguistic (e.g., primary language, region of origin) variables. We report benchmark results across key dialogue tasks, which reveal consistent performance disparities across languages, even among high-resource ones. To support future research, we release the dataset, a prototype system, and a toolkit for data collection and system evaluation.

</details>


### [118] [CCS: Clinical Consensus Selection for Radiology Report Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30131)
*Xi Zhang,Yingshu Li,Zaiqiao Meng,Jake Lever,Edmond S. L. Ho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper improves radiology report generation by selecting, at inference time, the most clinically consistent report among multiple candidates rather than relying on a single decoded output.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing radiology report generation systems focus on bigger models, more data, and better retrieval, but at inference they usually output just one report from a single decoding path. However, these models often internally produce alternative candidate reports that may be clinically better than the default choice, meaning inference-time selection is a bottleneck for quality.

Method: They propose Clinical Consensus Selection (CCS), an inference-time, decoder-agnostic framework. CCS samples multiple report candidates from a radiology MLLM, computes utilities for each based on (1) textual consensus among candidates, and (2) a radiology-specific, image–report-trained multimodal embedding model that measures agreement in a clinically grounded feature space. It then selects the candidate with the highest combined clinical consensus score.

Result: On three different datasets and across multiple radiology MLLMs, CCS outperforms standard single-path decoding and generic Best-of-N selection baselines. The improvements are especially pronounced on clinical quality metrics, indicating that CCS better captures clinically relevant correctness than naive selection strategies.

Conclusion: Inference-time decision making is a major, underused lever for improving radiology report generation. By exploiting both textual and image-grounded clinical consensus across multiple candidate reports, CCS can systematically select higher-quality outputs, and there is still significant potential for further gains using richer inference-time strategies.

Abstract: Radiology report generation (RRG) is commonly formulated as a single-path generation task, where a multimodal large language model (MLLM) produces one decoded report as the final output. While recent progress has largely been driven by scaling training data, model capacity, and retrieval mechanisms, improving report quality at inference time remains underexplored. In this work, we observe that fixed radiology MLLMs often generate clinically stronger reports elsewhere in their candidate pool than the one selected by default decoding, suggesting that inference-time decision making remains an overlooked bottleneck. To address this, we propose Clinical Consensus Selection (CCS), a decoder-agnostic inference-time selection framework that samples multiple candidate reports and selects the one with the highest clinical consensus across the rollout pool. CCS unifies text-based utilities with a radiology-adapted utility computed by an image--report-trained multimodal embedder, which measures candidate agreement beyond surface-level textual similarity. Across three datasets and multiple radiology MLLMs, CCS consistently improves inference-time performance over single-path decoding and generic Best-of-N baselines, with particularly clear gains on clinical metrics. Further analysis shows that image-grounded utility forms a selection axis distinct from textual consensus and that substantial headroom remains for improving RRG at inference time.

</details>


### [119] [CorPipe at CRAC 2026: Empty Nodes and Cross-Lingual Transfer in Multilingual Coreference Resolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30133)
*Milan Straka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: CorPipe 26 is the winning system in the CRAC 2026 multilingual coreference task, extending CorPipe 25 with joint prediction of mentions, empty nodes, and links, and achieving strong gains over other LLM and unconstrained systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To evaluate and advance multilingual coreference resolution, especially comparing large generative LLMs with specialized systems, and to handle more languages and datasets while improving modeling of empty nodes within coreference chains.

Method: They extend the previous CorPipe 25 architecture with a new variant that jointly predicts empty nodes, mentions, and coreference links in a single model. They run the system in the CRAC 2026 shared task across multiple languages and datasets and conduct ablation studies varying model size, strategies for empty node prediction, and cross-lingual zero-shot settings.

Result: CorPipe 26 outperforms all other submissions in the LLM track by 2.8 percentage points and all submissions in the unconstrained track by 9.5 percentage points. Ablation studies show the impact of model size, empty node prediction methods, and cross-lingual zero-shot evaluation, though specific numbers are not given in the abstract.

Conclusion: Joint prediction of mentions, empty nodes, and coreference links within a single model is highly effective for multilingual coreference resolution, enabling CorPipe 26 to substantially outperform competing systems in the CRAC 2026 shared task. The released code and models support further research and comparison.

Abstract: We introduce CorPipe 26, our winning submission to the CRAC 2026 Shared Task on Multilingual Coreference Resolution. The fifth edition of this shared task focuses mainly on the comparison of generative LLMs and specialized systems; additionally, 5 more datasets and 2 new languages are introduced. CorPipe 26 is an improved version of CorPipe 25, with a new variant predicting empty nodes together with mentions and coreference links in a single model. Our system outperforms all other submissions in the LLM track by 2.8 percent points and all submissions in the unconstrained track by 9.5 percent points. Furthermore, we perform a series of ablation experiments with different model sizes, empty node prediction methods, and cross-lingual zero-shot evaluation. The source code and the trained models are publicly available at https://github.com/ufal/crac2026-corpipe.

</details>


### [120] [Do Proactive Agents Really Need an LLM to Decide When to Wake and What to Anchor?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30152)
*Xiaoze Liu,Ruowang Zhang,Amir H. Abdi,Michel Galley,Zhikai Chen,Siheng Xiong,Xiaoqian Wang,Jing Gao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper replaces always-on LLM triggers with a temporal-graph-learning (TGL) encoder over structured user activity streams, using the LLM only downstream for selected events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing proactive agents inefficiently convert structured OS event streams into text and then ask a large language model to decide when to act, incurring unnecessary latency, compute, and privacy costs. There is a need for a more native, efficient, and stable mechanism to trigger proactive behaviors directly from the structured activity signal.

Method: Model user activity as a temporal graph of (actor, verb, object, timestamp) events and apply a small temporal-graph-learning model as an encoder over this stream. For each event, the model produces (1) a trigger probability indicating whether to call an LLM-based agent, and (2) per-entity routing scores to guide which agent or behavior to invoke. Only when the trigger fires is an LLM called, and then only to generate user-facing natural language from a compact structured handoff. The authors benchmark TGL across 14 backbone architectures and compare it to LLM-as-trigger baselines in different deployment regimes.

Result: Across 14 backbones, using TGL improves F1 scores consistently, with an average gain of +16.7 and up to +46.0. In trigger architecture comparisons, a single TGL checkpoint produces the best trigger AUCs and the most stable deployment threshold among the tested options. Runtime is 11.13 ms per event on a GPU server and 13.99 ms per event on a consumer laptop, yielding 4–7x and 12–83x speedups, respectively, over every single-forward LLM-trigger configuration tested in those regimes. The model footprint is approximately 220 MiB in BF16, small enough to run on-device alongside the privacy-sensitive activity stream.

Conclusion: Treating always-on user activity as temporal graph updates and using a compact TGL encoder for triggering and routing is more accurate, faster, and resource-efficient than using an LLM as the trigger. This architecture reduces latency and compute, stabilizes trigger thresholds, and enables on-device deployment next to sensitive activity data while still leveraging LLMs only for the final user-facing generation step.

Abstract: Proactive agents read user activity as text and call an LLM on every event to decide whether to act. But user activity is not natively text: it is a structured event stream of (actor, verb, object, timestamp) tuples that the operating system already maintains in graph form. Rendering the structure as text and asking an LLM to recover it is a round-trip the system never had to take. We treat the always-on signal as graph updates rather than text and use a small temporal-graph-learning (TGL) model as the encoder: one forward pass yields a per-event trigger probability and a per-entity routing score, and only the downstream agent (turning a small structured handoff into a fluent user-facing sentence) is an LLM call, invoked only when the trigger fires. TGL improves F1 on each of 14 backbones (mean +16.7, up to +46.0); in trigger-architecture comparisons, one TGL checkpoint gives the strongest trigger AUCs and the most stable deployed threshold. It runs at 11.13 ms per event on a GPU server and 13.99 ms on a consumer laptop, approximately 4--7x and 12--83x faster than every single-forward LLM-as-trigger configuration tested in each regime, with an approximately 220 MiB BF16 resident footprint deployable on-device alongside the privacy-sensitive activity stream it consumes.

</details>


### [121] [A Dual-Path Architecture for Scaling Compute and Capacity in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30202)
*Markus Frey,Behzad Shomali,Joachim Koehler,Mehdi Ali*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They propose a transformer layer with two parallel paths—a deep shared sublayer reused multiple times and a wide larger-FFN sublayer—so models can flexibly trade off compute vs. parameter capacity and outperform iso-FLOP baselines with fewer parameters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Looped (reused-block) transformers are compute-efficient but, at a fixed FLOP budget, have lower representational capacity than standard transformers because they reuse the same parameters across multiple steps. The authors want an architecture that can independently scale compute (depth of sequential operations) and capacity (parameters per step) without exceeding a given FLOP budget, and that also provides interpretable routing of tokens across these two axes.

Method: They design a dual-path transformer block with two parallel sublayers: (1) a "deep" sublayer that is applied K times with shared parameters (capturing the compute/sequence of operations dimension), and (2) a "wide" sublayer that is applied once but has an enlarged feed-forward network (capturing the capacity/parameter dimension). For each token, learnable gates control how much of its representation flows through the deep versus wide paths and how they are combined. This enables per-token analysis of the routing decisions. Models using this block are compared against iso-FLOP baselines on language modeling and downstream tasks under two overall FLOP budgets.

Result: Across two different total compute (FLOP) budgets, the dual-path transformer achieves better performance than baseline models that use the same FLOPs, both on standard language modeling metrics and on downstream evaluations. It also uses fewer parameters than a standard transformer at the same FLOP level. The gating patterns learned by the model are interpretable: function words and lexical content tokens are more often routed through the wide, high-capacity path, whereas punctuation, symbols, and arithmetic-related tokens are more often routed through the deep, multi-step path.

Conclusion: Introducing a dual-path transformer block that separates and jointly optimizes compute depth and parameter capacity yields models that are more FLOP-efficient and parameter-efficient than conventional or purely looped transformers. The learned token-dependent gating reveals systematic routing behavior that aligns with intuitive linguistic categories, suggesting that such architectures can both improve efficiency and provide interpretable insights into how models allocate computational resources across tokens.

Abstract: Looped transformers apply a shared block multiple times and have emerged as a parameter-efficient route to scaling compute in language models. However, at fixed FLOPs a looped model has strictly less capacity than a baseline transformer. We propose a novel dual-path block that can flexibly scale compute, the number of sequential operations applied to a hidden state, and capacity, the parameters available at a single step. For this we expose both axes as parallel pathways within a single layer: a deep sublayer re-applied K times with shared parameters, and a wide sublayer with an enlarged feed-forward network applied once. Independent per-token gates combine both axes and allow detailed per-token routing analyses. We show that across two FLOP budgets, our dual-path model surpasses iso-FLOP matched models on language modeling and downstream evaluations, while using fewer parameters than the baseline at matched FLOPs. The learned gates are directly interpretable and show systematic per-token allocation with function words and lexical content trend wide, while punctuation, symbols, and arithmetic tokens trend deep.

</details>


### [122] [GRUFF: LLM Pronoun Fidelity, Reasoning, and Biases in German](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30214)
*Fabian Mewes,Anne Lauscher,Vagrant Gautam*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces GRUFF, a large-scale German dataset to test LLM pronoun fidelity under rich gender agreement and distractors, revealing strengths in traditional gender agreement but weaknesses with neopronouns and robustness, plus mixed occupational stereotype patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior pronoun fidelity and bias research has focused almost exclusively on English, which has limited grammatical gender and agreement, making it inadequate for studying how gendered grammar affects LLMs’ referential reasoning and stereotyping. There is a need for resources in languages like German, where grammatical gender and agreement are pervasive, to better understand model behavior, especially in gender-inclusive contexts and with neopronouns.

Method: The authors construct GRUFF, a large-scale dataset of German texts designed to test pronoun fidelity—whether models correctly reuse a previously-given pronoun for a discourse entity despite intervening distractor entities. The dataset systematically covers four noun gender agreement systems and four pronoun sets (including neopronouns xier and en). They evaluate different types of language models, including encoder-only and other architectures, on pronoun agreement behavior without context, robustness to distractors, and correlations of occupational gender stereotypes across grammatical cases and models.

Result: LLMs generally exhibit strong grammatical agreement for masculine and feminine pronouns when no explicit contextual distractors are present, but they perform poorly for the neopronouns xier and en. Models tend not to be robust when distractor entities are introduced, although encoder-only models show comparatively higher robustness in German than in English, suggesting grammatical gender aids them. Occupational stereotype patterns show low correlation across grammatical cases and across different models, except among models with closely related architectures.

Conclusion: GRUFF demonstrates that current LLMs handle traditional masculine and feminine gender agreement in German relatively well in simple conditions but struggle with neopronouns and with maintaining pronoun fidelity under distraction. The findings highlight the influence of grammatical gender on model robustness and reveal that occupational gender stereotypes are not stable across cases or model families. The authors release GRUFF and associated code to support further research on gender-inclusive language and referential reasoning in German NLP.

Abstract: Third-person singular pronouns have long been used to study stereotypical biases in language models and to test their abilities to reason about reference. More recently, the interplay between reasoning and bias has been investigated with the task of pronoun fidelity, which assesses models' abilities to correctly reuse a previously-specified pronoun for a discourse entity, independent of other potentially distracting discourse entities mentioned in between. However, such research focuses on English, which is a language with limited grammatical gender and almost no gender agreement. In this paper we contribute a novel, large-scale dataset, GRUFF, to measure pronoun fidelity in German, covering four different gender agreement systems in nouns, and four sets of pronouns. With this dataset, we show that LLMs show strong grammatical agreement for masculine and feminine entities in the absence of explicit context, but not for neopronouns xier and en. Models are generally not robust to distractors, but encoder-only models are more robust in German than in English, reflecting the importance of grammatical gender. Finally, we show that occupational stereotypes in this context are poorly correlated across grammatical cases, and across most models, except ones with closely related architectures. We release all code and data to encourage further work on gender-inclusive language and referential reasoning in German.

</details>


### [123] [Do Language Models Track Entities Across State Changes?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30233)
*Zilu Tang,Qiao Zhao,Gabriel Franco,Derry Wijaya,Aaron Mueller,Sebastian Schuster,Najoung Kim*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper studies how transformer language models perform entity tracking with state changes and finds they use a non-sequential, last-token aggregation strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the mechanisms by which realistic, non-toy transformer language models handle entity tracking tasks that involve multiple state-changing operations, an area that has been less explored compared to simple entity binding without state changes.

Method: The authors analyze transformer LMs on complex entity tracking tasks expressed in natural language, perform mechanistic interpretability studies across tokens and layers, and dissect how specific operations (PUT, REMOVE, MOVE) are implemented internally.

Result: They find that LMs do not track world states incrementally across tokens or layers, but instead aggregate all relevant information at the final token when the query is clear. They also discover that the REMOVE operation is implemented via a fragile global suppression tag that leads to predictable failure modes, which they validate behaviorally.

Conclusion: Transformer LMs solve sequential entity tracking tasks using a non-sequential strategy centered on last-token aggregation and a brittle global suppression mechanism for removals. Combining behavioral and mechanistic analyses enables better understanding of these models and supports the design of stronger evaluations that expose previously undetected failure modes.

Abstract: Entity tracking (ET), the ability to keep track of states, is a fundamental skill that underlies complex reasoning. An increasing amount of work investigates how transformer language models (LMs) solve entity binding $\textit{without}$ state changes. However, there is limited understanding of how non-toy LMs address ET problems of realistic difficulties expressed in natural language. To this end, we investigate the mechanisms underlying ET in more complex scenarios featuring multiple state-changing operations. We find that LMs do not incrementally track world states across tokens or query-relevant states across layers, but simply aggregate relevant information in parallel at the last token when the query becomes evident. We further investigate mechanisms of individual operations ($\texttt{PUT}$, $\texttt{REMOVE}$, $\texttt{MOVE}$) to characterize this non-incremental ET mechanism. Surprisingly, LMs implement the $\texttt{REMOVE}$ operation with a fragile global suppression tag; this global removal mechanism predicts various failure modes that we confirm behaviorally. We provide a mechanistic solution of nullifying this tag to partially address this issue. Overall, our findings reveal that LMs solve a fundamentally sequential task using a non-sequential strategy. More broadly, our work illustrates how behavioral and mechanistic analyses can fruitfully interact. Behavioral results inform mechanistic hypotheses, and insights from mechanistic analyses help build stronger behavioral evaluations by predicting failure modes missing from existing evaluations.

</details>


### [124] [CommunityFact: A Dynamic, Multilingual, Multi-domain Benchmark for Misinformation Detection in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30241)
*Sahajpreet Singh,Insyirah Mujtahid,Min-Yen Kan,Kokil Jaidka*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents CommunityFact, a large, refreshable benchmark built from Community Notes to evaluate and improve LLM-based misinformation verification in realistic, multilingual, web-enabled settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing misinformation detection benchmarks are static, monolingual or limited in scope, and do not reflect the dynamic, multilingual, and public nature of real-world verification, especially as done via platforms like Community Notes. As LLMs are increasingly used for fact-checking and require evaluation in more realistic, web-enabled settings, there is a need for a benchmark that captures evolving claims, diverse languages and domains, and the evidence ecosystem behind human verification decisions.

Method: The authors construct CommunityFact using Community Notes data, compiling 15,992 standalone claims spanning five languages and two domains. They design the benchmark to be refreshable and to capture both claims and associated sources/evidence. They then evaluate ten large language models with different inference-time configurations: closed-input (no external tools), with explicit chain-of-thought or “thinking”, and with web-search/retrieval. For web-enabled models, they analyze the models’ source-selection behavior, comparing it to the sources most often used or agreed upon by Community Notes raters. They study how retrieval expansion or pruning strategies affect source alignment and verification performance, and they examine performance variation across language-domain subsets and across different evidence ecosystems.

Result: The study finds that verifying misinformation in a closed-input setting (without web access) is still difficult for current LLMs. Adding web access provides the largest performance improvements compared to reasoning-only enhancements. However, web-enabled LLMs tend to pick different sources than those Community Notes raters converge on, indicating misalignment in source selection. This misalignment can be significantly reduced when models use tailored retrieval expansion or pruning strategies. The authors also observe substantial performance variation across different language-domain slices and identify differences in the evidence ecosystems that various web-enabled systems rely on.

Conclusion: CommunityFact offers a more realistic, refreshable benchmark for misinformation verification that captures multilingual claims, dynamic evidence, and the human verification process via Community Notes. The findings underscore that web access is crucial for strong verification performance, but models must also learn to select and weight sources in ways that more closely match human raters’ judgments. The benchmark and analyses suggest that Community Notes can be leveraged not only for evaluation but also as supervision for training claim-conditioned source suggestion components, which could enhance factual verification on previously unseen claims.

Abstract: Misinformation verification increasingly occurs in public, fast-moving, and multilingual online settings, where static benchmarks provide an incomplete measure of model reliability. We introduce CommunityFact, a refreshable benchmark for misinformation detection in the wild, with three major goals: coverage, granularity, and redistributability. This release contains 15,992 standalone claims across five languages and two domains. We evaluate ten LLMs under varying inference-time capabilities, including thinking and web-search. Our results show that closed-input verification remains challenging, web access yields the largest gains, and web-enabled LLMs' source-selection policies are systematically misaligned with the sources human Community Notes raters converge on -- a gap that closes through model-specific mechanisms of retrieval expansion or pruning. We further find substantial variation across language-domain slices and across the evidence ecosystems used by web-enabled systems. Beyond evaluation, CommunityFact positions Community Notes as a training signal for claim-conditioned source suggesters that could improve factual verification on novel claims.

</details>


### [125] [Knowing What to Solve Before How: Preplan Empowered LLM Mathematical Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30245)
*Shaojie Wang,Liang Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They add an explicit “problem understanding” (preplan) step before planning and reasoning in LLMs, and show this improves math reasoning without extra inference cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing plan-based reasoning methods use a question→plan→chain-of-thought paradigm, but both stages focus on *how* to solve, not explicitly on *what* the problem fundamentally is, what tools apply, and what pitfalls exist. This missing explicit problem-understanding stage may limit reasoning quality and robustness.

Method: They propose the PPC (Preplan-Plan-CoT) framework, adding an explicit preplan stage: question→preplan→plan→CoT. They build a three-stage data synthesis pipeline to create supervision for preplans, with a spoiler-score detector to filter examples where the preplan leaks the solution or fails conceptually, ensuring “clean” preplan data. At training time, they use a composite GRPO (a type of RL) reward to enforce that generated plans are consistent with and derived from the preplan rather than ignoring it.

Result: Across four model backbones and five mathematical reasoning benchmarks, PPC beats strong plan-based baselines on 39 of 40 evaluation metrics. It improves maj@16 by +2.23 and pass@16 by +3.06 over the strongest baseline, with no extra tokens used at inference (i.e., no additional inference cost).

Conclusion: Making problem understanding explicit via a preplan stage, and training models to respect this stage using filtered supervision and GRPO rewards, leads to systematically better mathematical reasoning performance than standard question→plan→CoT approaches, without extra inference-time overhead.

Abstract: Current plan-based reasoning methods improve large language models (LLMs) by inserting a planning stage before execution, giving rise to the question $\rightarrow$ plan $\rightarrow$ cot paradigm. While effective, a closer examination reveals an inherent paradigm-level gap: both the planning and its execution stages decide how to solve a problem, while the prior question of what to solve; recognizing the problem type, the applicable tools, and the foreseeable pitfalls; remains entirely implicit. To bridge this gap, we propose PPC (Preplan-Plan-CoT), a framework that introduces an explicit problem-understanding stage, the preplan, yielding a new question $\rightarrow$ preplan $\rightarrow$ plan $\rightarrow$ cot paradigm. Realizing this paradigm requires safeguarding the conceptual integrity of preplan at both ends. Specifically, we design a three-stage synthesis pipeline with a spoiler-score detector that filters out leakage and spoiler failures to build clean preplan supervision, and a composite GRPO reward enforces that the generated plan genuinely follows from the preplan. Experiments across four backbones and five mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that PPC achieves the best results on 39 of 40 metrics, improving maj@16 and pass@16 by +2.23 and +3.06 over the strongest baseline without introducing additional inference token overhead.

</details>


### [126] [Same Evidence, Different Answers: Canonical-Context On-Policy Distillation for Multi-Turn Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30251)
*Zizhuo Lin,Quanling Liu,Jinsheng Quan,Chao Zhang,Yifan Zhu,Xing Shi,Jingtao Xu,Zhihui Li,Yawei Luo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper tackles why LLMs perform worse in multi-turn settings than with an equivalent single-shot prompt, and introduces a training method (CCOPD) that makes models more consistent and grounded across dialog turns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs can solve tasks well when all relevant instructions are presented in a single, clean prompt, but often fail when the same information is spread across a multi-turn conversation. This mismatch is problematic because real-world use is largely conversational. The authors observe that models, when answering under partial information, introduce unsupported assumptions that then bias later reasoning even after full information is available (self-anchored drift). They want a systematic way to align multi-turn behavior with the model’s best possible behavior under full context, without hurting that strong single-shot ability.

Method: They propose Canonical-Context On-Policy Distillation (CCOPD). A single base model is used in two roles: (1) a frozen teacher that sees the entire problem at once via a clean FULL prompt; and (2) a trainable student that receives the same evidence gradually as in a multi-turn RAW-SHARDED conversation. The student generates its own multi-turn trajectories (“on-policy”), and CCOPD trains it so that, by the end of the conversation, its answers and behavior match the teacher’s canonical full-context outputs. Training is done on math problem conversations, aligning the student’s final answers (and potentially intermediate behaviors) with the teacher’s outputs, thus discouraging drift from earlier unsupported assumptions.

Result: Models trained with CCOPD show a 32% average relative improvement on RAW-SHARDED (multi-turn) performance versus the original base model, even though training uses only math conversations. This improvement generalizes to five out-of-domain task families in zero-shot settings, while full-context performance is largely maintained. Additional analyses indicate that CCOPD-trained models rely more faithfully on user-provided evidence and are less affected by misleading or contaminating earlier assistant turns.

Conclusion: Self-anchored drift—unsupported assumptions made under partial information that later distort answers—is a key cause of LLM underperformance in multi-turn settings. CCOPD, by distilling from a full-context teacher to a multi-turn student on its own trajectories, effectively aligns conversational behavior with canonical full-context reasoning. This reduces sensitivity to earlier assistant outputs, strengthens grounding in user evidence, significantly boosts multi-turn performance across domains, and preserves strong single-shot abilities.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often solve a task when all instructions are given in a single prompt, but fail when the same information is revealed gradually across turns. When a clean FULL prompt and a RAW-SHARDED conversation contain the same complete user evidence, the model should still arrive at the same answer. We argue that a key reason for this gap is self-anchored drift: responses produced under partial information introduce unsupported assumptions, and those assumptions later distort the final answer. To reduce this effect, we propose Canonical-Context On-Policy Distillation (CCOPD). During training, the same base model is used in two roles: a frozen teacher conditioned on the clean FULL prompt and a trainable student that receives the same evidence incrementally through a multi-turn conversation; CCOPD aligns the student's behavior on its own trajectories with the teacher's canonical full-context behavior. Trained only on math problem conversations, CCOPD yields a 32\% average relative improvement in RAW-SHARDED performance over the original base model across math and five zero-shot out-of-domain task families, while largely preserving full-context performance. Further analyses suggest that CCOPD strengthens grounding in user evidence and reduces sensitivity to contamination from earlier assistant turns.

</details>


### [127] [How LoRA Remembers? A Parametric Memory Law for LLM Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30260)
*Ziwen Xu,Haiwen Hong,Linsong Yu,Benglei Cui,Longtao Huang,Hui Xue,Ningyu Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They study how much exact factual 'memory' LoRA-adapted LLMs can store, derive a quantitative law for it, analyze when tokens are perfectly recalled, and propose a more efficient fine-tuning strategy (MemFT) guided by this law.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs need continual updating of knowledge, but existing work mainly checks performance qualitatively and does not precisely quantify how much exact, verbatim information can be stored in LoRA parameters or how this capacity scales. The authors want a principled, quantitative understanding of parametric memory limits and dynamics in LLMs under LoRA-based updates.

Method: Use LoRA modules as a controlled, parameter-limited 'memory probe' in the model’s latent space. Systematically measure loss reductions when encoding factual sequences to uncover scaling relations between memory performance, number of effective trainable parameters, and sequence length. Analyze token-level prediction probabilities and their relation to exact recall under greedy decoding. Based on the discovered threshold phenomenon (p>0.5), design MemFT, which reallocates training effort from already well-learned tokens to under-threshold tokens using a probability-based criterion.

Result: They discover an empirical Parametric Memory Law: loss reduction ΔL follows a robust power-law scaling with both the effective number of LoRA parameters and the length of sequences being memorized. At token level, they identify a deterministic phase transition: once the model’s predicted probability for a token exceeds 0.5, that token is reliably recalled verbatim with greedy decoding. Using this characterization, they implement MemFT, which adaptively focuses learning on tokens below the recall threshold, leading to better use of training budget.
