Conclusion: Parametric memory in LoRA-adapted LLMs obeys a clear quantitative power law with respect to parameter count and sequence length, and exact recall under greedy decoding is governed by a simple p>0.5 threshold at the token level. Exploiting these findings, MemFT improves both memory fidelity and efficiency by dynamically prioritizing difficult tokens during fine-tuning. This work provides a more principled understanding and control of how LLMs store and update exact knowledge via LoRA.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) must continuously learn and update knowledge to remain effective in dynamic real-world environments. While Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is widely used for such memory updates, existing studies mainly rely on qualitative downstream evaluations, leaving the quantitative capacity limits and underlying dynamics of exact parametric memory largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we employ LoRA as a controlled memory capacity probe within the latent space to systematically quantify exact parametric memory. We introduce the Parametric Memory Law, a robust power law linking loss reduction Delta L to effective parameters and sequence length. At the token level, fine-grained analysis reveals a deterministic phase transition, demonstrating that a prediction probability of p > 0.5 constitutes a sufficient condition for verbatim recall under greedy decoding. Driven by these insights, we introduce MemFT, a threshold-guided optimization strategy that dynamically redistributes the training budget toward sub-threshold tokens. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that MemFT can enhance memory fidelity and efficiency. Code will be released at https://github.com/zjunlp/ParametricMemoryLaw.

</details>


### [128] [Loong: A Human-Like Long Document Translation Agent with Observe-and-Act Adaptive Context Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30274)
*Yutong Wang,Xuebo Liu,Derek F. Wong,Zhilin Li,Rongqing Jiang,Min Zhang,Shimin Tao,Daimeng Wei,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper presents Loong, a document-level machine translation agent that uses a structured memory and reinforcement learning to select and use only the most helpful context, significantly improving translation quality on long documents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Document-level translation with large language models is difficult because of limited context windows and the fact that feeding all available history can introduce redundant or harmful information that hurts translation quality and discourse coherence. The authors aim to mimic how humans selectively recall relevant information while translating long documents, rather than processing all context uniformly and passively.

Method: The authors design Loong, a long document translation agent equipped with a 3E memory module: Essence (high-level summaries), Exemplar (sentence-level translation pairs), and Entity (records of entities). Loong does not attend to the full historical context; instead, it executes an observe-and-act reasoning process to adaptively choose which memory elements to use as guidance. They then formulate context selection as a policy optimized through reinforcement learning, using preference data generated from the model’s own sampled reasoning trajectories to refine which context policies lead to better translations.

Result: In experiments on English↔Chinese, German, and French document-level translation tasks, Loong delivers notable improvements, reporting average gains of up to 13.0 points across three evaluation metrics compared with baselines. The model also generalizes well across domains, shows robustness to noisy or distracting context, and maintains stable performance even on ultra-long documents.

Conclusion: Structured, human-like memory combined with learned context selection via reinforcement learning can substantially improve document-level translation for long texts. By storing history in the 3E memory and learning when and how to use each component, Loong overcomes context window limits and redundant information, leading to more coherent and robust translations across languages and domains.

Abstract: Document-level translation remains one of the most challenging tasks for large language models, which are constrained by limited context windows that impede global cohesion, while simultaneously suffering from redundant contextual information that degrades translation quality. To address this, we propose a human-like long document translation agent called Loong, which leverages a 3E memory module (Essence-Exemplar-Entity) to store summaries, sentence pairs, and entity records as historical context. Instead of passively attending to all history, Loong performs deep reasoning to adaptively identify the optimal context for translation guidance. Loong optimizes its context policy through reinforcement learning, utilizing preference data derived from its own sampled observe-and-act reasoning trajectories. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that Loong achieves substantial translation quality improvements in English $\Leftrightarrow$ Chinese, German, and French directions, with average gains of up to 13.0 points across the three evaluation metrics. Furthermore, Loong exhibits strong generalization across domains and robustness against contextual noise, while maintaining remarkable stability in ultra-long document translation. Our code is released at https://github.com/YutongWang1216/LoongDocMT.

</details>


### [129] [MedCase-Structured: A Text-to-FHIR Dataset for Benchmarking Diagnostic Reasoning in Clinically Realistic EHR Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30295)
*Valentina Bui Muti,Eugénie Dulout,Ziquan Fu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They build a pipeline to convert unstructured clinical text into realistic HL7 FHIR bundles so LLMs can be evaluated in settings that match real electronic health records, and show LLMs perform worse on these structured inputs than on plain text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM clinical benchmarks mostly use static, unstructured datasets that do not match how data is represented in EHRs (e.g., HL7 FHIR). This gap makes it hard to know how well LLMs will perform when integrated into real clinical systems. A method is needed to generate realistic, structured EHR-like data from narrative cases for robust, deployment-relevant evaluation.

Method: They design a multi-stage LLM-based pipeline that takes unstructured clinical case text and produces HL7 FHIR R4 bundles. The pipeline uses staged generation, grounding on standard clinical terminologies, and a validation/repair phase to minimize hallucinated codes and ensure both structural and semantic correctness according to FHIR constraints. They apply this pipeline to the MedCaseReasoning corpus to produce a new structured dataset, MedCase-Structured.

Result: Using their pipeline, they successfully generate valid FHIR bundles for 82.5% of MedCaseReasoning cases. They then benchmark LLMs on this MedCase-Structured dataset and find that LLMs have consistently lower diagnostic accuracy when reasoning over the structured FHIR representations compared with the original plain-text cases.

Conclusion: Structured, EHR-aligned representations like HL7 FHIR materially change LLM performance, generally reducing diagnostic accuracy relative to plain text. Therefore, benchmarks for clinical decision support should use deployment-aligned, FHIR-congruent data to more realistically assess system capabilities. Their pipeline and MedCase-Structured dataset are proposed as tools for such evaluation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) show promise for clinical reasoning and decision support, but evaluation in realistic, electronic health record-congruent settings remains limited. Existing benchmarks often rely on static datasets or unstructured inputs that do not reflect the structured, interoperable data formats used in clinical systems. We introduce a pipeline for generating clinically realistic HL7 FHIR R4 bundles from unstructured text, enabling controllable evaluation of clinical decision support systems. The pipeline combines staged LLM generation with terminology-grounded validation and repair to reduce hallucinated codes and enforce structural and semantic consistency. Applying this approach to MedCaseReasoning, we construct MedCase-Structured, a synthetic dataset aligned with clinician-authored diagnostic cases, achieving valid FHIR generation for 82.5% of cases. Evaluation on MedCase-Structured reveals consistently lower diagnostic accuracy for LLMs on structured FHIR inputs than with plain text, highlighting the importance of deployment-aligned benchmarking.

</details>


### [130] [Resolution Diagnostics for Paired LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30315)
*Anany Kotawala*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Many leaderboard LLM rankings are statistically unresolved given proper paired-test power, and common power calculators substantially mis-estimate required sample size in close comparisons.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper aims to show that widely used LLM leaderboards report fine-grained model rankings that are not statistically reliable under standard hypothesis-testing and power criteria, and to highlight that current practice for planning paired LLM evaluations relies on incorrect unpaired shortcuts that underestimate the needed sample size.

Method: The authors treat paired LLM evaluation as a formal hypothesis-testing problem, invert level-alpha, power-(1-beta) tests to define the minimum required sample size N* for resolving a given effect, and introduce a per-pair resolution ratio q = N/N*. They analytically derive a higher-order small-effect expansion to compare the correct paired design with the common unpaired Cohen-h plus (1−rho) shortcut, and empirically assess resolution on two public leaderboards (Open LLM Leaderboard v1 and MMLU-Pro) under realistic clustering and bootstrap resampling. They also check robustness of the unresolved-pair phenomenon under multiplicity corrections and anytime-valid sequential testing frameworks.

Result: On the Open LLM Leaderboard v1, 11 of 40 displayed pairwise comparisons fail to reach the target power (1−beta=0.8) at alpha=0.05 under the actual paired design; on the MMLU-Pro top-10, 4 of 9 adjacent-rank pairs are unresolved, rising to 6 of 9 when real subject-level clustering is accounted for and remaining 5–6 of 9 in 99.9% of category-bootstrap resamples. Analytically, the authors show that the standard unpaired Cohen-h plus (1−rho) adjustment underestimates the required N* by roughly a factor of two in the small-effect, close-comparison regime, and they note that several common tools (Cohen 1988 tables, G*Power, R pwr) inherit this error when users post-multiply the per-arm sample size by (1−rho). The unresolved-pair pattern persists even after applying multiple-comparison corrections and anytime-valid sequential testing procedures.

Conclusion: A substantial fraction of leaderboard-claimed performance orderings between LLMs are not statistically resolved when evaluated with appropriate paired-test power analysis, meaning the apparent fine-grained rankings are often unsupported by the data. Common practice for planning paired LLM evaluations, based on unpaired Cohen-h shortcuts, can dramatically underpower close model comparisons. More rigorous, correctly specified paired power calculations and reporting of resolution ratios are necessary to ensure that LLM leaderboards and benchmarks convey statistically reliable rankings.

Abstract: Across two public LLM leaderboards, many displayed pairwise rankings do not meet a conventional paired-test resolution target under the actual paired evaluation design: 11 of 40 Open LLM Leaderboard v1 pairwise comparisons and 4 of 9 MMLU-Pro top-10 adjacent-rank pairs are unresolved at (alpha, 1-beta) = (0.05, 0.8). The MMLU-Pro count rises to 6/9 under real subject-level clustering and stays at 5-6 out of 9 in 99.9% of category-bootstrap resamples. We frame paired LLM evaluation as a hypothesis-testing problem, invert level-alpha, power-(1-beta) tests, and report a per-pair resolution ratio q = N/N* as the primary diagnostic. A sharp small-effect expansion with an explicit second-order constant shows that the widely-used unpaired Cohen-h-plus-(1-rho) shortcut deviates from the correct N* by approximately a factor of two in the close-comparison regime, a deficit that three of five off-the-shelf calculators(Cohen 1988, G*Power, R pwr) silently inherit when the user post-multiplies their per-arm output by (1-rho). The unresolved-pair pattern remains under multiplicity correction and anytime-valid sequential testing.

</details>


### [131] [COMPOSE: Composing Future Theorems from Citations and Formal Structure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30333)
*David Busbib,Michael Werman*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces COMPOSE, a dual-graph framework for generating plausible future mathematical theorems grounded in both scientific citation and formal theorem dependency graphs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods for generating future mathematical claims typically use either scientific context (citations) or formal dependencies (theorem structures), but not both, leading to claims that are either weakly grounded in the literature or insufficiently constrained by formal mathematics. The authors aim to better model what constitutes a plausible future theorem by integrating these two key sources of structure.

Method: They define the task of grounded future mathematical generation, where the system must propose theorem-like claims for an anchor paper using (1) its position and context in the scientific citation graph and (2) aligned formal theorem dependency graphs from a theorem prover library. They propose COMPOSE, a framework that conditions a language model jointly on both graphs via a dual-graph representation. They construct a large paired dataset (108K examples) linking arXiv papers to Mathlib theorems and create a benchmark of 47K real future papers (2024–2025) as a target for evaluation. COMPOSE is trained and evaluated on this setup, and its outputs are compared against baselines through retrieval to real future papers and LLM-based judging.

Result: COMPOSE outperforms strong baseline models on the task of retrieving real future papers based on generated claims, indicating that its predictions are more aligned with actual subsequent research. Under LLM-judge evaluation, COMPOSE is rated as producing more grounded and mathematically richer future theorem-like statements than competing methods.

Conclusion: Integrating both scientific citation context and formal theorem dependency structure leads to more plausible and richer generated future mathematical claims. The dual-graph COMPOSE framework and the new datasets/benchmarks demonstrate that future mathematical generation substantially benefits from combining these two complementary sources of information.

Abstract: A plausible future mathematical claim must satisfy two constraints: it should follow the direction of prior work and respect the formal dependencies that constrain what can validly follow. Existing approaches typically model only one of these sources, producing claims that are either weakly grounded or insufficiently motivated. We introduce grounded future mathematical generation, where the goal is to generate a plausible future theorem-like claim for an anchor paper using two complementary sources of context: its scientific citation graph and aligned formal theorem dependency graph. To address this setting, we propose COMPOSE, a dual-graph framework that conditions a language model on both scientific citation context and formal theorem structure. To support this setting, we construct a dataset of 108K paired scientific-formal graph examples from arXiv and Mathlib, together with a benchmark of 47K future papers from 2024--2025. Experiments show that COMPOSE outperforms strong baselines on retrieval to real future papers and achieves the best overall performance under LLM-judge evaluation, producing more grounded and mathematically richer outputs. These results show that future mathematical generation benefits from combining scientific context with formal structure. Project page is available at https://david-busbib.github.io/COMPOSE-page/.

</details>


### [132] [Unlocking the Working Memory of Large Language Models for Latent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30343)
*Lukas Aichberger,Sepp Hochreiter*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces Reasoning in Memory (RiM), a method that lets large language models perform internal, non-autoregressive reasoning using fixed memory tokens instead of generating chains of thought, achieving similar or better performance with less test-time compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ways to improve LLM reasoning typically rely on autoregressive generation of intermediate reasoning steps (e.g., chain-of-thought), which is slow, couples reasoning with external text generation, and may not reflect truly latent computation. Humans instead use internal working memory to reason without verbalizing every step. The authors want a mechanism that gives LLMs an analogous internal working memory for efficient, latent reasoning that does not require generating long intermediate texts.

Method: They propose Reasoning in Memory (RiM), which adds fixed-size memory blocks—predefined sequences of special tokens—into the model’s input or internal state. These act as working-memory slots that the model can use for internal computation in a single forward pass, instead of step-by-step generation. Training uses a two-stage curriculum: (1) grounding stage, where after each memory block the model is supervised to predict explicit intermediate reasoning steps, teaching it how to use the memory; (2) refinement stage, where step-level supervision is removed and the model instead iteratively refines the final answer after each memory block, encouraging fully latent reasoning that uses the memory but does not externalize thoughts.

Result: On multiple reasoning benchmarks and across different LLM architectures and sizes, RiM matches or outperforms existing latent reasoning and chain-of-thought style methods, while avoiding autoregressive generation of intermediate text. It achieves comparable or better accuracy with improved compute efficiency at test time due to single-pass processing of the fixed memory blocks.

Conclusion: Large language models can be trained to use a form of working memory—implemented as fixed token blocks—as an effective mechanism for latent reasoning. This decouples internal computation from external token generation and enables compute-efficient reasoning without explicit chains of thought, suggesting a path toward more human-like, internalized reasoning processes in LLMs.

Abstract: To improve the reasoning capabilities of large language models, test-time compute is typically scaled by generating intermediate tokens before the final answer. However, this couples reasoning to autoregressive generation and thereby conflates internal computation with external communication. In contrast, human cognition can use working memory to hold and manipulate information internally without the need to externalize intermediate thoughts. Drawing on this principle, we introduce Reasoning in Memory (RiM), a latent reasoning method that replaces the autoregressive generation of reasoning steps with memory blocks. These memory blocks are fixed sequences of special tokens that unlock the working-memory capacity of large language models. Since they are fixed rather than generated, they can be processed in a single forward pass, enabling compute-efficient latent reasoning. To operationalize these memory blocks, we employ a two-stage curriculum. First, we ground them by predicting explicit reasoning steps after each memory block. Second, we discard this step-level supervision and iteratively refine the final answer after each memory block. Our experiments on reasoning benchmarks show that, across language models of different families and sizes, RiM matches or exceeds existing latent reasoning methods while avoiding the autoregressive generation of thoughts. These results demonstrate that large language models can be trained to use working memory as an effective mechanism for latent reasoning.

</details>


### [133] [LLMSurgeon: Diagnosing Data Mixture of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30348)
*Yaxin Luo,Jiacheng Cui,Xiaohan Zhao,Xinyi Shang,Jiacheng Liu,Xinyue Bi,Zhaoyi Li,Zhiqiang Shen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: They develop a method (LLMSurgeon) to infer the domain composition of an LLM’s pretraining data just from its generated text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs’ pretraining data strongly shapes their behavior, but models’ data mixtures are usually undisclosed, making it hard to audit or understand what kinds of data they were trained on.

Method: They define Data Mixture Surgery (DMS): inferring the domain-level distribution of an LLM’s pretraining corpus under a given taxonomy using only its outputs. They model this as an inverse problem under a label-shift assumption. Instead of naive aggregation of domain classifier predictions over generated text, they estimate a calibrated soft confusion matrix capturing systematic domain confusions, then solve a constrained inverse problem to recover the latent mixture over domains.

Result: They build LLMScan, an evaluation suite using open-source LLMs with known pretraining mixtures, allowing recipe-verifiable testing. On this benchmark, LLMSurgeon accurately reconstructs the true domain mixture distribution under fixed experimental protocols.

Conclusion: Post-hoc auditing of an LLM’s “digital DNA” (its pretraining domain mixture) is feasible without direct access to training data. The LLMSurgeon framework provides a practical method to recover domain mixtures from generated text and can support transparency and auditing of foundation models.

Abstract: The pretraining data mixture of Large Language Models (LLMs) constitutes their "digital DNA", shaping model behaviors, capabilities, and failure modes. Yet this composition is rarely disclosed, making post-hoc auditing of data combination or provenance difficult. In this work, we formalize $\textbf{Data Mixture Surgery (DMS)}$: given only generated text from a target LLM, estimate the domain-level distribution of its pretraining corpus under a predefined taxonomy. We propose $\textbf{LLMSurgeon}$, a strong framework that casts DMS as an inverse problem under the label-shift assumption. Rather than directly aggregating classifier outputs, LLMSurgeon estimates a calibrated $\textit{soft}$ confusion matrix and solves a constrained inverse problem to correct systematic domain confusion and recover the latent mixture prior. To evaluate, we introduce $\textbf{LLMScan}$, a recipe-verifiable evaluation suite built from open-source LLMs with transparent pretraining mixtures. Across LLMScan, LLMSurgeon recovers domain mixtures with high fidelity under fixed protocols. Our work presents a practical, post-hoc approach for auditing the digital DNA of foundation models without access to their training data.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [134] [Behavior-Induced Mirror-Prox Temporal-Difference Learning for Faster Off-Policy Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28849)
*Xingguo Chen,Yuchen Shen,Shangdong Yang,Chao Li,Guang Yang,Wenhao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces STHTD-MP, a behavior-induced Mirror-Prox TD method that uses a behavior-policy-based metric to improve the update geometry and convergence properties for off-policy prediction with linear function approximation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Gradient TD methods’ performance depends heavily on the choice of metric in the auxiliary-variable space. Existing Mirror-Prox TD algorithms typically use the feature covariance metric, which may not fully capture the structure of the behavior policy’s dynamics. Hybrid TD results suggest that transition information from the behavior policy can yield more informative geometries, motivating a new Mirror-Prox TD variant that exploits this richer structure.

Method: The authors define STHTD-MP, a Mirror-Prox temporal-difference method in which the metric in the primal-dual saddle-point formulation is replaced by the symmetric part of the behavior-policy Bellman matrix, effectively encoding behavior-policy transition structure. The algorithm uses a single learning rate for both primal (value parameters) and auxiliary variables and applies a Mirror-Prox prediction-correction step to the resulting hybrid saddle-point operator. They conduct a formal convergence analysis via stochastic approximation, proving positive definiteness of the behavior-induced metric, Hurwitz stability of the joint mean system, boundedness via a Lyapunov function, and convergence of the stochastic recursion using the ODE method. They also derive projected-oracle ergodic gap bounds and compare the deterministic mean operator with GTD2-MP through the spectral radius of the Mirror-Prox error matrix.

Result: Theoretical results show that, under standard assumptions, STHTD-MP is convergent and can exhibit a smaller mean contraction factor than GTD2-MP when the behavior-induced metric leads to a better-conditioned saddle-point geometry. The authors provide explicit spectral-radius-based comparisons of the mean operators. Numerical mean-operator analyses on simple benchmarks (two-state MDP, Random Walk, Boyan Chain) empirically confirm cases where STHTD-MP has better contraction properties, while also identifying that Baird’s counterexample lies at the boundary where the needed assumptions (such as positive definiteness) fail.

Conclusion: STHTD-MP leverages behavior-policy transition information to define a more informative update geometry than standard covariance-metric Mirror-Prox TD methods, potentially improving contraction and convergence properties in off-policy linear prediction. The convergence guarantees and spectral-radius analysis clarify when and why this behavior-induced metric is beneficial, while highlighting pathological cases (like Baird’s counterexample) where its assumptions break down.

Abstract: Gradient temporal-difference methods provide stable off-policy prediction with linear function approximation, but their practical performance is strongly affected by the geometry induced by the auxiliary-variable metric. Existing Mirror-Prox TD methods typically use the feature covariance metric, whereas hybrid TD methods suggest that behavior-policy transition information can provide a more informative update geometry. This paper proposes a behavior-induced Mirror-Prox temporal-difference method, called STHTD-MP, which replaces the covariance metric in the primal-dual saddle-point formulation with the symmetric part of the behavior-policy Bellman matrix. The method keeps a single learning rate for the primal and auxiliary variables and applies a Mirror-Prox prediction-correction step to the resulting hybrid saddle-point operator. We provide a formal convergence analysis for fixed-policy linear prediction under standard stochastic approximation assumptions: the behavior-induced metric is positive definite, the joint mean system is Hurwitz, boundedness follows from a Lyapunov argument, and the stochastic recursion converges by the ODE method. We further derive projected-oracle ergodic gap bounds and an exact mean-operator comparison with GTD2-MP based on the spectral radius of the deterministic Mirror-Prox error matrix. The analysis shows that STHTD-MP can have a smaller mean contraction factor than GTD2-MP when the behavior-induced metric improves the saddle-point geometry. Exact numerical mean-operator analysis on two-state, Random Walk, and Boyan Chain benchmarks supports this condition, while Baird's counterexample is identified as a singular boundary case where the strict assumptions fail.

</details>


### [135] [Behavior-Aware Auxiliary Corrections for Off-Policy Temporal-Difference Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28855)
*Xingguo Chen,Zhiang He,Yuchen Shen,Shangdong Yang,Chao Li,Guang Yang,Wenhao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes behavior-aware variants of TDC/TDRC for stabilizing off-policy TD with function approximation and analyzes their convergence and performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Off-policy temporal-difference learning with function approximation is known to be potentially unstable. Existing stabilizing methods like TDC and TDRC rely on an auxiliary covariance-based correction, whose geometry may not fully exploit information about the behavior policy. There is also interest in understanding and designing suitable auxiliary geometries for more complex settings like neural networks, where the interaction between feature covariances and transition dynamics is non-trivial.

Method: In the linear value-prediction setting, the authors replace the auxiliary matrix C used in TDC with the behavior Bellman matrix A_μ to create a behavior-aware TDC variant (BA-TDC). They then apply a regularization to this behavior-aware equation to obtain BA-TDRC, mirroring how TDRC regularizes TDC but now in a behavior-aware way. They formulate a finite-state mean-system model, analyze the resulting linear error dynamics, and establish properties such as fixed-point preservation, almost-sure convergence under a Hurwitz stability condition, and mean convergence rates via the spectral radius of the exact linear recursion. Experiments are run on canonical counterexamples and benchmark problems.

Result: Theoretical results show that BA-TDC and BA-TDRC preserve the desired fixed point and converge almost surely when the mean system satisfies a Hurwitz stability condition. The analysis also characterizes deterministic mean convergence rates through the spectral radius of the linear error recursion. Empirically, on tasks including the two-state and Baird counterexamples, Random Walk, and Boyan Chain, the behavior-aware replacement of the auxiliary geometry yields significant benefits on some tasks, while combining it with regularization yields more consistently robust performance, particularly on harder problems.

Conclusion: Behavior-aware auxiliary geometry, achieved by replacing the covariance-based correction matrix with the behavior Bellman matrix, can substantially improve stability and performance of off-policy TD methods with function approximation. However, regularization (as in BA-TDRC) remains important for robustness across challenging tasks. The linear analysis also offers a useful framework for designing auxiliary geometries in more complex architectures such as neural networks, where both feature covariances and transition dynamics shape the learning dynamics.

Abstract: Temporal-difference learning with function approximation can be unstable under off-policy sampling. TDC stabilizes off-policy TD through an auxiliary covariance correction, and TDRC further regularizes this correction in a single-timescale recursion. This paper studies a behavior-aware replacement of the auxiliary covariance geometry in the linear prediction setting, which is the standard local model for understanding the feature-space dynamics of value-function approximation. We first replace the TDC auxiliary matrix (C) by the behavior Bellman matrix (A_μ), yielding BA-TDC, and then regularize the same behavior-aware equation to obtain BA-TDRC. This two-step construction separates the contribution of behavior-aware geometry from the contribution of regularization. The linear analysis also provides a tractable model for an auxiliary-geometry design question that arises in neural-network value approximation, where feature covariances and temporal transition matrices jointly shape the last-layer correction dynamics. We give a finite-state mean-system formulation, prove fixed-point preservation and almost-sure convergence under a Hurwitz stability condition on the instantiated mean system, and compare deterministic mean rates through the spectral radius of the exact linear error recursion. Experiments on the two-state counterexample, Baird's counterexample, Random Walk, and Boyan Chain show that the behavior-aware replacement can be highly beneficial by itself on some tasks, but that regularization is necessary for robust performance across harder settings.

</details>


### [136] [The Cognitive Categorical Transformer: Category-Theoretic Inductive Biases for Language Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28864)
*Al Kari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Cognitive Categorical Transformer (CCT), a GPT-2–based architecture with cognitively and category-theoretically inspired components that significantly reduces perplexity on WikiText-103, mainly due to simplicial message passing, and identifies a structure/consistency distinction for categorical priors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether cognitively grounded, category-theoretic structures—specifically simplicial message passing and other categorical priors—can measurably improve language-model performance (perplexity) beyond standard GPT-2 architectures, and to clarify which types of categorical priors are empirically beneficial.

Method: Augment a pretrained GPT-2 Small (306M parameters) with additional components: GT-Full simplicial message passing and several categorical priors inspired by cognitive science. Train CCT under a tightly controlled matched-step protocol (same data, 215k optimizer steps, same optimizer and schedule) on WikiText-103. Compare its validation perplexity with that of a baseline GPT-2 Small fine-tuned under identical conditions. Run a retrain-from-scratch ablation where GT-Full is disabled throughout the activation schedule to isolate its contribution. Evaluate additional negative-results priors (sheaf smoothing, adjunction round-trip, curvature regularization) and analyze joint effects with PrecisionWeightedPP to derive the structure/consistency distinction.

Result: CCT achieves 21.27 validation perplexity on WikiText-103 versus 24.19 for the fine-tuned GPT-2 Small baseline, a 2.92 absolute (12% relative) PPL reduction. When GT-Full simplicial message passing is bypassed in a from-scratch ablation, perplexity is 23.72, indicating that GT-Full accounts for 2.45 of the 2.92 PPL gain (84% of the improvement). This is presented as the first ablation-validated evidence that simplicial message passing improves LM perplexity at ~300M scale on WikiText-103. Comparison with published GPT-2 Large (22.05 zero-shot PPL with 6.2x more parameters) situates the result but is not used as a direct benchmark. Additional categorical priors that enforce consistency identities (sheaf smoothing, adjunction round-trip, curvature regularization) fail to improve perplexity, whereas topology-adding structural priors (GT-Full and PrecisionWeightedPP) do.

Conclusion: CCT demonstrates that incorporating category-theoretic, cognitively motivated structural components—especially simplicial message passing—into transformer architectures can significantly improve language-model perplexity at moderate scale, largely independent of parameter count. The ablations suggest a "structure/consistency distinction": categorical priors that introduce richer topological structure (new connectivity/topology in the representation space) are empirically useful for language modeling, while priors that primarily enforce internal consistency identities do not yield perplexity gains. This provides design guidance for future theory-inspired modifications to language models.

Abstract: The Cognitive Categorical Transformer (CCT) is a 306M-parameter architecture that augments a pretrained GPT-2 Small backbone with cognitively grounded components derived from category theory and several inspirations from cognitive science. Under a matched-step protocol (215,000 optimizer steps, matched data, matched optimizer and schedule) on WikiText-103, CCT reaches 21.27 validation perplexity, compared with 24.19 for an identically fine-tuned GPT-2 Small baseline. The architecture therefore contributes a 2.92 PPL (12% relative) reduction beyond what in-domain fine-tuning alone provides. A retrain-from-scratch ablation that holds GT-Full simplicial message passing bypassed across the entire seven-phase activation schedule reaches 23.72 PPL, localizing 84% of the architectural improvement (2.45 of 2.92 PPL) to GT-Full. We present the first ablation-validated evidence that simplicial message passing improves language-model perplexity at the 306M-parameter scale on WikiText-103. Published GPT-2 Large reaches 22.05 zero-shot PPL on WikiText-103 with 6.2x more parameters than GPT-2 Small; this paper treats that number as an external published reference, not as the architectural benchmark. Three negative results on consistency-style categorical priors (sheaf smoothing, adjunction round-trip, curvature regularization) and the joint structural-prior result for GT-Full and PrecisionWeightedPP together support an empirical pattern termed the *structure/consistency distinction*, in which categorical priors that add new topology improve language modeling and those that enforce a consistency identity do not.

</details>


### [137] [Ultra-Reduced-Impact-Encased-Logging (URIEL): propose a new method for selective sustainable logging and post-harvest silvicultural treatment in tropical forest using airborne robotics systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28883)
*Daniel Albiero,Gelton Fernando de Morais,Daniela Han,Flávio Roberto de Freitas Gonçalves,Artur Vitório Andrade Santos,Wesllen Lins de Araújo,Alessandra Maia Freire,Cláudio Kiyoshi Umezu,Mateus Peressin,Francesco Toscano,Admilson Írio Ribeiro,Alfeu J. Sguarezi Filho,Américo Ferraz Dias Neto,Angel Pontin Garcia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes URIEL, a high-tech, ultra-reduced-impact logging system for tropical forests using heli-logging, robotics, AI, and drones, and finds it can be economically viable while nearly eliminating forest damage, though its real-world success depends on cooperation among key stakeholders.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tropical forests face severe deforestation due to economic and political pressures, and this deforestation accelerates climate change. Existing logging practices often cause extensive collateral damage to forests and their ecosystem services. The authors are motivated to design a logging method that allows timber extraction while minimizing ecological impact and maintaining economic feasibility, thereby aligning forest use with conservation and climate goals.

Method: The authors conceptually design the URIEL logging system, which combines helicopter-based timber extraction (heli-logging) with intensive use of robotics and AI. Post-harvest silvicultural treatments are implemented via drones to restore and manage the forest after logging. They develop appropriate equipment specifications, determine dimensions, and build a digital proof of concept. They then perform digital simulations and an economic feasibility analysis across different helicopter–timber–distance configurations to assess performance and costs.

Result: Simulations and economic analyses indicate that the URIEL method is highly economically viable for a range of helicopter-timber-distance combinations. The modeling suggests that URIEL can practically eliminate collateral damage to surrounding forest stands, thereby preserving ecosystem services while still enabling commercial timber extraction.

Conclusion: The study concludes that URIEL is technically and economically promising and could allow near-zero-damage logging in tropical forests, maintaining ecosystem services. However, successful deployment is not guaranteed by technology alone; it critically depends on coordinated involvement and alignment of interests among key stakeholders, including high-tech industries, political authorities, certified logging firms, and indigenous or local forest-dependent communities.

Abstract: Tropical forests worldwide are under intense deforestation pressure driven by economic and political interests, and scientific evidence suggests this deforestation contributes to climate change. This paper proposes a novel logging method for tropical forests, Ultra-Reduced-Impact-Encased-Logging (URIEL). This new method is based on heli-logging techniques combined with intensive use of robotics and AI integrated with post-harvest silvicultural treatments performed by drones. The concept of appropriate equipment for this method was developed, dimensions were determined, details were completed in a digital proof of concept, and an effective digital simulation and economic feasibility analysis were carried out for various helicopter-timber-distance combinations. The results demonstrated that a URIEL method has high economic viability and makes it possible to virtually eliminate collateral damage to forests while maintaining ecosystem services. The main conclusion of this paper is that, despite the satisfactory scientific and technological results, the feasibility of a Uriel method depends on the integration of stakeholders intrinsic to the context: high-tech industry; political governments; certified logging companies; and native populations.

</details>


### [138] [Review Arcade: On the Human Alignment and Gameability of LLM Reviews](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28897)
*Hans Ole Hatzel,Sebastian Steindl,Jan Strich*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper empirically studies how well LLM-generated reviews align with human reviews in ACL Rolling Review 2025, and how much authors can improve paper scores by iteratively revising drafts based on LLM feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-written reviews are being piloted in real scientific conferences, it is crucial to understand both how reliably they reflect human judgments and how easily they can be "gamed" by authors who optimize their papers for LLM feedback rather than scientific quality.

Method: The authors run experiments on submissions from ACL Rolling Review 2025. They generate LLM-based reviews with different prompts and models, compare these to human reviews to measure alignment, and simulate an iterative draft-revise workflow where the author repeatedly edits the paper based on LLM feedback to see how the scores change.

Result: They find that LLM-human review alignment is limited and highly variable across prompts and models: at best, it is reasonable but far from perfect. In simulated author workflows where papers are revised according to LLM feedback, up to 35% of papers achieve statistically significant increases in their overall scores, demonstrating that LLM reviews can be effectively gamed in certain conditions.

Conclusion: LLM-generated peer reviews are not yet robust substitutes for human evaluation: their alignment with human reviewers is inconsistent, and they are vulnerable to gaming via LLM-guided revision. As conferences adopt LLM-based reviewing, they must carefully design protocols and safeguards to mitigate misalignment and manipulation risks.

Abstract: LLM-generated reviews for scientific papers are gaining considerable traction and are even being officially piloted by major conferences. We have to assume that not only reviewers are using LLM-assistance, but also that authors use LLMs to revise their papers before submitting. In this work, we perform empirical experiments on papers from the 2025 ACL Rolling Review (ARR) to evaluate LLM reviews from both the author and the reviewer perspective. First, we identify a limited alignment of LLM reviews with human ones. In the best-case scenario, the alignment is reasonable. However, we also find that LLM-human alignment varies substantially across prompts and models. Finally, we investigate the scenario in which the author uses an iterative draft-revise workflow to improve the submission according to the LLM review. We find that this "gaming" of LLM reviews can be effective in specific scenarios, leading to a statistically significant increase of overall scores for up to 35\% of papers. We publish our code: https://github.com/uhh-hcds/reviewarcade.

</details>


### [139] [Orthogonal Concept Erasure for Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28902)
*Yuhao Sun,Lingyun Yu,Haoxiang Xu,Fengyuan Miao,Zhuoer Xu,Hongtao Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE), a geometric, multiplicative-edit method for diffusion models that erases undesired concepts more precisely while preserving overall generation quality and scaling to many concepts efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current concept erasure in diffusion models either relies on expensive retraining or efficient but imprecise editing that harms overall generative ability because they use additive parameter updates that entangle concept removal with global model geometry. The authors want a method that is cheap, deployment-friendly, and can erase many concepts while minimally affecting other content.

Method: They introduce Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE), which views concept erasure as a geometric transformation in parameter space. Instead of additive parameter edits, OCE computes layer-wise orthogonal (multiplicative) transformations with a closed-form solution that rotate neurons to remove concept directions while preserving neuron magnitudes and angular relations. For multi-concept erasure, they define a subspace-level objective and structured manipulation of the subspace spanned by multiple concept directions to resolve conflicting constraints and make erasure scalable.

Result: Experiments on both single- and multi-concept erasure benchmarks show that OCE achieves better removal of targeted concepts and better preservation of non-target content compared to prior training-based and editing-based approaches. The method can erase up to 100 concepts in 4.3 seconds, demonstrating strong scalability and efficiency.

Conclusion: Orthogonal, multiplicative parameter updates provide a more effective geometric mechanism for concept erasure in diffusion models than additive edits, enabling precise and scalable removal of many concepts with minimal degradation to overall generative capacity. OCE thus offers a practical, deployment-friendly solution for controlling undesired content in generative models.

Abstract: Concept erasure has emerged as a promising approach to mitigate undesired or unsafe content in diffusion models, yet existing methods still face significant limitations. While training-based methods are effective, their high computational cost limits scalability. Editing-based methods are more efficient and deployment-friendly, yet they struggle to simultaneously achieve precise concept erasure and preserve overall generative capacity. We identify this core limitation of the editing-based methods as reliance on additive parameter updates. Our empirical analysis reveals that concept semantics primarily depend on neuron direction rather than neuron magnitude, while overall generative capacity relies on the angular geometry of neurons. As additive updates inherently entangle direction, magnitude, and angular geometry, they inevitably introduce unintended interference between concept erasure and overall generation performance. To address this, we propose Orthogonal Concept Erasure (OCE), which reformulates editing-based erasure as multiplicative parameter updates from a geometric perspective. Specifically, OCE applies layer-wise orthogonal transformations derived from a closed-form solution to the parameters, enabling precise concept erasure while preserving the neuron magnitude and angular geometry. Furthermore, to address conflicting constraints in multi-concept erasure, OCE introduces a subspace-level objective with structured subspace manipulation, yielding a more effective and scalable erasure. Extensive experiments on single- and multi-concept erasure demonstrate that OCE outperforms existing methods in concept erasure and non-target preservation, erasing up to 100 concepts in 4.3 s. Code: https://github.com/HansSunY/OCE.

</details>


### [140] [Frontier LLM-based agents can overcome the ontology curation bottleneck for natural phenotypes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28965)
*James P. Balhoff,Hilmar Lapp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates large language model (LLM) “agentic curator” systems for mapping free-text phenotype descriptions to ontology terms and shows they reach human-level variability and outperform a prior NLP tool.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Phenotype annotation (linking free-text morphological descriptions to ontology terms) is crucial for integrating comparative morphological data across studies, but has been a manual, labor-intensive, and non-scalable process handled by expert human curators. Prior work (Dahdul et al. 2018) defined a Gold Standard for Entity-Quality annotations and found that an earlier NLP system (Semantic CharaParser) performed substantially worse than human curators, leaving a gap in automated, scalable annotation capabilities. With the emergence of powerful hosted LLMs, it is important to assess whether they can now match human curators and relieve this bottleneck.

Method: The authors re-use the Gold Standard EQ annotations from Dahdul et al. (2018), which span seven phylogenetic studies, as a benchmark. They set up five state-of-the-art hosted LLMs (from Anthropic and OpenAI) as “agentic curators”: each model works in a controlled environment that provides (1) the full source publication PDFs, (2) the same annotation guide used by human curators in the original study, (3) four relevant ontologies (UBERON, PATO, BSPO, GO), and (4) a validation script. Each LLM agent generates EQ annotations for the benchmark data, which are then evaluated against the Gold Standard using the same ontology-based semantic similarity metrics used in the original study, enabling a direct comparison with both human curators and the Semantic CharaParser tool.

Result: Across the Gold Standard benchmark, all five LLM agents achieved performance within the range of inter-curator variability previously observed among the three trained human biocurators. The top-performing LLM agent nearly reached, but did not surpass, the best human curator’s performance. Importantly, all agentic LLM systems significantly outperformed the Semantic CharaParser NLP tool on all four evaluation metrics considered.

Conclusion: Modern LLM-based “agentic curator” systems, when provided with appropriate resources (source PDFs, curation guidelines, and ontologies), can produce phenotype ontology annotations with quality comparable to trained human biocurators and clearly superior to earlier automated tools. Although they do not yet exceed the best human curator, they narrow the human–machine performance gap and show strong potential for scaling phenotype annotation and reducing a key bottleneck in comparative morphology data integration.

Abstract: Linking free-text phenotype descriptions to ontology terms, typically referred to as phenotype annotation, is essential for the cross-study integration of comparative morphological data. This labor intensive process has heavily relied on highly trained human experts, which makes it challenging to scale and thus a key bottleneck. Dahdul et al. (2018) established a Gold Standard (GS) of Entity-Quality (EQ) annotations across seven phylogenetic studies and used it to evaluate three human curators and the Semantic CharaParser NLP tool with ontology-based semantic similarity metrics; they reported that machine-human consistency was significantly lower than inter-curator (human-human) consistency. Here we revisit that benchmark with five frontier hosted LLMs from Anthropic and OpenAI, each operating as an "agentic curator" within a self-contained workspace that supplies the source publication PDF, the same annotation guide used by the original human curators, the four project ontologies (UBERON, PATO, BSPO, GO), and a validation script. Evaluated against the same Gold Standard, every agent fell within the range of inter-curator variability of the three trained human biocurators of the original study; the best performing agents approached but did not reach the best performing human curator. Agents substantially outperformed Semantic CharaParser on all four metrics.

</details>


### [141] [VFEAgent: A Multimodal Agent Framework for End-to-End Automated Finite Element Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28978)
*Jiachen Zhang,Junyi Lao,Chenghao Liu,Siyuan Liu,Shixin Wu,Linsen Zhang,Boyu Wang,Songfang Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents VFEAgent, an end-to-end multi-agent system that automates FEA modeling and simulation directly from images and text descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Finite Element Analysis is central to engineering design but requires complex, expertise-heavy workflows. Existing attempts to use LLMs in FEA struggle with multimodal inputs and complex task execution, motivating a more capable, automated, and robust system.

Method: The authors design VFEAgent, combining (1) a multimodal vision-language multi-agent pipeline using ReAct-style reasoning to convert heterogeneous inputs (images plus text) into structured FEA specifications, and (2) a verification-first code synthesis framework with self-debugging and fallback to ensure generated simulation code is both executable and physically valid.

Result: In evaluations over diverse engineering mechanics scenarios, VFEAgent generates complete and physically valid FEA simulations with a high success rate, outperforming LLM-only baselines in reliability and correctness.

Conclusion: The study shows that fully automating the FEA workflow from raw problem statements and images is feasible. VFEAgent can reliably produce valid simulations, indicating strong potential to reduce engineers’ manual modeling burden and streamline engineering analysis workflows.

Abstract: Finite Element Analysis (FEA) serves as the cornerstone of modern engineering design. However, its workflow is inherently complex and relies heavily on domain expertise. Although recent efforts have integrated Large Language Models (LLMs) into FEA, existing approaches face limitations in handling multimodal inputs and executing complex tasks. To address these limitations, we propose VFEAgent, an end-to-end multi-agent system designed to automate FEA modeling and simulation directly from input images and problem descriptions. Our methodology integrates two core components: (1) a multimodal vision-language multi-agent pipeline that employs ReAct-driven reasoning to extract structured FEA specifications from heterogeneous inputs and (2) a verification-first code synthesis framework, incorporating robust self-debugging and fallback mechanisms to ensure executability and physical validity. We systematically evaluated the system across various engineering mechanics scenarios. The results demonstrate that VFEAgent achieves a high success rate in generating complete and physically valid simulations, outperforming LLM-based baseline methods in reliability and correctness. These findings validate the feasibility of automating the complete FEA workflow, highlighting the framework's potential to liberate engineers from tedious manual analysis.

</details>


### [142] [BEAMS: Benchmarking and Evaluating AI for Modeling and Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.28994)
*Sara Metcalf,William Schoenberg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the BEAMS Initiative and the open-source sd_ai project to benchmark and evaluate AI tools for modeling and simulation, focusing on human-centered, interpretable, and ethically responsible practices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI tools increasingly support real-world decision making, there is a need to ensure that the simulation models they build are interpretable, align with human modeling practice, and are used in responsible, ethical ways. Existing AI tools vary in quality and lack standardized, human-centered benchmarks that evaluate not only predictive performance, but also how well they support experts in qualitative and quantitative modeling tasks and in understanding, critiquing, and improving models.

Method: The BEAMS Initiative creates an open, collaborative infrastructure for benchmarking AI-assisted modeling and simulation tools. It organizes work through a steering group (to prioritize and design benchmarks) and a technical group (to implement them as automated tests). Using the open-source sd_ai project, they implement and run tests across multiple categories—such as causal translation, model iteration, causal reasoning, conformance, model behavior explanation, suggested model building steps, and suggested model fixes—on different AI “engines” connected to various large language models (LLMs).

Result: Automated benchmark tests have been implemented for multiple qualitative and quantitative modeling tasks and applied to different AI tools and LLM configurations. Results show substantial variability in performance across LLMs and engine types. AI tools generally perform better on model discussion and basic qualitative modeling than on deeper causal reasoning or quantitative error fixing. No single LLM consistently performs best across all tasks, and there are notable tradeoffs between speed and accuracy.

Conclusion: Benchmarking AI for modeling and simulation must be task-specific and human-centered, as different tools and LLMs excel at different aspects of the modeling workflow. Current AI tools are relatively strong at qualitative support and discussion but weaker on causal reasoning and quantitative correction. Open, transparent benchmarking infrastructures like the BEAMS Initiative and sd_ai can guide responsible development and highlight biases and limitations, and future work will extend benchmarks to incorporate concerns about bias, diverse perspectives, and real human use cases.

Abstract: AI tools to support real world decision making must be able to build simulation models that inform their recommendations and render them interpretable. Tools that can automate aspects of modeling practice must complement human expertise, not replace it. The BEAMS Initiative aims to guide the development of AI tools for modeling and simulation toward forms that are responsible and ethical by establishing benchmarks for human centered modeling and simulation practices. The initiative uses open digital and organizational infrastructure to collaboratively evaluate AI tools for modeling and simulation. The open source sd ai project hosted by the initiative establishes transparency and enables contributions to be shared broadly. A steering group focuses on prioritizing potential benchmarks, while a technical group focuses on implementing the benchmarks in the form of automated tests. Tests for several distinct categories of evaluation have been implemented and applied to AI tools that support qualitative model building, quantitative model building, and model discussion. These include tests for causal translation, model iteration, causal reasoning, conformance, model behavior explanation, suggested model building steps, and suggested model fixes. When engines from the sd ai project are coupled with different LLMs, their performance on these evaluations reveals variability across different AI tools. The evaluations implemented by the initiative demonstrate that AI enabled modeling tools perform better at discussion and basic qualitative tasks than with causal reasoning and quantitative error fixing. No single LLM dominates across engine types, highlighting the importance of specific tasks and tradeoffs between speed and accuracy. Ongoing efforts of the initiative aim to incorporate benchmarks that address concerns about bias by considering alternative perspectives and human centered use cases.

</details>


### [143] [Adopt $\neq$ Adapt: Longitudinal Analyses of LLM Conversations in the Wild](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29018)
*Rebecca M. M. Hicke,Kiran Tomlinson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how individual users’ interactions with large language models (LLMs) evolve over time, finding that individual habits are very stable, heavy users are more skilled and professional in their usage, and public datasets like WildChat are biased toward power users and don’t represent average behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most existing research on user–LLM interaction looks at snapshots or aggregates of behavior and does not capture how single users change or develop over time. This limits understanding of habit formation, learning, and heterogeneity in LLM usage, and may also mislead downstream research that relies on public logs like WildChat. The authors want to fill this gap and question whether widely used datasets are representative of typical users.

Method: They analyze longitudinal conversational logs from about 12,000 randomly sampled Microsoft Bing Copilot users, reconstructing user-level trajectories and behavior patterns. They quantify trends both at the population level and within individual users, and stratify users by activity level to compare success rates, task complexity, and professional orientation of queries. They then compare these patterns with those found in the large public WildChat-4.8M dataset to assess representativeness and bias toward power users.

Result: Population-wide, Copilot usage shows noticeable changes over time, but the same is not true at the individual level: single users’ habits change very little, so individual trajectories exhibit weak trends. More active users have higher success, pose more complex queries, and use the LLM more for professional tasks. Some of these patterns are visible in WildChat-4.8M, but that dataset is strongly skewed toward highly proficient power users and underrepresents typical users.

Conclusion: Existing user behaviors with LLMs are highly persistent and difficult to shift, and there is substantial heterogeneity between casual users and power users. Public datasets like WildChat overrepresent advanced users and therefore do not capture ordinary user–AI interaction patterns, which is a critical limitation for research or product decisions based on such logs.

Abstract: Although a growing body of research has begun to describe user--LLM interactions, the picture it paints is largely static; little is known about how individual users change their behavior over time. To address this gap, we analyze the conversational trajectories of $\sim$12,000 randomly sampled Microsoft Bing Copilot users and compare these with data from WildChat-4.8M. While the Copilot data contains significant population-level trends, we find that trends in individual user trajectories are much weaker; user habits prove to be overwhelmingly sticky. We also find stark differences between users of different activity levels: more active users have more successful conversations and use the LLM for more complex and professionally oriented tasks. Some user trends also appear in WildChat-4.8M, but we find evidence that this dataset is significantly skewed towards highly proficient "power" users. Ultimately, our results suggest that existing user behavior is difficult to change and demonstrate the extent of user heterogeneity. Our comparison between datasets highlights that WildChat does not represent typical user-AI interactions, an important caveat for downstream uses of the data.

</details>


### [144] [When Models Disagree: Rethinking LLM Evaluation for Public Comment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29025)
*Aisha Najera,Alvin Moon,Vedant Srinivasan,Rajesh Veeraraghavan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces an 'Interpretive Audit Pipeline' to evaluate how different large language models categorize public comments for federal agencies, focusing on disagreements between models as signals of interpretive complexity rather than relying solely on stance accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Federal agencies increasingly use LLMs to categorize public comments, which directly shapes policy-relevant insights. Existing evaluations mainly focus on stance accuracy against a small gold-standard set, which cannot reveal when different LLMs interpret or categorize the same input in materially different ways—differences that can affect which arguments policymakers see. The authors want a framework that exposes and manages interpretive disagreement rather than hiding it behind aggregate accuracy metrics.

Method: They propose an Interpretive Audit Pipeline that compares outputs from multiple LLMs on the same public comment corpus and uses inter-model disagreement as a diagnostic tool. The pipeline flags comments with high disagreement for focused human review, treating disagreement as evidence of interpretive complexity. Empirically, they apply this to 1,260 comments on a USDA docket using four LLMs, compare inter-model thematic divergence to within-model prompt variation, and run a two-stage labeling study on a stratified 40-comment subset, where four LLMs and a human annotator first label independently and then revise after seeing all labels.

Result: They find that the thematic divergence across different models is larger than the variation caused by changing prompts within a single model, indicating that model choice matters more than prompt choice for interpretive outcomes. An expert-designed rubric significantly reduces apparent disagreement but does not actually resolve deeper interpretive differences. In the revision phase, labelers behave differently across models, and the human annotator often adds new framings that were not present in any model outputs, showing that ensemble model agreement can still miss important interpretive perspectives.

Conclusion: Accuracy-based evaluation is insufficient for LLM-assisted coding of public comments in policymaking, because it obscures important interpretive disagreements between models and between models and humans. Disagreement-based evaluation, operationalized through an Interpretive Audit Pipeline, is a necessary complementary approach that highlights ambiguous or contentious inputs and guides targeted human review, making the use of LLMs in public comment analysis more transparent and accountable.

Abstract: Federal agencies are deploying large language models (LLMs) to categorize public comment corpora, where the model's organization of the record shapes what policymakers see and which arguments register. Standard evaluation, anchored on stance accuracy against a small validated set, cannot detect when different models produce materially different categorizations of the same public input. We propose an Interpretive Audit Pipeline that treats multi-model disagreement as diagnostic of interpretive complexity and directs human review toward genuinely ambiguous public input. Analyzing 1,260 public comments on a federal USDA docket across four LLMs, we find that inter-model thematic divergence exceeds within-model prompt variation, and that an expert rubric suppresses deep interpretive disagreement without resolving it. In a two-stage labeling study on a stratified 40-comment subsample, four LLMs and a human annotator labeled independently and then revised after seeing the others' labels. Revision behavior varied across labelers, and the human annotator's revisions frequently introduced framings absent from the ensemble's collective output. We argue disagreement-based evaluation is a necessary complement to accuracy metrics for LLM-assisted interpretive coding.

</details>


### [145] [Mind Your Tone: Does Tone Alter LLM Performance?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29027)
*Om Dobariya,Akhil Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how different tones in prompts (e.g., polite, demanding) systematically affect LLM accuracy on multiple-choice questions, showing these effects are model- and subject-dependent and can be substantial.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in real applications, users often assume that changing the tone or style of a prompt (polite vs. rude, formal vs. casual) does not meaningfully affect accuracy on objective tasks. However, anecdotal evidence suggests that tonal changes can shift model behavior, which could undermine reliability, fairness, and reproducibility. The authors aim to rigorously quantify how much tone alone can alter accuracy and to understand the mechanisms behind such variations.

Method: The authors construct two evaluation datasets of multiple-choice questions with systematically varied tones of prompts. One is a 50-question base set, each question rewritten in five tonal styles; the other is a 570-question subset of MMLU spanning 57 subjects, each with seven tonal variants. They test four widely used, cost-efficient LLMs (ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-5-nano, Gemini 2.5 Flash, Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite), measure accuracy under each tonal condition, and perform statistical analyses to detect significant differences. They also conduct a subject-level analysis of tone sensitivity and propose a routing-style framework hypothesizing that different tones trigger different internal reasoning modes within the models.

Result: All four models show systematic tone effects, but the magnitude and direction are highly model-specific. Some models exhibit only small yet statistically significant accuracy shifts between tones, while others experience large swings in accuracy depending on the tone used. Tone sensitivity varies across subjects within MMLU, with some domains more affected by prompt tone than others. The analyses support the idea that tone can modulate internal reasoning pathways or modes, consistent with the proposed routing explanation.

Conclusion: Prompt tone is not a neutral cosmetic choice: it can measurably and sometimes substantially change LLM accuracy even on objective multiple-choice questions. These effects differ across models and subjects, indicating that current LLMs are not tone-robust. The authors conclude that practitioners and researchers should not assume that results will generalize across prompt tones, and they recommend more careful evaluation and deployment practices that account for tonal sensitivity, potentially using routing frameworks or calibration techniques to mitigate these effects.

Abstract: The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) is proliferating, yet their performance is observed to vary based on prompting styles and tones. In this study, we investigate both whether and how tonal variations in prompts lead to disparate LLM accuracy for objective multiple-choice questions. We use two datasets: a 50-base question dataset with five tone variants and a 570-base question MMLU subset spanning 57 subjects with seven tone variants. Experiments were conducted to evaluate the performance of four cost-efficient, popular LLMs: ChatGPT-4o, ChatGPT-5-nano, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite. Across models, tonal effects are systematic but highly model-dependent. Some models show small, yet statistically significant, shifts, while others exhibit large accuracy swings across tones. Further, we identify subject-level differences in tone sensitivity and present a routing framework to explain how tones may attune internal reasoning modes. Our findings caution users against assuming tone-robust reliability in LLM deployments.

</details>


### [146] [Practitioner Beliefs and Behaviors in AI-Enhanced Education: DOT Framework Survey Evidence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29041)
*David Gibson,M. Elizabeth Azukas,Gerald Knezek*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Cross-sectional survey of 72 higher education practitioners on AI in teaching/learning, using the DOT Framework, finds broadly positive views of AI as support tool, strong emphasis on human oversight, practice focused on prompting/content generation, and institutional barriers in policy/training; offers a 3-factor belief structure and calls for further validation and outcome research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how higher education practitioners currently think about, use, and are institutionally supported (or constrained) in integrating AI into teaching and learning, and to empirically explore and preliminarily validate the DOT Framework as a model of these beliefs and practices.

Method: Cross-sectional survey of 72 higher education practitioners measuring AI familiarity, usage, design-oriented practices, and pedagogical beliefs, followed by exploratory factor analysis of 19 belief items to derive underlying dimensions related to AI and instructional design (using the DOT Framework as conceptual grounding).

Result: Exploratory factor analysis yielded a three-factor structure—AI Functional Capabilities, Oversight and Governance, and Instructor Collaboration and Planning—with high internal consistency (α = .90). Participants reported generally positive beliefs about AI as a pedagogical support tool, coupled with strong endorsement of human oversight and critical evaluation; they commonly used AI for iterative prompting and content generation but did not systematically engage in needs assessment or feedback loops. Many reported institutional barriers such as gaps in policy, limited training, and insufficient infrastructure.

Conclusion: The study offers initial empirical support for the DOT Framework as a descriptive model of practitioner beliefs and practices regarding AI in higher education, proposes an initial measurement structure for these beliefs, and identifies a misalignment between design-oriented theory and real-world implementation, indicating the need for confirmatory validation and future research connecting AI-supported design practices to instructional quality outcomes.

Abstract: This study reports findings from a cross-sectional survey (n = 72) of higher education practitioners examining beliefs, behaviors, and institutional conditions related to artificial intelligence (AI) integration in teaching and learning. Grounded in the DOT Framework, which integrates design thinking and open systems theory, the study investigates AI familiarity, usage patterns, design-oriented practices, and pedagogical beliefs. Exploratory factor analysis of 19 belief items identified a three-factor structure: AI Functional Capabilities, Oversight and Governance, and Instructor Collaboration and Planning (α = .90). Results indicate that practitioners hold favorable views of AI as a pedagogical support while maintaining strong commitments to human oversight and critical evaluation. Reported practices emphasize iterative prompting and content generation, with less consistent use of needs assessment and feedback loops. Institutional barriers including limited policy, training, and infrastructure were widely reported. These findings provide preliminary empirical support for the DOT Framework as a descriptive model of practitioner beliefs and practices, while also highlighting gaps between design-oriented theory and current implementation. The study contributes an initial measurement structure and identifies directions for confirmatory validation and outcome-based research linking AI-supported design practices to instructional quality.

</details>


### [147] [Differentiable Belief-based Opponent Shaping](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29042)
*Aarav G Sane,Karthik Sivachandran,Rohan Paleja*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose D-BOS, a differentiable belief-based opponent shaping method for multi-agent RL that manipulates agents' beliefs directly and outperforms baselines in hidden-role games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing opponent-shaping methods work in parameter/policy/value space and hidden-role belief manipulation uses hand-crafted deception/coordination objectives, limiting flexibility and realism. The authors want a more general, principled way to shape opponents via their beliefs, letting strategies emerge from the environment rewards.

Method: Introduce D-BOS, a first-order differentiable method that treats observers' beliefs as the opponent state and backpropagates through k-step softmax-Bayes belief updates. The agent optimizes with respect to these belief dynamics, using the belief state as the shaping target instead of manually rewarding deception/cooperation. For multiple observers, they aggregate gradients over each observer's inferred belief trajectory.

Result: On hidden-role game benchmarks, D-BOS performs better than PPO and BBM baselines, particularly in mixed-motive scenarios where incentives to cooperate and compete are both present.

Conclusion: Belief-space opponent shaping via differentiable belief dynamics is effective and flexible, enabling emergent strategic behavior without hand-coded deception/cooperation objectives and yielding superior performance in hidden-role multi-agent environments.

Abstract: Human coordination often relies on the ability to influence the beliefs of others through strategic action. In multi-agent reinforcement learning, opponent shaping attempts to replicate this influence, though existing methods typically operate within an opponent's parameter, policy, or value space. Meanwhile, belief-manipulation techniques in hidden-role games often rely on hard-coded objectives, such as deception or belief saturation. We propose Differentiable Belief-based Opponent Shaping (D-BOS), a first-order method that treats each observer's belief as the shaped opponent state and differentiates through $k$-step softmax-Bayes belief dynamics. Rather than explicitly rewarding deceptive or cooperative behavior, our method treats the belief state as the target for shaping. This allows the optimal strategy to emerge naturally from the environment's reward structure. This belief-space formulation provides an opponent-shaping signal by differentiating through opponent belief updates, and naturally extends to multiple observers by aggregating gradients over their individual inferred belief trajectories. Empirically, D-BOS outperforms PPO and BBM in hidden-role games, with the largest gains in mixed-motive settings.

</details>


### [148] [Hallucination Mitigation with Agentic AI, Nested Learning, and AI Sustainability via Semantic Caching](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29055)
*Diego Gosmar,Deborah A. Dahl*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates a multi‑agent, memory‑augmented LLM pipeline to reduce hallucinations and improve observability using a custom benchmark and composite hallucination score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM hallucinations, especially in multi‑agent pipelines, are a key barrier for production deployment because unsupported claims can propagate across stages. The authors want an approach that improves factuality, observability, and efficiency without retraining base models.

Method: They adapt a HOPE-inspired Nested Learning architecture with Continuum Memory Systems and semantic similarity caching. A three-stage agent pipeline (high-temperature generator plus two corrective reviewers) is orchestrated via the Open Floor Protocol. They test on 310 prompts mixing epistemic-uncertainty and hallucination-induction cases.
