Five KPIs (FCD, FGR, FDF, ECS, OSR) are combined into a Total Hallucination Score (THS) under five weighting schemes to explore mitigation–observability trade-offs.

Result: The asymmetric pipeline reduces THS by about 31–36% across weighting configurations versus the high-stochasticity baseline. Semantic caching yields a 47.3% cache hit rate (440/930 potential calls), cutting effective LLM calls to 490 and lowering estimated energy and CO2e costs. The configuration emphasizing observability (ExtremeObservability) achieves the best, most negative THS (-0.0709).

Conclusion: Multi-agent LLM systems augmented with memory and semantic caching can simultaneously reduce hallucinations, increase observability/auditability, and improve operational efficiency, all without retraining the underlying models. High observability does not harm, and may even strengthen, hallucination mitigation.

Abstract: Hallucination remains a major reliability barrier for production LLM systems, particularly in multi-agent pipelines where unsupported claims can propagate unchecked across stages. This paper adapts a HOPE-inspired Nested Learning architecture with Continuum Memory Systems (CMS) and semantic similarity caching to a hybrid benchmark of 310 prompts combining 217 epistemic-uncertainty prompts and 93 fabrication-induction stress-test prompts. A three-stage agentic pipeline orchestrated via the Open Floor Protocol (OFP) is evaluated with five KPIs -- FCD (Factual Claim Density), FGR (Factual Grounding References), FDF (Fictional Disclaimer Frequency), ECS (Explicit Contextualization Score), and OSR (Observability Score Ratio) -- aggregated into THS (Total Hallucination Score) across five weighting configurations to study mitigation-observability trade-offs. FDF, ECS, OSR, and FGR are subtracted as mitigation signals, so that a more negative THS indicates stronger mitigation. The FrontEndAgent is configured as a high-stochasticity generator (temperature = 1.0) to produce a realistic hallucination baseline, while the SecondLevelReviewer and ThirdLevelReviewer operate as progressive correctors. This asymmetric design yields end-to-end THS reductions of -31.3% to -35.9% across five weighting configurations. Semantic caching achieves 440 cache hits over 930 potential calls (47.3% hit rate), reducing LLM invocations to 490, lowering energy and CO2e footprint, and making multi-stage review pipelines operationally viable at production scale. ExtremeObservability attains the most negative final THS (-0.0709), confirming that observability-heavy configurations reinforce rather than compromise mitigation. These findings suggest that memory-augmented multi-agent designs can jointly improve factual reliability, operational efficiency, and auditability without model retraining.

</details>


### [149] [Robust and Efficient Guardrails with Latent Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29068)
*Siddharth Sai,Xiaofei Wen,Muhao Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A new guardrail model, COLAGUARD, compresses multi-step safety reasoning for LLM moderation into latent representations, achieving similar safety performance to explicit reasoning guardrails with far lower latency and token cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Safety guardrails for LLMs are essential but current reasoning-based approaches, while more accurate than simple classifiers, are too slow and token-intensive for high-throughput, real-world deployment.

Method: Introduce COLAGUARD, which learns to transfer multi-step safety reasoning into a continuous latent space using a stage-wise curriculum. At inference, it performs safety moderation via direct hidden-state propagation instead of generating explicit rationales, thereby avoiding multi-step decoding.

Result: Across ten prompt and response moderation tasks covering eight safety benchmarks, COLAGUARD improves macro-F1 by 8.24 points over Llama Guard 3 and matches the macro-F1 of an explicit reasoning baseline (GuardReasoner), while providing a 12.9× speedup and 22.4× reduction in token usage.

Conclusion: Latent reasoning can replicate the robustness of explicit reasoning guardrails but with far better efficiency, offering a practical, deployable approach where safety robustness and inference efficiency are jointly improved instead of being trade-offs.

Abstract: Maintaining the safety of large language models (LLMs) is crucial as they are increasingly deployed in real-world applications. Existing safety guardrails typically rely on single-pass classification or, more recently, distilled reasoning. Reasoning-based guardrails significantly outperform classification-only baselines, but they incur substantial query latency and token overhead that make them impractical for highthroughput deployment. To address this challenge, we propose COLAGUARD, a guardrail model that transfers multi-step safety reasoning into a continuous latent space through a stage-wise training curriculum, enabling direct hidden-state propagation at inference. Evaluated on ten prompt- and response-moderation settings spanning eight safety benchmarks, COLAGUARD improves macro-F1 by 8.24 points over Llama Guard 3 and matches our explicit reasoning baseline, GuardReasoner, in macroF1 while delivering a 12.9X speedup and 22.4X reduction in token usage. Our results suggest that latent reasoning offers a practical alternative to explicit rationale generation for deployable guardrails, jointly improving safety robustness and inference efficiency rather than treating them as competing objectives.

</details>


### [150] [Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap in Reinforcement Learning-Based Industrial Dispatching through Execution Semantics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29078)
*Jonathan Hoss,Noah Klarmann*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce a policy-neutral execution layer that builds consistent decision snapshots from async events, defines explicit action admissibility, and logs structured divergences between intended and actual execution to improve reliability and interpretability of industrial scheduling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Event-driven scheduling in industry suffers from asynchronous, partially observed states, unclear action admissibility, and ambiguous causes of execution errors, which reduce reliability and interpretability. There is a need for a way to separate decision-making logic from messy execution details while making execution issues observable and explainable.

Method: They design a policy-neutral execution and measurement layer that sits between scheduling policies and the industrial environment. It builds decision-valid snapshots from asynchronous event streams, formalizes a standardized execution contract specifying when actions are admissible, and logs execution as structured divergences between policy intent, transactional results, physical actions, and human intervention. They then evaluate this framework using discrete-event simulation across different observation lag regimes.

Result: Simulation experiments show that the framework converts previously undifferentiated execution failures into structured, typed outcomes with full attribution, across all observation lag settings. Under low observation lag, the framework also delivers operational benefits by preventing avoidable execution errors before they are committed.

Conclusion: The proposed layer successfully decouples decision semantics from execution behavior, makes deployment mismatches observable and attributable, and transforms execution uncertainty into useful supervisory data that can be used to evaluate and refine scheduling policies.

Abstract: Event-driven scheduling policies are increasingly deployed in industrial environments, where decisions are made under asynchronous and partially observed system states. As a result, decision states are not temporally consistent, action admissibility is not explicitly defined, and the origin of execution errors remains ambiguous. These issues limit both reliability and interpretability.
  To address this gap, a policy-neutral execution and measurement layer is proposed to mediate between scheduling policies and the industrial execution environment. The layer constructs decision-valid snapshots from asynchronous event streams, defines a standardized execution contract with explicit action admissibility, and records outcomes as divergences between policy intent, transactional outcomes, physical execution, and human intervention. This enables a separation between decision semantics and execution behavior and makes deployment mismatch observable and structurally attributable.
  The proposed framework is evaluated using a discrete-event simulation. The results show analytical benefits across all observation lag regimes, as undifferentiated execution failures are transformed into structured, typed outcomes with full attribution coverage. Operational benefits are strongest under low observation lag, where avoidable execution errors can be prevented before commitment. Overall, the layer turns execution uncertainty into supervisory data for evaluation and policy refinement.

</details>


### [151] [The Importance of Out-of-Band Metadata for Safe Autonomous Agents: The Redpanda Agentic Data Plane](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29082)
*Tyler Akidau,Tyler Rockwood,Johannes Brüderl,Marc Millstone*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Redpanda Agentic Data Plane (ADP), an architecture that uses out-of-band metadata channels to enforce security and governance for AI agents acting as digital employees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents acting as digital employees can access sensitive enterprise systems and data, but they are less predictable and more easily manipulated than humans. Their high technical capability and speed make errors or attacks particularly dangerous, and it is unsafe to trust them with interpreting or enforcing security-critical metadata (like access policies or constraints).

Method: Propose the Redpanda Agentic Data Plane (ADP) architecture, which creates out-of-band metadata channels separate from the agents’ normal data paths. These deterministic channels carry security context, policy signals, and audit data across diverse infrastructure, enforcing governance at data access time, during agent action execution, and when recording outputs.

Result: Implemented ADP in a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system where autonomous agents monitor markets, make decisions, and execute trades across segregated client accounts. In this system, client-specific data scoping, trade approval rules, and tamper-proof audit logs are all enforced via the out-of-band channels that agents cannot observe or circumvent.

Conclusion: Out-of-band metadata channels, as embodied in the Redpanda ADP, can provide robust, deterministic enforcement of governance, security, and auditing for autonomous AI agents, even in complex, high-stakes applications like portfolio management, by decoupling critical control metadata from the agents’ own data and action pathways.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly expected to operate as digital employees: accessing enterprise data, making decisions, and taking actions autonomously. But agents are simultaneously less predictable than humans -- prone to hallucination, misinterpretation, and adversarial manipulation -- and more technically capable: with deep system knowledge and high-throughput interfaces cascading damage at machine speed. This combination makes it unsafe to rely on agents to faithfully interpret or propagate security-critical metadata such as access policies, data classifications, and behavioral constraints.
  We present the Redpanda Agentic Data Plane (ADP), an architecture built around out-of-band metadata channels: infrastructure pathways that carry security context, policy signals, and audit trails deterministically, entirely outside the agent's read and write path and across heterogeneous infrastructure. These channels enforce governance at every stage of the agent lifecycle -- scoping data access on the way in, constraining actions during execution, and capturing tamper-proof transcripts on the way out.
  We demonstrate ADP with a multi-agent portfolio rebalancing system in which autonomous agents monitor markets, make trade decisions, and execute orders across isolated client accounts -- with per-client data scoping, trade approval thresholds, and tamper-proof audit trails all enforced by out-of-band channels the agents can neither see nor bypass.

</details>


### [152] [Trends in AI and Human-AI Interaction in Clinical Trials -- A Hybrid Human-AI Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29096)
*Sandra Woolley,Tim Collins,Khalid Khattak,Illia Chernomorets,Ariane Arevalo,Chris Richardson*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes ClinicalTrials.gov to track trends in AI-related clinical trials over time and across countries, and evaluates a hybrid human–AI workflow for classifying human–AI interaction in these trials.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is rapid growth in AI use in healthcare, but it is unclear how AI is actually being incorporated into clinical trials worldwide and how reliably AI systems can help classify such trials. Understanding temporal and geographic patterns, and testing whether a hybrid human–AI approach can feasibly screen and categorize AI-related trials, can inform research oversight, policy, and future methodological practice.

Method: The authors queried ClinicalTrials.gov using AI-focused terminology, then used a hybrid workflow combining a generative AI model (GPT-5.5) and human reviewers to screen and categorize trial records. They assessed temporal trends in AI terms (e.g., machine learning, deep learning, chatbots, GPTs, large language models) and mapped geographic distribution of AI trials by country. In a random sample of 100 records, they compared human and AI classifications regarding substantive AI use and types of human–AI interaction, focusing on agreement and sources of discrepancy.

Result: AI-related trials have increased markedly over time, with especially strong recent growth in trials mentioning machine learning, deep learning, chatbots, GPTs, and large language models. Geographically, China and the United States had the highest numbers of AI-related trials, with recent growth in countries such as Italy, France, Spain, the UK, and Turkey. In the 100-record subsample, human and AI classifiers agreed well on identifying trials that did not substantively use AI, but agreement dropped when categorizing human–AI interaction, especially in cases where interactions with health professionals were ambiguous or poorly documented in the trial records.

Conclusion: The study concludes that hybrid human–AI screening of clinical trial records is feasible and potentially effective for large-scale characterization of AI in clinical research. However, limitations in trial reporting and ambiguity in definitions of human–AI interaction reduce classification reliability. Improving the clarity and detail of trial records, and standardizing interaction definitions, would enhance the performance and utility of such hybrid workflows in monitoring AI use in clinical trials.

Abstract: This paper examines records retrieved from the ClinicalTrials.gov registry to characterize temporal trends in AI terminology and the geographical distribution of AI trials. The work also reports on an exploratory hybrid human-AI approach to analyzing human-AI interaction trends in registered clinical trials. The hybrid workflow comprised a frontier generative AI model (GPT-5.5) and human review to screen and categorize records returned by an AI-focused search. The findings indicate a marked increase in AI-related trials over time, with recent growth in references to machine learning, deep learning, chatbots, GPTs, and large language models. Geographically, China and the United States accounted for the largest numbers of AI-related trials, with notable recent increases in several other countries including Italy, France, Spain, the UK and Turkey (Türkiye). In a random sample of 100 records, human and AI classifiers showed good agreement in identifying studies not substantively using AI, but lower agreement in classifying human-AI interaction, particularly where health professional interaction was ambiguous or insufficiently described. Overall, the results suggest that hybrid human-AI screening of clinical trial records is potentially viable, but clearer trial reporting and more precise interaction definitions will benefit the process.

</details>


### [153] [Beyond Consensus: Trace-Level Synthesis in Mixture of Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29116)
*Shreyas Fadnavis,Praitayini Kanakaraj,Felix Wyss*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They show that instead of voting on final answers from multiple LLM agents, you should aggregate their full reasoning traces; this solves more problems and reveals an 'aggregation paradox' where reading traces helps even when all agents agree.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard multi-agent LLM setups use majority voting or consensus on final answers, which throws away intermediate reasoning and hits a performance ceiling. The authors suspect that rich, complementary information in the reasoning traces is being lost, and that input perturbations alone don't fix correlated errors when using only answer-level voting.

Method: They empirically compare majority voting vs. a reasoning-trace-level aggregator across tasks, analyze error correlations and the effect of semantic-preserving input perturbations, and then propose Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents (SCMA). SCMA generates diverse reasoning traces via semantic-preserving perturbations, uses anchored refinement with theoretical guarantees to avoid harming correct majorities, and always synthesizes an answer from traces rather than gating on consensus.

Result: They find an 'aggregation paradox': a trace-reading LLM aggregator often recovers correct answers even when all agents' final answers are identical (and sometimes wrong), because useful partial reasoning appears in minority traces that voting would discard. Majority voting shows a performance ceiling that diversity of perturbations alone does not lift. Their SCMA approach, using a single base model with perturbation-induced trace diversity, outperforms heterogeneous ensembles of models on multiple benchmarks (structured reasoning, advanced science, competition math, and programming).

Conclusion: The key unit for combining multi-agent LLM outputs should be the full reasoning trace, not just the final answer. Trace-level aggregation with self-consistent mixture of agents and anchored refinement can safely exploit complementary intermediate steps, surpass majority voting and heterogeneous ensembles while maintaining non-degradation guarantees for correct majorities.

Abstract: When multiple LLM agents solve the same problem, standard practice compresses each agent's reasoning into a majority vote or layered synthesis, treating agreement as the finish line. We show this is unnecessarily lossy: an LLM aggregator that reads complete reasoning traces recovers correct solutions even when agents unanimously agree, with beneficial corrections consistently outweighing harmful ones -- the \emph{aggregation paradox}. Majority voting has a ceiling that perturbation diversity does not raise (error correlations are identical); the aggregator's gain comes from trace-level complementarity, assembling correct intermediate steps from minority chains that voting discards. These findings motivate Self-Consistent Mixture of Agents which generates trace diversity through semantic-preserving input perturbations, safeguards the majority via anchored refinement with provable non-degradation guarantees, and always synthesizes -- never gates on consensus. A single model with perturbation-induced trace variation outperforms heterogeneous model pools across structured reasoning, PhD-level science, competition mathematics, and competitive programming. The unit of aggregation should be the reasoning trace, not the answer.

</details>


### [154] [PRO-CUA: Process-Reward Optimization for Computer Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29119)
*Yifei He,Rui Yang,Hao Bai,Tong Zhang,Han Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PRO-CUA is a process-reward optimization framework that trains computer use agents with iterative step-level reinforcement learning guided by a process reward model, improving credit assignment and reducing distribution shift without requiring expert trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training computer use agents is difficult because live environment interaction is costly and high-quality supervision is scarce. Filtered behavior cloning suffers from imitation bottlenecks such as distribution shift and lack of negative signals, while traditional trajectory-level reinforcement learning struggles with sparse rewards, ambiguous credit assignment, and high infrastructure costs in long-horizon GUI tasks. A new approach is needed that can provide dense, flexible feedback at the step level, avoid reliance on expensive expert demonstrations, and remain scalable for real web environments.

Method: The paper introduces PRO-CUA, a framework that decouples environment interaction from policy optimization for computer use agents. The current policy performs live rollouts to collect states, then generates multiple diverse candidate actions per state. A process reward model (PRM) provides step-level feedback on these candidate actions. Using group-relative advantages, the policy is optimized based on the PRM feedback, enabling reinforcement learning at the step level. Training is done on the agent’s own execution states, reducing distribution shift and allowing dense credit assignment without golden answers or offline expert trajectories.

Result: On live web benchmarks, PRO-CUA-trained agents show improved performance over baselines, demonstrating that step-level reinforcement learning guided by a PRM can effectively train computer use agents. The experiments validate that PRM feedback is reliable for complex GUI interactions and that the group-relative advantage optimization leads to better policies compared to imitation-only or trajectory-level RL methods.

Conclusion: PRO-CUA provides an effective and scalable way to train computer use agents by leveraging step-level reinforcement learning with a process reward model. By separating data collection from optimization and using group-relative advantages, it achieves dense credit assignment, reduces distribution shift, and eliminates reliance on expert demonstrations or golden labels. The empirical results on live web tasks suggest that PRM-guided step-level training is a promising direction for future CUA development and may overcome key limitations of existing behavior cloning and trajectory-level RL approaches.

Abstract: Computer use agents (CUAs) have shown strong potential for automating complex digital workflows, yet their training remains constrained by costly live environment interaction and limited high-quality supervision. Existing filtered behavior cloning pipelines suffer from imitation bottlenecks, including distribution shift from the expert demonstration and the absence of negative learning signals. Meanwhile, standard trajectory-level reinforcement learning struggles with sparse rewards, ambiguous credit assignment, and high infrastructure costs for long-horizon GUI interaction. In this work, we propose PRO-CUA, a process-reward optimization framework for training CUAs with iterative step-level reinforcement learning. PRO-CUA decouples on-policy environment interaction from policy optimization: the current policy collects states through live rollouts, generates diverse candidate actions for each state, receives step-level feedback from a process reward model (PRM), and is optimized with group-relative advantages. This design enables dense and flexible credit assignment without relying on golden answers or offline expert trajectories, while reducing distribution shift by training on the agent's own execution states. Experiments on live web benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of PRO-CUA and the reliability of PRM-guided step-level training.

</details>


### [155] [The Confidence Shortcut: A Reasoning Failure Mode of Masked Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29123)
*Dueun Kim,Albert No*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that the standard confidence-based decoding for masked diffusion language models conflicts with how complex reasoning should unfold, and that training to imitate this confidence pattern makes things worse, while simple random masking better preserves the needed reasoning steps, especially on hard cases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Masked diffusion language models can generate tokens in any order, and current practice is to decide the next tokens by model confidence. Recent work even adjusts training masks to match this confidence-driven generation. However, the authors suspect that this practice may be harmful for tasks that require multi-step logical reasoning, because high-confidence predictions may be made before their logical prerequisites are resolved. They want to understand and demonstrate this misalignment and its consequences.

Method: The authors study confidence-based decoding versus random masking in masked diffusion language models across several reasoning tasks. They build a concrete testbed with multi-digit addition, where correct reasoning requires carrying information across positions. They compare traditional random masking training with a "confidence-aligned" scheme where mask patterns during training mimic those seen under confidence-based decoding. They then evaluate model behavior, focusing on how errors arise, especially for difficult inputs, and analyze the generated reasoning trajectories.

Result: In multi-digit addition, confidence-based decoding leads the model to fill in easy digits early, even when these depend on unresolved long-range carry operations, yielding confident but wrong outputs on harder examples. When training is also aligned with this confidence pattern, the failure rate on such challenging cases increases by roughly an order of magnitude compared to standard random masking. Across five different reasoning tasks, they observe the same qualitative behavior: confidence-based decoding causes systematic failure on highly complex instances, and confidence-aligned training further amplifies this effect, whereas random masking maintains lower error rates on the hard tail.

Conclusion: Confidence-based decoding, and training schemes that align masking to it, are structurally misaligned with the step-by-step logical dependencies required for complex reasoning in masked diffusion language models. These approaches encourage the model to make high-confidence local predictions before the necessary global context is resolved, particularly harming performance on the most complex cases. In contrast, plain random masking, though seemingly less efficient, better preserves the conditional structure of reasoning trajectories and thus yields more robust performance on challenging reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDMs) uniquely support any-order generation, with confidence-based decoding currently serving as the de facto standard inference policy. To optimize for this, recent training schemes attempt to align training mask patterns directly with those observed during generation. However, we argue that confidence-based decoding is inherently misaligned with the logical-flow trajectories required for complex reasoning, and that confidence-aligned training actively entrenches this misalignment. We make this concrete using multi-digit addition, where the decoding strategy prematurely predicts locally easy digits before resolving their long-range dependencies, producing high-confidence errors on challenging inputs. While traditional random masking keeps the failure rate low on this challenging tail, confidence-aligned training amplifies the error rate by an order of magnitude. Across five distinct reasoning tasks, this same pattern emerges with task-dependent severity: confidence-based decoding induces failures on highly complex inputs, and confidence-aligned training exacerbates them. In contrast, random masking -- despite its perceived inefficiency -- robustly preserves the reasoning-trajectory conditionals essential for solving the challenging tail.

</details>


### [156] [Governing Technical Debt in Agentic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29129)
*Muhammad Zia Hydari,Raja Iqbal,Narayan Ramasubbu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces the concepts of Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax to describe new governance and operational challenges created by AI agent systems, and proposes practical ways for managers to monitor and control them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI shifts from static predictive models to agentic systems that reason in multiple steps, use tools, and adapt via memory and feedback, existing notions of software or ML technical debt no longer adequately capture the resulting risks and governance issues. There is a need to conceptualize and manage the unique liabilities and operating burdens of these probabilistic, tool-using agents.

Method: The authors conceptually define two key constructs—Agentic Technical Debt and Stochastic Tax—and distinguish them from traditional technical debt. They analyze the components that contribute to these constructs (prompts, memory, tools, orchestration graphs, policies, observability) and then propose simple managerial artifacts like dashboards and governance controls to make these liabilities and costs visible and manageable.

Result: The paper yields clear definitions and a conceptual framework separating stock-like design/governance liabilities (Agentic Technical Debt) from flow-like operational burdens (Stochastic Tax). It identifies concrete sources of each within agentic AI systems and provides guidance on instrumentation and oversight mechanisms to track and limit them in practice.

Conclusion: Agentic AI systems introduce new forms of technical debt and operating costs that organizations must explicitly recognize and govern. By distinguishing Agentic Technical Debt from Stochastic Tax and by implementing lightweight dashboards and governance controls, managers can better oversee complex AI agents and maintain their behavior within acceptable risk and performance bounds.

Abstract: Agentic AI systems are increasingly being explored as production infrastructure: they reason over multiple steps, call tools, act through workflows, and adapt through memory and feedback. These systems create governance challenges that are not fully captured by traditional software or predictive ML technical debt. We define Agentic Technical Debt as the accumulated liability created when prompts, memory, tool schemas, orchestration graphs, control policies, and observability routines are patched together faster than they can be validated, standardized, and governed. We define Stochastic Tax as the recurring operating burden of keeping probabilistic agent behavior within acceptable bounds. The distinction matters: debt is a stock of design and governance liability, while the tax is a flow of operating cost that arises because stochastic agents act through tools and workflows. We outline how managers can make both visible through lightweight dashboards and governance controls.

</details>


### [157] [Better Later Than Sooner: Neuro-Symbolic Knowledge Graph Construction via Ontology-grounded Post-extraction Correction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29168)
*Lorenzo Loconte,Timothy Hospedales,Cristina Cornelio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a neuro-symbolic framework to build consistent, ontology-grounded knowledge graphs (KGs) for complex question answering, reducing LLM calls and token usage while preserving QA quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) methods, including graph-based ones, struggle with complex questions that require symbolic operations like aggregation and exhaustive listing. Symbolic, KG-based approaches can support such operations via logic-predicate relations and SQL-like querying, but current pipelines rely heavily on LLMs for KG extraction, introducing ontology-inconsistent facts and high computational cost. There is a need for a more efficient and ontology-consistent KG construction process for robust QA.

Method: They introduce a neuro-symbolic KG construction framework that: (1) performs open-domain knowledge extraction to build an initial graph, (2) uses embedding-based canonicalization to normalize types and predicates according to an ontology, and (3) applies targeted LLM-based corrections only to fix detected ontology violations in a post-extraction stage. This design minimizes repeated LLM calls while enforcing ontology consistency in the resulting KG.

Result: The resulting knowledge graphs exhibit higher ontology consistency compared to baselines that use LLMs directly for extraction, while consuming substantially fewer LLM tokens due to deferred, targeted corrections. Despite these reductions, downstream QA performance is maintained, and analysis of SPARQL graph patterns shows that the KGs support symbolic querying structures useful for complex question answering.

Conclusion: A post-hoc, neuro-symbolic KG construction pipeline that combines embedding-based canonicalization with selective LLM corrections can efficiently produce ontology-grounded, symbolically queryable knowledge graphs. This approach cuts token costs, improves consistency, and still preserves the quality of complex question answering, demonstrating the practical advantages of ontology-aware KG construction for RAG-style systems.

Abstract: Question answering (QA) is a core challenge in AI, particularly for complex queries requiring multi-hop reasoning across documents, or symbolic operations like aggregation or exhaustive listing. Retrieval-augmented generation has become the dominant approach to QA, with recent graph-based variants addressing part of these issues by organizing knowledge to better support compositional questions. However, most textual graph-based RAG methods still lack the structure needed for symbolic operations useful to answer complex questions reliably. This motivates symbolic graph-based approaches, which extract knowledge graphs (KGs) whose relations are logic predicates that enable SQL-like querying. Yet these pipelines typically use LLMs for KG extraction, which can introduce consistency issues, where extracted facts may violate commonsense ontology constraints. We propose a neuro-symbolic framework for ontology-grounded KG construction combining open-domain extraction, embedding-based canonicalization of types and predicates, and targeted LLM-based correction of ontology violations. By deferring corrections to a post-extraction stage, our method avoids repeated LLM calls, substantially reducing token usage while improving KG consistency and preserving downstream QA quality. Finally, we show that the extracted KGs are well suited for symbolic querying by measuring the occurrence of SPARQL graph patterns.

</details>


### [158] [Paper Agents, Paper Gains: An Empirical Analysis of DeFi Investment Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29174)
*Jay Yu,Amy Zhao,Danning Sui*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes the current landscape and performance of DeFi investment agents—AI-driven on-chain trading systems—showing that they are early, heterogeneous, often not truly autonomous, and currently destroy value for most token holders despite large paper gains in agent treasuries and high token valuations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand whether AI-based DeFi investment agents are actually autonomous, profitable, and beneficial for users, given that their token valuations have rapidly exceeded USD 3 billion. The authors want to move beyond hype to empirically assess what is really happening on-chain, how these systems are architected, who captures value, and how immature this emerging market is.

Method: They survey over 1,900 AI-tagged crypto projects and narrow down to investment-focused agents, curating 10 representative projects across strategy and observability axes. They perform: (1) qualitative architectural analysis of two leading agent frameworks (ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol); and (2) quantitative on-chain analysis of 11 Solana-based AI agent treasuries with identifiable trading activity, covering 925,323 token holders, to measure autonomy, profit distribution, token valuations vs AUM, and user returns over time.

Result: (1) Many projects lack verifiable autonomous trade execution; many are simple API wrappers rather than fully autonomous agents. (2) Agent treasuries hold over USD 30M in paper gains, but token holders collectively lost USD 191.7M; gains are extremely concentrated, with the top 1% of wallets capturing 81.4% (USD 1.81B). (3) Token valuations are only weakly linked to treasury performance, with market-cap-to-AUM ratios often above 10,000x, far out of line with mature DeFi norms (<1x). (4) User gains initially reached USD 2.4B but subsequently reversed to net losses; median user returns are negative on every platform and tokens have fallen an average of 93% from their all-time highs.

Conclusion: Current DeFi investment agents represent a first-generation, permissionless experimentation phase: infrastructure enables fast deployment but lacks robust standards for autonomy, performance, and alignment, leading to speculative and often value-destructive outcomes for most participants. To guide evolution toward investment-grade systems, the authors propose a maturity framework with three dimensions—autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment—to evaluate and compare future AI agent deployments.

Abstract: DeFi investment agents, systems that use AI for autonomous on-chain trading, have attained over USD 3 billion in combined token valuations since late 2024. We survey over 1,900 AI-tagged crypto projects, filter to investment-focused agents, and curate 10 representative projects spanning strategy and observability dimensions. We then conduct a deep-dive architectural analysis of two prominent agent frameworks, ElizaOS and Virtuals Protocol, and a quantitative on-chain performance analysis of 11 Solana-based agent treasuries with publicly attributable trading activity, covering 925,323 token holders. We find that current deployments remain early and heterogeneous: (1) in our sample, many projects do not yet provide clear evidence of autonomous trade execution, and developer interviews suggest that many visible deployments remain basic API integrations; (2) agent treasuries retain over USD 30M in paper gains while token holders collectively lost USD 191.7M, with the top 1% of wallets capturing 81.4% of all gains (USD 1.81B); (3) token valuations are weakly connected to treasury fundamentals, with market-cap-to-AUM ratios exceeding 10,000x versus below 1x for established DeFi protocols; and (4) aggregate user gains peaked at USD 2.4B before declining to net losses, with median returns negative on every platform and tokens declining 93% on average from all-time highs. We interpret these outcomes as characteristic of a permissionless, first-generation market in which open infrastructure enables rapid experimentation but also allows naive or speculative agents to launch before robust standards for autonomy, performance, and stakeholder alignment emerge. We therefore propose a maturity framework along three dimensions: autonomous execution, risk-adjusted profitability, and stakeholder alignment, to characterize the gap between current deployments and future investment-grade agent systems.

</details>


### [159] [ReasonOps: Operator Segmentation for LLM Reasoning Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29192)
*Daniel Lee,Owen Queen,James Zou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ReasonOps, an unsupervised framework that discovers recurring reasoning operators in chain-of-thought traces from large language models and shows these operators can explain, characterize, and help predict model behavior and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Chain-of-thought traces from large reasoning models are long and complex, and current analysis tools are either inflexible or not expressive enough to capture their internal, cross-domain structure. There is a need for a universal, data-driven way to describe how LLMs reason step-by-step, compare models, and connect trace structure to performance and correctness.

Method: The authors propose ReasonOps, an unsupervised pipeline that segments chain-of-thought traces and clusters short sentence-initial token spans (3-token "pivots") to discover common reasoning operators. They apply this pipeline to 44,662 traces from 12 LLMs across 6 model families and 8 reasoning benchmarks. They then validate the discovered operators using independent LLM judges, analyze operator usage patterns on easy vs. hard problems, examine operator sequence distributions for model fingerprinting, and train classifiers using operator-based features to predict answer correctness and enable early quality estimation before a trace finishes.

Result: ReasonOps discovers seven recurring discourse-level reasoning operators (e.g., backtracking, inferring, hypothesizing) that appear consistently across all tested models and benchmarks. Independent LLM judges can reliably label new traces with these operators at 70–76% accuracy. Operator sequences uniquely identify model families, allowing a classifier using only operator distributions to recover the source model with high macro-AUC. Structural operator features predict within-problem correctness substantially better than baseline methods, and classifiers using operators achieve strong WP-AUC overall and on AIME. ReasonOps also supports early quality estimation, reaching competitive WP-AUC using just the first 50% of a trace.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that LLM chain-of-thought traces have a shared, compositional structure that can be captured via a small set of universal reasoning operators discovered in an unsupervised way. These operators provide interpretable building blocks for understanding and comparing LLM reasoning, enable model fingerprinting, and significantly improve prediction of answer correctness, including from partial traces. ReasonOps is presented as an annotation-free, scalable tool for analyzing and leveraging LLM reasoning behavior across models and tasks.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought traces from large reasoning models can span tens of thousands of tokens, yet we lack a vocabulary for describing their internal structure. Previous methods developed to analyze chain-of-thought traces are either too rigid or not expressive enough, failing to capture features across domains and models. To remedy this, we develop ReasonOps, an unsupervised, expressive method for annotating chain-of-thought traces, providing succinct universal operators. Using ReasonOps, we analyze 44,662 traces from 12 thinking LLMs spanning 6 families across 8 reasoning benchmarks and discover that they share a common compositional structure: 7 recurring reasoning operators -- discourse-level moves such as backtracking, inferring, and hypothesizing -- that emerge from unsupervised clustering of sentence-initial 3-token pivots. These operators appear across every model family and benchmark domain, confirmed by three independent LLM judges who classify held-out samples at 70 -76% accuracy. We analyze the structure of operators on easy vs. hard problems, revealing that reflective operators are more helpful on hard problems and harm performance on easy problems. Operator sequences are highly model-identifying: a classifier trained on operator distributions alone recovers the source model with macro-AUC, revealing that each model family has a distinctive reasoning fingerprint. Structural operator features predict within-problem answer correctness well above baselines. Classifiers built on these operators reach WP-AUC and on AIME specifically. ReasonOps further enables early quality estimation well before the trace completes: we predict at WP-AUC for only 50% of the trace. The ReasonOps pipeline is unsupervised and annotation-free, enabling deep insights into LLM reasoning traces as well as strong downstream results on model identification and correctness prediction.

</details>


### [160] [GTA: Generating Long-Horizon Tasks for Web Agents at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29218)
*Tenghao Huang,Kung-Hsiang Huang,Prafulla Kumar Choubey,Yilun Zhou,Muhao Chen,Jonathan May,Chien-Sheng Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents GTA, a scalable framework for generating realistic, multi-hop web navigation tasks and their executable trajectories, enabling better training and evaluation of web agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Progress in web agents is limited by the lack of scalable, fine-grained (process-level) supervision. Existing benchmarks are manually built, provide only coarse start-goal labels without intermediate steps, and recent automatic methods are costly, biased, and shallow. This hampers agents’ ability to generalize to realistic, multi-hop, cross-page tasks and prevents reliable evaluation.

Method: The authors design GTA, a task-generation pipeline that combines (1) large-scale crawling of websites, (2) retrieval-based seeding to select relevant pages, (3) in-context LLM-based generation of tasks and trajectories grounded in the site graph, and (4) automated quality control via deterministic replays and validation. Crawling is decoupled from task generation for efficiency, and tasks are constrained by the site’s link structure to enforce compositional, multi-hop navigation. The pipeline is instantiated over 50 diverse websites in multiple domains and languages.

Result: GTA produces a large set of realistic, executable web tasks with dense supervision (full trajectories) across more than 50 websites spanning e-commerce, government, forums, and news, including multilingual and multi-hop scenarios. The benchmark reveals a notable performance gap between humans and current web agents and supports granular diagnostic analysis of agent behavior.

Conclusion: The proposed GTA framework provides an efficient and validated way to automatically generate realistic multi-hop web-agent tasks with executable trajectories. By formalizing the task generation problem, offering a scalable pipeline, and releasing a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation, the work supplies crucial infrastructure for training and evaluating next-generation web agents and highlights substantial remaining headroom between human and agent performance.

Abstract: Web agents, which couple language models with browsing and tool-use capabilities, show promise as open web assistants. Yet progress is increasingly limited by the lack of scalable, process-level supervision. Existing benchmarks are largely manually constructed, providing only coarse start-goal annotations without intermediate trajectories, while recent automatic generation efforts remain expensive, biased, and shallow. These limitations prevent reliable training and evaluation of agents that must generalize to realistic, multi-hop, cross-page tasks. We introduce a scalable framework, GTA, that integrates crawling, retrieval-based seeding, in-context generation, and automated quality control to produce realistic tasks paired with executable trajectories. This design decouples crawling from generation for greater efficiency, grounds tasks in the site graph to enforce compositionality, and ensures dense supervision through deterministic replays and systematic validation. We instantiate the pipeline on over 50 websites covering e-commerce, government, forums, and news, with multilingual and multi-hop coverage. The resulting benchmark reveals a significant human-agent performance gap and enables detailed diagnostics. Our contributions are three-fold: (i) formalizing multi-hop web-agent task generation, (ii) proposing an efficient and validated pipeline for automatic data creation, and (iii) releasing a dynamic benchmark with reproducible evaluation.

</details>


### [161] [BenchTrace: A Benchmark for Testing Reflection Ability and Controlled Evolution in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29225)
*Jiahao Huang,Fei Cheng,Junfeng Jiang,Zefan Yu,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce BenchTrace, a benchmark to evaluate how well LLM agents can reflect on past failures and evolve, and show current agents perform poorly and forget lessons over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation of self-evolving LLM agents only looks at task scores and depends on agents’ own runs, so it can’t directly assess reflection quality or systematically probe specific failure modes. A new, controlled and targeted evaluation framework is needed.

Method: They build BenchTrace from 1,821 annotated agent episodes across six tasks, define two evaluations: Reflection Evaluation (targeted QA to test failure identification) and Evolution Evaluation (simulated self-evolution to examine if agents avoid past failures). They also propose the Failure Avoidance Rate (FAR) metric capturing how often the agent avoids a specific failure case.

Result: On BenchTrace, Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1 get under 30% pass rate on reflection, with diagnosis being the main weakness. Self-evolution improves FAR vs. a non-evolving baseline but suffers from forgetting as noisy episodes accumulate and from poor generalization of reflections, sometimes causing negative transfer across contexts. Only fully correct reflections correlate strongly with higher FAR.

Conclusion: BenchTrace reveals concrete weaknesses in current self-evolution methods—poor failure diagnosis, forgetting, and limited generalization—and offers a model-agnostic, controlled benchmark and FAR metric for precise, targeted assessment of self-evolving LLM agents.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents improve over time by reflecting on past failures, but existing evaluation is limited in two ways: it measures only task scores, leaving reflection quality unknown, and it relies on agents' own episode runs, offering no mechanism to target specific failure patterns. We present \textbf{BenchTrace}, a benchmark for evaluating self-evolution ability in LLM agents. BenchTrace is built on a snapshot-reflection dataset of 1,821 annotated episodes spanning six diverse tasks, and comprises a \textbf{Reflection Evaluation} that probes failure identification through targeted QA tasks, and an \textbf{Evolution Evaluation} that tests whether past failure experience translates into avoidance behavior in a controlled self-evolution simulation. Building on BenchTrace, we propose \textbf{failure avoidance rate (FAR)}, a new evaluation metric measuring the fraction of test cases in which the agent successfully avoids the target failure instance. Experiments with Qwen3-32B and GPT-4.1 reveal that both models fall below a 30\% end-to-end pass rate on reflection evaluation, with diagnosis as the primary bottleneck. Evolution evaluation shows that self-evolution methods generally improve FAR over the non-evolving baseline, but agents forget early lessons as noise episodes accumulate, and agents fail to generalize their reflections beyond the specific context, causing negative transfer across task contexts. Our correlation analysis further reveals that only a fully correct reflection is strongly associated with higher FAR. BenchTrace exposes concrete limits of current self-evolution approaches and provides a controlled, model-agnostic framework for targeted evaluation.

</details>


### [162] [Tailoring the Curriculum: Student-Centered Reasoning Distillation via Dynamic Data-Model Compatibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29229)
*Jiahao Huang,Fei Cheng,Junfeng Jiang,Akiko Aizawa*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Data-Model Compatibility (DMC) metric to evaluate how suitable a dataset is for reasoning distillation into a specific student model, and shows that using DMC for data selection—both static and dynamic—improves distilled reasoning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although reasoning distillation can transfer complex reasoning skills from large LLMs to smaller ones, its effectiveness heavily depends on how well the training data match the capabilities and needs of the student model. Existing approaches lack a principled way to quantify this alignment, often leading to suboptimal or trial-and-error data selection. The authors aim to provide a quantitative metric to systematically assess and choose datasets that best support reasoning distillation for a given student model.

Method: The authors define the Data-Model Compatibility (DMC) metric that jointly captures three aspects: (1) data quality; (2) relative difficulty of the data with respect to the student; and (3) the student model’s capability. They compute DMC for different datasets and student models, then conduct experiments where they (a) correlate DMC scores with actual reasoning distillation performance, and (b) use DMC as a criterion to select or prioritize datasets, both in static selection (fixed before training) and dynamic selection (updated as training progresses, since DMC changes over time).

Result: Empirically, DMC is strongly correlated with actual reasoning distillation outcomes across various student models and reasoning tasks, showing that higher DMC predicts better distillation performance. When DMC is used to select datasets for training, the resulting student models achieve better reasoning performance than baselines that do not use DMC-based selection. Furthermore, dynamically adapting dataset selection according to the evolving DMC during training yields additional performance gains beyond static DMC-based selection.

Conclusion: The proposed DMC metric is an effective tool for evaluating and selecting datasets for reasoning distillation into smaller LLMs. It captures the interaction between data characteristics and student model capability and serves both as a predictor of distillation success and as a practical guide for data selection. Using DMC—especially in a dynamic fashion during training—can systematically improve reasoning distillation performance across models and tasks.

Abstract: Reasoning distillation transfers complex reasoning abilities from large language models (LLMs) to smaller ones, yet its success depends on how well the training data align with the student model. This paper introduces the Data-Model Compatibility (DMC) metric, which can be used to assess the suitability of a dataset for reasoning distillation on a student model. DMC provides an assessment by jointly considering data quality, relative difficulty, and student capability. We validated the effectiveness of DMC from two perspectives: (1) DMC exhibits a strong correlation with reasoning distillation performance; and (2) using DMC as the criterion for data selection leads to improved reasoning distillation performance. Both findings are consistently demonstrated across multiple student models and tasks. Moreover, since the DMC of each dataset dynamically changes during training, our experiments demonstrate that dynamically selecting datasets based on DMC can further enhance performance.

</details>


### [163] [Rethinking Literature Search Evaluation: Deep Research Helps, and Human Citation Lists Are Not a Ground Truth](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29234)
*Gaurav Sahu,Laurent Charlin,Christopher Pal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes and evaluates a new large-scale literature search pipeline and shows that standard human reference lists are an imperfect evaluation gold standard.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing literature search tools and benchmarks rely heavily on human-curated reference lists, which may be incomplete, biased, and collaborator-heavy. Standard API-based search has low recall on realistic tasks, and current evaluation practices focus mainly on a single metric (recall vs. a human reference list) without probing other dimensions of citation quality.

Method: 1) Build a Deep Research retrieval pipeline that ingests the full text of a query paper, retrieves initial candidates via APIs, then recursively expands candidates by following their bibliographies in a breadth-first manner. Evaluate recall on a 250-paper benchmark (RollingEval-Jun25) against the paper’s true references. 2) Use an LLM-as-a-judge, configured to be neutral, to rate topical relevance of citations from humans vs. AI-based re-rankers. 3) Analyze co-authorship patterns on the OpenAlex author graph to measure how often citations go to direct collaborators, comparing humans and AI systems.

Result: The Deep Research pipeline increases recall on the RollingEval-Jun25 benchmark from below 20% (for standard API-only search) to above 80%. The LLM-judge finds that only about 51% of human references are at least moderately relevant, whereas 86–88% of citations selected by the best AI re-rankers reach that level of relevance. Graph analysis on OpenAlex shows humans are 2.5 times more likely than top AI systems to cite a direct collaborator, indicating substantial social or network bias in human citation behavior.

Conclusion: Human reference lists are not a clean, unbiased gold standard for evaluating literature search systems, and single-metric evaluations (e.g., pure recall vs. human references) are misleading. Effective assessment of literature-search quality should jointly consider recall, topical relevance, ranked-list diversity, and co-authorship distance. The proposed Deep Research pipeline substantially improves recall, and AI systems can produce citation sets that are, by several metrics, more topically relevant and less collaborator-biased than typical human references.

Abstract: We study large-scale literature search from two complementary angles: improving the retrieval pipeline, and stress-testing the human reference list as an evaluation target. First, we implement a Deep Research pipeline that processes the full query paper and expands the retrieved results breadth-first along their bibliographies, and show that it substantially outperforms vanilla API-only search, raising recall on RollingEval-Jun25 (a 250-paper literature-search benchmark) from below 20% to above 80%. Second, we use a neutral LLM-as-a-judge to determine if human references are sound ground truth for the task. We find significant limitations: only 51% of human citations are judged moderately relevant or higher, against 86--88% for the strongest AI-based re-rankers. We study this gap on the OpenAlex co-authorship graph, finding that humans are 2.5x more likely than the best AI re-rankers to cite a direct collaborator. Together, our results argue against single-axis literature-search evaluation: recall, topical-relevance scoring, ranked-list diversity, and a co-authorship-distance diagnostic each measure complementary properties of citation quality and should be reported jointly.

</details>


### [164] [Surfacing Isolated Learners with Outcome-Independent Mediation of Feedback between Teachers and Students Using AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29240)
*Junsoo Park,Youssef Medhat,Htet Phyo Wai,Ploy Thajchayapong,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces an interpretable decision layer for AI-augmented classrooms that ranks course topics needing attention using early, non-grade signals and shows that these rankings align well with instructor concerns and student-reported difficulties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In AI-augmented classrooms, there is a wealth of early feedback from teacher and student interactions before grades are available, but instructors lack clear, timely ways to translate these signals into actionable instructional decisions. Existing systems often depend on post-hoc outcome labels (like grades) or opaque algorithms, which limits their usefulness for real-time teaching adjustments and undermines trust and co-agency between humans and AI.

Method: The authors design a transparent decision layer that integrates three kinds of signals for each course topic: (1) prevalence of observed student learning difficulties, (2) disagreement between students’ self-reports and observed difficulties (indicating unrecognized or unreported struggles), and (3) unresolved teacher concerns. The mechanism outputs a ranked list of topics needing attention, along with per-topic decision records explaining why each topic is prioritized. They evaluate the approach in a graduate CS course via instructor interviews (n=5) and student surveys (n=279), and analyze correlations between the model’s rankings, instructor priorities, and student-reported difficulties, as well as learner-level detection performance and behavioral constructs (reflective thinking, help-seeking, self-efficacy).

Result: The ranked topic priorities strongly aligned with instructor concerns (3 of top 5 topics overlapped and Spearman ρ=0.80) and moderately aligned with student-reported topic difficulty (ρ=0.46, p=0.048). Integrating multiple signals improved identification of struggling learners compared to using gap prevalence alone (AUC=0.96 vs. 0.91). Additional analyses showed that behavioral signals such as reflective thinking, help-seeking, and self-efficacy were consistent with learning-related constructs, supporting the validity of the signals.

Conclusion: The study provides preliminary evidence that an interpretable, multi-signal decision layer can meaningfully prioritize course topics needing instructional attention before grades are available, while remaining transparent and aligned with instructor judgments and student perceptions. Such transparent coordination mechanisms can support human-AI co-agency by making AI recommendations more understandable and trustworthy when feedback is incomplete, potentially improving timely instructional interventions in AI-augmented classrooms.

Abstract: AI-augmented classrooms generate rich teacher and student feedback before graded outcomes become available, yet these signals can be difficult to translate into timely instructional decisions. We propose an interpretable decision layer: a transparent mechanism that ranks course topics requiring attention without using grades or post-hoc outcome labels. The approach combines three signals: student learning difficulty prevalence, disagreement between learner self-reports and observed difficulties, and unresolved teacher concerns. The output is a ranked set of topic priorities with per-topic decision records explaining each ranking. In one graduate CS course offering ($n=5$ instructor interviews; $n=279$ survey responses), prioritized topics aligned with instructor concerns (top-5 overlap 3/5; Spearman $ρ=0.80$) and student-reported topic difficulty ($ρ=0.46$, $p=.048$). Multi-signal integration also surfaced learners not identified through individual signal sources alone (AUC $=0.96$ vs. $0.91$ for gap prevalence alone). Reflective thinking, help-seeking, and self-efficacy provided additional evidence that student behavioral signals align with learning-related constructs. While preliminary, these findings suggest that transparent coordination mechanisms may help support human-AI co-agency when feedback is incomplete.

</details>


### [165] [DenseSteer: Steering Small Language Models towards Dense Math Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29247)
*Yang Ouyang,Shuhang Lin,Jung-Eun Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies a ‘dense reasoning’ pattern in strong LLMs and proposes DenseSteer, a training-free method that nudges small models toward this pattern at inference time, improving math reasoning without hurting likelihood.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Smaller language models (≤3B parameters) perform poorly on multi-step reasoning tasks compared to large LLMs, especially in mathematical reasoning. The authors want to understand what makes large models better reasoners and to exploit that insight to boost small models without expensive retraining.

Method: They empirically study the Qwen-2.5 model family on math benchmarks, analyzing internal reasoning traces and the structure of chain-of-thought. They discover that better reasoning correlates with fewer steps but more information per step, termed ‘dense reasoning’. Based on this, they design DenseSteer, an inference-time steering framework that adjusts internal representations of small models so that their reasoning pattern mimics this dense style. The method is training-free and operates purely at inference by modulating hidden states toward dense-reasoning directions.

Result: DenseSteer consistently improves accuracy of small models on math reasoning benchmarks. Importantly, these gains come without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), indicating that the method alters the structure of reasoning rather than just making outputs more verbose or less probable under the base model.

Conclusion: Dense reasoning—fewer but more information-dense steps—is a key characteristic of proficient CoT reasoning in LLMs. By steering small models’ internal representations toward this pattern at inference time, DenseSteer can substantially improve their mathematical reasoning ability without retraining or increasing NLL, suggesting a promising structural lever for enhancing reasoning in compact models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning abilities, while smaller models (<= 3B parameters) significantly underperform on multi-step reasoning tasks. Based on empirical analyses of the Qwen-2.5 model family on math reasoning benchmarks, we find that more proficient reasoning is associated with fewer reasoning steps but higher information density per step, a property we term Dense Reasoning. Motivated by this observation, we propose DenseSteer, a training-free inference-time steering framework that enhances small-model reasoning by modulating internal representations toward dense reasoning patterns. Experiments show that our method yields consistent accuracy improvements without increasing token-level Negative Log-Likelihood, highlighting dense reasoning as an effective structural approach to mathematical problem solving.

</details>


### [166] [Provably Secure Agent Guardrail](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29251)
*Benlong Wu,Weiming Zhang,Kejiang Chen,Han Fang,Nenghai Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new security paradigm for AI agents that constrains their actions using formal logic proofs instead of relying on natural-language guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As language models become powerful agents with broad execution capabilities, conventional semantic guardrails and probabilistic adjudicators cannot guarantee deterministic safety, especially under sophisticated adversarial attacks that exploit semantic ambiguity and symbol decoupling. There is a need for a defense paradigm that can offer formal, verifiable safety guarantees rather than empirical, heuristic protections.

Method: The authors introduce a security paradigm rooted in the inherent limits of logical reasoning and develop an executable Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework with a neural-symbolic isolation architecture. In this framework, agents must convert their intended actions into first-order logical constraints, and only actions that can be formally verified against safety properties are allowed. The design explicitly rejects semantic trust in raw natural language and uses formal verification to gate all physical operations.

Result: Through experiments on both macroscopic and microscopic two-dimensional dynamic adversarial systems, the proposed formal verification mechanism reportedly achieves zero attack success rate and zero false positives within the tested scenarios, while maintaining very low computational latency.

Conclusion: The work provides a conditional formal security foundation for AI agents under clear system assumptions, and introduces an engineering blueprint for building future intelligent systems whose core safety relies on provable, logic-based action constraints rather than empirical semantic guardrails.

Abstract: As large language models transition from bounded generative engines to agents with expansive execution privileges, AI going out of control precipitates a fundamental crisis in artificial intelligence security. Existing defense architectures heavily rely on empirical semantic guardrails and probabilistic large model adjudicators, mechanisms that fail to provide deterministic security lower bounds when facing complex semantic symbol decoupling attacks. To overcome this empirical semantic guardrail dilemma, this paper proposes a new security paradigm for agents based on the fundamental limitations of logical reasoning. Based on this paradigm, we further introduce an executable Proof-Constrained Action (ePCA) framework with a neural symbolic isolation architecture. This framework abandons semantic trust in natural language, forcing agents to losslessly formalize their intentions into first-order logical mathematical constraints before performing physical operations. Empirical evaluations of macroscopic and microscopic two-dimensional dynamic adversarial systems demonstrate that our formal verification mechanism achieves zero attack success rate and zero false positive rate across the evaluated scenarios, with extremely low computational latency. This research provides a conditional formal foundation under explicit system assumptions and an engineering paradigm for constructing the underlying defense foundation for future intelligent systems.

</details>


### [167] [OpenClawBench: Benchmarking Process-side Anomalies in Real-world Agent Execution Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29253)
*Yibing Liu,Yangze Liu,Xiaolong Yin,Bin Wang,Chong Zhang,Hao Yin,Zhongyi Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces OpenClawBench, a large-scale benchmark and taxonomy for detecting and supervising process-level anomalies in real-world agent executions, revealing that many seemingly successful tasks still contain significant procedural failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of agents often rely on task success judged by a final oracle, which can hide serious issues in how the task was completed. Agents may reach the correct final answer while having unresolved ambiguities, unsafe actions, ignored errors, poorly grounded decisions, or commitments that exceed their real capabilities. This mismatch between outcome and underlying process, termed the Outcome-Process Gap, poses a challenge for reliably deploying agents in real-world settings. The authors are motivated to make this gap observable, measurable, and actionable by turning execution logs into structured supervision for reliability monitoring and research.

Method: The authors construct OpenClawBench from BFCL-driven OpenClaw sessions generated by six different source models, resulting in 31,264 annotated agent trajectories. They align task-oracle judgments of success or failure with detailed, structured evidence about what happened during each execution. They then define FullTax, a comprehensive anomaly taxonomy and labeling scheme that converts these aligned trajectories into rich supervision signals: binary anomaly labels, supporting evidence snippets, temporal onset/span localization of anomalies, severity and recoverability annotations, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy that captures different types of process failures. Finally, they train a LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B model as an anomaly detector using a high-confidence subset of the FullTax-labeled data and evaluate it on a held-out test split.

Result: Analysis of OpenClawBench reveals that out of 31,135 executions that passed the task oracle, 2,904 (a substantial fraction) still contain process anomalies according to the FullTax labeling, empirically demonstrating the Outcome-Process Gap. The LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector trained on the high-confidence FullTax labels achieves a binary F1 score of 0.729 on a held-out test set with cleaner labels, indicating that process anomalies are detectable to a useful degree using the proposed supervision. The dataset and detector together show that process-side failures can be systematically identified even when final task outcomes appear successful.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that success-only evaluation is insufficient for assessing real-world agent reliability, as it misses a significant class of process-side failures captured by the Outcome-Process Gap. OpenClawBench, together with the FullTax schema, transforms raw agent execution logs into structured, auditable supervision that enables quantitative study, diagnosis, and operational monitoring of runtime agent behavior. The benchmark and initial detector results demonstrate that process anomalies are both prevalent and learnable, providing a foundation for more robust agent evaluation and oversight beyond final-task correctness.

Abstract: Task success can hide process anomalies in real-world agent executions. An agent may pass the final task oracle while still accumulating unresolved ambiguity, unsafe external writes, ignored errors, weakly grounded commitments, or capability-boundary overcommitment. We study this mismatch as the Outcome-Process Gap and introduce OpenClawBench, a large-scale dataset for measuring and supervising process-side anomalies in real agent execution processes. OpenClawBench is built from BFCL-driven OpenClaw sessions produced by 6 source models and contains 31,264 annotated trajectories. It aligns task-oracle outcomes with structured process evidence. FullTax converts the aligned trajectories into structured anomaly supervision: binary labels, supporting evidence, onset/span localization, severity, recoverability, and a 5-class anomaly taxonomy. Using OpenClawBench, we make the Outcome-Process Gap measurable. Among 31,135 oracle-passing executions, 2,904 are still labeled process-anomalous under FullTax. These results show that success-only evaluation misses a concrete class of process-side failures in real agent executions. A LoRA-fine-tuned Gemma 3 12B detector trained on the high-confidence FullTax supervised pool reaches binary F1=0.729 on the cleaner-labels held-out test split. Together, OpenClawBench turns real agent execution logs into auditable and reusable supervision for studying, diagnosing, and operationally monitoring runtime agent reliability.

</details>


### [168] [When and How Human Curation Backfires: Preference Alignment under Multi-Model Self-Consuming Loop](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29267)
*Yang Zhang,Xiukun Wei,Xueru Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper analyzes how multiple AI models that train on each other’s synthetic data behave over time, especially when one model’s data is curated by humans for alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As foundation models increasingly rely on synthetic data, there is a risk of model collapse and misalignment. Previous work considered only a single model training on its own outputs, showing that human curation can keep it aligned. In reality, many models interact and train on each other’s outputs. The authors are motivated to understand how such multi-model interactions change the dynamics of alignment and whether human curation remains beneficial in this more realistic setting.

Method: The authors formalize a dynamical systems framework for multiple interacting self-consuming models, where each model’s future behavior depends on synthetic data produced either by itself or other models. Within this framework, they derive conditions for convergence to stable points of behavior. They then analyze the effect of adding human curation to one model’s training data, quantifying how this curation changes that model’s alignment (self-influence) and how these changes propagate through the network of models (cross-influence).

Result: They characterize when the system of interacting models converges to a stable state and show that, unlike the single-model case, human curation of one model does not necessarily improve long-term alignment for all models. Cross-model interactions can attenuate the benefits of curation or even reverse them, causing overall alignment to degrade over time despite human intervention in one part of the system.

Conclusion: Human curation is not guaranteed to improve alignment in ecosystems of interacting, self-consuming models. The collective dynamics can dampen or invert the beneficial effects observed in isolated models, meaning that alignment strategies must account for multi-model interactions rather than treating each model in isolation.

Abstract: Foundation models are increasingly trained on synthetic data generated by prior model iterations rather than exclusively on real data. This self-consuming training paradigm can lead to model collapse, divergence, or bias amplification. Recent work (Ferbach et al., 2024) shows that incorporating human curation into the loop can steer a self-consuming model toward human-aligned behavior, but these analyses focus on a single, isolated model that solely consumes its own outputs. In practice, however, models often interact and train on input-output pairs produced by other models. This paper studies self-consuming training in the multi-model regime. We first formalize a framework for interacting self-consuming models and characterize when the resulting dynamical system converges to a stable point. We then examine how human curation of one model affects its own alignment (self-influence) and how such effects propagate to other models (cross-influence). Unlike isolated settings where human curation always enhances model alignment, we show that cross-model interactions can dampen or even invert this effect, ultimately degrading long-term alignment.

</details>


### [169] [Indexing the Unreadable: LLM-Native Recursive Construction and Search of Service Taxonomies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29270)
*Wei Zheng,Yang Yan,Yiyang Shao,Jinyang Li,Zeze Chang,Yukuang Jia,Qiming Mao,Chihyung Wang,Jingbin Zhou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes A2X, an LLM-native, progressive-disclosure service discovery system for the Internet of Agents that hierarchically organizes and incrementally explores services, overcoming context-window limits and improving retrieval accuracy and token efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In the emerging Internet of Agents, LLM agents must select and orchestrate appropriate services (MCP servers, A2A endpoints, skills, tools) from a rapidly expanding registry. Directly feeding thousands of service descriptions into an LLM prompt is infeasible due to context-window limits and the Lost-in-the-Middle effect, causing poor attention to relevant information and inefficient token use.
