Existing approaches like full-context dumping or naive retrieval do not scale and waste context. A more scalable, LLM-native context management and service discovery mechanism is needed.

Method: The paper introduces a progressive-disclosure scheme for context management and instantiates it as A2X, an LLM-driven service discovery pipeline. A2X automatically organizes registered services into a hierarchical taxonomy. At query time, the LLM walks this taxonomy layer by layer, progressively narrowing down the candidate set so that each LLM call only sees a small, highly relevant subset of services. This decouples effective-context usage from the total registry size and reduces prompt length. The taxonomy construction and traversal are performed by the LLM, aligning the discovery mechanism with the LLM’s strengths.

Result: Empirically, A2X outperforms both a full-context baseline (which dumps all service descriptions into the prompt) and an open-source embedding-based retrieval baseline. Against full-context dumping, A2X improves Hit Rate by 6.2 points while using only about one-ninth of the prompt tokens. Compared with the state-of-the-art open-source embedding-based method, A2X achieves more than a 20-point improvement in Hit Rate, demonstrating both better retrieval effectiveness and substantial token savings.

Conclusion: A2X demonstrates that LLM-native, progressive-disclosure schemes can effectively address context scarcity and Lost-in-the-Middle issues in large service registries for the Internet of Agents. By hierarchically organizing services and performing layer-wise traversal, the approach scales service discovery without linearly increasing context consumption, simultaneously improving retrieval accuracy and token efficiency. This suggests that similar hierarchical, LLM-steered context management strategies may be key infrastructure for scalable multi-agent and tool-based LLM ecosystems.

Abstract: The era of the Internet of Agents (IoA) is taking shape: LLM agents are expected to fulfill user goals by orchestrating fast-growing populations of Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers, Agent-to-Agent (A2A) endpoints, reusable skills, and other LLM-callable services. Yet LLMs face a structural mismatch with this regime: effective context is a scarce resource that does not scale with the number of services. Concatenating thousands of service descriptions into a prompt overflows the context window, and even when the window is large enough, models systematically under-attend to information in the middle of long inputs, the well-documented Lost-in-the-Middle phenomenon. This is fundamentally a question of context management for service discovery. To address this, we propose an LLM-native progressive-disclosure scheme and its concrete instantiation, A2X (Agent-to-Anything service discovery): an LLM-driven pipeline that automatically organizes the registered services into a hierarchical taxonomy and walks it layer by layer at query time, so that every LLM call sees only a small candidate set highly relevant to the user query. This decouples effective-context scarcity from registry size and significantly reduces token consumption while improving retrieval accuracy. Compared to full-context dumping, A2X achieves a 6.2-point Hit Rate gain at one-ninth the prompt-token cost; compared to the state-of-the-art open-source embedding-based baseline, A2X improves Hit Rate by more than 20 points.

</details>


### [170] [CoHyDE: Iterative Co-Training of LLM Rewriter & Dense Encoder for Tool Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29271)
*Vaishali Senthil,Ashutosh Hathidara,Sebastian Schreiber*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoHyDE is a co-training framework that jointly optimizes an encoder and an LLM query rewriter to improve tool/API retrieval, especially for vague, colloquial queries.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Tool retrieval for LLM agents is hard because user queries are colloquial and underspecified, while API catalogs are written in technical language. Existing approaches—contrastive encoder fine-tuning and HyDE-style query expansion—perform well in different regimes but fail when queries are either too mismatched or already well-formed, leaving a gap in robust retrieval across query types.

Method: CoHyDE iteratively co-trains two components: (1) a dense encoder retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style hypothetical descriptions generated by an LLM-based query rewriter; and (2) the LLM rewriter, which is preference-aligned via DPO based on the encoder’s retrieval scores. Both encoder and rewriter are warm-started on the tool catalog before running several rounds of this co-evolution loop.

Result: On a ~10k-tool subset of ToolBench, three CoHyDE rounds outperform the strongest single-method baseline by +2.5 percentage points NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 points on held-out vague queries, with gains up to +8 points on the hardest vague queries. Ablation studies show that using either the encoder or the rewriter alone cannot reach CoHyDE’s performance, especially on vague queries where performance can drop by up to 8 points.

Conclusion: Joint, iterative co-training of an encoder and an LLM rewriter (CoHyDE) yields significantly more robust tool retrieval across both well-formed and vague queries than either contrastive encoder tuning or standalone HyDE-style expansion. The co-evolution between retrieval and rewriting, rather than either component alone, is the crucial factor driving the performance gains.

Abstract: Tool retrieval over large API catalogs is a core bottleneck for LLM agents: user queries arrive in colloquial, often underspecified language, while the catalog uses technical API vocabulary that no fixed encoder can bridge on its own. The two dominant training approaches, contrastive encoder fine-tuning and HyDE-style query expansion with a frozen LLM, address this problem from opposite ends and fail in complementary directions: the fine-tuned encoder excels when the query's surface form already matches the catalog but collapses when it does not, while zero-shot HyDE is more robust to underspecified queries yet generates catalog-unaware hypothetical descriptions that degrade retrieval when queries are well-formed. We introduce CoHyDE, an iterative procedure that trains the dense encoder and the LLM rewriter as a single co-evolving system: the encoder is retrained with InfoNCE on catalog-style hypothetical descriptions produced by the rewriter, and the rewriter is preference-aligned via DPO against the encoder's retrieval scores, with both sides warm-started on the tool catalog before the loop begins. On a ~10k tool subset of the ToolBench catalog, three rounds of CoHyDE improve over the strongest single-component baseline by +2.5 pp NDCG@5 on standard queries and +6.3 pp on held-out vague queries, with gains as large as +8 pp on the hardest vague tier. Ablations confirm that co-training is the key ingredient: using either component in isolation fails to match CoHyDE on both well-formed and vague queries, with losses of up to -8 pp on vague queries.

</details>


### [171] [Diagnosing Harmful Continuation in Answer-Correct Long-CoT Training Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29288)
*Chen He,Yuhao Wu,Lei Wang,Wenxuan Zhang,Fumin Shen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that extra reasoning steps in long chain-of-thought traces, after the correct answer is already reached, can actually hurt fine-tuning, and proposes ways to identify and cut these harmful suffixes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long chain-of-thought rationales are widely used to fine-tune reasoning LLMs, but even when answers are correct, the resulting models can differ a lot. The authors suspect that the part of the reasoning that comes after the answer is already sufficiently justified might be responsible for some of this variation and harm, so they want to isolate and study it.

Method: They define and study "post-conclusion continuation": the portion of an answer-correct CoT that occurs after the answer is already well supported, yet is still included in the supervised target. Using a delete-only editor, they construct answer-preserving suffix removals to remove such continuations and run comparative CoT-based supervised fine-tuning on original vs. truncated traces. They also analyze these removed continuations via measures of predictive uncertainty and hidden-state geometry, and design a lightweight boundary proxy called Harmful Continuation Cut (HCC) that approximates the editor-found boundary.

Result: Models fine-tuned on CoT traces with the identified post-conclusion continuation removed perform better than those trained on the original full traces, so this continuation is empirically harmful in their SFT setting. The removed segments exhibit persistent local uncertainty and reduced progress toward the final-answer representation in hidden-state space, indicating an "uncertainty--geometry mismatch." The proposed HCC proxy approximately recovers the beneficial cut without needing the heavy editor procedure.

Conclusion: Not all parts of long CoT supervision are beneficial: the post-conclusion continuation segment, even in answer-correct traces, can be harmful to SFT. By characterizing this segment and proposing a practical proxy (HCC) for cutting it, the paper suggests that carefully trimming CoT data can improve the training of reasoning-oriented LLMs and sheds light on the dynamics of uncertainty and representation geometry during reasoning.

Abstract: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) traces are widely used as supervision for reasoning-oriented LLM SFT, yet answer-correct traces can still lead to markedly different fine-tuning outcomes. We study post-conclusion continuation in answer-correct long-CoT data: a continuation where the answer appears sufficiently supported, but the trace continues with additional reasoning that remains in the supervised target. To test its training effect, we use a delete-only editor to construct answer-preserving suffix removal and compare CoT-based SFT on the original and processed traces. We observe improved SFT outcomes after removing the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation, suggesting that this continuation is harmful to training in our setting. We therefore refer to this empirically supported phenomenon as harmful continuation. Beyond this intervention, we further characterize the removed post-conclusion continuation through uncertainty and hidden-state progress. We observe persistent local uncertainty together with weakened terminal-directional progress, forming an uncertainty--geometry mismatch. Finally, we instantiate Harmful Continuation Cut (HCC), a lightweight boundary proxy that approximates the editor-identified post-conclusion continuation boundary.

</details>


### [172] [Entropy-KL Divergence-based Token Masking: A Novel Approach for Selective Fine-tuning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29303)
*Qi Liu,Mingdi Sun,Yongyi He,Zhi Zheng,Tong Xu,Yi Zheng,Zhefeng Wang,Enhong Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a new fine-tuning method (EKSFT) for low-data settings that masks high-uncertainty or distribution-shifting tokens during supervised fine-tuning, improving both direct performance and subsequent RL training for LLMs, especially on math reasoning tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SFT+RL pipelines for LLM post-training often use very little SFT data, leading the model to overfit to small datasets and drift away from its pretrained distribution. This distribution shift harms exploration and effectiveness during the later RL phase, especially in tasks like mathematical reasoning where high-quality exploration is key. They want an SFT procedure that activates relevant capabilities without memorizing specific, uncertain, or distribution-misaligned content in low-data regimes.

Method: They introduce EKSFT (Entropy-KL Selective Fine-Tuning). For each training token, they compute both the entropy of the model’s predictive distribution and the KL divergence between this distribution and that of a reference model. Tokens with high entropy (uncertain predictions) or high KL (large deviation from the reference/pretrained distribution) are selectively masked out from the supervised loss. This way, SFT focuses learning on low-uncertainty, distribution-consistent parts of the data, injecting task-relevant knowledge while avoiding strong updates on tokens that would cause harmful distribution shift. Then RL is applied starting from the EKSFT-tuned model.

Result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, models trained with EKSFT outperform those using standard SFT under comparable conditions. When further fine-tuned with RL, EKSFT-initialized models achieve consistently better post-RL performance than standard-SFT-initialized models, suggesting that EKSFT preserves better exploration behavior for RL. The paper provides empirical evidence that selectively masking high-entropy/KL tokens during SFT improves both immediate task performance and the effectiveness of subsequent RL training.

Conclusion: EKSFT offers a simple, effective modification to SFT for low-data post-training of LLMs: avoid imitating tokens that are highly uncertain or diverge heavily from a reference distribution. This reduces harmful distribution shift, preserves pretrained strengths, and creates a better starting point for RL. The approach yields consistent gains on math reasoning tasks and leads to more effective RL fine-tuning, indicating that capability activation rather than content memorization is key for SFT in low-data regimes.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) followed by reinforcement learning (RL) has become a standard post-training paradigm for large language models. This paradigm provides a cold-start for RL exploration, avoiding the inefficiency of pure RL where on-policy sampling yields insufficient positive samples. However, in practice, existing approaches often use a small amount of data for SFT initialization compared to the RL phase, which can cause the model to fit the limited samples and shift away from its pre-trained distribution. This distribution shift impedes the model's ability to effectively explore during subsequent RL training. To address this challenge, we propose that in low-data regimes, SFT should prioritize activating task-relevant capabilities rather than memorizing specific content. Along this line, we propose EKSFT (Entropy-KL Selective Fine-Tuning), which selectively masks tokens that exhibit either high entropy or high KL divergence from a reference model. By excluding these high-uncertainty, distribution-shifting tokens from imitation, EKSFT injects task-specific knowledge while preserving the integrity of the model's pre-trained distribution. Empirical evaluations on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that EKSFT consistently outperforms standard SFT. Further RL fine-tuning from the EKSFT model yields consistently better post-RL performance, indicating improved exploration for the RL stage. Our codes and datasets are available at https://github.com/MINE-USTC/EKSFT.

</details>


### [173] [Rubric-Guided Process Reward for Stepwise Model Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29310)
*Shenghao Ye,Yu Guo,Zhengheng Li,Shuangwu Chen,Jian Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RoRo introduces rubric-guided process rewards for stepwise model routing in Large Reasoning Models, improving accuracy–cost trade-offs over outcome-only RL routing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing stepwise model routing methods for LRMs use reinforcement learning with only final outcome rewards, which evaluate correctness of the final answer but ignore the quality of intermediate routing decisions. This can weaken both performance and generalization, especially when fine-grained control over computation cost and reasoning quality is needed. The authors aim to provide more informative feedback for each routing step to achieve better accuracy–cost efficiency.

Method: RoRo is a rubric-guided process reward framework. It first collects diverse routing trajectories (sequences of model choices over reasoning steps) and constructs preference pairs that jointly consider final outcome, computation cost, and process quality. Then it alternately trains two components: (1) a Rubricor that generates a query-specific evaluation rubric describing what constitutes good routing behavior, and (2) a Judge that scores routing trajectories using this rubric. The scores serve as process-level rewards. These process rewards are combined with conventional outcome rewards and used to optimize the routing policy with GRPO (a policy optimization algorithm).

Result: On five reasoning benchmarks, evaluated under both same-family (models from the same family) and cross-family (heterogeneous models) routing settings, RoRo consistently outperforms strong baseline routing methods. It achieves higher task accuracy while also better controlling or reducing computation cost, thereby offering improved accuracy–cost trade-offs compared with outcome-only reward approaches.

Conclusion: Incorporating rubric-guided process rewards into stepwise model routing helps overcome the limitations of outcome-only supervision in LRMs. By explicitly evaluating the quality of intermediate routing decisions via a Rubricor and Judge, RoRo enables more efficient and effective routing policies, leading to consistent gains in accuracy and cost-efficiency across diverse reasoning benchmarks and model-family settings.

Abstract: Stepwise model routing improves the efficiency of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) by assigning each reasoning step to a suitable model. Recent methods formulate routing as a sequential decision process and train the router with reinforcement learning. However, although they model routing as a process, they still supervise the router with outcome rewards. Such rewards only reflect final answer correctness and fail to evaluate intermediate routing decisions, which can weaken performance and generalization. To address this gap, we propose RoRo, a rubric-guided process reward framework for stepwise model routing. RoRo first collects diverse routing trajectories and constructs preference pairs based on outcome, cost, and process quality. It then trains a Rubricor to generate a query-specific evaluation rubric and a Judge to score routing trajectories under this rubric through alternating optimization. The resulting process rewards are combined with outcome rewards to optimize the routing policy via GRPO. Experiments on five reasoning benchmarks under both same-family and cross-family settings show that RoRo consistently outperforms strong baselines and achieves better accuracy and cost trade-offs.

</details>


### [174] [ConMoE: Expert-Pool Consolidation via Prototype Reassignment for MoE Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29350)
*Yilun Yao,Jiaming Pan,Elsie Dai,Peizhuang Cong,Yaoming Li,Tong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce ConMoE, a train-free method to compress Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models by consolidating experts into a smaller prototype pool while preserving routing behavior, achieving strong performance compared to pruning/merging baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MoE language models lower compute but still demand large memory because all experts must be stored and served. Existing compression mainly focuses on pruning or merging experts, which may hurt performance or require extra training. The paper aims to reduce memory with minimal or no retraining, while preserving the MoE routing interface and effectiveness.

Method: They reframe post-training MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation: keep a smaller set of pretrained experts as shared prototypes and deterministically map each original expert call to a chosen prototype, without changing router behavior. The proposed ConMoE framework is train-free: it (1) scores experts using calibration-based signals of contribution and replaceability, (2) selects a subset as prototypes, and (3) rewires original expert references to these prototypes, optionally allowing sharing within layers and exploring cross-layer sharing and weight fusion, all without further fine-tuning.

Result: On three pretrained MoE LMs (deepseek-moe-16b-base, Qwen3-30B-A3B, OLMoE-1B-7B-0125), ConMoE matches or outperforms strong pruning and merging compression baselines at various levels of expert reduction. It achieves the best average performance on deepseek-moe-16b-base at both 25% and 50% reduction in routed experts, and remains competitive on the other models. Ablation studies show which design choices contribute most to stability and performance.

Conclusion: Viewing MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation enables a simple, training-free compression strategy. ConMoE can substantially cut the number of routed experts (and thus memory cost) while preserving or improving performance over prior pruning/merging methods, with deterministic reassignment being especially robust, while more aggressive sharing and fusion bring model-dependent benefits.

Abstract: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models reduce per-token computation but still require storing and serving all experts, making deployment memory-intensive. Existing post-training compression methods mainly shrink this cost by pruning experts or merging their weights. We formulate post-training MoE compression as expert-pool consolidation: retaining a smaller set of pretrained experts as reusable prototypes and deterministically remapping each original expert reference to one selected prototype. This view separates the reduced expert pool from the reuse structure that represents the original expert slots, and allows prototype sharing within local layer scopes while preserving the original router interface. We propose ConMoE, a train-free prototype remapping framework that selects retained experts using calibration-based contribution and replaceability signals, then redirects original expert calls to the selected prototypes without weight updates or post-compression fine-tuning. Experiments on three pretrained MoE language models show that ConMoE matches or outperforms strong pruning and merging baselines in several settings, achieving the best average score on deepseek-moe-16b-base at both 25% and 50% routed-expert reduction, while remaining competitive on Qwen3-30B-A3B and OLMoE-1B-7B-0125. Ablations indicate that deterministic reassignment is the most stable component, whereas broader cross-layer sharing and post-hoc weight fusion are model-dependent.

</details>


### [175] [PassNet: Scaling Large Language Models for Graph Compiler Pass Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29357)
*Yiqun Liu,Yingsheng Wu,Ruqi Yang,Enrong Zheng,Honglei Qiu,Sijun He,Tai Liang,Jingjing Wu,Yuhan Zhou,Yiwei Zhang,Dongyan Chen,Weihan Yi,Xinqi Li,Siqi Bao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PassNet introduces an LLM-driven framework for generating compiler passes that optimize tensor computation graphs, targeting long-tail workloads where existing compilers underperform.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although modern tensor compilers like TorchInductor speed up mainstream models, they often degrade performance on many real-world, less-common subgraphs. Existing LLM-based approaches mostly focus on generating individual kernels, which does not integrate well with compiler pipelines or address graph-level optimization needs. There is a need for a scalable, principled way to use LLMs to generate structured compiler passes that systematically improve performance on these long-tail cases, while measuring both correctness and speed.

Method: The authors build PassNet, an ecosystem for LLM-based compiler pass generation. It includes PassNet-Dataset, a collection of over 18K unique computational graphs from about 100K real-world models, and PassBench, a benchmark of 200 challenging long-tail fusible tasks (2,060 subgraphs). They define an Error-aware Speedup Score (ES_t) that combines correctness, stability, and performance into a single metric and design layered integrity defenses to prevent LLMs from exploiting the benchmark. They then use LLMs to author structured graph transformation passes that plug into existing compiler pipelines and evaluate their performance against TorchInductor and across models. They also fine-tune a smaller LLM on ~4K optimization trajectories from PassNet to test data efficiency and performance gains.

Result: PassBench effectively differentiates model capabilities and remains far from saturated: the best frontier LLM still underperforms TorchInductor by 37% on aggregate performance. However, on individual subgraphs, LLM-generated passes can deliver up to 3x speedup relative to TorchInductor, showing strong point-wise optimization capability. Fine-tuning a smaller LLM on around 4K PassNet trajectories yields a 2.67x improvement, bringing it close to the performance of the larger frontier models. This suggests substantial performance headroom and demonstrates that consistency and reliability, not raw optimization ability, are the primary bottlenecks.

Conclusion: LLMs are well-suited to generating compiler passes—structured graph transformations integrated into compilation pipelines—rather than just standalone kernels. PassNet, consisting of a large real-world dataset, a challenging benchmark, and a combined correctness-performance metric, provides an effective infrastructure for advancing LLM-driven compiler optimization. The results show that while current LLMs are not consistently better than state-of-the-art compilers overall, they can significantly outperform them on specific subgraphs, and targeted fine-tuning markedly closes the gap, indicating strong potential for future improvements. All associated resources are publicly released to support continued research.

Abstract: Modern tensor compilers such as TorchInductor deliver substantial speedups on mainstream models, yet face a systematic performance ceiling on long-tail workloads -- our profiling shows that 43% of real-world subgraphs experience end-to-end slowdowns under default compilation. While LLMs offer a path toward automated optimization, existing efforts focus on standalone kernel generation. We argue that pass generation -- where LLMs author structured graph transformations that integrate directly into compiler pipelines -- is the more appropriate abstraction. We propose PassNet, the first large-scale ecosystem for LLM-based compiler pass generation, comprising: (1) PassNet-Dataset, over 18K unique computational graphs from 100K real-world models; and (2) PassBench, 200 curated long-tail fusible tasks (comprising 2,060 subgraphs in total) evaluated under the Error-aware Speedup Score (ES_t) -- a metric unifying correctness, stability, and performance -- with layered integrity defenses against systematic LLM exploitation. Experiments reveal that PassBench is both highly discriminative and genuinely unsaturated: the best frontier model trails TorchInductor by 37% in aggregate, yet on individual subgraphs LLMs achieve up to 3x speedup over the same compiler -- indicating that the bottleneck is consistency, not capability. Fine-tuning a small model on merely ~4K PassNet trajectories yields a 2.67x improvement approaching frontier-model performance, demonstrating substantial headroom and validating PassNet as live training infrastructure for advancing LLM-driven compiler optimization. All data, benchmarks, and tooling are publicly available.

</details>


### [176] [Scaling Monosemanticity: Extracting Interpretable Features from Claude 3 Sonnet](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29358)
*Adly Templeton,Tom Conerly,Jonathan Marcus,Jack Lindsey,Trenton Bricken,Brian Chen,Adam Pearce,Craig Citro,Emmanuel Ameisen,Andy Jones,Hoagy Cunningham,Nicholas L Turner,Callum McDougall,Monte MacDiarmid,Alex Tamkin,Esin Durmus,Tristan Hume,Francesco Mosconi,C. Daniel Freeman,Theodore R. Sumers,Edward Rees,Joshua Batson,Adam Jermyn,Shan Carter,Chris Olah,Tom Henighan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that sparse autoencoders can learn interpretable features from a large, real-world language model (Claude 3 Sonnet), scaling interpretability methods beyond small models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether dictionary learning and sparse autoencoders, which have worked on small transformers, can scale to production-scale language models and yield useful, interpretable internal features, including those related to potentially harmful behaviors.

Method: Train very large sparse autoencoders (up to 34M features) on the middle-layer residual stream of Claude 3 Sonnet, using empirically derived scaling laws to choose hyperparameters; analyze the resulting learned features for interpretability, multilingual and multimodal behavior, causal impact on model outputs, and their geometric and computational properties.

Result: They obtain a large set of multilingual, multimodal, and semantically meaningful features (e.g., famous entities, locations, sarcasm, coding errors) that activate on both concrete examples and abstract mentions. Manipulating these features can steer model behavior in ways consistent with their interpretations, including for features tied to deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, and bias. They also characterize aspects of feature geometry and computational roles, but find that coverage is incomplete and interpretability is imperfect.

Conclusion: Sparse autoencoders can scale to large production LMs and produce causally meaningful, interpretable features that can be used to steer behavior, including around risky concepts. Yet, coverage gaps and the lack of rigorous faithfulness metrics mean the method is still far from a complete solution for understanding or controlling model internals.

Abstract: We demonstrate that sparse autoencoders can extract interpretable features from Claude 3 Sonnet, a production-scale language model, addressing the open question of whether dictionary learning methods scale beyond small transformers. We trained sparse autoencoders with up to 34 million features on the model's middle layer residual stream, using scaling laws to guide hyperparameter selection. The resulting features are multilingual and multimodal (generalizing to images despite text-only training), respond to both concrete instances and abstract discussions of concepts, and can be used to steer model behavior in ways consistent with their interpretations. We find features corresponding to famous entities and locations, as well as more abstract concepts like sarcasm or errors in code. We also identify features relevant to ways in which language models might cause harm--including features representing deception, power-seeking, sycophancy, and bias--and show that these causally influence model outputs when manipulated. Additionally, we conduct analyses of feature interpretability, geometry, and computational function. However, significant limitations remain: our suite of features is incomplete, and we lack rigorous methods for evaluating whether our features faithfully capture model computations.

</details>


### [177] [MiraBench: Evaluating Action-Conditioned Reliability in Robotic World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29360)
*Tianzhuo Yang,Zihan Shen,Zirui Mi,Zhaoyi Zhang,Jiayi Zhou,Jiaming Ji,Juntao Dai,Jiawei Chen,Boyuan Chen,Yaodong Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces MiraBench, a benchmark to evaluate how reliably action-conditioned world models follow physics and commanded actions, and how often they are over-optimistic about success.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of robotic world models focus mostly on visual quality of predicted futures, not on whether those predictions are physically plausible, respect the input actions, and correctly represent failures when actions should fail. This gap makes it unclear whether such models are trustworthy simulators for robot learning.

Method: The authors design MiraBench as a hierarchical benchmark with three levels: Physics Adherence (reference-free checks of physical consistency), Action-Following Fidelity (whether predictions follow task-relevant actions), and Optimism Bias Detection (how often models predict success under failure-inducing actions). They curate a human-annotated corpus with 16,000+ judgments and use it to evaluate 12 model configurations, including different conditioning modalities (vector, text), open vs closed models, and multiple scales.

Result: Using MiraBench, they find: (1) visual fidelity of predictions correlates poorly with correct action following; (2) simply scaling up models does not reliably improve how well they follow actions; and (3) optimism bias—predicting success even when actions should fail—is widespread across evaluated systems.

Conclusion: MiraBench shifts the evaluation focus for robotic world models from appearance to action-conditioned reliability, offering a diagnostic tool to assess and improve these models as faithful, physically grounded simulators for robot learning.

Abstract: Action-conditioned world models are increasingly used as scalable simulators for robot learning, yet current evaluations provide limited evidence that their predictions are reliable under the actions they condition on. Existing benchmarks largely emphasize visual fidelity, leaving unclear whether predicted futures are physically plausible, faithful to commanded actions, and calibrated to failure when actions should not succeed. We introduce \textsc{MiraBench}, a hierarchical benchmark that defines \emph{action-conditioned reliability} as a core evaluation target for robotic world models. MiraBench decomposes this target into three progressively demanding levels: \emph{Physics Adherence}, which evaluates reference-free physical consistency; \emph{Action-Following Fidelity}, which measures whether predictions respect task-relevant action inputs; and \emph{Optimism Bias Detection}, which probes the tendency to predict successful outcomes under failure-inducing actions. To support this evaluation, we curate a human-annotated corpus with over 16,000 judgments across tasks, failure categories, and leading world models. We evaluate 12 representative model configurations spanning vector-conditioned robotic world models, text-conditioned generative world models, open-weight systems, closed-source systems, and multiple model scales. Across this broad model landscape, MiraBench reveals three central findings: visual fidelity is a poor proxy for action fidelity; increasing model scale does not reliably improve action following; and optimism bias is pervasive across current systems. By shifting evaluation from appearance to action-conditioned reliability, MiraBench provides a diagnostic foundation for assessing and improving robotic world models as faithful simulators.

</details>


### [178] [EvoMD-LLM: Learning the Language of Species Evolution in Reactive Molecular Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29394)
*Zhichen Tang,Zhengzheng Dang,Yulin Chen,Jixin Wu,Haiwen Li,Yanming Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces EvoMD-LLM, which reformulates reactive molecular dynamics simulations as a symbolic temporal language modeling task so that standard LLMs can learn and predict the evolution of chemical species over time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing large language models are strong at static scientific reasoning but weak at capturing temporal structures in dynamic physical processes such as molecular dynamics. Traditional MD models or sequence models struggle with compositional, long-horizon evolution and often generate invalid or hallucinated molecular states. There is a need for a framework that grounds LLMs in time-evolving physical simulations while reducing invalid outputs and leveraging their reasoning capabilities.

Method: EvoMD-LLM converts reactive molecular dynamics trajectories into sequences of discrete molecular events. Each token corresponds to a chemical species plus an associated persistence duration, turning the trajectory into a symbolic temporal sequence. A temporal scaffolding mechanism explicitly encodes event duration as a token, providing inductive bias for time-aware predictions. Standard autoregressive LLMs are then fine-tuned on these event sequences to model the compositional evolution of species over time. The authors compare this to typical sequence modeling and sequential neural network baselines on temporal prediction tasks.

Result: On multiple temporal prediction benchmarks derived from reactive MD, EvoMD-LLM attains up to 66.14% prediction accuracy and consistently outperforms sequential neural network and language-based baselines. Temporal scaffolding notably reduces the rate of invalid or hallucinated molecular outputs compared with conventional approaches. Qualitatively, the model can also output chemically sensible explanations for its predictions despite not being trained with explicit trajectory–explanation pairs.

Conclusion: Symbolic temporal language modeling—representing molecular dynamics trajectories as sequences of event-duration tokens—effectively grounds LLMs in dynamic physical simulations. EvoMD-LLM improves quantitative accuracy and validity in temporal molecular predictions and exhibits emergent interpretability. This suggests that treating time-evolving physical systems as temporal language modeling tasks is a promising direction for integrating LLMs with scientific simulations.

Abstract: While large language models (LLMs) excel at static scientific reasoning, they struggle to model the temporal structure of dynamic physical processes. We present EvoMD-LLM (Evolutionary Molecular Dynamics Large Language Model), a framework that reformulates species-level molecular dynamics as a symbolic temporal language modeling problem. Reactive MD trajectories are discretized into sequences of molecular events, where each token represents a chemical species augmented with its persistence duration, enabling standard autoregressive LLMs to learn compositional evolution over time through efficient fine-tuning. A key component of EvoMD-LLM is temporal scaffolding, which treats event duration as an explicit linguistic token and serves as a structured inductive bias, significantly reducing invalid or hallucinated molecular outputs compared to conventional sequence modeling approaches. We evaluate EvoMD-LLM on multiple temporal prediction tasks, achieving up to 66.14% accuracy and consistently outperforming sequential neural networks and language-based baselines. Beyond quantitative improvements, we qualitatively observe that the model is capable of generating interpretations for its own predictions by incorporating relevant chemical knowledge, even though it was not explicitly supervised with paired trajectory-explanation data. These results demonstrate that symbolic temporal language modeling provides an effective framework for grounding LLMs in dynamic physical simulations.

</details>


### [179] [Aligned but Fragile: Enhancing LLM Safety Robustness via Zeroth-Order Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29396)
*Zhihao Liu,Yifan Wu,Jian Lou,Di Wang,Yuxi Zhou,Yuke Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how to make safety alignment of LLMs robust to small perturbations by viewing it as an optimizer design problem and introducing a zeroth-order refinement stage after standard alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety alignment methods for LLMs are fragile: minor manipulations like parameter or activation noise and quantization can degrade safety behavior. Prior work has focused on data, losses, and identifying critical parameters, but has not systematically examined how the choice of optimizer can improve robustness of safety alignment.

Method: Adopt an optimizer-centric perspective and introduce a hybrid training framework. First, perform conventional first-order safety alignment of an LLM. Then, run a few steps of zeroth-order optimization that explicitly evaluates safety alignment performance under parameter perturbations. This refinement uses the perturbation evaluations both to update parameters and to estimate layer-wise robustness sensitivity, allowing it to concentrate updates on robustness-critical layers and reduce training cost.

Result: Theoretically and empirically, the authors show that a small number of zeroth-order refinement steps on top of standard first-order alignment significantly improves robustness of safety alignment to various post-alignment manipulations such as noise and quantization, without sacrificing the level of safety achieved. The layer-wise sensitivity-guided refinement further improves efficiency by focusing computation on robustness-critical layers.

Conclusion: Considering the base optimizer is crucial for robust safety alignment of LLMs. A simple hybrid approach—first-order alignment followed by targeted zeroth-order refinement under perturbations—can yield safety behaviors that are substantially more robust to common post-alignment perturbations, at modest additional training cost, while maintaining overall safety and utility.

Abstract: Safety alignment for large language models (LLMs) aims to reduce harmful or unsafe behavior while preserving general utility. However, recent findings reveal that alignment effects can be fragile: lightweight post-alignment manipulations, such as parameter noise, activation noise, or quantization, can easily weaken the intended safety behavior. Prior efforts to improve robustness have primarily focused on data curation, modified alignment objectives, and safety-critical parameter identification, leaving the role of the optimizer itself largely unexplored.
  In this paper, we are the first to study the robustness of safety alignment from the perspective of the base optimizer. This optimizer-centric view naturally points to zeroth-order optimization, which provides a robustness-oriented signal by evaluating safety alignment under perturbations. Based on this insight, we propose a hybrid framework that first performs standard first-order safety alignment and then applies zeroth-order refinement to improve robustness. Both theoretically and empirically, we show that only a few zeroth-order refinement steps can enhance robustness while preserving safety alignment. We further improve the efficiency of zeroth-order refinement by exploiting its inherent perturbation-based evaluations to estimate layer-wise robustness sensitivity, enabling the refinement process to concentrate updates on robustness-critical layers with modest training overhead.

</details>


### [180] [Architecture-Sensitive Supervised Fine-Tuning for Screen-Conditioned Action Prediction: A PiSAR Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29400)
*Rahul Bissa,Abhishek Vyas,Yash Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper benchmarks fine-tuned versus frontier zero-shot models on a screen-anchored rationale dataset (PiSAR) and finds large gains from targeted fine-tuning on Qwen3-VL-8B but not on Gemma-4-26B, suggesting model–recipe mismatch.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how much supervised fine-tuning on a domain-specific rationale dataset (PiSAR) can improve performance over strong frontier models used in zero-shot mode, and whether the same fine-tuning recipe works equally well across different base models.

Method: They construct or use PiSAR, a 12,929-tuple corpus of persona/intent/screen/action/rationale data from app-store reviews, Pew demographics, and shopper traces. They then fine-tune supervision on this corpus for different base models (e.g., Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct and Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT). All models, including frontier zero-shot models (Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5), are evaluated on the same 661-row held-out slice using a consistent semantic similarity (sem_sim) scoring pipeline.

Result: Zero-shot frontier models achieve sem_sim scores around 0.46–0.48 and almost never exceed 0.7 on individual rows, whereas the fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct reaches 0.783 average sem_sim and surpasses 0.7 on 79% of rows. However, Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT, fine-tuned with the same data and recipe, only reaches 0.441, similar to the zero-shot frontiers and far below the fine-tuned Qwen.

Conclusion: Targeted supervised fine-tuning on PiSAR can massively outperform strong frontier models in zero-shot mode, but gains are highly model-dependent. Qwen3-VL-8B responds very well to the recipe, whereas Gemma-4-26B does not, implying a mismatch between the fine-tuning recipe and the model’s prior training (e.g., reasoning tuning and higher parameter count). Adjusted methods or more data may be required for such models to benefit similarly.

Abstract: We benchmark three supervised fine-tuned models against frontier zero-shot baselines on a 661-row held-out slice of PiSAR (Persona, intent, Screen, Action, Rationale), a 12,929-tuple corpus of screen-anchored behavioural rationales curated from public app-store reviews, Pew American Trends Panel demographics, and the OPeRA shopper traces. Every model, frontier or fine-tuned, is evaluated on the same 661-row slice with the same scoring pipeline. Two findings. First, frontier zero-shot baselines (Claude Opus 4.7 and GPT-5.5) reach sem_sim 0.459 and 0.482 respectively; a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct reaches 0.783 and clears sem_sim >= 0.7 on 79% of rows, against 1-2% for either frontier baseline, a gap of 0.30 absolute on the same test set. Second, the same training data and recipe on Gemma-4-26B-A4B-IT scores only 0.441, in the same band as the frontier zero-shot baselines rather than the fine-tuned Qwen. We read this as a recipe-vs-model mismatch: the reasoning-tuned high-parameter model resists displacement and would likely need either more data or a stronger fine-tuning method.

</details>


### [181] [When Does Persona Prompting Actually Help? A Retrieval and Metric Analysis of Expert Role Injection in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29420)
*Shuai Xiao,Su Liu,Weikai Zhou,Jialun Wu,Xinjie He,Zhiyuan Lin,Qiyang Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper empirically tests persona (expert-role) prompting and finds it mostly reshapes style—trading clarity for depth—rather than uniformly boosting capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Persona prompts like "You are a medical expert" are widely used to steer LLMs, but it is unclear whether they actually improve answer quality or just change how answers look. Existing evaluations rely on single aggregate scores, which can hide opposing effects on different quality dimensions. The authors want to understand when, how, and along which metrics expert-role prompting helps or hurts.

Method: They run a controlled comparison of four prompting setups on 1,140 open-ended questions covering 38 expert personas across six domains: (1) no role prompt, (2) a generic domain-expert prompt, (3) persona selection via embedding-based retrieval, and (4) a hybrid approach that combines embedding search with LLM-based role selection. They evaluate responses with multiple metrics, decomposing quality into dimensions like expertise depth and clarity, and analyze performance by question type (e.g., advisory vs conceptual) and domain (medicine, psychology, finance, legal, science, technology).

Result: Overall differences between prompting conditions are small when measured by a single aggregate score. However, breaking results down by metric shows a consistent pattern: persona prompts increase perceived expertise depth but reduce clarity. These effects are not uniform; they depend on domain and question type. Persona prompting helps most with advisory questions and in medicine/psychology, whereas the baseline (no persona) works better for conceptual and explanatory questions in finance, legal, science, and technology. The hybrid retrieval method beats embedding-only role selection but does not remove the depth–clarity tradeoff.

Conclusion: Persona prompting should not be assumed to universally improve LLM performance; it mainly shifts responses toward more in-depth, expert-like but less clear communication. Its benefits are context-dependent, especially useful for advisory tasks and certain domains, and less so for straightforward explanatory tasks where plain language clarity is paramount. Improved role retrieval (via hybrid methods) helps but does not solve the fundamental tradeoff. Therefore, multi-metric evaluation—not single aggregate scores—is crucial to understand and properly use persona prompting.

Abstract: Persona prompting is widely used to steer large language models, yet its practical value remains unclear. Prior work often evaluates persona prompting using aggregate scores, making it difficult to determine whether expert-role prompting consistently improves response quality or instead changes responses along different quality dimensions. We study this question through a controlled comparison of four prompting conditions across 1,140 open-ended questions spanning 38 expert roles and six domains: no role prompt, a generic domain-expert prompt, embedding-based role retrieval, and a hybrid retrieval method combining embedding search with LLM-based role selection. Aggregate results show only small overall differences between conditions. However, metric-level analysis reveals a consistent tradeoff that aggregate averages obscure: role prompting systematically increases expertise depth while reducing clarity. These effects are highly conditional rather than universal. Role prompting performs best on advisory questions and in domains such as medicine and psychology, where structured expert framing and risk communication are intrinsically valuable. In contrast, baseline prompting performs better on conceptual and explanatory questions in finance, legal, science, and technology domains, where concise plain-language explanation is more important. We further show that hybrid retrieval significantly improves over embedding-only role selection, although better role retrieval does not eliminate the broader expertise-depth versus clarity tradeoff. Overall, our findings suggest that persona prompting primarily reshapes response characteristics rather than broadly improving capability, and that multi-metric evaluation is necessary for understanding its effects.

</details>


### [182] [ReasonLight: A Multimodal Foundation Model-Enhanced Reinforcement Learning Framework for Zero-Shot Traffic Signal Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29425)
*Aoyu Pang,Maonan Wang,Yuejiao Xie,Chung Shue Chen,Zhiwei Yang,Man-On Pun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ReasonLight is a multimodal, foundation-model-enhanced RL framework that refines standard RL traffic signal decisions using images and sensor data to handle unseen, rare events at intersections without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional RL-based traffic signal control depends on predefined state representations and training data, making it brittle to rare, real-world events (e.g., emergency vehicles, temporary traffic rules) that were not present during training. With IoT intersections producing rich, heterogeneous data from sensors and cameras, there is a need for a method that can leverage these multimodal observations to adapt RL policies on the fly and in a zero-shot manner.

Method: ReasonLight combines three inputs: (1) structured traffic measurements from sensors, (2) multi-view camera images, and (3) the candidate signal phase suggested by a pre-trained RL controller. A multimodal foundation model extracts visual semantics from the images and aligns them with compact scene descriptions derived from sensors. A semantic-guided refinement module then decides whether to keep or modify the RL-proposed phase based on traffic rules and inferred event semantics. Any refined action is checked against the feasible phase set; invalid phases are discarded and the controller reverts to the original RL suggestion, ensuring safety and reliability.

Result: In simulations involving rare events that were never seen during RL training—specifically, emergency vehicle priority scenarios and temporary traffic regulations—ReasonLight achieves effective zero-shot adaptation. It significantly reduces emergency vehicle waiting time by up to 88.7% compared to the underlying RL-only controller, while maintaining nearly the same performance as the baseline RL for normal, routine traffic conditions.

Conclusion: Integrating a multimodal foundation model with an existing RL traffic signal controller enables robust, zero-shot adaptation to rare, unseen events at IoT-equipped intersections. ReasonLight can refine RL decisions using semantic understanding of visual and sensor data, substantially improving priority handling for special cases like emergency vehicles without degrading everyday traffic performance or requiring retraining.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in traffic signal control (TSC). However, its reliance on predefined states limits responsiveness to observable open-world events that are absent from training data. IoT-enabled intersections provide heterogeneous observations from roadside sensors and cameras, creating opportunities to improve RL adaptability to such events. To this end, we propose ReasonLight, a multimodal foundation model-enhanced RL framework for zero-shot TSC. ReasonLight integrates three sources of information: structured traffic measurements, multi-view camera observations, and candidate phase decisions from a pre-trained RL controller. Given an RL-proposed phase, ReasonLight extracts visual semantics from multi-view images and aligns them with compact sensor-derived scene descriptions. This alignment enables a semantic-guided refinement module to either preserve or adjust the proposed action according to traffic rules and event semantics. To ensure operational reliability, refined actions are constrained by the set of available phases. Any invalid decision is rejected, and the system falls back to the original RL action. We evaluate ReasonLight on two types of rare events not seen during RL training: emergency vehicle priority and temporary traffic regulation. Experimental results show that ReasonLight achieves zero-shot adaptation without retraining. It reduces emergency vehicle waiting time by up to 88.7% compared with the RL-only backbone while preserving comparable routine traffic performance.

</details>


### [183] [Towards Human-Like Interactive Speech Recognition With Agentic Correction and Semantic Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29430)
*Zixuan Jiang,Yanqiao Zhu,Peng Wang,Qinyuan Chen,Xinjian Zhao,Xipeng Qiu,Wupeng Wang,Zhifu Gao,Xiangang Li,Kai Yu,Xie Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Interactive ASR, a multi-turn, agent-based speech recognition framework that iteratively corrects semantic errors, along with a new sentence-level semantic error metric (S^2ER) and an interactive simulation benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional ASR is single-pass and evaluated with token-level metrics like WER/CER, which fail to capture and correct meaning-critical errors. Human conversation, in contrast, uses interactive clarification and refinement. The authors want an ASR paradigm and evaluation metric that better align with human communication and downstream LLM-based assistants, focusing on semantic correctness rather than surface token accuracy.

Method: They formulate Interactive ASR as a multi-turn refinement task. They propose Agentic ASR, a closed-loop system combining a standard single-pass ASR with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing powered by LLMs. They also design Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate (S^2ER), an LLM-based metric that scores semantic correctness at the sentence level, and build an Interactive Simulation System to systematically simulate and evaluate multi-turn interactions. The framework is tested on multilingual, named-entity-heavy, and code-switching datasets, with ablations and human alignment studies to validate the semantic judge and components.

Result: Iterative interaction within Agentic ASR consistently reduces semantic errors across all tested benchmarks. The improvements are especially large when measured with S^2ER, compared to relatively modest gains in traditional token-level metrics like WER/CER. Human–AI alignment experiments show that the LLM-based S^2ER judge aligns well with human judgments, and ablation studies demonstrate that the full agentic, closed-loop design is more robust than simpler baselines.

Conclusion: An interactive, agent-based ASR paradigm better matches human communication and delivers more meaningful gains in semantic accuracy than conventional single-pass systems, particularly when evaluated with a semantic metric. The proposed Agentic ASR framework and S^2ER metric offer a practical path to more robust, meaning-preserving speech interfaces for LLM-based assistants, and their open-source code and demo enable further research and benchmarking.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) is a core component of human--computer interaction and an increasingly important front-end for LLM-based assistants and agents. However, most current ASR systems still follow a single-pass paradigm, which is poorly aligned with human communication, where misunderstandings are resolved through iterative clarification and refinement. This mismatch makes it difficult to correct meaning-critical errors once they occur. Meanwhile, token-level metrics such as WER or CER cannot adequately reflect such a problem. To address these limitations, we formulate \emph{Interactive ASR} as a multi-turn refinement task and propose \textbf{Agentic ASR}, a closed-loop framework that combines a single-pass ASR front-end with semantic correction, intent routing, and reasoning-based editing. We further introduce the \textbf{Sentence-level Semantic Error Rate} ($S^2ER$), an LLM-based semantic evaluation metric, together with an \textbf{Interactive Simulation System} for scalable and reproducible benchmarking. Experiments on multilingual, named-entity-intensive, and code-switching benchmarks show that iterative interaction consistently reduces semantic errors, with much larger gains in $S^2ER$ than in conventional token-level metrics. Human--AI alignment and ablation studies further validate the reliability of the semantic judge and the robustness of the proposed framework. The code is available at: https://interactiveasr.github.io/ and the live demo is available at https://i-asr.sjtuxlance.com/

</details>


### [184] [CrystalXRD-Bench: Benchmarking Vision-Language Models for XRD Peak Indexing Across Diverse Crystalline Materials](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29446)
*Chengliang Xu,Xiaogang Li,Peiyao Xiao,Beng Wang,Hu Wei,Bing Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build CrystalXRD-Bench, a benchmark to test if vision-language models can read XRD peak positions from images and infer the contributing Miller indices, and show current models perform poorly and systematically fail in certain conditions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal benchmarks don’t test the combined ability to extract precise quantitative information from scientific plots and perform domain-specific crystallographic reasoning. Powder XRD analysis, specifically identifying the Miller indices (HKLs) responsible for peaks, is a critical and nontrivial scientific task that stresses both visual understanding and multi-step reasoning, but has lacked a focused benchmark.

Method: They construct CrystalXRD-Bench, a 250-sample dataset drawn from 10 public crystallographic databases. Each sample includes a rendered powder XRD pattern image, the corresponding CIF (crystallographic information file) text, and the chemical formula. The task is to predict the complete set of HKLs corresponding to the highest-intensity peak. They then evaluate seven vision-language models on this benchmark, computing Jaccard similarity and exact-match scores, and analyze error patterns across conditions such as double-peak cases and access to CIF text.

Result: The best-performing model (GPT-5.4) achieves a Jaccard score of 0.5888 and an exact-match rate of 37.6%, while six of seven models score below 0.50 Jaccard, showing substantial room for improvement. Error analyses reveal systematic weaknesses: double-peak patterns are especially challenging; some models increase recall by over-predicting HKLs; and giving models direct access to CIF text still does not enable accurate crystallographic calculations.

Conclusion: CrystalXRD-Bench exposes that current vision-language models significantly struggle with quantitative interpretation of scientific figures combined with crystallographic reasoning. The benchmark not only ranks models but also clarifies where and why they fail, particularly in complex peak scenarios and crystallographic computation, providing a focused testbed for future VLM development. The authors will release all data and evaluation code to support further research.

Abstract: Miller-index identification from powder XRD patterns requires capabilities untested by existing multimodal benchmarks: the model must read a narrow peak location from a rendered scientific curve and then connect that observation to multi-step crystallographic reasoning. We introduce CrystalXRD-Bench, a 250-sample benchmark built from 10 public crystallographic databases for a single task: recover the full set of HKLs contributing to the highest-intensity peak in an XRD pattern. Each sample pairs the rendered XRD image with the source CIF text and chemical formula, so visual extraction errors and reasoning errors can be examined side by side. We evaluate seven vision-language models. The best Jaccard score is 0.5888 (GPT-5.4) with an exact-match rate of 37.6%, yet six of seven models remain below Jaccard 0.50; the task is far from solved. Error patterns vary systematically: double-peak cases are especially brittle, recall-heavy models gain coverage by over-predicting HKLs, and access to CIF text does not close the gap in crystallographic calculation. Alongside model rankings, the benchmark identifies the conditions under which current VLMs fail on quantitative scientific figures. All data and evaluation code will be publicly available.

</details>


### [185] [VitalAgent: A Tool-Augmented Agent for Reactive and Proactive Physiological Monitoring over Wearable Health Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29483)
*Di Zhu,Yu Yvonne Wu,Hong Jia,Aaqib Saeed,Vassilis Kostakos,Ting Dang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Proposes VitalAgent, a tool-augmented agentic framework for ECG/PPG mHealth that enables both reactive QA and proactive monitoring using longitudinal physiological memory, and introduces VitalBench, a benchmark for evaluating such systems, showing >30% improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mHealth systems are mostly task-specific or limited to reactive QA over static summaries, lacking temporal reasoning, persistent physiological context, and proactive long-term monitoring of physiological signal streams.

Method: Design VitalAgent, an agentic framework equipped with a longitudinal physiological memory and tool-augmented reasoning interface that can dynamically compute over raw ECG/PPG signals for both QA and monitoring; create VitalBench, a benchmark dataset with 1,862 QA pairs and 90.2 hours of continuous ECG/PPG recordings across cardiac, activity, and stress tasks; evaluate VitalAgent against prompt-based and ReAct baselines on reactive QA and proactive monitoring tasks.

Result: VitalAgent outperforms prompt-based and ReAct baselines by over 30% on reactive QA evaluation and can successfully perform proactive alert monitoring on long-term physiological signals in VitalBench.

Conclusion: Dynamic tool-augmented reasoning combined with longitudinal physiological memory substantially improves both reactive and proactive mHealth capabilities over ECG/PPG data, underscoring the importance of long-term physiological monitoring and sophisticated temporal reasoning in next-generation mHealth systems.

Abstract: Wearable devices enable continuous monitoring of physiological signals such as ECG and PPG, but existing mHealth systems are largely limited to task-specific prediction pipelines or reactive question answering over static summaries. They lack the ability to support temporal reasoning, persistent physiological context, and proactive monitoring over long-term signal streams. We propose VitalAgent, a tool-augmented agentic framework for ECG/PPG-based mHealth that supports both reactive question answering and proactive monitoring. VitalAgent is built on a longitudinal physiological memory and a tool-augmented reasoning interface that enables dynamic computation over raw signals. We further introduce VitalBench, a longitudinal physiological monitoring benchmark dataset comprising 1,862 QA pairs for reactive question answering and 90.2 hours of continuous ECG/PPG recordings for proactive monitoring, covering cardiac, physical activity, and stress-related tasks. Experiments demonstrate that VitalAgent achieves over 30% improvement over prompt-based and ReAct baselines in reactive evaluation and supports proactive alert monitoring over long-term physiological signals, highlighting the importance of dynamic tool use and long-term physiological monitoring.

</details>


### [186] [The Curse of Helpfulness: Inverse Scaling Law in Robustness to Distractor Instructions via DistractionIF](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29491)
*Zeli Su,Zhankai Xu,Tianlei Chen,Longfei Zheng,Xiaolu Zhang,Jun Zhou,Wentao Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DistractionIF, a benchmark to test how robust LLMs are to instruction-like noise in reference text, finds that larger models are paradoxically more vulnerable, analyzes why via perplexity, and shows that GRPO reinforcement learning can significantly improve robustness without hurting general instruction following.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs used in agentic and RAG systems must follow user instructions while treating reference context as data, not as additional instructions. In real-world settings, reference text often contains instruction-like but irrelevant content (e.g., logs, editor notes). Existing evaluations do not specifically measure robustness to such distractor instructions, and there is anecdotal evidence that models sometimes follow these spurious cues. The authors are motivated to systematically measure and understand this failure mode, especially as models scale, and to explore methods to fix it.

Method: The authors construct DistractionIF, a benchmark where models must complete tasks using reference text that is intentionally contaminated with benign but instruction-like distractors that should be ignored. They evaluate a variety of LLMs of different sizes on this benchmark to measure robustness and look for scaling trends. They then perform perplexity-based analysis to understand how model scaling affects the likelihood of robust vs distracted behaviors, effectively probing the probabilistic decision boundary between following the user versus the distractor. Finally, they apply a reinforcement learning approach, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), to train models specifically to ignore distractor instructions while preserving general instruction-following ability, and evaluate the gains on DistractionIF and standard tasks.

Result: Empirically, models across families exhibit an inverse scaling pattern: larger LLMs tend to be less robust to distractor instructions than smaller ones, with performance sometimes dropping by up to 30 points as model size increases. Perplexity analysis shows that scaling blurs the probabilistic distinction between correct (robust) and incorrect (distracted) continuations, meaning that larger models assign relatively higher probability to following the distractors. When trained with GRPO, models recover much of the lost robustness, with improvements up to 15.5% on the DistractionIF benchmark, while maintaining their overall instruction-following performance on other tasks.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that current LLMs have a substantial and underappreciated robustness gap in reference-grounded settings: they too often misinterpret instruction-like noise in context as authoritative instructions, and this problem worsens with scale. This exposes risks for real-world RAG and agentic applications where logs, comments, or traces may be present in the context. The authors show that reinforcement learning via GRPO can re-establish a clearer boundary between data and instructions, improving robustness without sacrificing general capabilities. They argue that enforcing strict data-instruction separation should be a key design and training objective for large-scale deployed LLM systems and that DistractionIF provides a useful benchmark to drive progress on this front.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in agentic and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, where they must execute user-specified tasks over externally provided reference text. In practice, such context is often unstructured and contaminated with benign but instruction-like semantic noise, such as editorial comments and system traces, which should be treated strictly as data. We introduce DistractionIF, a benchmark designed to evaluate robustness against such distractor instructions in reference text. Across a broad range of models, we observe a consistent inverse scaling phenomenon: larger models are often less robust, with performance dropping by up to 30 points as scale increases. Mechanistically, our perplexity analysis reveals that scaling erodes the probabilistic boundary between robust and distracted behaviors, making models increasingly prone to over-interpreting noise as instructions. To address this, we demonstrate that reinforcement learning, specifically Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), can restore this boundary, improving robustness by up to 15.5% without compromising general instruction-following capability. Our findings highlight a critical instruction-following robustness gap in reference-grounded tasks and establish reinforcement learning as a promising path for enforcing strict data-instruction separation at scale.

</details>


### [187] [Xetrieval: Mechanistically Explaining Dense Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29507)
*Zhixin Cai,Jun Bai,Yang Liu,Jiaqi Li,Yichi Zhang,Taichuan Li,Zhuofan Chen,Zixia Jia,Zilong Zheng,Wenge Rong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Xetrieval, a framework to explain dense retrievers at the embedding level by discovering sparse, human-interpretable features that underlie retrieval decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Dense retrievers rely on opaque high-dimensional embeddings, so it is difficult to understand why they assign high relevance scores. Existing explanation methods focus on surface signals like lexical overlaps or post-hoc rationales, which do not reveal the latent embedding-level factors influencing dense retrieval behavior.

Method: Xetrieval introduces a reasoning internalizer that approximates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly in embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information without expensive autoregressive generation. These reasoning-enhanced embeddings are then decomposed into sparse, human-interpretable features, each linked to a natural language description. Explanations are produced by aggregating overlaps of these sparse features across multiple document-side views to explain individual retrieval decisions at the feature level.

Result: Experiments across various dense retrievers and benchmarks demonstrate that Xetrieval discovers coherent interpretable features, shows stronger pair-level intervention effects (i.e., modifying features leads to predictable changes in retrieval scores), and enables task-level feature steering to influence retrieval behavior.

Conclusion: Embedding-level mechanistic explanations for dense retrieval are feasible and effective. By internalizing reasoning in the embedding space and decomposing embeddings into interpretable sparse features, Xetrieval provides more faithful and actionable explanations of dense retriever decisions than prior surface-level approaches.

Abstract: Explaining why dense retrievers assign high relevance scores remains challenging because retrieval decisions are made through opaque high-dimensional embeddings. Existing explanations often focus on surface signals, such as lexical matches, token alignments, or post-hoc textual rationales, and thus provide limited insight into the latent factors that shape dense retrieval behavior at the embedding level. We propose \textit{Xetrieval}, an embedding-level mechanistic framework for explaining dense retrieval. \textit{Xetrieval} first introduces a lightweight reasoning internalizer that approximates Chain-of-Thought reasoning directly in the embedding space with a single forward pass, enriching sentence embeddings with reasoning-oriented information while avoiding expensive autoregressive generation. It then decomposes these reasoning-enhanced embeddings into sparse, human-interpretable features, each associated with a coherent natural language description. By aggregating sparse feature overlaps across multiple document-side views, \textit{Xetrieval} provides feature-level explanations of individual retrieval decisions. Experiments on diverse retrievers and benchmarks show that \textit{Xetrieval} uncovers coherent interpretable features, yields stronger pair-level intervention effects, and supports task-level feature steering. The project page and source code are available at https://hihiczx.github.io/Xetrieval .

</details>


### [188] [MINDGAMES: A Live Arena for Evaluating Social and Strategic Reasoning in Multi-Agent LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29512)
*Kevin Wang,Anna Thöni,Benjamin Kempinski,Bobby Cheng,Jianzhu Yao,Benjamin Finch,Leon Guertler,Viraj Nadkarni,Yihan Jiang,Aliaksei Korshuk,Alexander Buyantuev,Ilya Makarov,Siyuan Wu,Yu-Chi Cheng,Yan-Ru Ju,Ti-Rong Wu,I-Hsuan Chu,Yu-Yu Yang,I-Chen Wu,Yitian Huang,Qinlu Cao,Yiheng Sun,Yuhong Dai,Hongkun Yao,Jingxuan Fu,Jiwei Zhang,Hao Liao,Mossimo Ebeling,Govind Arun,Sadhvik Bathini,Mihir S Arya,Avinash Anish,Aditya Ranjan,Kirtana Sunil Phatnani,Paval KS,Vrushali Mehta,Aravind S,Nikhil Arora,Tanya Upadhyay,Amol Bandagale,Yuan Lu,ChunEn Hsiao,YuTing Lin,Arvin Chung,Jerry John Thomas,Mathieu Laurière,Leshem Choshen,Yoram Bachrach,Pramod Viswanath,Maria Polukarov,Cheston Tan,Tal Kachman,Atlas Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces Mindgames, a multi-game arena to evaluate LLMs’ sustained social and strategic reasoning via four diverse games and a large-scale competition dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM evaluations focus on static or single-shot tasks and thus fail to measure multi-step, interactive, and socially grounded reasoning (e.g., theory of mind, opponent modeling, sustained deception) that real-world multi-agent deployments require. There is a need for a standardized, multi-game benchmark and dataset to probe these capabilities systematically and at scale.

Method: Build Mindgames, a multi-game evaluation platform on top of TextArena featuring four games that each emphasize different aspects of social/strategic reasoning: Colonel Blotto (resource allocation under hidden info), Iterated Prisoner’s Dilemma (repeated cooperation/defection and opponent modeling), Codenames (cooperative inference under asymmetrical knowledge), and Secret Mafia (social deduction and sustained deception). Provide a unified interaction API, TrueSkill-based rating, and full trajectory logging. Run a 2025 competition at a major conference, collecting 944 agents from 76 teams, and analyze their behavior and performance, with particular attention to rule adherence, use of structural scaffolding, and environment-specific leaderboard validity. Develop MG-Ref, a deterministic offline tournament protocol using a frozen pool of strong, low-error reference agents to evaluate new submissions under a consistent error-attribution framework.

Result: Collected a dataset of 29,571 multi-agent games with turn-level observations, actions, and rewards. Empirically observed that many agents struggle with reliable rule following, that top systems tend to depend heavily on explicit structural scaffolding, and that leaderboard rankings vary meaningfully across games—especially in environments where failures and mistakes are common. Identified a strong “error-survival” confound in the Secret Mafia environment, where success can hinge as much on robustness to others’ mistakes as on true strategic sophistication.

Conclusion: Mindgames demonstrates that multi-game, interactive benchmarks can reveal nuanced limitations of current LLM agents that static tasks miss, especially around rule adherence, robustness, and environment-specific artifacts in evaluation. The released platform, dataset, and MG-Ref protocol form a reproducible basis for future research on social and strategic reasoning in LLMs, enabling consistent comparison of new agents against a standardized pool and under a principled error-aware evaluation regime.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as interactive agents, yet their capacity for social and strategic reasoning over extended interaction remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations rely on static vignettes or single-game benchmarks that cannot capture the sustained, multi-faceted reasoning that real-world multi-agent settings demand. We introduce Mindgames, a multi-game arena and evaluation platform for LLM agents that operationalizes complementary reasoning demands relevant to ``theory of mind'': belief attribution under hidden information, opponent modeling through repeated strategic interaction, cooperative inference under knowledge asymmetries, and sustained deception in social deduction. Built on TextArena, Mindgames provides a unified interaction interface, TrueSkill-based rating, and full trajectory logging across four game environments. We instantiate Mindgames through a 2025 competition cycle hosted at a major AI conference, which assessed 944 submitted agents from 76 teams across four games: Colonel Blotto, Iterated Prisoner's Dilemma, Codenames, and Secret Mafia. Our analysis surfaces both agent-level and evaluation-level limitations: brittle rule adherence remains a major bottleneck, top-performing systems repeatedly rely on explicit structural scaffolding, and leaderboard validity differs sharply across environments. In particular, failure-heavy environments can reward robustness to opponent errors as much as strategic ability, with Secret Mafia exhibiting a pronounced error-survival confound in this cycle. We release a dataset of 29,571 multi-agent games with turn-level observations, actions, and rewards, together with MG-Ref, a deterministic offline tournament protocol that scores new agents against a frozen reference pool of top-ranked, low-error Stage~II submissions under the same error-attribution lens used in this analysis.

</details>
