### [189] [DeepSurvey: Enhancing Analytical Depth and Citation Reliability in Automated Survey Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29522)
*Ziyue Yang,Da Ma,Hanqi Li,Zijian Wang,Tiancheng Huang,Zijian Hu,Chenrun Wang,Yunzhe Zhang,Xiaobao Wu,Kai Yu,Lu Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeepSurvey is an agentic system for generating deep, reliable automated surveys from scientific literature using full-text analysis, structured cross-paper reasoning, and robust citation grounding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated survey systems mainly use abstracts and treat papers in isolation, leading to shallow, sometimes misleading surveys with weak analytical depth and unreliable citations. There is a need for a system that can both deeply analyze technical content (including implementations) and ensure that claims are strictly supported by correctly retrieved and assigned citations.

Method: DeepSurvey builds an agentic pipeline that: (1) extracts structured keynotes from full-text papers; (2) models cross-paper relationships via clustering and comparative analysis to capture themes and differences; (3) analyzes associated code repositories to recover implementation-level details; (4) improves retrieval by expanding along the citation graph and applying hybrid filtering for topic-focused paper selection; (5) enforces evidence-constrained citation assignment so claims must be supported by retrieved texts; and (6) uses multi-granularity agentic refinement to iteratively check and align claims with citations.

Result: Experiments show DeepSurvey achieves the highest content quality scores (8.644/10) among compared systems, significantly improves citation quality with 12.3% recall and 9.3% precision gains over the strongest baseline, generalizes better across domains with a smaller performance drop from CS to non-CS fields (0.14 vs 0.22–0.69), and is often preferred over human-written surveys by domain experts (83.3% overall quality preference, 100% preference for content depth).

Conclusion: By jointly enhancing analytical depth through full-text, cross-paper, and code-level reasoning, and improving reliability via focused retrieval and strict citation-claim alignment, DeepSurvey produces automated surveys that are both deeper and more trustworthy than prior systems and are competitive with or superior to human-written surveys according to expert evaluations.

Abstract: As scientific literature grows rapidly, automated survey generation has become a key capability for AI scientists and human researchers. However, existing systems suffer from limited analytical depth due to reliance on abstracts and isolated paper processing, and unreliable citations from imprecise retrieval and post-hoc grounding, producing superficial surveys and may mislead researchers. We present DeepSurvey, an agentic system that addresses both. To enhance depth, DeepSurvey extracts structured keynotes from full-text papers, models cross-paper relationships through clustering and comparative analysis, and integrates code-repository analysis to recover implementation-level details. To fortify reliability, it combines citation-graph expansion with hybrid filtering for topic-focussed retrieval, enforces evidence-constrained citation assignment, and deploys multi-granularity agentic refinement to validate citation-claim alignment. Experiments show that DeepSurvey achieves the highest content score (8.644/10) and citation quality (12.3% and 9.3% recall and precision gains over the strongest baseline), generalizes more robustly across domains (0.14 vs 0.22 to 0.69 CS-to-non-CS drop), and is preferred over human-written surveys by domain experts (83.3% overall quality, 100% content depth).

</details>


### [190] [UI-KOBE: Knowledge-Oriented Behavior Exploration for Lightweight Graph-Guided GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29534)
*Yuxiang Chai,Han Xiao,Xinyu Fu,Jinpeng Chen,Rui Liu,Hongsheng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: UI-KOBE is a framework that lets small, on-device mobile GUI agents use an automatically built app knowledge graph to plan actions more reliably and efficiently.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing mobile GUI agents work well when powered by large vision-language models that can understand screenshots and plan long-horizon actions, but these models are too heavy and costly for practical on-device deployment and raise privacy concerns. Lightweight agents are preferable but struggle to reliably plan and execute tasks end-to-end using only screenshots because of their limited capacity. The paper aims to bridge this gap by giving small agents structured, reusable app-specific knowledge so they can perform complex tasks without relying on large models.

Method: UI-KOBE autonomously explores a target mobile app offline and constructs an app knowledge graph whose nodes represent distinct UI states and whose edges represent executable transitions between those states. At runtime, a lightweight GUI agent no longer plans purely from raw screenshots: given a user task and the current screenshot, it first locates the corresponding node in the knowledge graph and then chooses among four types of app-specific options: (1) self-loop actions on the current state, (2) transitions to neighboring states along graph edges, (3) declaring task completion when appropriate, or (4) using fallback free-form actions linked to that node if graph guidance is insufficient. This graph-guided decision process offloads much of the planning burden from the small model to the structured knowledge graph.

Result: The framework enables lightweight mobile GUI agents to perform mobile tasks more effectively than purely end-to-end screenshot-based approaches using the same small models. By leveraging the app-specific knowledge graph, the agents demonstrate improved reliability and efficiency in planning and executing GUI actions, with reduced dependence on large vision-language models.

Conclusion: UI-KOBE shows that augmenting small, on-device mobile GUI agents with automatically built, app-specific knowledge graphs can significantly improve their task performance while preserving efficiency and privacy. Graph-guided runtime decisions reduce the need for heavy end-to-end reasoning, making GUI agents more practical, interpretable, and deployable directly on mobile devices.

Abstract: Recent advances in mobile GUI agents have shown strong potential for automating mobile tasks, but most effective systems still depend on large vision-language models for screenshot understanding and long-horizon planning. Small GUI agents that can be deployed directly on mobile devices are more attractive for practical use, offering lower inference cost and better protection of sensitive on-device information. However, due to limited model capacity, such lightweight agents remain unreliable when planning and executing GUI tasks end-to-end from screenshots alone. We propose Knowledge-Oriented Behavior Exploration (\textbf{UI-KOBE}), a framework that improves lightweight mobile GUI agents with reusable app-specific graph knowledge. UI-KOBE first autonomously explores a mobile application and constructs an app knowledge graph, where nodes represent distinct UI states and edges represent executable transitions. At runtime, a lightweight GUI agent uses the graph as external guidance: given a user task and the current screenshot, it identifies the current graph node and selects among self-loop actions, neighboring transitions, task completion, or fallback free actions associated with that node. By supporting runtime decisions with app-specific graph guidance, UI-KOBE reduces the burden of end-to-end GUI planning and helps lightweight models perform mobile GUI tasks more effectively, offering a practical step toward efficient, interpretable, and privacy-conscious on-device GUI agents.

</details>


### [191] [Opt-Verifier: Unleashing the Power of LLMs for Optimization Modeling via Dual-Side Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29556)
*Haoyang Liu,Jie Wang,Boxuan Niu,Xiongwei Han,Yian Xu,Mingxuan Ye,Zijie Geng,Fangzhou Zhu,Tao Zhong,Mingxuan Yuan,Jianye Hao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Opt-Verifier, an LLM-based framework that boosts the correctness of automatically generated optimization models by verifying them from both structural and solution perspectives, achieving over 20% accuracy improvement on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automating the construction of mathematical optimization models in operations research with LLMs is promising but current approaches lack robust mechanisms to verify whether generated models correctly represent the original problem, especially in terms of constraints, variables, and solution validity. This verification gap leads to inaccurate models and undermines their practical usefulness, motivating a framework that can systematically check and improve modeling correctness.

Method: The authors propose Opt-Verifier, a dual-side verification framework wrapped around LLM-based model generation. On the structure side, the framework checks whether the variables, constraints, and overall model structure faithfully align with the natural-language problem description, ensuring that all requirements are captured. On the solution side, it examines the logical and mathematical soundness of solutions produced by the generated model, validating that solutions are feasible and consistent with the intended problem. These two complementary verification loops are used to refine and correct the generated optimization models.

Result: On standard optimization modeling benchmarks, Opt-Verifier improves the accuracy of automatically generated optimization models by more than 20% compared to existing LLM-based methods that lack such dual-side verification.

Conclusion: Incorporating dual-side verification—both structural alignment with the problem description and solution-level validity checks—significantly enhances the reliability and accuracy of LLM-generated optimization models. The proposed Opt-Verifier framework demonstrates that systematic verification is key to making LLM-based OR modeling more robust and practical.

Abstract: Building mathematical optimization models is critical in operations research (OR), while it requires substantial human expertise. Recent advancements have utilized large language models (LLMs) to automate this modeling process. However, existing works often struggle to verify the correctness of the generated optimization models, without checking the rationality of the constraints and variables or the validity of solutions to the generated models. This hampers the subsequent verification and correction steps, and thus it severely hurts the modeling accuracy. To address this challenge, we propose a novel LLM-based framework with Dual-side Verification (Opt-Verifier) from both structure and solution perspectives, thereby improving the modeling accuracy. The structure-side verification ensures that the modeling structure of the generated optimization models aligns with the original problem description, accurately capturing the problem's constraints and requirements. Meanwhile, the solution-side verification interprets and evaluates the solutions' validity, confirming that the optimization models are logically and mathematically sound. Experiments on popular benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves over 20\% improvement in accuracy.

</details>


### [192] [Battery-Sim-Agent: Leveraging LLM-Agent for Inverse Battery Parameter Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29560)
*Jiawei Chen,Xiaofan Gui,Shikai Fang,Shengyu Tao,Shun Zheng,Weiqing Liu,Jiang Bian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose Battery-Sim-Agent, an LLM-driven agent that closes the loop with a high-fidelity battery simulator to infer model parameters more efficiently and accurately than standard black-box optimization methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: High-fidelity battery "digital twins" require accurate parameter identification, which is a difficult inverse problem. Current black-box optimization approaches are sample-inefficient and ignore known battery physics, slowing down model deployment and battery innovation. A more efficient, physics-aware parameterization approach is needed.

Method: They reframe parameter estimation as a reasoning task and build an LLM agent that interacts iteratively with a battery simulator. The agent takes multi-modal simulator feedback, interprets discrepancies, forms physically grounded hypotheses, and proposes structured updates to parameters in a closed-loop manner. They evaluate this system on a benchmark suite covering various chemistries, operating conditions, and difficulty levels, and also on long-horizon degradation fitting and real battery data.

Result: Across the benchmark suite, the Battery-Sim-Agent consistently and significantly outperforms strong black-box optimization baselines, such as Bayesian optimization, in finding accurate battery model parameters. It also successfully handles complex degradation modeling tasks and works on real-world datasets, demonstrating robustness and practical relevance.

Conclusion: Treating battery parameter estimation as a reasoning problem and solving it with an LLM-controlled agent in a simulator loop leads to more efficient and accurate parameter identification than conventional BBO methods. LLM-agents show strong potential as general reasoning-based optimizers for scientific discovery and, in particular, for battery digital-twin parameterization.

Abstract: Parameterizing high-fidelity "digital twins" of batteries is a critical yet challenging inverse problem that hinders the pace of battery innovation. Prevailing methods formulate this as a black-box optimization (BBO) task, employing algorithms that are sample-inefficient and blind to the underlying physics. In this work, we introduce a new paradigm that reframes the inverse problem as a reasoning task, and present Battery-Sim-Agent, the first framework to deploy a Large Language Model (LLM) agent in a closed loop with a high-fidelity battery simulator. The agent mimics a human scientist's workflow: it interprets rich, multi-modal feedback from the simulator, forms physically-grounded hypotheses to explain discrepancies, and proposes structured parameter updates. On a systematically constructed benchmark suite spanning diverse battery chemistries, operating conditions, and difficulty levels, our agent significantly outperforms strong BBO baselines like Bayesian optimization in identifying accurate parameters. We further demonstrate the framework's capability in complex long-horizon degradation fitting tasks and validate its practical applicability on real-world battery datasets. Our results highlight the promise of LLM-agents as reasoning-based optimizers for scientific discovery and battery parameter estimation.

</details>


### [193] [ParaTool: Shifting Tool Representations from Context to Parameters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29561)
*Zekai Yu,Qi Meng,Qizhi Chu,Yu Hao,Chuan Shi,Cheng Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ParaTool introduces a parameter-based framework for tool calling in LLMs that removes the need for long in-context tool descriptions, reducing inference cost and improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tool calling with LLMs either relies on long in-context documentation and examples, which increases context length, cost, and hallucination risk, or on tuning-based methods that improve general tool use but still cannot store detailed knowledge of each tool without external docs. The authors want a way for LLMs to use many tools accurately and efficiently without packing tool manuals into every prompt.

Method: They propose ParaTool, which represents each tool as its own learnable parameter module that can be loaded dynamically. The framework has three stages: (1) parametric tool pre-training, in which knowledge about each tool’s usage is encoded into separate parameter modules; (2) soft tool selection, where a gating network learns to select and weight relevant tool-parameter modules for a given query; and (3) parametric tool fine-tuning, where the tool modules and selection mechanism are trained jointly so that training and inference are aligned.

Result: On Stable ToolBench and BFCL benchmarks, ParaTool outperforms strong in-context learning baselines for tool calling, achieving higher task performance while using less computation thanks to eliminating long tool documentation from the context.

Conclusion: Parameterizing tools as loadable modules and integrating them via soft selection enables LLMs to perform tool calling more accurately and efficiently, without relying on long in-context documentation, and scales better as the number and complexity of tools increase.

Abstract: Tool calling extends large language models (LLMs) by enabling grounded interaction with external executable interfaces, thereby supporting environment-coupled problem solving. However, mainstream in-context learning (ICL) approaches typically incorporate detailed tool documentation and usage examples directly into the context. This results in substantial inference overhead and heightened risks of hallucination as the context length grows. Conversely, while tuning-based methods improve general tool-calling capabilities, they often fail to effectively internalize the specific details of previously seen tools, thereby retaining a dependency on in-context documentation. To address these limitations, we propose ParaTool, a framework that projects each tool into a dedicated, loadable set of parameters. By equipping a dynamic integration of these parameterized tools, the LLM can perform tool calling without relying on in-context documents or examples. Specifically, our approach consists of three stages: (1) parametric tool pre-training encapsulates the knowledge of different tools into independent parameter modules; (2) soft tool selection employs a gating network to dynamically weigh and aggregate relevant tool parameters; and (3) parametric tool fine-tuning jointly updates tool parameters to align the training and inference processes. Experiments on Stable ToolBench and BFCL demonstrate that ParaTool significantly outperforms strong ICL-based baselines, achieving superior performance while reducing computational complexity.

</details>


### [194] [Planning with the Views via Scene Self-Exploration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29563)
*Kangrui Wang,Linjie Li,Zhengyuan Yang,Shiqi Chen,Zihan Wang,Li Fei-Fei,Jiajun Wu,Leonidas Guibas,Lijuan Wang,Manling Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies whether vision-language models (VLMs) can plan sequences of camera movements (view planning) in 3D environments, finds a major weakness in multi-step planning, and proposes a self-exploration plus view-graph distillation framework that dramatically improves performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While VLMs show strong static perception and single-step action understanding, it is unclear if they can reason about how sequences of camera movements change views and thus actively navigate to target viewpoints in 3D scenes. This capability is crucial for interactive agents, robotics, and embodied AI that must plan over long horizons. The authors aim to diagnose this capability and to close the gap between single-step understanding and multi-step compositional planning.

Method: The authors introduce ViewSuite, a 3D point-cloud environment built on real ScanNet scenes, to evaluate view planning. They define two core abilities: (1) understanding how a single camera action transforms the view and (2) composing multiple such actions to reach a target view over multi-turn interactions. They benchmark 13 state-of-the-art VLMs on these tasks and observe that while models know basic view-action relations, they fail badly on long-horizon view planning, especially as target viewpoints become more distant. To address this, they design an iterative self-exploration framework where a VLM interacts with the environment, generating exploration trajectories that collectively define a view graph capturing viewpoint connectivity. This view graph is then distilled into various supervised learning tasks to reshape the policy distribution, alleviating sparse reward issues that hinder pure reinforcement learning. The approach is applied to Qwen2.5-VL-7B.

Result: Empirical evaluation in ViewSuite shows that existing frontier VLMs exhibit a sharp planning gap: they handle local, single-step view changes but break down on multi-step compositional planning, with performance degrading as viewpoint distance increases. Using the proposed self-exploration plus view graph distillation framework, the authors boost Qwen2.5-VL-7B's accuracy on interactive view planning from 2.5% to 47.8%. This fine-tuned model significantly outperforms much larger or more advanced closed-source systems such as GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%) on the same benchmark.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that current VLMs lack robust multi-step view planning ability, revealing a substantial gap between local view-action knowledge and long-horizon compositional planning in 3D environments. The proposed self-exploration and view graph distillation framework effectively closes much of this gap, showing that leveraging exploration trajectories as a structured view graph and distilling them into diverse supervised tasks can overcome sparse rewards and dramatically improve planning performance. This suggests that self-exploration is a powerful paradigm for developing VLMs that can actively reason and plan in 3D space, with implications for embodied agents and robotics.

Abstract: Can VLMs predict how each camera move changes the view, and plan many such moves ahead? We call this capability view planning, requiring (1)understanding how a single action transforms the view, and (2)composing many such transformations across multi-turn plans to identify a target view. We probe both abilities in our proposed ViewSuite, a 3D point-cloud environment on real ScanNet scenes. Across 13 frontier VLMs, a critical planning gap emerges: they possess basic view-action knowledge but fail to compose it across multi-turn plans, with the gap widening as viewpoint distance grows. To close this gap, we propose an iterative framework that alternates self-exploration with view graph distillation. The key insight is that all exploration trajectories, regardless of their outcome, collectively form a view graph that compactly captures how viewpoints connect across a scene. Distilling this graph into diverse supervised tasks reshapes the policy distribution and overcomes the sparse rewards that stall pure RL. This improves Qwen2.5-VL-7B from 2.5% to 47.8% on interactive view planning, surpassing GPT-5.4 Pro (18.5%) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (21.4%). Self-exploration emerges as a promising path toward VLMs that can actively reason and plan in 3D space.

</details>


### [195] [DeepTool: Scaling Interleaved Deliberation in Tool-Integrated Reasoning via Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29568)
*Yang He,Xiao Ding,Bibo Cai,Yufei Zhang,Kai Xiong,Zhouhao Sun,Bing Qin,Ting Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DeepTool is a reinforcement-learning framework that improves LLM tool use by supervising intermediate, interleaved reasoning-and-action steps rather than only final outcomes, yielding large gains on math/tool benchmarks with good token efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tool-integrated reasoning methods let LLMs call external tools, but they lack rich deliberation and strategic planning across multiple tool calls, and standard RL methods suffer from sparse, outcome-only rewards that fail to guide intermediate reasoning and tool invocation. The authors want a way to scale deliberate, stepwise thinking and self-correction within tool use, and to give RL dense, process-level feedback for each turn of thinking–acting–observing.

Method: They propose DeepTool, which has two core components: (1) a data synthesis pipeline that converts extended chain-of-thought solutions into interleaved trajectories of model thoughts, tool actions, and observations, including adversarial perturbations to encourage robustness and self-correction; and (2) a Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning algorithm based on GRPO, where an Action-Centric Process Reward is assigned to intermediate steps to reward good interleaved thinking and accurate tool invocation at every turn, rather than only rewarding final correctness.

Result: On six benchmarks, including AIME24 and HMMT25, DeepTool substantially boosts the performance of Qwen2.5-7B, e.g., from 3.2% to 40.4% on AIME24 and from 0.0% to 28.6% on HMMT25, demonstrating much better tool-integrated reasoning. Additional analysis shows that interleaved thinking under DeepTool achieves these gains with favorable token cost-effectiveness compared to alternatives.

Conclusion: Process-supervised RL with interleaved, tool-centric trajectories can effectively scale deliberate thinking in tool-integrated LLMs, overcoming sparse-reward issues and enabling both robust, self-correcting tool use and strong performance–token efficiency trade-offs. DeepTool thus offers a practical framework for training LLMs to think and act in a tightly coupled, stepwise manner when using external tools.

Abstract: Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) extends LLM capabilities by leveraging external environments. However, existing methods lack the deliberation during sequential tool invocation required for strategic planning and self-correction. While RL mitigates this, conventional approaches for Tool-Integrated Reasoning are hindered by sparse outcome-based rewards, failing to supervise intermediate reasoning steps and tool invocations. To address this, we propose DeepTool, a novel framework that scales deliberate thinking within the interleaved process of thinking, action, and observation at each turn. In DeepTool, we first introduce a synthesis pipeline that evolves extended thinking into interleaved trajectories, integrating adversarial perturbations to ensure robustness and self-correction. Secondly, we devise Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning based on GRPO, which utilizes an Action-Centric Process Reward to reinforce intermediate interleaved thinking and enforce precise tool invocation at every turn. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DeepTool achieves superior performance, boosting Qwen2.5-7B significantly across six benchmarks (e.g., AIME24: 3.2% -> 40.4% and HMMT25: 0.0% -> 28.6%). Furthermore, the token cost-effectiveness analysis confirms the utility of interleaved thinking, demonstrating DeepTool's optimal balance between performance and token efficiency.

</details>


### [196] [GPS-Enhanced Tourist Mobility Modeling with Seasonal Spatial Priors and LLM-Based Activity Chain Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29578)
*Yifan Liu,Yanling Sang,Xishun Liao,Morgan Sun,Bo Yang,Zhiyuan Zhang,Chris Stanford,Haoxuan Ma,Jiaqi Ma*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A four-stage simulation framework is proposed to generate synthetic, demographically aware tourist mobility schedules using GPS-derived spatial priors, survey data, and LLM-based activity-chain generation, validated on Tokyo tourism data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban transportation planning struggles to model tourist mobility because it is non-routine, attraction-driven, and sensitive to factors like season, purpose, and travel group composition. Existing methods either focus only on aggregate spatial patterns without individual-level schedules, or generate synthetic mobility without capturing tourist-specific structures such as conditioning on trip duration, seasonal variation in attraction demand, and household co-travel behavior. There is a need for a method that can create realistic, individual-level tourist schedules that respect these complexities while preserving data privacy.

Method: The paper proposes a four-stage simulation framework: (1) derive month-conditioned spatial priors from aggregated GPS and survey data to represent where tourists tend to go in different months; (2) predict the extent of each tourist trip (e.g., length/duration) based on tourist demographics; (3) assign distance-feasible sequences of city wards to tourists, respecting spatial and travel distance constraints; and (4) use a large language model (LLM) to generate detailed activity chains (schedules) that satisfy both household co-travel rules and spatial constraints. GPS traces are used only in aggregated, privacy-preserving form to build the spatial priors, with no individual trajectories retained.

Result: In experiments on Tokyo tourism, the method first extracts tourist cohorts from GPS data, and this extraction reproduces spatial visitation patterns that match survey-based reference data. The simulated schedules produced by the framework are demographically aligned with observed populations, and the resulting ward-level visitation shares closely match both survey distributions and staypoint-based monthly visitation patterns derived from GPS. This indicates that the model can realistically reproduce tourist spatial and temporal behavior at the ward level.

Conclusion: The framework is shown to be an effective, geographically grounded, and demographically aware approach to tourist mobility modeling. It successfully integrates aggregated GPS data, survey information, demographic-based trip prediction, and LLM-based activity generation to produce realistic synthetic tourist schedules, while preserving privacy and capturing key tourist-specific structures like seasonal variation, trip duration, and household co-travel behavior.

Abstract: Tourist mobility poses a distinct challenge for urban transportation planning. Unlike resident commuting, tourist travel is largely non-routine, attraction driven, and highly sensitive to trip purpose, travel season, and trip member composition. Existing approaches either measure aggregate tourist spatial patterns without generating individual schedules, or synthesize mobility without tourist specific structure such as trip duration conditioning, month varying attraction demand, and household co-travel rules. To address these challenges, we propose a four stage simulation framework combining month conditioned spatial priors derived from GPS and survey data, trip extent prediction from tourist demographics, distance feasible ward sequence assignment, and LLM-based activity chain generation under household and spatial constraints. GPS data are used only in privacy preserving aggregated form as month conditioned spatial priors, with no individual traces retained or exposed. Experiments on tourism in Tokyo demonstrate that the GPS based tourist cohort extraction recovers spatial visitation signatures consistent with survey references, and our framework produces demographically aligned synthetic schedules whose ward-level visitation shares align closely with both survey distributions and staypoint derived monthly visitation patterns. The results demonstrate the framework's effectiveness as a geographically grounded, demographically aware approach to tourist mobility modeling.

</details>


### [197] [FinVerBench: Benchmark Validity and Calibration in Large Language Model Financial Statement Verification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29586)
*Silu Panda*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FinVerBench is a benchmark to test how well LLMs can verify the numerical consistency of financial statements, highlighting that this task requires calibrated judgment under incomplete information, not just arithmetic checks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rigorously evaluate and understand how well large language models can detect inconsistencies in corporate financial statements, recognizing that existing evaluations often reduce this to simple arithmetic despite real-world complexity and incomplete observability.

Method: The authors build FinVerBench from SEC 10-K XBRL filings for 43 S&P 500 companies, define a four-category error taxonomy (arithmetic, cross-statement linkage, year-over-year, and magnitude perturbations), and construct both clean and error-injected statement variants. They run fifteen contemporary LLM configurations (with 14 usable), using a guided-checklist prompt and different rendering choices (unrounded vs realistically rounded), and measure false positive/negative behavior on an observable diagnostic subset of 105 instances.

Result: Most LLM runs exhibit extremely high false positive rates (95–100%) on clean financial statements under the default prompting and unrounded setup, though one calibrated configuration achieves 0% observed false positives. Rendering numerical values more realistically (rounded) materially reduces recall for this calibrated model from 100% to 79% while maintaining 0% observed false positives, showing the sensitivity of performance to presentation and prompting choices.

Conclusion: Financial statement verification for LLMs is a nuanced judgment task influenced by incomplete information, prompt framing, and numerical formatting, not simply arithmetic error detection. FinVerBench offers a construct-valid benchmark for studying these behaviors and calibrating models rather than serving as a static leaderboard, and its resources are publicly released.

Abstract: We introduce FinVerBench, a benchmark and validity study for financial statement verification: determining whether a set of corporate financial statements is numerically consistent from the information shown to the model. FinVerBench is built from SEC 10-K XBRL filings for 43 S&P 500 companies and defines a four-category error taxonomy covering arithmetic, cross-statement linkage, year-over-year, and magnitude perturbations. We attempt fifteen contemporary LLM evaluations and report fourteen complete runs; a Gemini 2.5 Pro run is excluded from the main comparison because 40/108 gateway calls failed. All binary metrics exclude underdetermined positive instances whose perturbed line item is not rendered, leaving a 105-instance observable diagnostic subset (43 clean, 62 error-injected). Under the original guided-checklist prompt on the unrounded diagnostic subset, nine of fourteen complete LLM runs produce 95-100% false positives on clean statements, while one run achieves 0% observed false positives. Benchmark rendering choices materially affect measured recall: on a realistic rounded variant of the same observable subset, the calibrated model's recall is 79.0% with 0% observed FPR, compared with 100.0% recall on the unrounded diagnostic variant. These results support a construct-validity conclusion rather than a final leaderboard: financial statement verification is not merely arithmetic detection, but calibrated judgment under incomplete observability, prompt-induced assumptions, and realistic numerical rendering. FinVerBench and all code are publicly available.

</details>


### [198] [Mind-Omni: A Unified Multi-Task Framework for Brain-Vision-Language Modeling via Discrete Diffusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29591)
*Yizhuo Lu,Changde Du,Qingyu Shi,Hang Chen,Jie Peng,Liuyun Jiang,Shuangchen Zhao,Huiguang He*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Mind-Omni is a unified multi-task brain-computer interface framework that uses discrete diffusion and a brain tokenizer to convert continuous neural signals into tokens, enabling versatile cross-modal encoding/decoding and brain question answering, achieving state-of-the-art multi-task performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing BCI research largely relies on specialized models tailored to single tasks (e.g., only decoding or only encoding), which limits versatility, prevents re-use of knowledge across tasks, and misses potential performance gains from multi-task learning. There is a need for a unified framework that can handle many neural encoding and decoding tasks jointly and reason over neural data in a shared semantic space.

Method: They design Mind-Omni, a discrete diffusion-based framework that unifies seven encoding and decoding tasks. A central component is a Brain Tokenizer that converts heterogeneous, continuous neural signals into standardized discrete tokens. These tokens live in a shared semantic space with other modalities, enabling token-level interactions and transformations between brain activity and other data types. They also construct a Brain Question Answering (BQA) instruction-tuning dataset to train the model for advanced reasoning about neural data.

Result: Mind-Omni achieves new state-of-the-art performance among unified multi-task neural modeling frameworks and shows clear benefits from multi-task synergy. It matches or outperforms larger, task-specific models on several tasks, demonstrating that a single, versatile model can be both competitive and more flexible than specialized systems.

Conclusion: A discrete-token, diffusion-based, multi-task framework can effectively model neural activity across diverse encoding and decoding tasks, revealing strong cross-task synergies. Mind-Omni serves as evidence that unified brain modeling is feasible and competitive with specialized approaches, suggesting a promising direction toward foundation models for brain activity. The released code supports reproducibility and future extensions.

Abstract: Modeling the interplay between external stimuli and internal neural representations is a pivotal research area for Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs). A major limitation of prior work is the prevailing paradigm of specialized, single-task models, which curtails versatility and neglects inter-task synergies. To address this, we propose Mind-Omni, the first versatile framework that unifies seven distinct encoding and decoding tasks through a discrete diffusion paradigm. At its core is a novel Brain Tokenizer that transforms heterogeneous, continuous brain signals into standardized, discrete tokens. This enables direct, token-level interactions for mutual understanding and generation between any two or more modalities within a shared semantic space. To unlock advanced reasoning capabilities, we further curate a specialized Brain Question Answering (BQA) instruction-tuning dataset. Our model not only establishes a new state-of-the-art among multi-task unified frameworks but also provides strong evidence for multi-task synergy. By demonstrating performance competitive with, and at times superior to, larger specialized models, our work offers a powerful new paradigm for neural modeling and paves the way for foundation models of neural activity. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/ReedOnePeck/Mind-Omni.

</details>


### [199] [HiKEY: Hierarchical Multimodal Retrieval for Open-Domain Document Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29606)
*Joongmin Shin,Gyuho Shim,Jeongbae Park,Jaehyung Seo,Heuiseok Lim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: HiKEY is a hierarchical, multimodal retrieval framework for RAG in open-domain QA over large document corpora.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems on large industrial document corpora suffer from routing failure (choosing the wrong document among thousands) and evidence fragmentation (relevant information is scattered across text, tables, and figures, and within token limits). Existing flat chunk or page-based approaches cannot precisely locate documents or connect multimodal evidence efficiently.

Method: HiKEY builds a hierarchical tree/graph of each document via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP), encoding parent-child relationships (document, sections, subsections, elements like tables/figures). It then uses a hierarchical coarse-to-fine retrieval: (1) global routing with hierarchical indexing to quickly prune the search space, and (2) fine-grained retrieval that ranks sections using multimodal fusion of text, tables, and figures. Finally, it constructs a compact evidence subgraph using a hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy to stay within token budgets while preserving key evidence links.

Result: On open-domain QA benchmarks, HiKEY outperforms page-based and flat chunk baselines, improving retrieval recall by up to 12.9% and end-to-end QA accuracy/metrics by up to 6.8%.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling document hierarchy and multimodal structure as a heterogeneous graph, and using hierarchical coarse-to-fine retrieval plus structure-aware packing, substantially improves RAG retrieval and QA performance over large industrial corpora compared to flat chunk or page-level approaches.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) for document-based Open-domain Question Answering (ODQA) on large-scale industrial corpora faces two critical bottlenecks: routing failure in locating the correct document and evidence fragmentation in integrating scattered information. Existing approaches relying on flat text chunks or page-level images inherently struggle to (i) precisely pinpoint the target document among thousands of candidates and (ii) organically connect multimodal evidence, such as tables and figures, within a limited token budget. To address these challenges, we propose HiKEY, a hierarchical tree-based multimodal retrieval framework that elevates document hierarchy to a first-class retrieval signal. Instead of simple chunking, HiKEY reconstructs a logical heterogeneous graph via Document Hierarchical Parsing (DHP), explicitly encoding parent-child relationships. Adopting a hierarchical coarse-to-fine strategy, the framework (1) performs global routing to rapidly prune the search space using hierarchical indexing, and (2) conducts fine-grained retrieval to rank sections by employing a multimodal fusion strategy that captures the most discriminative evidence. Finally, HiKEY assembles a token-efficient evidence subgraph via a hybrid structural-semantic packing strategy. Experiments on ODQA benchmarks demonstrate that HiKEY significantly outperforms page- and chunk-based baselines, improving retrieval recall by up to 12.9% and end-to-end QA performance by up to 6.8%.

</details>


### [200] [Improving Collaborative Storytelling with a Multi-Agent Framework Based on Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29625)
*Arturo Valdivia,Paolo Burelli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies a child-focused, physical-board-game setting where children and LLMs co-create written stories, using a multi-agent Writer-Editor LLM framework that iteratively improves story quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Co-creation with AI has mostly been studied with adults and in digital environments, leaving a gap in understanding and supporting AI-assisted creative writing experiences for children, especially in tangible, playful contexts like board games. The authors aim to design systems that generate narratives appropriate and engaging for young players.

Method: They propose a multi-agent LLM framework centered on an iterative Writer-Editor loop: one LLM acts as a story writer, another as an editor that evaluates the story and gives feedback, and the writer revises accordingly. They run a simulation study with multiple LLMs, iterating this loop several times and assessing the perceived quality of the resulting stories at each step.

Result: The simulation shows that iterative writer–editor interactions reliably enhance the perceived quality of generated children’s stories over successive refinement loops, with noticeable improvements after each round of feedback and revision.

Conclusion: An LLM-based Writer-Editor multi-agent framework can effectively improve narrative quality in a child-oriented co-creative board game context, and only a few refinement iterations are typically needed to reach high-quality interactive storytelling outputs.

Abstract: The topic of Co-creation, i.e., AI agents interacting with humans to generate outputs (e.g., art), has gained significant attention recently. However, most studies focus on adult-human interactions in a digital setting. This paper explores a novel ludic co-creation scenario involving children and Large Language Models (LLMs) interacting through a physical board game to create written stories. Our goal is to develop a multi-agent framework capable of producing high-quality narratives suitable for young players. At the core of our approach is an iterative Writer-Editor process in which one LLM generates stories while another evaluates them and provides feedback for refinement. Through a simulation study involving multiple LLMs, we show that this iterative interaction consistently improves the perceived quality of generated stories across successive loops. The results indicate that a small number of refinement steps may be sufficient to achieve high-quality outputs in interactive storytelling systems.

</details>


### [201] [Beyond Attack Success Rate: Temporal Logit Observability for LLM Safety Failures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29629)
*Junyoung Park,Sunghwan Park,Seongyong Ju,Jaewoo Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Temporal Logit Observability (TLO), a training-free diagnostic that analyzes how jailbreak attacks unfold over time in language models by tracking a compliance–refusal logit margin, offering richer insight than binary Attack Success Rate (ASR) and enabling an effective early-stop defense.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of jailbreak attacks rely heavily on Attack Success Rate (ASR), which only provides a binary outcome (success/failure) at the end of generation. This coarse metric cannot distinguish between different temporal dynamics or mechanisms that lead to equally harmful outputs. Consequently, it obscures nuanced failure modes, limits understanding of model behavior during attacks, and hinders the design of more targeted defenses. The paper aims to expose and characterize the hidden trajectories that models follow during jailbreaks using only logits, without extra training or complex instrumentation.

Method: The authors propose Temporal Logit Observability (TLO), which monitors, step by step during decoding, the margin between logits associated with “compliance” and “refusal” behaviors. Using a fixed lexicon for compliance and refusal tokens, they project these margins into a calibrated 2D plane, placing each model–attack instance as a trajectory or point that characterizes how the model’s stance evolves over time. They evaluate TLO on four aligned LLMs across three jailbreak paradigms, compare the resulting geometry with hidden-state-based refusal-direction probes, and derive a simple early-stop rule that triggers when trajectories enter risky regions of the plane.

Result: Across the tested models and jailbreak paradigms, TLO reveals that attacks with similar ASR can follow distinct temporal patterns, landing at clearly separated regions in the 2D plane. This shows that the same model can fail for genuinely different reasons, which ASR alone cannot capture. The geometric structure produced by TLO largely aligns with refusal-direction probes derived from hidden states, validating that the logit-based diagnostic tracks meaningful safety-related dynamics. One model exposes the limitations of the fixed-lexicon approach, indicating boundary conditions for TLO’s effectiveness. Using these insights, the authors design an early-stop rule that more than halves successful jailbreaks while introducing no false positives on benign queries in their experiments.

Conclusion: Attack evaluation should move beyond a single binary success label and instead capture when and how safety failures unfold. Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) offers a practical, training-free way to make these dynamics visible directly from logits, providing a richer diagnostic signal than ASR. It differentiates between qualitatively different jailbreak mechanisms, aligns well with more complex hidden-state probes, and supports simple yet effective defenses such as early stopping. The work suggests that incorporating temporal and geometric views of refusal–compliance behavior can significantly improve both safety analysis and defense design for aligned language models.

Abstract: Attack Success Rate (ASR) evaluates each jailbreak with a single yes/no label at the end of generation, telling us whether a failure happened but not how it unfolded. Two attacks that produce equally harmful outputs may have followed completely different paths, and ASR cannot tell them apart. We make those hidden paths observable from logits alone. Temporal Logit Observability (TLO) is a training-free diagnostic that watches a compliance-refusal margin during decoding and places each model-attack condition on a calibrated 2D plane. By design, this plane is most informative exactly where ASR is least informative: among attacks that succeed for genuinely different reasons. Across four aligned LLMs and three jailbreak paradigms, attacks with nearly identical ASR land at clearly different points on the plane: the same model can fail through different temporal patterns. The geometry matches refusal-direction probes from hidden states on most conditions, with one model showing the limit of our fixed-lexicon approach. A simple early-stop rule derived from TLO cuts successful jailbreaks by more than half, without false alarms on plain benign queries. Safety evaluation should report when and how a failure unfolds, not only whether it occurred. TLO makes the first two observable from logits alone.

</details>


### [202] [VikingMem: A Memory Base Management System for Stateful LLM-based Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29640)
*Jiajie Fu,Junwen Chen,Mengzhao Wang,Aoxiang He,Maojia Sheng,Xiangyu Ke,Yifan Zhu,Yunjun Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Memory Base, a general paradigm and system (VikingMem) for managing long-term, stateful memories for LLM-driven applications, achieving better memory retrieval with low latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have limited context windows, making it hard to maintain state over long-term interactions. Existing memory methods either extract memories too simplistically or are tightly tailored to a single use case (like chatbots), harming generalization to other tasks.

Method: They define a Memory Base paradigm with three principles: (1) selective extraction of high-value memories from raw interaction streams; (2) stateful and evolving memories that are summarized, corrected, and temporally weighted; (3) a generalizable abstraction for transfer across applications. They implement this as VikingMem on the VikingDB vector engine, using interconnected event and entity abstractions, event-centric extraction, dynamic entity updates, topic-wise temporal compression, and time-weighted recall to create and manage summary memories over time.

Result: On long-term memory benchmarks, VikingMem improves memory retrieval effectiveness by up to 30% over baselines, while keeping latency low enough for interactive use.

Conclusion: The Memory Base paradigm and its implementation VikingMem provide an effective, general, and efficient way to manage the persistent state of long-term interactions for LLM-based systems, outperforming existing memory approaches in retrieval quality without sacrificing latency.

Abstract: Large Language Models have revolutionized interactive applications; however, their finite context windows pose a critical data management challenge for maintaining stateful, long-term interactions. Existing memory approaches often rely on simplistic extraction methods that lead to incomplete memories or use rigid, single-purpose memory extraction prompts tailored to a single use case, such as chatbots. Consequently, they lack generalizability and perform poorly across diverse downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we introduce the Memory Base, a novel data management paradigm for managing the persistent state of long-term interactions. It is characterized by three core principles: selective extraction of high-value memories from raw information streams; inherent statefulness and evolution, where memory content is progressively summarized, corrected, and temporally weighted to prioritize recent interactions; and a generalizable abstraction paradigm designed for robust transferability across diverse applications, including education, recommendation, and agent memory. Building on this foundation, we present VikingMem, an end-to-end Memory Base Management System implemented on the VikingDB vector engine. VikingMem materializes this paradigm through interconnected event and entity abstractions. It features event-centric memory extraction to selectively handle complex information streams, while entities are dynamically updated by events to achieve stateful evolution. Using temporal compression via a topic-wise timeline and time-weighted recall, the system progressively produces high-level summary memories, prioritizes recent items, and compresses and fades older ones. Extensive evaluations on long-term memory benchmarks demonstrate that VikingMem outperformes baselines by up to 30% in memory retrieval effectiveness while maintaining the low latency essential for interactive applications.

</details>


### [203] [LLM-Evolved Domain-Independent Heuristics for Symbolic AI Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29649)
*Elliot Gestrin,Jendrik Seipp*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evolves domain-independent planning heuristics using LLM-guided evolutionary search that outperform hand-crafted state-of-the-art heuristics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional symbolic AI planning relies on hand-engineered heuristics, which are strong but costly to design and often domain-specific. While LLMs have recently been used to generate heuristics, existing LLM-generated heuristics work only for specific domains, not arbitrary planning tasks. There is a need to automatically generate general-purpose, domain-independent heuristics that can match or exceed the performance of human-designed ones, and to better understand the tradeoff between heuristic informedness and computational speed.

Method: The authors use an evolutionary search framework where an LLM mutates parent heuristic implementations written in C++. Candidate heuristics are stored in a MAP-Elites archive indexed by two axes: heuristic informedness and evaluation speed. Fitness is computed by combining problem coverage (how many tasks are solved) with solving time. Evolution is seeded either from a trivial blind heuristic or from the strong FF heuristic. The resulting heuristics are compiled and evaluated on planning benchmarks, and compared to a wide range of standard hand-engineered heuristics, whose informedness-speed tradeoffs are also systematically benchmarked.

Result: The evolved LLM-generated heuristics are the first domain-independent heuristics to surpass state-of-the-art hand-engineered baselines in symbolic AI planning. On unseen test domains, the best evolved heuristic solves more tasks than even the strongest existing heuristic. The whole evolved heuristic suite spans the Pareto frontier of the tradeoff between informedness and speed. Evolution seeded from the trivial blind heuristic unexpectedly outperforms evolution seeded from the strong FF heuristic, even for evolved heuristics that resemble FF variants. Increased LLM reasoning effort significantly improves the fraction of candidate heuristics that successfully compile, but has limited impact on the quality of those that do compile.

Conclusion: LLM-guided evolutionary search can automatically discover domain-independent planning heuristics that outperform carefully engineered human baselines, while remaining compatible with existing symbolic planners. By evolving C++ heuristics that act as drop-in replacements, the approach preserves the soundness and completeness properties of the underlying search algorithms. The analysis of informedness-speed tradeoffs and seeding strategies suggests new ways to design and optimize heuristics, and demonstrates that starting from minimal heuristics can be more effective than refining strong existing ones. The work opens a path toward automated heuristic discovery for general planning and potentially other search-based AI tasks.

Abstract: Heuristic search is the dominant paradigm in symbolic AI planning, and the strongest heuristics are the result of decades of work by planning researchers. Recent work has shown that large language models (LLMs) can design heuristics for individual planning domains, but no LLM-generated heuristic has so far worked on arbitrary planning tasks. In this paper, we use evolutionary search to produce the first LLM-generated domain-independent heuristics that exceed the hand-engineered state of the art. We let an LLM mutate parent heuristics written in C++, store candidates in a MAP-Elites archive keyed on informedness and speed and calculate fitness scores by blending coverage with solving time. To place the evolved programs in context, we additionally benchmark a broad set of hand-engineered heuristics on their informedness-speed tradeoff, which to our knowledge has not been done before. On unseen testing domains, our best evolved heuristic solves more tasks than even the strongest baseline, with our full heuristic suite spanning the Pareto frontier of said tradeoff. We also find that seeding evolution from the trivial blind heuristic outperforms seeding from the strong FF heuristic, even when the resulting program is itself an FF variant, and that LLM reasoning effort affects how often candidates compile much more than the quality of those that do. Because the evolved programs are plain C++, they slot into existing planners as drop-in replacements and inherit the soundness and completeness guarantees of the underlying search.

</details>


### [204] [Think Fast, Talk Smart: Partitioning Deterministic and Neural Computation for Structured Health Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29652)
*Kai-Chen Cheng,Haejun Han,David Q. Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a pipeline, Think Fast, Talk Smart, where deterministic code handles recurring analysis of structured health data and a single bounded LLM call generates patient-facing text, improving accuracy, policy compliance, and cost versus standard prompting baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to turn structured health data (e.g., wearable data, biomarkers, vitals) into text, but naive prompting leads to issues: numerical inaccuracies, unsupported causal claims, poor policy adherence, and high cost for recurring outputs. The authors want to identify which parts of this generation task should be handled by deterministic computation rather than left to the LLM at runtime.

Method: They build a sleep-health insight pipeline, Think Fast, Talk Smart, in which deterministic code first performs all recurring analyses, computes key metrics, and structures facts; then a single, constrained LLM "writer" call turns these verified facts into natural language. They compare this against structured zero-shot and few-shot one-call LLM baselines across 280 user-nights and six different models. Through layer replacement experiments, they systematically swap deterministic components with LLM-based ones (for comparison, ranking, attribution, and interface generation) to observe how performance changes.

Result: The proposed pipeline yields lower numeric error, fewer instruction-compliance mistakes, and lower overall cost than baselines that rely on one-shot or few-shot prompting directly over structured data. Layer replacement experiments show specific failure modes when LLMs are used for tasks better handled by code: LLM-based comparison increases numeric error; LLM-based ranking harms policy selection; LLM-based attribution encourages unsupported causal and explanatory language; and having an LLM generate the interface for the writer reintroduces factual and structural errors despite deterministic upstream processing.

Conclusion: For structured health-text generation, recurring analytical responsibilities should be implemented as deterministic code, while LLMs should be confined to expressing already-verified facts through carefully bounded interfaces. This division of labor improves accuracy, policy adherence, and cost-efficiency, suggesting a general design principle for LLM applications in safety-critical, data-driven domains.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being used to generate health text from structured records such as wearable time series, biomarkers, vitals, and care-management logs. For recurring health outputs, fluency is not enough: systems must remain faithful to source data, ground explanatory claims in available evidence, follow stated policies, emit machine-readable outputs, and run cheaply enough for repeated use. We ask which responsibilities in structured health generation should be deterministic computation rather than runtime LLM prompting.
  We introduce Think Fast, Talk Smart, a sleep-health insight pipeline in which deterministic code performs recurring analysis before one bounded LLM writer call. Across 280 user-nights and six models, achieves lower numeric error, lower instruction-compliance error, and lower end-to-end cost than structured zero-shot and few-shot one-call baselines.
  Layer replacement reveals contract-specific failures: LLM comparison raises numeric error, LLM ranking degrades policy selection, LLM attribution increases unsupported causal language, and an LLM-generated writer interface reintroduces errors even after upstream facts are deterministic. The results support a broader design rule: let code own recurring analysis, and let LLMs express verified facts within bounded interfaces.

</details>


### [205] [PTCG-Bench: Can LLM Agents Master Pokémon Trading Card Game?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29653)
*Dongdong Hua,Yifei Sun,Renhong Huang,Feng Gao,Chunping Wang,Yang Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PTCG-Bench is a benchmark based on the Pokémon Trading Card Game for evaluating how well LLM agents make strategic decisions and improve over time in a complex, realistic environment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for autonomous agents do not adequately capture human-like strategic learning and adaptation in complex, evolving environments. Humans can quickly develop strategies in intricate games after limited experience, but current LLM-based agents lack strong evaluation frameworks for such capabilities. The authors aim to provide a benchmark that tests both immediate decision quality and longer-term self-evolution of agents in a realistic, strategically rich domain.

Method: The authors build PTCG-Bench on the Pokémon Trading Card Game, which offers a strategically complex and realistic interactive environment. They design the benchmark to evaluate LLM agents on two levels: (1) in-game decision-making performance within this single complex environment, and (2) the agents’ ability to self-evolve based on accumulated experience across games. They also introduce a modular harness with ablation studies that separate the impact of the harness (e.g., prompting, tools, scaffolding) from the underlying model capability, enabling clearer interpretation of results.

Result: Experiments using PTCG-Bench demonstrate that current LLM agents can reach non-trivial levels of gameplay performance in this complex card game. However, the agents struggle to achieve sustained and stable improvement over time, indicating limitations in self-evolution. Additionally, the results show that performance is quite sensitive to the design of the agent harness, meaning that implementation details significantly affect outcomes.

Conclusion: PTCG-Bench provides a new benchmark for studying LLM agents’ strategic decision-making and self-evolution in a realistic, complex environment. The findings reveal that while LLMs can play reasonably well, robust long-term self-improvement remains difficult and is heavily influenced by harness design. The authors expect this benchmark to drive future work on harness-aware methods and more capable self-evolving agents for interactive environments.

Abstract: Given a strategically complex board game, human players can quickly learn to devise strategies after playing a few rounds. Autonomous agents require similar capabilities in realistic interactive environments, yet existing agent benchmarks often fail to fully capture such strategic and evolving decision-making scenarios. We present PTCG-Bench, a benchmark built on the Pok'{e}mon Trading Card Game (PTCG) that evaluates LLM agents at two complementary levels: (1) their decision-making performance within a single complex environment, and (2) their ability to self-evolving through accumulated experience. We further include a modular harness ablation to better interpret agent performance without conflating it with model capability. Our experiments show that, although LLM agents can achieve non-trivial gameplay performance, sustained and stable self-evolution remains challenging, and performance is sensitive to harness design. We hope that PTCG-Bench will facilitate future research on harness-aware and self-evolving agents in realistic interactive environments.

</details>


### [206] [TRACE: Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements for LLM CoT Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29656)
*Yundong Kim,Heyoung Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces TRACE, a metric for evaluating LLM chain-of-thought reasoning using Toulmin’s argumentation theory and metacognition, correlating well with accuracy and useful as an RL reward.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating open-ended LLM outputs is hard because there is often no ground-truth answer, and existing metrics focus on final-answer accuracy or shallow statistics without examining the reasoning steps themselves. The authors want a way to directly assess the quality and structure of the reasoning process in chain-of-thought outputs.

Method: They design TRACE, a reasoning assessment metric grounded in Toulmin’s argumentation model (claims, data, warrants, etc.) combined with Flavell’s metacognitive framework. TRACE analyzes chain-of-thought traces to evaluate how arguments are constructed, focusing on logical structure rather than only correctness of the final answer. They run experiments on 26.3K QA examples across 7 reasoning models, compute TRACE scores on CoTs, and compare these scores with standard accuracy metrics. They also use TRACE as a reward signal in reinforcement learning to train models and compare against accuracy-only reward baselines.

Result: TRACE scores show a strong correlation (r=0.74) with benchmark QA accuracy across 26.3K samples and 7 models. When used as a reward in RL, TRACE-based training outperforms baselines that rely only on accuracy as the reward signal, indicating that encouraging better reasoning structure improves performance.

Conclusion: TRACE provides a complementary and more process-aware evaluation metric for LLM outputs by directly inspecting the structure and soundness of reasoning. Because logically sound reasoning correlates with and improves answer quality, TRACE is useful both for evaluation of open-ended outputs and as a training signal (via RL) to elicit better reasoning from models.

Abstract: Evaluating open-ended outputs from large language models (LLMs) remains challenging due to the absence of ground truth. Existing metrics rely on final-answer accuracy or surface-level statistics, leaving the reasoning process itself unexamined. We introduce TRACE (Toulmin-based Reasoning Assessment through Constructive Elements), a metric that analyzes Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning processes. Rather than judging outcomes, TRACE inspects how arguments are constructed by integrating Toulmin's argumentation theory with Flavell's metacognitive framework to assess reasoning structure. Experiments on 26.3K QA samples across 7 reasoning models show strong correlation with benchmark accuracy (r=0.74). Furthermore, TRACE is effective as a reinforcement learning reward signal, outperforming accuracy-only baselines. Together, these results indicate that logically sound reasoning leads to higher-quality answers. TRACE thus serves as a complementary metric for evaluating open-ended outputs. Code is available at https://github.com/hyyangkisti/trace.

</details>


### [207] [GRASP: Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer for Self-Improving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29668)
*Johannes Moll,Jean-Philippe Corbeil,Jiazhen Pan,Martin Hadamitzky,Daniel Rueckert,Lisa Adams,Keno Bressem*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GRASP is a regression-aware method to iteratively improve LLM agents’ procedural skills by editing a bounded skill library under strict validation, yielding large reliability gains in structured environments such as clinical FHIR benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents in structured, operational environments often fail due to lacking procedural knowledge, and existing self-improvement techniques accumulate natural language skills or notes without verifying that new guidance does not break previously correct behaviors, leading to silent regressions. There is a need for a principled way to update an agent’s skill set that maximizes improvement while explicitly controlling regressions.

Method: The authors propose GRASP (Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer), which models agent improvement as a sequence of edits to a limited-size skill library. New skill candidates are generated (with comparative proposal generation), and each candidate is evaluated on a balanced held-out probe dataset. A hard regression budget and an acceptance gate ensure that a candidate skill is only admitted if it yields a net performance improvement while not exceeding allowed regressions relative to the current library. Skills are written in natural language but are tightly validated before incorporation. The procedure is tested with multiple base LLMs acting as agents in FHIR-based clinical environments and other domains.

Result: On the MedAgentBench clinical benchmark, GRASP improves the gpt-oss-120b model’s performance from 40.6% to 88.8%, outperforming the strongest of five self-improvement baselines by 21 percentage points and improving four other base models by 17.2–40.3 points. Ablation studies show that the main performance gains come from comparative proposal generation, the acceptance gate, and the hard regression budget; naïve skill writing without such validation provides no benefit over using no skills at all. Beyond clinical tasks, GRASP improves performance in three out of four additional non-clinical environments, with limited gains only where the action space is highly open-ended. Frozen skill libraries transfer across models, where skills learned with a stronger model significantly help weaker executors, while the converse does not hold; this asymmetric transfer is not matched by ungated baselines.

Conclusion: GRASP provides a robust, regression-aware framework for improving LLM agents in structured environments by viewing learning as carefully gated edits to a constrained skill library. It substantially increases reliability and performance across multiple models and domains, highlights the importance of explicit regression control over naïve self-improvement, and demonstrates that validated skill libraries can transfer across models, especially from stronger to weaker agents. The approach appears particularly effective when the environment’s action space is structured rather than open-ended.

Abstract: LLM agents acting in structured environments fail in operational rather than conversational ways, and reliability depends on procedural knowledge of the environment. Prior self-improvement methods accumulate natural-language guidance without checking that each new item preserves previously correct behavior, so a note that fixes one trajectory can silently regress another. We introduce GRASP (Gated Regression-Aware Skill Proposer), which treats agent improvement as a sequence of edits to a bounded skill library, admitting each candidate only if it produces a net improvement on a balanced held-out probe under a hard regression budget. We evaluate GRASP across five base models (gpt-oss-120b, DeepSeek V4 Flash, Gemini 3.1 Flash Lite, GPT-4.1, GPT-5.4) on two FHIR-based clinical benchmarks. On MedAgentBench, GRASP lifts gpt-oss-120b from 40.6% to 88.8%, exceeds the strongest of five self-improvement baselines by 21.0 points, and improves every other base model by 17.2 to 40.3 points. Ablations attribute the gain to comparative proposal generation, the acceptance gate, and the hard regression budget rather than to skill writing itself, which without validation is no better than using no skills. The mechanism generalizes beyond the clinical domain, improving agents on three of four non-clinical environments and remaining flat only where the action space is open-ended. Frozen libraries transfer across models, where skills from a stronger model improve weaker executors beyond what they learn for themselves while the reverse does not, an asymmetry that no ungated baseline reproduces.

</details>


### [208] [NICE: A Theory-Grounded Diagnostic Benchmark for Social Intelligence of LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29685)
*Yunjin Qi,Zhaojun Jiang,Xuan Wu,Hanxi Pan,Yixuan Wang,Yanfang Liu,Xiang Ji,Churu Yu,Chunyuan Zheng,Yingze Chen,Jie He,Liuqing Chen,Zaifeng Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes and validates a theory-grounded framework and benchmark, NICE, to holistically and diagnostically evaluate the social intelligence of large language models in Chinese contexts, revealing specific weaknesses in communication-related capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing social intelligence benchmarks for LLMs lack a unified, theory-based structure of social abilities, making it hard to perform fine-grained, diagnostic evaluation of models used in social applications like emotional support and customer service. The authors want to systematically measure and localize social intelligence strengths and weaknesses in LLMs to improve safety and quality in human-AI interaction.

Method: The authors conduct a literature review on social intelligence and perform multi-stage expert validation following psychometric principles to construct a structured framework: 4 categories and 11 dimensions, each with finer-grained capability facets. They then build NICE, a 137-item diagnostic benchmark instantiated in representative Chinese social contexts, and evaluate 5 state-of-the-art LLMs plus a human reference group on these items, analyzing aggregate performance and facet-level weaknesses.

Result: The resulting framework organizes social intelligence of LLMs into 4 categories, 11 dimensions, and multiple capability facets, and is instantiated as the NICE benchmark with 137 Chinese-context items. Empirical evaluation shows that frontier LLMs achieve higher overall accuracy than the human reference group but exhibit consistent deficiencies in Communication, particularly in multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony, which the framework successfully localizes.

Conclusion: NICE provides the first holistic, theory-grounded and psychometrically informed diagnostic evaluation of LLM social intelligence, enabling fine-grained identification of socially consequential weaknesses. The findings highlight that even high-performing LLMs struggle with specific communication facets, suggesting that future model development and safety work should target these localized social capabilities rather than relying solely on aggregate performance scores.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in social contexts such as emotional companionship and customer service, measuring their social intelligence has become critical to the quality and safety of human-AI interaction. However, existing social intelligence benchmarks lack a unified framework that organizes social abilities into a unified structure, and therefore cannot enable fine-grained diagnosis. To build the first holistic diagnostic evaluation grounded in social theory, we first construct a social intelligence framework through a literature review and multi-stage expert validation guided by psychometric principles. The resulting framework includes 4 categories and 11 dimensions, each further specified by fine-grained capability facets. Building on this framework, we introduce NICE (Norm, Interaction, Cognition, Experience), a diagnostic benchmark of 137 items operationalized through representative Chinese contexts. Across 5 frontier LLMs and a human reference group, models score higher in aggregate accuracy yet show a consistent weakness in Communication, which the framework localizes to 3 specific capability facets: multi-turn communication, nonverbal communication, and synchrony. NICE thus reframes social intelligence evaluation toward theory-grounded diagnosis of socially consequential weaknesses in LLMs.

</details>


### [209] [Reliable Reasoning with Large Language Models via Preference-Based Maximum Satisfiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29687)
*Pedro Orvalho,Marta Kwiatkowska,Guillem Alenyà,Felip Manyà*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They use large language models to write Python code that formulates user preferences and constraints as MaxSAT problems, then solve them with an exact solver and verify the solutions, greatly improving correctness on preference-based optimisation tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are good at understanding natural language but perform poorly on complex optimisation with many constraints and preferences, which is crucial in domains like robotics. Existing prompting strategies (direct answers, chain-of-thought, program-of-thought) rarely yield feasible or optimal solutions in such settings, so there is a need for a method that leverages LLMs’ language abilities while delegating exact optimisation to reliable solvers.

Method: Given a natural-language description of a constrained, preference-based optimisation problem, an LLM generates Python code that encodes the problem as a preference-based MaxSAT instance. An exact MaxSAT solver then optimises this encoding. The authors introduce an independent verification step that checks feasibility and optimality of the solver’s output against a canonical MaxSAT encoding, allowing for differences in encodings and handling multiple optimal solutions. They evaluate several open-source and closed LLMs on three families of preference-based reasoning tasks, comparing their hybrid pipeline with direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines using the same models.

Result: Across the three families of preference-based reasoning benchmarks, the MaxSAT-based hybrid pipeline yields much higher acceptance rates (feasible and optimal solutions) than direct-answer, chain-of-thought, or program-of-thought approaches, in some cases surpassing 80% acceptance. The baselines, by contrast, rarely produce even feasible solutions. The solver-backed code generation and verification pipeline consistently improves reliability across different LLMs.
