Conclusion: Combining LLM-driven code generation with preference-based MaxSAT solving provides a robust way to handle natural-language optimisation problems with complex constraints and preferences. The approach allows solver-verifiable optimisation under generated encodings and, through independent verification against a canonical encoding, substantially improves correctness relative to standard prompting-based baselines. This hybrid symbolic–neural framework is an effective path toward trustworthy LLM usage in constrained decision-making domains.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at understanding natural language but struggle with optimisation tasks involving multiple constraints and user-defined preferences, which commonly arise in domains such as robotics. We propose a hybrid reasoning approach in which LLMs externalise reasoning through code generation. Given a natural language problem description, an LLM generates Python code that encodes user-defined constraints and preferences as a preference-based Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT) problem, which is then solved by an exact MaxSAT solver. To ensure correctness, solutions returned by the model-generated code are independently verified for feasibility and optimality against a canonical MaxSAT encoding, allowing for different encodings and multiple optimal solutions. We evaluate our approach using both open-source and closed-access LLMs on three families of preference-based reasoning tasks, and compare it against direct-answer, chain-of-thought, and program-of-thought baselines using the same models. While these baselines rarely produce feasible solutions, the MaxSAT-based pipeline achieves substantially higher acceptance rates, in some cases exceeding 80%. Our results demonstrate that LLM-driven code generation combined with preference-based MaxSAT enables solver-verifiable optimisation with respect to generated encodings, and substantially improves correctness under independently verified reference semantics.

</details>


### [210] [FHRFormer: A Self-Supervised Masked Transformer Framework for Fetal Heart Rate Time-Series Inpainting and Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29695)
*Kjersti Engan,Neel Kanwal,Anita Yeconia,Ladislaus Blacy,Yuda Munyaw,Estomih Mduma,Hege Ersdal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a masked transformer-based autoencoder to reconstruct missing fetal heart rate (FHR) signals, improving data quality for AI-based risk prediction and enabling robust signal inpainting and forecasting, even with long gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A significant proportion of newborns need breathing assistance at birth, and continuous fetal heart rate monitoring is key for early risk detection. Wearable FHR monitors enable long-term monitoring but suffer from signal dropouts due to sensor displacement and maternal/fetal movement. These missing data segments degrade the usefulness of recordings and hinder the development of reliable AI-based risk prediction algorithms. Existing interpolation methods cannot adequately preserve the temporal and spectral properties of FHR signals, motivating a more advanced reconstruction approach.

Method: The paper introduces a masked transformer-based autoencoder that learns to reconstruct missing portions of fetal heart rate signals. The model jointly captures local temporal dynamics and frequency-domain characteristics by operating on appropriately transformed representations of the FHR data. During training, portions of the signal are masked to simulate missing data, and the autoencoder is optimized to reconstruct them, enabling both signal inpainting and short-term forecasting. The architecture is designed to be robust to different gap lengths and positions in the time series.

Result: The proposed method successfully reconstructs FHR signals across a range of missing-data durations, outperforming simple interpolation techniques, particularly in its ability to preserve the spectral structure and clinically relevant variability patterns. It demonstrates stable performance for both inpainting of internal gaps and forecasting of future segments, indicating good generalization across different missingness scenarios.

Conclusion: A masked transformer-based autoencoder is an effective solution for reconstructing missing fetal heart rate data, capturing both temporal and frequency characteristics better than traditional interpolation. This enables higher-quality continuous FHR time series suitable for downstream AI-based risk modeling. The method can be applied retrospectively to improve existing research datasets and, in the future, integrated into wearable monitoring systems to provide more reliable real-time fetal risk assessment and earlier detection of infants who may need breathing assistance or other interventions at birth.

Abstract: Approximately 10% of newborns require assistance to initiate breathing at birth, and around 5% need ventilation support. Fetal heart rate (FHR) monitoring plays a crucial role in assessing fetal well-being during prenatal care, enabling the detection of abnormal patterns and supporting timely obstetric interventions to mitigate fetal risks during labor. Applying artificial intelligence (AI) methods to analyze large datasets of continuous FHR monitoring episodes with diverse outcomes may offer novel insights into predicting the risk of needing breathing assistance or interventions. Recent advances in wearable FHR monitors have enabled continuous fetal monitoring without compromising maternal mobility. However, sensor displacement during maternal movement, as well as changes in fetal or maternal position, often lead to signal dropout, resulting in gaps in recorded FHR data. Such missing data limits the extraction of meaningful insights and complicates automated (AI-based) analysis. Traditional approaches to handling missing data, such as simple interpolation techniques, often fail to preserve the spectral characteristics of the signals. In this paper, we propose a masked transformer-based autoencoder approach to reconstruct missing FHR signals by capturing both local temporal and frequency components of the data. The proposed method demonstrates robustness across varying durations of missing data and can be used for signal inpainting and forecasting. The proposed approach can be applied retrospectively to research datasets to support the development of AI-based risk algorithms. In the future, the proposed method could be integrated into wearable FHR monitoring devices to achieve earlier and more robust risk detection.

</details>


### [211] [Beyond Trajectory Rewards: Step-level Credit Assignment for Agentic Search via Graph Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29697)
*Yuchen Liu,Yingjie Feng,Lixiong Qin,Jiasi Chen,Jianing Yu,Sheng Gao,Sheng Yang,Weiran Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a new step-level reward (GDCR) and a training algorithm (SAPO) for agentic search by leveraging an entity-relation graph to measure each step’s contribution toward the final answer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In agentic search, we want to train agents that take effective intermediate steps. Existing outcome (trajectory-level) rewards don’t tell us which steps were helpful, and existing step-level rewards usually require expensive tree sampling. The authors want a cheaper and more accurate way to measure each step’s contribution toward solving an information-seeking (IS) task.

Method: Model world knowledge as a latent world graph and each information-seeking task as a search in a latent task graph. Assume a training-time Entity-Relation (ER) graph and define Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR): when the agent retrieves or cites new entities, reward them based on their graph distance to the ground-truth answer node. Then propose Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO), which converts GDCR into step-level advantages and combines these with standard trajectory-level advantages in a policy optimization framework.

Result: On four challenging agentic search benchmarks, the GDCR+SAPO framework leads to better performance than baselines that use only trajectory-level rewards or costly step-level estimation methods.

Conclusion: Encoding world knowledge as an ER graph enables a principled, inexpensive step-level reward (GDCR) that, when integrated via SAPO into policy optimization, improves training of agentic search agents. This shows that graph-distance-based process rewards can effectively guide step-wise reasoning and search without expensive tree sampling.

Abstract: In Agentic Search, trajectory-level outcome rewards fail to quantify the behavioral contributions of individual steps, while existing step-level reward methods typically rely on costly tree sampling. We view world knowledge as a latent world graph and each IS task as search within a latent task graph, where effective steps should make graph progress toward the answer node. Based on this prior, we propose Graph-Distance Contribution Reward (GDCR), a step-level process reward that scores newly-retrieved and newly-cited entities by their distance to the answer node in a training-time Entity-Relation (ER) graph. We further propose Step Advantage Policy Optimization (SAPO), which converts GDCR into step-level advantages and combines them with trajectory-level outcome advantages. Experiments on four challenging benchmarks validate the effectiveness of our method.

</details>


### [212] [BitTP: The Lightweight Trajectory Prediction Model with BitLLM for Edge-Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29705)
*Mincheol Kang,Hyunjin Lim,Bomin Kang,Daehee Park*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: BitTP is a lightweight, bitlinear version of an LLM-based trajectory predictor that uses ultra-low-bit (1.58-bit) weight-only quantization to improve both prediction quality and efficiency for edge deployment, while keeping activations in full precision for stable reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based trajectory prediction models perform strong multi-agent spatio-temporal reasoning but are too memory- and compute-intensive for resource-constrained edge devices like on-board computers in autonomous robots. There is a need to retain LLM-level reasoning performance while drastically reducing model size and latency, particularly without sacrificing prediction accuracy in safety-critical trajectory forecasting tasks.

Method: The paper proposes BitTP, which converts an existing LLM-based trajectory predictor into a bitlinear architecture. The key technique is aggressive weight-only quantization to approximately 1.58 bits per weight (BitTP-Weight), determined empirically as an optimal trade-off between compression and performance. Activations are explicitly kept in full precision because their quantization was found to destabilize and degrade spatio-temporal reasoning. The work compares BitTP against a full-precision BF16 baseline and other quantization schemes in terms of accuracy (ADE, FDE), memory consumption, and inference latency.

Result: BitTP-Weight achieves better trajectory prediction than the full-precision BF16 LLM baseline, reducing Average Displacement Error (ADE) by 14.29% and Final Displacement Error (FDE) by 20.97% on average. At the same time, it significantly cuts memory usage and inference latency compared to both the baseline and other quantization methods, demonstrating that extremely low-bit weight quantization can be effective if applied carefully and if activations are kept in full precision.

Conclusion: Carefully designed weight-only ultra-low-bit quantization (down to 1.58 bits) can act as an effective regularizer for LLM-based trajectory predictors, improving accuracy while greatly enhancing efficiency. Maintaining full-precision activations is crucial for stable spatio-temporal reasoning. BitTP thus enables practical deployment of sophisticated, LLM-based trajectory prediction models on resource-constrained edge devices, suggesting a promising direction for combining LLM-style reasoning with efficient bitlinear architectures.

Abstract: Trajectory prediction is a fundamental task for autonomous systems, requiring complex reasoning about multi-agent interactions and intents. Large language models (LLMs) have recently been adopted for this task, as they provide strong contextual reasoning and interpretable, language-based trajectory representations. However, these LLM-based predictors are extremely memory- and compute-intensive, making them difficult to deploy on resource-constrained edge devices such as on-board computers in autonomous robots. To bridge this gap, we propose BitTP, which converts an LLM-based trajectory predictor into a lightweight bitlinear architecture. We demonstrate that weight-only quantization to 1.58-bit (BitTP-Weight) is optimal. Crucially, activations must remain in full precision, as quantizing them leads to severe degradation and instability in spatio-temporal reasoning. Empirically, BitTP-Weight not only preserves but improves prediction quality over the full-precision (BF16) LLM baseline, reducing ADE by 14.29% and FDE by 20.97% on average, while simultaneously reducing memory usage and inference latency relative to other quantization methods. These results demonstrate that carefully designed quantization acts as an effective regularizer, enabling the practical deployment of sophisticated LLM-based reasoning on edge devices. Code is available at: https://github.com/MintCat98/BitTP.

</details>


### [213] [NaRA: Noise-Aware LoRA for Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Diffusion LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29716)
*Shuaidi Wang,Zhan Zhuang,Ruping Huang,Yu Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Noise-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (NaRA), a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method tailored for diffusion LLMs that makes adaptation explicitly depend on the diffusion noise level, yielding better performance than standard PEFT like LoRA.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT methods such as LoRA were designed for autoregressive LLMs and use static adaptation parameters that do not change with the noise level in diffusion models. In diffusion LLMs, the input distribution and generation difficulty vary substantially along the denoising trajectory, so noise-agnostic PEFT may be suboptimal. There is a need for a PEFT method that respects the dynamics of the diffusion process without incurring high parameter or latency costs.

Method: The authors introduce NaRA, a noise-aware low-rank adaptation framework. Instead of using fixed low-rank adaptation matrices as in LoRA, NaRA employs a lightweight, globally shared hypernetwork that takes the current noise level as input and outputs a core low-rank matrix. This matrix is then used to form the adaptation (update) matrices, which thus vary smoothly with noise level throughout the diffusion process. The design keeps both parameter count and inference latency overhead small. The paper also offers a theoretical analysis supporting NaRA’s suitability for diffusion LLMs.

Result: Across tasks including commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation, NaRA consistently outperforms standard, noise-agnostic PEFT baselines such as LoRA when applied to diffusion LLMs. The gains are achieved without substantial increases in parameters or computational cost.

Conclusion: NaRA effectively tailors parameter-efficient fine-tuning to the specific dynamics of diffusion LLMs by conditioning low-rank updates on the noise level. This noise-aware design yields consistent performance improvements over traditional PEFT approaches while maintaining efficiency, suggesting it is a better fit for fine-tuning diffusion-based language models.

Abstract: Diffusion Large Language Models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising non-autoregressive generative paradigm. Given the prohibitive computational cost of full fine-tuning, Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) has become the standard approach. However, existing PEFT methods (e.g., LoRA), originally tailored for autoregressive models, rely on static parameters that are agnostic to the noise level. Consequently, they ignore the intrinsic dynamics of the diffusion process, where input distributions and generation difficulty shift significantly along the denoising trajectory, rendering them suboptimal for dLLMs. To address this, we propose Noise-aware Low-Rank Adaptation (NaRA), which introduces a low-rank core matrix generated by a lightweight, globally shared hypernetwork conditioned on the noise level. This design enables the update matrices to vary continuously along the diffusion process while keeping parameter and latency overhead negligible. We provide a theoretical justification for the proposed NaRA framework and empirically demonstrate consistent improvements over noise-agnostic baselines across commonsense reasoning, mathematical reasoning, and code generation benchmarks. Our code is available at https://github.com/generaldi/NaRA.

</details>


### [214] [Uncertainty-Aware Transfer Learning for Cross-Building Energy Forecasting: Toward Robust and Scalable District-Level Energy Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29733)
*Shadmehr Zaregarizi,Khashayar Yavari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose an uncertainty-aware transfer learning framework using Temporal Fusion Transformers to forecast building energy use across different buildings, quantify how robustly models transfer with a new metric, and show that minimal fine-tuning of only the output layer can outperform full fine-tuning while maintaining good uncertainty calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As data-driven energy forecasting scales to the district level, we need models that work across many buildings with limited data for each building and that provide reliable uncertainty estimates. Existing models often require substantial retraining per building and lack robust ways to assess cross-building generalization quality under domain shift.

Method: They build a transfer learning framework based on the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT) for cross-building energy forecasting, using one building as source and another multi-typology building as target. They introduce the Transfer Robustness Index (TRI) as an architecture-agnostic metric for generalization under domain gaps, perform a layer-freezing ablation with four fine-tuning strategies (including Probe-Only, which only updates the output layer), and use Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty estimation. They also conduct a data-scarcity analysis by varying amounts of labeled target data.

Result: Probe-Only fine-tuning, which updates only 455 output-layer parameters out of 806K total, achieves the highest transfer quality with TRI = 3,097, surpassing full-model fine-tuning and indicating that TFT encoders learn transferable temporal features. Monte Carlo Dropout delivers well-calibrated uncertainty with a prediction interval coverage probability of 93.2% against a 95% nominal target. Performance improves monotonically as more target-domain data becomes available, according to the data-scarcity analysis.

Conclusion: Temporal Fusion Transformers can learn temporal representations that transfer well across buildings for energy forecasting, and effective transfer can be achieved with minimal parameter updates, which is computationally efficient and data-efficient. The proposed TRI metric provides a general way to quantify cross-domain robustness, while Monte Carlo Dropout gives trustworthy uncertainty estimates. These findings support scalable, practical deployment of data-driven energy forecasting at district scale, with guidance on how much target data is beneficial.

Abstract: Scaling data-driven energy forecasting to district level requires models that can be re-used across buildings with minimal target-domain data and honest uncertainty estimates. We present an uncertainty-aware transfer learning (TL) framework for cross-building energy forecasting based on the Temporal Fusion Transformer (TFT), evaluated on a newly released high-resolution real sub-meter dataset: an educational building at Aalborg University, Denmark (source) and the multi-typology NEST building at EMPA, Switzerland (target). We introduce the Transfer Robustness Index (TRI), an architecture-agnostic metric for quantifying generalization quality across domain gaps. A four-strategy layer-freezing ablation shows that Probe-Only fine-tuning, updating only 455 output-layer parameters out of 806K, achieves the best transfer quality (TRI = 3,097), outperforming full fine-tuning and suggesting that TFT encoders learn transferable temporal representations. Monte Carlo Dropout yields a prediction interval coverage probability of 93.2%, close to the nominal 95% target. A data-scarcity analysis further shows monotonic improvement with increasing target-domain data, providing practical guidance for district energy deployment.

</details>


### [215] [Citation-Closure Retrieval and Per-Rule Attribution for Real-World Regulatory Compliance Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29742)
*Yeong-Joon Ju,Seong-Whan Lee*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces RegOps-Bench and RefWalk for citation-accurate regulatory compliance QA using LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based compliance assistants need rigorous, structured citation and traceability across multi-tier regulatory documents, which current RAG and legal QA systems handle poorly due to flattened citation structures and weak attribution.

Method: They formalize Regulatory Compliance QA and build RegOps-Bench, a benchmark based on an Operational Knowledge Graph from complex R&D regulations. They then propose RefWalk, a shared-topic-anchor framework that walks citation chains across documents, aggregates multi-view retrieval candidates using max-based fusion, and enforces per-rule attribution that explicitly links each claim to its supporting regulatory sources.

Result: RefWalk significantly improves retrieval recall and citation accuracy compared with existing RAG systems. Experiments on RegOps-Bench and a contrastive evaluation on a HIPAA dataset show that conventional systems saturate on simpler, flat-structure regulations, while RefWalk handles the more complex, hierarchical regimes better.

Conclusion: RegOps-Bench exposes limitations of current RAG/legal QA setups for real-world regulatory compliance tasks, and RefWalk provides a strong, more faithful baseline for traceable, citation-complete compliance QA over multi-tier regulatory corpora.

Abstract: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) for regulatory compliance demands rigorous traceability via comprehensive citations across multi-tiered authority structures. Unlike traditional multi-hop or legal QA, this task requires structured procedural lookups and evidence-set closure rather than entity resolution or case-law reasoning. Existing RAG systems struggle here due to flattened citation edges, fragmented retrieval expansions, and fragile post-hoc attribution. We formalize Regulatory Compliance QA with RegOps-Bench, a novel benchmark featuring an Operational Knowledge Graph derived from complex national R\&D regulations. To address these bottlenecks, we propose RefWalk, a unified framework driven by a shared topic anchor. RefWalk traverses cross-document citations, fuses multi-view candidates via max-based aggregation, and enforces per-rule attribution to explicitly map claims to sources. We establish a strong baseline with substantial improvements in retrieval recall and citation accuracy. Finally, a contrastive evaluation on a U.S. health compliance dataset (HIPAA) reveals that existing systems exhibit saturation on flat-structure rules, underscoring the need for RegOps-Bench. Our code is available at https://github.com/yeongjoonJu/RefWalk.

</details>


### [216] [Why Specialist Models Still Matter: A Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Paradigm for Medical Artificial Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29744)
*Yanan Wang,Shuaicong Hu,Jian Liu,Guohui Zhou,Aiguo Wang,Cuiwei Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces HetMedAgent, a heterogeneous medical multi-agent framework that coordinates generalist LLMs, domain-specific specialist models, and clinicians to improve clinical decision making.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generalist LLMs like GPT and Claude perform impressively in healthcare tasks, leading to the question of whether specialized medical models are still needed. Existing directions focus either on building ever-larger medical foundation models or on replacing human expertise, both of which have limitations. There is a need for an approach that leverages the complementary strengths of generalist and specialist models while keeping clinicians in the loop for safety and reliability.

Method: The authors design HetMedAgent, a heterogeneous medical multi-agent framework. It coordinates multiple agents: generalist LLMs for broad reasoning and language understanding, domain-specific specialist models for modality-specific analysis (e.g., imaging, labs), and clinicians for oversight. Key mechanisms include: (1) conflict-aware evidence fusion to reconcile potentially inconsistent outputs from different models; (2) uncertainty-based triggers to decide when to involve a clinician; and (3) adaptive threshold calibration to tune decision thresholds based on task requirements and risk tolerance.

Result: On three real-world clinical decision-making tasks, HetMedAgent is empirically evaluated. The framework that combines generalist LLMs with domain-specific specialist models achieves significantly better performance than using only generalist LLMs or only specialist models. This empirically supports the claim that specialist models provide irreplaceable value for modality-specific analysis, and that orchestrated collaboration yields superior outcomes.

Conclusion: Domain-specific specialist models will not become obsolete; instead, their value is maximized when orchestrated with generalist LLMs and clinician input. HetMedAgent exemplifies a shift in medical AI from monolithic medical foundation models toward heterogeneous multi-agent collaboration, striking a balance between broad reasoning power and precise modality-specific analysis, and supporting safer, more reliable clinical decision-making.

Abstract: The impressive performance of generalist large language models (LLMs) such as GPT and Claude in healthcare raises a critical question: will domain-specific medical specialist models become obsolete? We argue that the future of medical artificial intelligence (AI) lies not in building monolithic medical foundation models, nor in replacing human expertise, but in orchestrating collaboration among generalist LLMs, domain-specific specialist models, and clinicians. We propose HetMedAgent, a heterogeneous medical multi-agent framework that enables conflict-aware evidence fusion, uncertainty-based clinician intervention triggering, and adaptive threshold calibration. Experiments on three real-world clinical decision-making tasks demonstrate that the synergy between generalist LLMs and domain-specific specialist models significantly outperforms using either type of model alone, validating the irreplaceable value of specialist models in modality-specific analysis. HetMedAgent represents a shift from building medical LLMs or foundation models to multi-agent collaboration, achieving a balance between general reasoning capabilities and domain-specific precision.

</details>


### [217] [Benchmarking Positional Encoding Strategies for Transformer-Based EEG Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29754)
*Ayse Betul Yuce,Sebastian Stober*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how different positional encodings for EEG electrodes affect transformer-based EEG foundation models, finding that the best strategy depends on the downstream task.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised EEG decoding models fail to generalize well across tasks, subjects, and datasets. Transformer-based self-supervised EEG foundation models need positional encodings, but unlike text, EEG electrodes lie on a 3D scalp surface, so it is unclear how to best encode their spatial positions. Clarifying this is important for robust EEG representation learning.

Method: Within the CBraMod transformer backbone, the authors systematically benchmark five different positional encoding strategies for EEG electrodes. They evaluate these strategies using linear probing and full fine-tuning on two representative downstream tasks: motor imagery classification and emotion recognition.

Result: No single positional encoding method dominates across all tasks and evaluation protocols. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) performs particularly well for motor imagery but degrades on emotion recognition. Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) offers more stable and balanced performance across the different tasks.

Conclusion: Positional encoding design is crucial for transformer-based EEG models, but there is no universally optimal choice. The most effective encoding is task-dependent: SPE is well-suited for motor imagery, while ACPE provides more consistent cross-task performance. This implies future EEG foundation models may need task-aware or adaptive positional encoding schemes rather than a one-size-fits-all solution.

Abstract: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely used non-invasive technique for measuring brain activity in brain-computer interface (BCI) applications. Supervised EEG decoding models often struggle to generalize across tasks, subjects, and datasets, motivating transformer-based EEG foundation models trained with self-supervised learning. Since transformers are permutation-invariant, they require explicit positional information. Unlike textual tokens, EEG electrodes are spatially distributed across the scalp, raising the question of how electrode positions should be encoded in transformer-based EEG models. In this study, we benchmark five positional encoding strategies within the CBraMod backbone and evaluate them under linear probing and fine-tuning protocols on motor imagery classification and emotion recognition. Our results show that no single strategy consistently outperforms across tasks. Spherical Positional Encoding (SPE) yields strong representations for motor imagery but underperforms on emotion recognition, while Asymmetric Conditional Positional Encoding (ACPE) demonstrates more consistent performance across tasks. These findings suggest that the optimal positional encoding strategy is task-dependent, with no universal solution across EEG decoding scenarios.

</details>


### [218] [LFQ: Logit-aware Final-block Quantization for Boosting the Generation Quality of Low-Bit Quantized LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29756)
*Jung Hyun Lee,June Yong Yang,Jungwook Choi,Eunho Yang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper improves low-bit post-training quantization of large language models for generative tasks by logit-aware final-block optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing block-wise post-training quantization methods can match full-precision performance on language modeling and understanding, but they underperform on complex generative tasks, especially for long responses and chain-of-thought reasoning. The authors identify that this degradation stems from ignoring the unembedding (LM head) in block-wise optimization and using an MSE loss that does not directly align token probability distributions, leading to misaligned logits and accuracy drops.

Method: They propose Logit-aware Final-block Quantization (LFQ), which modifies standard block-wise PTQ by specially treating the final Transformer block. Instead of optimizing that block with a mean squared error reconstruction objective, they quantize it by minimizing cross-entropy between the logits of the full-precision model and those of the quantized model. This explicitly aligns the token probability distributions without changing the overall PTQ workflow for earlier blocks.

Result: LFQ yields consistently better accuracy than state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ on complex generation benchmarks across multiple LLM families, particularly for long-form outputs and extended chains of thought, while preserving near-equal performance to full-precision models on standard language modeling and understanding tasks.

Conclusion: Aligning logits in the final block during quantization, via a cross-entropy objective that accounts for the LM head, is a simple yet powerful way to close the quality gap between low-bit quantized and full-precision LLMs on challenging generative tasks, without harming performance on other NLP benchmarks.

Abstract: As large language models continue to scale, low-bit weight-only post-training quantization (PTQ) offers a practical solution to their memory-efficient deployment. Although block-wise PTQ is capable of matching the full-precision (FP) baseline on basic language modeling and understanding, its quality is degraded for generative tasks -- especially at longer responses and extended chains of thought, which is critical in boosting task accuracy. We attribute this shortfall to two factors: (i) the omission of the unembedding layer (the LM head) in block-wise optimization and (ii) the reliance on the mean squared error (MSE) objective. Both factors cause the token probability distribution of the quantized model to misalign with that of the FP model, yielding notable accuracy drops on text generation benchmarks. To rectify the discrepancy, we introduce Logit-aware Final-block Quantization (LFQ), a simple yet effective enhancement to block-wise PTQ that quantizes the final Transformer block by minimizing the cross-entropy between the logits of the FP model and those of its quantized counterpart. By aligning token probabilities at the logit level in the final block, LFQ consistently improves the accuracy of complex generation tasks over state-of-the-art block-wise PTQ across diverse model families, while maintaining parity with FP baselines on language modeling and understanding.

</details>


### [219] [From XXLTraffic to EvoXXLTraffic: Scaling Traffic Forecasting to Sensor-Evolving Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29768)
*Du Yin,Hao Xue,Arian Prabowo,Shuang Ao,Flora Salim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: XXLTraffic and EvoXXLTraffic provide new ultra-long-term and sensor-evolving traffic forecasting benchmarks built from up to 27 years of PeMS and Transport for NSW data, revealing that many current SOTA models fail under realistic, growing road networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most traffic forecasting benchmarks assume a fixed set of sensors, whereas real-world road and sensor networks evolve over time, with sensors being added and network topology changing. This mismatch means current benchmarks may overestimate model robustness and limit research on continual and evolutionary forecasting in realistic settings.

Method: The authors construct XXLTraffic, a dataset family built from up to 27 years of California PeMS and Transport for NSW data. They define fixed-sensor subsets for very long-horizon and standard long-horizon forecasting. They then reorganize the data into EvoXXLTraffic, which exposes per-year active sensors, yearly traffic flow matrices, and annual graph snapshots across nine PeMS districts with large growth ratios. On EvoXXLTraffic they propose a yearly streaming forecasting protocol where each calendar year is treated as a continual task. They benchmark diverse forecasting approaches, including static spatio-temporal GNNs, naïve online schemes, evolving-graph continual learning methods, and retrieval/test-time adaptation methods, under this new protocol.

Result: Across the proposed EvoXXLTraffic benchmark and streaming protocol, many existing state-of-the-art traffic forecasting and spatio-temporal GNN models see their performance degrade or their advantages disappear when faced with ultra-long-term, sensor-evolving conditions. The dataset reveals substantial gaps between performance on traditional fixed-sensor benchmarks and performance in realistic evolving-network settings.

Conclusion: The XXLTraffic and EvoXXLTraffic datasets, together with the proposed streaming forecasting protocol, form a more realistic and challenging benchmark suite for traffic forecasting under evolving road networks. They show that current SOTA approaches are not robust to long-term network evolution, indicating the need for new models and methods explicitly designed for continual, evolutionary spatio-temporal forecasting.

Abstract: Existing traffic forecasting benchmarks assume a fixed sensor set, but real road-sensor networks grow continuously as the road network changes year by year. We introduce the XXLTraffic dataset family, which spans up to 27 years of California PeMS and Transport for NSW data. The fixed-sensor subsets of XXLTraffic support extremely long forecasting with multi-year gaps and standard hourly / daily long-horizon forecasting. We extend it to EvoXXLTraffic, a sensor-evolving reorganization that exposes per-year active sensors, yearly traffic-flow matrices, and yearly graph snapshots across nine PeMS districts, with growth ratios ranging from +305% to over +10,000%. We define a yearly streaming forecasting protocol on EvoXXLTraffic in which each calendar year is a continual task, and benchmark a wide range of representative baselines drawn from static spatio-temporal GNNs, naïve online schemes, evolving-graph continual methods, and retrieval / test-time methods. We find that our ultra-large evolutionary dataset better reflects the real world, and many state-of-the-art (SOTA) results no longer work. Our dataset complements existing benchmarks by enabling more realistic forecasting under ultra-long evolutionary road networks.

</details>


### [220] [Croissant Tasks: A Metadata Format for Reproducible Machine Learning Evaluations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29786)
*Omar Benjelloun,Leonardo Martins Bianco,Isabelle Guyon,Thanh Gia Hieu Khuong,Jonathan Lebensold,Sebastian Lobentanzer,Luis Oala,Benedictus Kent Rachmat,Ihsan Ullah,Peyman Vahidi,Joaquin Vanschoren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Croissant Tasks, a machine-readable task specification format that lets autonomous agents reproduce ML benchmarks conceptually, without depending on original code, improving scalable reproducibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning research often lacks reproducibility because papers and benchmarks under-specify execution details, and their software environments are brittle or hard to rebuild. Existing human-centric solutions like checklists and manual reviews are labor-intensive and do not scale with the fast pace and volume of ML work. The authors aim to create a scalable, automated way to verify and reproduce ML claims that does not rely on fragile code re-use or exact environment cloning.

Method: The authors design Croissant Tasks, a declarative metadata format that describes ML tasks at a high abstraction level, separating the definition of the problem from any particular solution implementation. They then build an automated pipeline using large language models to convert existing ML benchmarks into this Croissant Tasks format. Finally, they run experiments with autonomous agents that ingest only these high-level task specifications and attempt to generate end-to-end pipelines—from data handling to model training and evaluation—without access to the original benchmark code, in order to assess functional correctness and accuracy.

Result: The pipeline successfully converts existing benchmarks into Croissant Tasks, and autonomous agents using only these specifications are able to generate working reproduction pipelines. These pipelines not only run end-to-end but achieve accurate results that are consistent with the original benchmark claims, demonstrating that the high-level task descriptions are sufficient for practical reproduction. This provides empirical evidence that the format can support conceptual reproducibility driven by agents rather than human engineers or original source code.

Conclusion: Croissant Tasks offers a new, machine-actionable way to specify ML tasks that separates what is being evaluated from how it is implemented. This enables autonomous agents to reconstruct experimental pipelines and validate results based solely on high-level descriptions, moving beyond brittle code-level replication. The authors argue that such a format can serve as a foundation for scalable, automated, and conceptually grounded reproducibility in machine learning research and benchmarking.

Abstract: Reproducibility is fundamental to the scientific method, yet remains a critical challenge in machine learning. Contributing factors include underspecified execution details and brittle software environments. Human-centric remedies, such as checklists and manual verification, help but require intensive effort and fail to scale. To address this, we introduce Croissant Tasks: a declarative, machine-actionable metadata format that abstracts low-level implementation details into high-level specifications. This format enables conceptual reproducibility: verifying claims via independent, agent-generated implementations rather than brittle source code replication. We contribute: (1) the Croissant Tasks specification, formally decoupling task problem from solution; (2) an automated LLM pipeline that retrofits existing benchmarks into this format; and (3) empirical validation showing autonomous agents can ingest these specifications to generate functional, accurate reproduction pipelines from scratch. We envision this format as a new foundation for automated and conceptual reproducibility in machine learning.

</details>


### [221] [Certified Policy Optimisation for Nested Causal Bandits via PAC-Bayes Risk](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29788)
*Tim Woydt,Paul-David Zuercher*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduce Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs) and Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS) for multi-timescale causal decision-making with theoretical guarantees and certified safe deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world sequential decision problems involve multiple coupled timescales, where high-level strategic choices determine the environment and context for lower-level tactical decisions. Standard bandit and RL frameworks usually assume a single timescale and do not explicitly model how actions at one level causally shape the context distributions at others, making them inadequate for planning and safety in such hierarchical settings. The authors want a framework that captures this causal nesting and supports off-policy certification of deployment risk using historical data.

Method: They formalize a new problem class called Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs), modeled as a hierarchical structural causal model (SCM) where each level’s action determines the context distribution for the next level. They then propose Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS), which samples a factorized belief over the mechanisms of the SCM once per episode and then acts recursively under this sampled model across all levels. They derive a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound that can be applied off-policy and anytime, providing a certificate on the risk of any candidate deployment policy using historical data.

Result: In experiments using a hierarchical SCM benchmark, NCTS is compared against a baseline that uses a joint random feature mapping Gaussian process (RFF-GP) regression on the same function class. The factorized SCM-based posterior in NCTS exhibits significantly better zero-shot transfer performance under exogenous distribution shifts. The recursive meta-to-inner commit strategy (committing to high-level decisions that induce lower-level contexts) statistically dominates a joint-commit alternative. Additionally, the PAC-Bayesian risk certificate tightens (contracts) as more offline data is collected, empirically validating the theoretical guarantee.

Conclusion: The authors show that explicitly modeling multi-timescale causal structure via NCCBs, and leveraging NCTS with causal PAC-Bayesian certification, enables more robust and transferable decision-making than joint non-causal baselines. By allowing each timescale to be switched from a legacy controller to the learned NCTS policy once certified performance gains are available—independently across timescales—they introduce "progressive certified handover," a practical safe-deployment paradigm for hierarchical decision systems.

Abstract: Critical sequential decisions are rarely single-timescale: a strategic decision causally shapes the context in which every subsequent tactical choice is made; standard bandit and reinforcement-learning theory does not capture this causal coupling between timescales. We formalise the problem class as Nested Contextual Causal Bandits (NCCBs), a hierarchical SCM where each level's action sets the next level's context distribution, and propose Nested Causal Thompson Sampling (NCTS), which draws one mechanism-factorised belief per episode and acts recursively under it. Our main theoretical result is a causal PAC-Bayesian excess-risk bound that certifies any candidate deployment policy from historic data alone, off-policy and anytime, answering the deployment question: can we trust this agent here, and at what risk? Experiments on a hierarchical SCM show that, against a matched RFF-GP joint regression on the same function class, the factorised SCM-mechanism posterior transfers significantly better zero-shot under exogenous distribution shifts, the recursive meta-to-inner commit significantly dominates the joint-commit alternative in distribution, and the certificate significantly contracts as offline data accumulates. Combining these results, we establish progressive certified handover, a safe-deployment method: each timescale flips from a legacy controller to NCTS when gains can be certified, independently of the others.

</details>


### [222] [SkillsInjector: Dynamic Skill Context Construction for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29794)
*Yanchao Li,Wanhao Liu,Ben Gao,Jiaqing Xie,Zhehong Ai,Na Zou,Yuqiang Li,Tianfan Fu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SkillsInjector, an adaptive method for selecting and presenting skills to large language model agents, which improves task performance over static skill injection strategies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agents rely on libraries of skills/tools, but simply adding more skills or selecting them with static rules does not consistently improve performance and can even hurt it. Current approaches fix which skills are injected, how many, and how they are described, ignoring that these factors dynamically interact with the task context and with each other. The authors aim to address the under-optimization of the skill injection process itself, hypothesizing that adaptive, execution-grounded decisions about skill exposure and presentation can lead to more effective agents.

Method: The authors propose SkillsInjector, a two-stage adaptive framework. In the first stage, a context planner learns execution-grounded preferences over skills and decides an adaptive, per-task skill budget, i.e., how many and which skills to inject based on the task context and observed performance. In the second stage, a set-aware renderer rewrites or tailors the descriptions of the selected skills, explicitly conditioning on the full set of co-injected skills so that the presented descriptions are complementary and non-redundant. This joint treatment of skill selection, budgeting, and description rendering is integrated into LLM agents and evaluated on several benchmarks.

Result: On three benchmarks—tau2-bench, SkillsBench, and ALFWorld—SkillsInjector attains the highest performance among compared methods, outperforming the strongest baseline by 3.9, 6.1, and 7.3 percentage points respectively. Ablation experiments show that each component (skill selection, adaptive budgeting, and set-aware rendering) provides measurable benefits, and that removing any part reduces the gains, supporting the claim that all elements of adaptive injection matter.

Conclusion: The study concludes that skill-augmented LLM agents benefit significantly from optimizing not just which skills are available but how many are used and how they are contextualized in prompts. Static, one-size-fits-all skill injection pipelines are suboptimal, whereas adaptive, execution-grounded selection and set-aware rendering of skill descriptions lead to better downstream task completion. The authors suggest that future work on LLM agents should treat the injected skill context as a first-class object of optimization and plan to release code to facilitate further research.

Abstract: LLM agents now draw on growing skill libraries to handle complex tasks. However, injecting more skills does not always improve task completion and can even degrade it. Existing methods still treat skill injection as a static step, selecting skills with fixed criteria, fixing the budget in advance, and leaving descriptions unchanged. We argue that this static treatment can undermine the utility of skills, because which skills are exposed, how many are included, and how they are presented all affect downstream performance. We propose SkillsInjector, a two-stage adaptive method that jointly addresses these decisions. First, a context planner learns execution-grounded skill preferences and admits an adaptive number of skills for each task. A set-aware renderer then tailors how selected descriptions are presented relative to their co-injected neighbors. Across tau2-bench, SkillsBench, and ALFWorld, SkillsInjector achieves the highest score, improving over the strongest baseline by 3.9, 6.1, and 7.3 percentage points, respectively. Ablation studies show that skill selection, adaptive budgeting, and set-aware rendering each contribute to the gain. These results show that skill-augmented agents benefit from optimizing the injected context itself. Code will be released upon publication

</details>


### [223] [MEMENTO: Leveraging Web as a Learning Signal for Low-Data Domains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29795)
*Ashutosh Ojha,Vinay Aggarwal,Ashutosh Srivastava,Siddharth Yedlapati,Yaman K Singla,Jitendra Ajmera*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MEMENTO is a web-interacting agent framework that learns domain expertise and search strategies over time from its own web browsing, improving performance in low-data professional tasks without extra model training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many real-world tasks have little labeled data, and current methods (few-shot prompting, instruction tuning, synthetic labels) still rely heavily on labeled or pseudo-labeled data as the main learning signal. In contrast, humans gain expertise by repeatedly exploring the open web, refining both their knowledge and how they search. The paper is motivated by the gap between how humans learn from the web and how current LLM agents typically use the web as a stateless retrieval tool, missing the chance to accumulate and reuse experience.

Method: They introduce MEMENTO, a framework that treats web interaction itself as a learning signal. Within a single task session, it uses an Adaptive Exploration Tree (AET) to iteratively decompose the task into evolving questions, search the web, and reflect on intermediate findings. Across multiple sessions, it stores experience in a dual-channel memory: declarative memory for factual/domain knowledge and procedural memory for search and reasoning strategies. The agent then reuses this accumulated memory in later tasks, enabling continual improvement without updating model weights.

Result: On two low-data professional domains—sales automation and legal research—MEMENTO outperforms ReAct-style web agents. It achieves about +25.6% performance gain in sales automation and +36.5% in legal research, showing that its memory and exploration mechanisms lead to more effective task completion under data scarcity.

Conclusion: The work concludes that the open web can act as a scalable, ongoing learning source for LLM-based agents, not just a retrieval backend. By structuring web interaction through adaptive exploration and persistent dual-channel memory, agents can acquire reusable domain expertise and search strategies in low-data settings, improving over standard ReAct baselines without additional model training.

Abstract: Real-world tasks often lack large labeled datasets, motivating extensive work on learning in low-data regimes. However, existing approaches such as few-shot prompting, instruction tuning, and synthetic data generation, continue to treat labeled or pseudo-labeled data as the primary learning signal. In contrast, human practitioners acquire expertise through repeated, self-directed interaction with the open web, progressively refining both domain knowledge and search strategies. We propose MEMENTO, a framework that treats the web as a learning signal rather than a stateless retrieval interface. MEMENTO operates at two levels: within each session, it conducts iterative web exploration via an Adaptive Exploration Tree (AET) that decomposes tasks into evolving questions and reflects on intermediate findings; across sessions, it accumulates experience through dual-channel memory, separating declarative knowledge (facts) from procedural knowledge (search strategies). This design enables agents to learn reusable research strategies and domain expertise from trajectories of web interaction without additional model training. We evaluate MEMENTO on two low-data professional domains: sales automation and legal research. Our empirical results show consistent improvements in performance over ReAct based baselines (+25.6% on sales automation and 36.5% on legal research), demonstrating that the web can serve as a scalable learning source for acquiring task-specific expertise in data-scarce settings.

</details>


### [224] [SAAS: Self-Aware Reinforcement Learning for Over-Search Mitigation in Agentic Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29796)
*Yunbo Tang,Chengyi Yang,Shiyu Liu,Zhishang Xiang,Zerui Chen,Qinggang Zhang,Jinsong Su*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces SAAS, an RL-based framework that teaches LLM-based agentic search systems to know when to search and when to stop, cutting unnecessary searches while keeping accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing agentic search systems for LLMs can iteratively call external search tools to solve complex multi-hop questions. However, in practice they lack self-awareness of their own knowledge boundaries. As a result, they frequently over-search: triggering external search even when the LLM already knows the answer, and continuing to search after sufficient evidence has been gathered. This causes large inference latency and computational cost. The motivation is to build a principled mechanism so the agent can dynamically decide when search is actually needed and when to terminate it, without hurting task performance.

Method: The authors propose SAAS, a reinforcement learning framework that adds dynamic self-awareness to LLM agents. SAAS has three main components: (1) A search boundary modeling mechanism that estimates the agent’s search boundary under its evolving policy by contrasting rollouts where search is disabled versus enabled. This reveals when external search is truly necessary. (2) A boundary-aware reward module that converts this boundary information into trajectory-level penalties, discouraging unnecessary or redundant search calls during RL training. (3) A stage-wise optimization strategy (sequential curriculum) that first focuses on learning strong reasoning skills and only later emphasizes search regularization, reducing the risk of reward hacking and preserving accuracy.

Result: Through extensive experiments on multi-hop question-answering or similar benchmarks, SAAS significantly reduces over-search behavior: the trained agents make fewer and more targeted search calls. At the same time, task accuracy is maintained, indicating that the system is not simply searching less but is using search more intelligently. The empirical results show improved efficiency (lower latency and computational cost) while preserving performance compared to baseline agentic search systems.

Conclusion: SAAS effectively instills dynamic self-awareness into LLM-based search agents, enabling them to recognize when their internal knowledge suffices and when external search is needed. By modeling the search boundary, using boundary-informed rewards, and applying stage-wise RL training, SAAS curbs over-search without sacrificing accuracy. This demonstrates that carefully designed RL frameworks can substantially improve the efficiency and practicality of agentic search systems.

Abstract: Agentic search enables LLMs to solve complex multi-hop questions through iterative reasoning and external search. Despite the effectiveness, these systems often suffer from a critical limitation in practice: agents fail to recognize their own knowledge boundaries, blindly triggering searches when internal knowledge suffices and failing to terminate search even when adequate evidence has been collected. The lack of self-awareness leads to severe \textbf{over-search}, incurring substantial inference latency and prohibitive computational cost. To this end, we propose SAAS, a novel RL framework designed to cultivate dynamic self-awareness that precisely regulates search behavior without compromising accuracy. SAAS introduces three key components: (i) a search boundary modeling mechanism, which identifies the search boundary under the evolving policy by contrasting search-disabled and search-enabled rollouts; (ii) a boundary-aware reward module, which translates this boundary awareness into trajectory-level penalties, suppressing unnecessary and redundant searches; and (iii) a stage-wise optimization strategy, which leverages a sequential curriculum to prioritize reasoning over search regularization, thereby avoiding reward hacking. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SAAS substantially reduces over-search, while maintaining accuracy. Our code is anonymously released at https://github.com/XMUDeepLIT/SAAS.

</details>


### [225] [AgentDoG 1.5: A Lightweight and Scalable Alignment Framework for AI Agent Safety and Security](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29801)
*Dongrui Liu,Yu Li,Zhonghao Yang,Peng Wang,Guanxu Chen,Yuejin Xie,Qinghua Mao,Wanying Qu,Yanxu Zhu,Tianyi Zhou,Leitao Yuan,Zhijie Zheng,Qihao Lin,Yimin Wang,Haoyu Luo,Shuai Shao,Chen Qian,Qingyu Liu,Ling Tang,Ruiyang Qin,Qihan Ren,Junxiao Yang,Kun Wang,Zhiheng Xi,Linfeng Zhang,Ranjie Duan,Bo Zhang,Wenjie Wang,Wen Shen,Qiaosheng Zhang,Yan Teng,Chaochao Lu,Rui Mei,Man Li,Jialing Tao,Xi Lin,Tianhang Zheng,Yong Liu,Quanshi Zhang,Lei Zhu,Xingjun Ma,Junhua Liu,Hui Xue,Xiaoxiang Zuo,Xiangnan He,Chao Shen,Xianglong Liu,Minlie Huang,Jing Shao,Xia Hu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces AgentDoG 1.5, a lightweight yet powerful safety alignment framework and model suite for open-world agents like OpenClaw, focusing on real-time safety moderation and efficient training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing open-world agents have strong cross-environment execution abilities but also introduce new safety risks. Frontier AI models make it easier for attackers to exploit these agents, and current alignment frameworks are insufficient for safe, real-world deployment.

Method: The authors (1) extend an agent safety taxonomy to cover new risks from Codex- and OpenClaw-style execution, (2) build a taxonomy-guided data engine with influence-function-based data purification, (3) train a family of small AgentDoG 1.5 models (0.8B–8B parameters) on ~1k curated samples, (4) design an SFT+RL training environment optimized for agentic safety in Docker-like deployments, and (5) deploy AgentDoG 1.5 as a training-free online guardrail for moderating agent behavior in real time.

Result: AgentDoG 1.5 models, despite being relatively small and trained on a limited dataset, achieve performance comparable to leading closed models such as GPT-5.4 on agentic safety tasks. The proposed training environment reduces deployment overhead for Docker-level environments by about 100x. Experiments across varied interactive agent scenarios show state-of-the-art safety performance.

Conclusion: A taxonomy-driven, data-efficient approach can produce lightweight safety models that rival large closed-source systems for moderating open-world agents. AgentDoG 1.5 provides a practical, scalable framework and toolkit—released openly—for improving the safety of real-world agent deployments, including as an online guardrail without additional training.

Abstract: Modern open-world agents such as OpenClaw exhibit powerful cross-environment execution capabilities yet introduce broad new safety risk sources. Meanwhile, advanced frontier AI models drastically lower attack barriers, rendering current agent alignment frameworks inadequate for real-world deployment. To tackle these emerging threats, we propose a lightweight and scalable agent safety alignment framework. Specifically, we update the agent safety taxonomy to accommodate emergent risks from Codex and OpenClaw execution scenarios. We further build a taxonomy-guided data engine with influence-function purification to train lightweight AgentDoG 1.5 variants (0.8B, 2B, 4B, and 8B parameters) using only around 1k samples, achieving comparable performance with leading closed-source models (e.g., GPT-5.4). Based on AgentDoG 1.5, we construct a highly efficient agentic safety SFT and RL training environment, which reduces deployment overhead in Docker-level environments by two orders of magnitude. Finally, we deploy AgentDoG 1.5 as a training-free online guardrail for real-time safety moderation. Extensive experimental results indicate that AgentDoG 1.5 achieves state-of-the-art performance in diverse and complex interactive agentic scenarios. All models and datasets are openly released.

</details>


### [226] [PRAIB: Peer Review AI Benchmark of Behaviour of LLM-Assisted Reviewing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29815)
*Krzysztof Żurawicki,Julia Farganus,Arkadiusz Gaweł,Mateusz Bystroński,Tomasz Jan Kajdanowicz*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build a benchmark and metrics (PRAIB) to compare LLM-written peer reviews to human reviews and show that LLM reviews systematically differ from human ones.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The number of submissions to conferences is rapidly increasing, putting pressure on the peer-review system. LLMs are being explored as tools to help with reviewing, but it is unclear whether they truly engage with papers like humans do or just generate superficially plausible review text. The authors want a principled way to measure and understand these differences before deploying LLMs in the review pipeline.

Method: They design PRAIB, a framework with well-defined metrics that capture review specificity, style, and engagement behavior (e.g., cross-references to sections of the paper). Using PRAIB, they run a large-scale empirical study on about 11,000 LLM-generated reviews from five different models for 1,000 ICLR and NeurIPS papers (2021–2025). They compare these LLM reviews with the original human reviews under various prompting strategies, and analyze rating distributions, biases, overconfidence, and cross-reference patterns, as well as how well reviews capture fine-grained (atomic) weaknesses.

Result: LLM-generated reviews differ substantially from human reviews. LLM ratings show less variance, are skewed more positively, and express higher confidence than human ratings. Patterns of referring to and cross-linking parts of the paper vary by model and do not match human norms. PRAIB shows that LLM reviews tend to be longer and more linguistically complex but still miss many of the specific, fine-grained weaknesses that human reviewers point out.

Conclusion: Current LLMs do not yet behave like human reviewers: they produce review-like text but with systematic biases and gaps in critical engagement, particularly in identifying atomic weaknesses. PRAIB offers a diagnostic framework to pinpoint which aspects of peer review LLMs can currently assist with and which aspects remain unreliable and need further research and development before LLMs are integrated into real reviewing workflows.

Abstract: The growing number of submitted papers has motivated the exploration of Large Language Models (LLMs) as a means to support and augment the peer review process, particularly in terms of improving its speed and scalability. Yet, it remains unknown whether LLMs engage with scientific manuscripts in the same manner as human reviewers, or whether they merely produce review-looking text. To address this, we introduce the Peer Review AI Benchmark (PRAIB), a novel framework comprising thoroughly defined metrics that measure review specificity, style, and behavior of engagement. To complement the PRAIB framework, we conduct a large-scale empirical study leveraging a dataset of 11,000 reviews generated by five proprietary and open-source models for 1,000 ICLR and NeurIPS papers. Spanning the 2021--2025 period, these machine-generated reviews are compared against original human feedback across diverse prompting strategies to identify systematic behavioral divergences. Our analysis reveals that the generated reviews diverge significantly from feedback provided by human reviewers: LLM ratings are less variable, positively biased, and overconfident, and their cross-reference patterns are model-dependent and distinct from human norms. Furthermore, when evaluated through PRAIB, we observe that LLMs tend to generate longer, more complex reviews, yet frequently overlook the atomic weaknesses noted by human reviewers. By characterizing where and how LLMs reviewing behavior departs from human norms, PRAIB provides the community with a diagnostic tool for identifying which aspects of the review process LLMs can reliably support today and which require further development before deployment.

</details>


### [227] [Harnessing non-adversarial robustness in large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29816)
*Qinghua Zhou,Ellina Aleshina,Andrey Lovyagin,Oleg Somov,Mikhail Seleznyov,Alexander Panchenko,Ivan Oseledets,Elena Tutubalina,Ivan Y. Tyukin*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a simple fine-tuning method, called debiasing for robustness, to make LLMs robust to semantically-neutral but textually different prompt variations, without retraining the full model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are highly sensitive to small, semantically-neutral changes in prompts, which can drastically change their performance. Full retraining to improve robustness is expensive, so there is a need for a cheaper, targeted method to make models robust to random but meaning-preserving prompt perturbations.

Method: The authors first conduct a theoretical analysis of how semantically-neutral prompt perturbations propagate through neural network modules and identify a key factor: a systematic expected shift, or perturbation-induced bias, in module outputs. Based on this analysis, they propose a simple fine-tuning procedure that explicitly debiases these module outputs to counteract the shift, thereby improving robustness without retraining the entire model.

Result: Theoretically, they derive conditions under which debiasing improves robustness and when it fails to help. Empirically, they run extensive experiments showing that their debiasing fine-tuning procedure substantially improves robustness of LLMs to random, semantically-neutral prompt perturbations and can even offer a form of certification against such perturbations.

Conclusion: Robustness of LLMs to semantically-neutral prompt variations can be significantly improved without expensive full retraining, by identifying and correcting perturbation-induced biases in neural module outputs through a lightweight debiasing fine-tuning process. This debiasing for robustness can be an efficient and practical tool to enhance and certify robustness in deployed LLMs.

Abstract: The work presents an approach for addressing the challenge of robustness in Large Language Models (LLMs) to alterations and potential errors caused by semantically similar but textually different prompts. Recent works have shown that these kinds of prompt variations can significantly impact the performance of LLMs on tasks. The central question is: can LLMs' robustness to semantically-neutral prompt alterations be acquired without expensive retraining of the entire model? We address this question both theoretically and through experiments. Our theoretical analysis reveals a crucial factor impacting model robustness - a systematic expected shift or perturbation-induced bias in neural network module outputs. Motivated by this analysis, we show that robustness can be achieved via a simple fine-tuning process: debiasing for robustness. We identify conditions when debiasing helps and when it does not, and demonstrate, through both theory and extensive experiments, that debiasing for robustness may indeed be a quick and efficient tool to enhance robustness and provide certification against random prompt perturbations.

</details>


### [228] [Quantifying and Optimizing Simplicity via Polynomial Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29823)
*Tianren Zhang,Xiangxin Li,Minghao Xiao,Guanyu Chen,Feng Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a way to measure and use ‘simplicity’ of neural networks via low-degree polynomial approximations of their behavior along data-dependent paths, and show it predicts and improves generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although deep networks tend to favor simple solutions that generalize well, there is no widely accepted, quantitative, and broadly applicable measure of functional simplicity for neural networks. Existing proxies for generalization, such as sharpness, are imperfect and often task- or architecture-specific. The authors aim to build a practical, distribution-aware simplicity metric that correlates with generalization and can also be used to regularize training.

Method: They approximate a trained network’s predictions along data-dependent interpolation paths (trajectories through input space informed by the data distribution) using orthogonal polynomial bases, obtaining a compact, low-dimensional polynomial representation of the network’s input–output behavior. The key quantity is the effective polynomial degree needed for a good approximation, which they propose as a simplicity measure. They empirically compare this metric with other generalization proxies like sharpness and construct a differentiable regularizer that penalizes high effective degree, integrating it into training across diverse tasks.

Result: The effective degree of the polynomial representation correlates strongly with test performance across multiple tasks and architectures, outperforming commonly used generalization measures like sharpness in its predictive power. Moreover, when used as a regularizer during training or fine-tuning, the polynomial-based simplicity penalty consistently improves generalization in settings including image classification, text classification, fine-tuning of contrastive vision–language models, and reinforcement learning benchmarks.

Conclusion: Polynomial representations provide a practical, distribution-aware way to summarize the functional behavior of neural networks in terms of low-degree approximations. The effective polynomial degree acts as a robust simplicity metric that both predicts and enhances generalization, performing better than traditional proxies like sharpness. This suggests that data-dependent functional approximations can serve as powerful tools for understanding and controlling the simplicity bias in deep learning.

Abstract: Deep networks often exhibit a preference for "simple" solutions, and such a simplicity bias is widely believed to play a key role in generalization. Yet a broadly applicable, quantitative measure of simplicity remains elusive. We introduce polynomial representations as a distribution-aware, low-dimensional surrogate for neural functions: we approximate a network's predictive behavior along data-dependent interpolation paths using orthogonal polynomial bases, yielding a compact functional representation. We show that the effective degree of this representation serves as a practical simplicity metric that is predictive of generalization across tasks and architectures, and consistently outperforms existing generalization proxies such as sharpness. Finally, polynomial representations naturally yield a differentiable simplicity regularizer, which consistently improves generalization in image and text classification, fine-tuning contrastive vision-language models, and reinforcement learning.

</details>


### [229] [OptSkills: Learning Generalizable Optimization Skills from Problem Archetypes via Cluster-Based Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29829)
*Haochen Yang,Ke Zhao,Mengyuan Ma,Xingyu Lu,Xiangfeng Wang,Hong Qian*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OptSkills is a skill-based LLM agent system that clusters optimization problems by underlying archetypes, learns reusable modeling/solving workflows (skills), and uses them to robustly solve both in-distribution and out-of-distribution optimization problems, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on several benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based optimization systems can convert natural language descriptions into optimization models and solutions, but they generalize poorly: they are sensitive to small wording changes, reuse experience only per-instance, and fail on new or shifted problem types. The authors want a more robust and scalable way to transfer and reuse problem-solving knowledge in optimization, akin to human experts who recognize archetypal structures and apply workflows instead of starting from scratch each time.

Method: The authors design OptSkills, an agent system that operates around archetype-centric skill learning. First, it clusters natural-language optimization problems by their underlying structural archetypes rather than by superficial narrative details. Within each cluster, the system explores multiple modeling paradigms and solver configurations, generating solution trajectories for various problems. It then distills successful trajectories into reusable skills at the workflow level (i.e., structured procedures for modeling and solving). For out-of-distribution adaptation, when encountering new or shifted problem types, the system either refines existing skills or creates new ones based on newly collected trajectories, thereby expanding a skill library that guides subsequent reasoning and problem solving.

Result: OptSkills attains a micro-averaged accuracy of 68.27% on datasets spanning diverse optimization problem types and usage scenarios, establishing a new state of the art among compared methods. On MIPLIB-NL, a particularly hard large-scale, high-dimensional benchmark, it reaches 26.91% accuracy, surpassing DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking by 4.53 percentage points. After performing skill learning on the Nano-CO dataset, OptSkills achieves 72.79% accuracy on the out-of-distribution NLCO benchmark, demonstrating strong transfer and OOD generalization.

Conclusion: Archetype-centric skill learning and reasoning provides a more robust and generalizable way to leverage LLMs for automated optimization modeling and solving. By organizing problems into archetype clusters and distilling successful trajectories into reusable workflow-level skills, OptSkills improves both in-distribution and out-of-distribution performance, outperforming strong baselines on multiple benchmarks. The released code and learned skills can facilitate further research and practical deployment of LLM-based optimization agents.

Abstract: Leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to automatically formulate and solve optimization problems from natural language has emerged as an efficient paradigm for automated optimization. However, existing methods still exhibit limited generalization: they are sensitive to superficial narrative variations, reuse experience mainly at the case level, and struggle to adapt to shifted or emerging problem types. We propose OptSkills, an archetype-centric skill learning and reasoning agent system for optimization modeling and solving. To improve robust generalization, our system clusters problems by their underlying archetypes rather than surface narratives. To improve in-distribution generalization, it explores diverse modeling paradigms and solver configurations within each cluster, then distills successful trajectories into reusable workflow-level skills. To improve out-of-distribution generalization, it refines existing skills or expands the skill library using newly obtained trajectories. Our system achieves a state-of-the-art micro-averaged accuracy of 68.27% on datasets encompassing diverse problem types and scenarios. In addition, on MIPLIB-NL, a highly challenging large-scale and high-dimensional benchmark, it achieves 26.91% accuracy, outperforming DeepSeek-V3.2-Thinking by 4.53%. After skill learning on Nano-CO, it reaches 72.79% on the OOD NLCO benchmark. Code and skills are available at https://github.com/fujiwaranoM0kou/OptSkills.

</details>


### [230] [OmniMatBench: A Human-Calibrated Multimodal Reasoning Benchmark Across 19 Materials Science Subfields](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29833)
*Wanhao Liu,Jiaqing Xie,Qian Tan,Weida Wang,Jue Wang,Ran Sun,Zhuo Yang,Wanli Ouyang,Lei Bai,Tianfan Fu,Lu Chen,Xin Chen,Yuqiang Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OmniMatBench is a new, human-calibrated multimodal reasoning benchmark for materials science that tests how well multimodal language models can reason from materials knowledge to applications, revealing large performance gaps across 19 subfields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multimodal language models are increasingly used in scientific research, but in materials science most existing benchmarks only test narrow tasks like property prediction, factual QA, or interpreting characterization data. They do not evaluate the broader reasoning chain from fundamental knowledge through processing to real-world applications, which is crucial in this applied, interdisciplinary domain. A dedicated benchmark is needed to systematically measure and analyze these reasoning capabilities.

Method: The authors construct OmniMatBench, a benchmark of 3,171 expert-curated questions and calculation problems covering 19 subfields of materials science, organized from fundamentals to structural/engineering materials, processing/manufacturing, and functional/applied materials. They then evaluate 13 state-of-the-art open- and closed-source multimodal large language models under different assisted settings (formula, retrieval, code) and analyze performance patterns, including subfield variation, reasoning heuristics, and knowledge coverage.
