Result: The best-performing model reaches only an overall score of 0.372 on OmniMatBench, indicating that current multimodal LLMs struggle significantly with comprehensive materials-science reasoning. Performance varies strongly across different subfields, and models exhibit rigid reasoning heuristics, uneven distribution of materials knowledge, and difficulty in performing high-level application-oriented reasoning even when given formula, retrieval, or code assistance.

Conclusion: OmniMatBench exposes substantial limitations of current multimodal language models in performing end-to-end reasoning in materials science and highlights where they fall short across topics and reasoning types. The benchmark serves both as a diagnostic tool to understand these weaknesses and as a standardized foundation to develop, evaluate, and improve more reliable AI assistants for materials-science research.

Abstract: As multimodal language models play an increasingly important role in scientific research, materials science offers a critical testbed due to its interdisciplinary, multimodal, and application-driven nature. However, existing materials benchmarks mainly focus on property prediction, knowledge QA, or characterization understanding, leaving the broader reasoning process from materials knowledge to application underexplored. To fill this gap, we present OmniMatBench, a human-calibrated multimodal reasoning benchmark for materials science. OmniMatBench contains 3,171 expert-curated QA and calculation problems across 19 materials-science subfields, spanning fundamental materials knowledge, structural and engineering materials, materials processing and manufacturing, and functional and applied materials. We evaluate 13 open-source and closed-source MLLMs and find that the best model achieves only a 0.372 overall score, revealing a substantial gap in current materials-science reasoning. Further analysis shows strong variation across subfields, fixed reasoning heuristics, uneven materials knowledge, and limited high-level knowledge application under formula-, retrieval-, and code-assisted settings. OmniMatBench provides crucial insights into the capabilities and limitations of current MLLMs and establishes a foundation for reliable AI assistants in materials-science research.

</details>


### [231] [Moment-KV: Momentum-Based Decode-Time KV Cache Compression for Long Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29873)
*Soumyadeep Jana,Sagar Nishad,Sanasam Ranbir Singh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Moment-KV, a momentum-based KV cache compression method tailored for the decoding phase of LLMs to reduce memory without harming accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: KV cache is a memory and latency bottleneck for long generations. Existing uniform compression across prefill and decoding harms quality because prefill context is critical, and decoding-specific compression is underexplored and naive (recency windows, instantaneous attention) that mishandle which tokens to keep.

Method: Analyze temporal attention patterns in LLMs and define a momentum-style token importance score that aggregates attention over time with decay. Use this evolving importance state at decoding time to selectively compress/evict entries in the KV cache, preserving tokens with lasting influence and recent relevance while discarding stale, low-impact tokens.

Result: On long-generation tasks, Moment-KV improves generation fidelity by 2.3–3.2% compared with prior compression baselines, without increasing decoding latency.

Conclusion: Focusing compression on the decoding cache and modeling token importance as a decayed, temporally aggregated attention signal yields better KV cache compression: it alleviates the KV bottleneck while preserving or improving long-context generation quality and maintaining speed.

Abstract: Key-Value (KV) cache remains a major bottleneck for deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) in long-generation tasks. Prior work often applies uniform compression across both prefill and decoding caches, but compressing the prefill cache degrades performance by corrupting critical context. While preserving the prefill cache is essential, decoding-phase compression remains underexplored, with existing methods relying on rigid recency windows or instantaneous attention. Our analysis of attention dynamics reveals strong temporal patterns: critical tokens receive sustained attention over long horizons, while local reasoning involves short-lived bursts. Static heuristics fail to capture this behavior, leading to premature eviction of important tokens or retention of stale ones. We propose Moment-KV, a decoding-time KV cache compression method based on momentum-driven temporal attention aggregation. Our method models token importance as a continuously evolving state, where attention is aggregated with decay, capturing both long-term influence and recent relevance. Experiments show that Moment-KV significantly improves generation fidelity in long-generation tasks (2.3-3.2 %) while maintaining decoding latency.

</details>


### [232] [Redundant or Necessary? A Benchmark for Detecting Redundant Steps in Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29893)
*Minyang Hu,Bo Yang,Zhinuo Zhou,Jiachen Liang,Guo Jiahao,Yiyang Yin,Xiongwei Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces RedundancyBench and the new task of detecting redundant steps in LLM agent trajectories, showing current methods perform poorly.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents often use many steps and tools to complete tasks, but evaluations typically only measure success, not how many unnecessary steps are taken. Redundant steps waste computation and time, so the authors want a way to systematically study and detect such inefficiencies.

Method: The authors formalize “redundant step detection” as a task on agent trajectories where each step is labeled by its contribution to task completion. They build RedundancyBench, a benchmark of diverse tasks with carefully annotated trajectories, and then implement three representative detection methods that, given a step in a trajectory, must decide whether it is redundant or necessary. They evaluate these methods on the benchmark.

Result: On RedundancyBench, the best method reaches only 24.88% in detecting redundant steps, and some methods perform worse than random guessing, indicating that current approaches are highly unreliable for this task.

Conclusion: Redundant step detection in LLM agent trajectories is challenging, and existing methods are far from adequate. RedundancyBench exposes this gap and motivates further research on making LLM agents more execution-efficient by better identifying and reducing unnecessary steps.

Abstract: LLM-based agents have demonstrated strong capabilities in solving complex tasks through multi-step reasoning and tool use. However, existing evaluation protocols primarily focus on task success, overlooking a critical aspect of agent behavior: execution efficiency. In practice, agent trajectories often contain redundant steps that consume substantial resources while contributing little to task completion. In this work, we propose and formulate a new research area: \textbf{redundant step detection} for agent trajectories. To support this initiative, we introduce \textbf{RedundancyBench}, a new benchmark that contains diverse tasks with carefully annotated trajectories, where each step is labeled according to its contribution to task completion. Using RedundancyBench, we develop and evaluate 3 representative methods to answer whether a step within trajectory is redundant or necessary. Our results show that even the best-performing method achieves only 24.88\% score in detecting redundant steps, while some methods perform worse than random guessing. These results highlight the task's complexity and the need for further research in this area. \footnote{Code and dataset in this paper are both available in \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/RedundancyBench}{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/RedundancyBench}.}

</details>


### [233] [On the Geometry of Games and their Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29919)
*Yaqi Sun,Julian Ma,David Mguni*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a learned, structure-aware framework that adaptively selects and combines game-theoretic solvers based on a low-dimensional representation of each game, revealing a continuous geometry of solvability across games.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches to equilibrium computation analyze algorithms separately on specific classes of games, producing strong but local guarantees and fragmented understanding. Discrete taxonomies of games and solvers often fail to capture intermediate or overlapping regimes where classical theory offers little guidance. There is a need for a unified, data-driven way to understand where different solver dynamics succeed or fail and how game structure governs solvability.

Method: The authors introduce structure-aware solver synthesis. First, they learn a structure recogniser that maps each game instance to a low-dimensional, solver-aligned representation capturing latent structural properties relevant for optimisation dynamics. Then, they learn a policy that maps this representation to a composition of primitive solver mechanisms (e.g., different update rules or dynamics), effectively generating a solver adapted to the game’s position in the solver-game map. They also incorporate a bounded residual component that serves as a local corrector to improve performance when the primitive basis or representation is incomplete, and as a diagnostic signal indicating where the current solver basis fails to fully capture game structure. They empirically study the induced geometry by clustering games based on their learned representations and associated solver behaviours.

Result: The framework yields an adaptive solver that adjusts its dynamics across different game regimes by selecting and mixing primitive mechanisms according to the learned representation. Empirical results show that fixed, hand-designed primitives systematically mismatch certain game regimes, whereas the learned structure recogniser organizes the space of games into coherent regions aligned with solver effectiveness. The solver-game map reveals zones where particular dynamics work well, where mixtures are needed, and where residual corrections become important, thus exposing overlapping regions of algorithmic validity.

Conclusion: Equilibrium computation is better viewed as a joint problem of (i) learning solver mechanisms and (ii) learning a geometry of solvability that maps games to solver-aligned representations. By doing so, one can move beyond discrete, hand-crafted taxonomies to a continuous, data-driven cartography in which games with similar optimisation dynamics cluster together and appropriate solver behaviours are automatically synthesised. This provides both a practically adaptive solver and an analytical tool for understanding solver performance across heterogeneous games.

Abstract: A central challenge in game theory and learning systems such as GANs is understanding which algorithms can efficiently compute equilibria across the heterogeneous landscape of games. Equilibrium computation is typically studied solver by solver and game class by game class, yielding strong local guarantees but a fragmented view of solver behaviour. Existing discrete taxonomies often provide an incomplete account of where algorithms succeed. We study this problem through a solver-game map linking games to effective solver dynamics. Classical theory identifies isolated regions of this map but provides limited insight into intermediate or overlapping regimes, suggesting that solvability is governed by latent structural properties defining a continuous solver-aligned geometry of games. We formalise this perspective through structure-aware solver synthesis. A learned structure recogniser maps each game to a low-dimensional solver-aligned representation, and a policy maps this representation to effective primitive mechanisms, adapting solver behaviour across regimes. This reveals regions where particular solver dynamics are effective and where mixtures of primitives are required rather than a single dominant solver. A bounded residual acts as a local corrector and diagnostic signal for incomplete solver bases or representations. The framework yields both an adaptive solver and an analytical lens: games with similar optimisation dynamics cluster together, revealing continuous regions of algorithmic validity and overlapping solver behaviour. Empirically, we show that fixed primitives exhibit systematic regime mismatch, while the learned representation organises game space into a structured cartography aligned with solver behaviour. These results suggest viewing equilibrium computation as the joint problem of learning solver mechanisms and mapping the geometry of solvability.

</details>


### [234] [Toward AI Systems That Understand Self and Others: A Multi-Phase Inference Framework for Human Cognitive Diversity and World-Model Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29930)
*Toru Takahashi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a formal framework (MIM) to explain why people with similar observations still form different world models, and how to align these heterogeneous models without forcing value convergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explain and systematically model how deep mutual misunderstanding arises even when people share the same data, and to provide tools for constructive alignment across divergent world models in philosophy, society, and AI.

Method: Conceptual and formal development of a Multi-Phase Inference Mechanism (MIM) involving phase-formation space, foregrounding field, subject-specific profile states, and alignment maps between state representations, used to characterize heterogeneous world-model construction and alignment.

Result: A multi-phase inference framework that shows how heterogeneous world models emerge and how they can be made mutually processable via alignment maps, reframing alignment as interoperability of representations instead of convergence of beliefs or values; links are drawn to philosophical disputes, cognitive types, social fragmentation, and AI alignment problems.

Conclusion: World-model alignment should be understood as making differing representations interoperable and transformable rather than forcing consensus; the proposed MIM framework offers a vocabulary and structure for AI systems to expose, compare, and transform differences in meaning, value, and prediction, thereby supporting better mutual understanding among agents.

Abstract: Mutual misunderstanding in contemporary society does not arise merely because people hold different opinions or values. Even under the same observations, different subjects may form different inferential targets, state representations, prediction errors, and update priorities. This paper proposes a multi-phase inference framework and defines its core internal mechanism as the Multi-Phase Inference Mechanism (MIM). MIM formalizes how heterogeneous world models arise through a phase-formation space, a foregrounding field, subject-specific profile states, and alignment maps between state representations. On this basis, the paper reframes world-model alignment as the problem of making heterogeneous representations mutually processable, rather than forcing agreement or convergence to a single value system. It further connects this formalism to philosophical disagreements, cognitive typology, social fragmentation, and AI alignment. The aim is to provide a constructive vocabulary for AI systems that can help humans understand self and others by making differences in meaning, value, and prediction error visible, comparable, and transformable.

</details>


### [235] [It`s All About Speed: AI`s Impact on Workflow in Music Production](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29931)
*Finn McClellan,Fabio Morreale*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Ethnographic study of how AI and automated tools affect professional music production workflows.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how AI and automated software are actually used by professional recording engineers, mixers, and producers, and how these tools impact their work practices and creative processes.

Method: Conduct an ethnographic study with professional music production participants, examining their workflows, tool usage, and sentiments regarding AI and automated tools.

Result: Identified patterns of use of AI and automated tools among professionals and surfaced key tensions around speed/efficiency, controllability, and creative agency in music production workflows.

Conclusion: AI and automated tools create both opportunities and tensions in professional music production; thoughtful tool design can help alleviate conflicts around efficiency, control, and creative agency.

Abstract: In this paper, we present the results of an ethnographic study into the impact of AI and automated tools on music production workflow. Focusing specifically on professional participants who identified as recording engineers, mixers, and producers, we discuss their usage of common AI and automated software, as well as their sentiments on the proliferation of these tools. We discuss tensions that may be created between users and automated tools in key areas such as the need for speed and efficiency, controllability, and maintaining creative agency, and how these tensions may be alleviated through tool design.

</details>


### [236] [Make LLM Learn to Synthesize from Streaming Experiences through Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29940)
*Zhenlin Hu,Yan Wang,Zhen Bi,Zihao Xue,Bingyu Zhu,Longtao Huang,Xiongtao Zhang,Zeyu Yang,Zhixuan Chu,Jungang Lou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a new setting, StreamSynth, where data synthesis tasks arrive sequentially and past synthesis experience is reused to improve future tasks, and proposes SynLearner, a framework that learns reusable synthesis strategies over a task stream.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based synthetic data generation methods treat each synthesis task independently and ignore the possibility that a model could accumulate and transfer synthesis experience from earlier tasks to later ones. The authors want to investigate whether synthetic data generation can be made experience-driven, improving efficiency and quality across a stream of tasks.

Method: They define the StreamSynth setting, where synthesis tasks are presented in sequence and models must leverage historical task experience. They then propose SynLearner, a general framework that: (1) encourages exploration of diverse synthesis patterns, (2) learns from feedback over time, and (3) jointly optimizes sample-level quality and set-level diversity as tasks evolve. SynLearner reuses synthesis strategies learned on earlier tasks to guide generation on later ones.

Result: Through extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks, SynLearner shows that it can effectively utilize past-task experience to enhance data synthesis on later tasks, achieving better quality/diversity trade-offs and demonstrating consistent cross-task transferability compared to baselines that treat tasks independently.

Conclusion: The study validates the feasibility and benefits of the StreamSynth setting, showing that synthetic data generation can be treated as an experience-driven process where models accumulate and transfer synthesis knowledge over task streams. SynLearner provides a practical framework for such continual, transferable synthetic data generation.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have been widely adopted for synthetic data generation, significantly reducing annotation costs. However, most existing studies treat synthesis as a set of isolated tasks and overlook a more fundamental question: whether a model can learn to synthesize by accumulating experience from past tasks and transferring it to future ones. In this work, we introduce StreamSynth, a new setting in which synthesis tasks arrive sequentially and experience from historical tasks provides informative signals for future synthesis. To address this setting, we propose SynLearner, a general framework that enables synthesis models to acquire reusable synthesis experience over a task stream. Instead of generating data independently for each task, SynLearner encourages the model to explore diverse synthesis patterns, learn from feedback, and balance sample quality with set-level diversity as tasks evolve. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show that SynLearner effectively leverages experience from earlier tasks to improve synthesis performance on later ones, exhibiting consistent cross-task transferability. These findings provide evidence for the feasibility of StreamSynth and highlight synthetic data generation as an experience-driven process that can benefit from task streams.

</details>


### [237] [MuPHI: Learning Implicit Multimodal Harm Reasoning via Semantically Grounded Reward Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29951)
*Anisha Saha,Varsha Suresh,Teodora Kamova,Sophia Wiedmann,Timothy Hospedales,Vera Demberg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how harmful meaning can emerge from seemingly benign image-text pairs and proposes a new dataset and training framework to improve vision-language models’ ability to detect such subtle multimodal harm and explain their reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current vision-language models mainly capture surface-level perceptual cues and literal meanings, so they struggle with context-dependent, implicit harms that only emerge when combining image and text pragmatically. There is a lack of benchmarks and methods focused specifically on compositional, intent-aware multimodal harm understanding.

Method: The authors build MuPHI, a dataset of image-text pairs where harmful intent or impact is encoded via subtle, compositional multimodal cues; the dataset covers multiple harm categories and provides human-annotated harm rationales. They then propose MuPHIRM, a reasoning-augmented training framework that aligns VLMs with joint multimodal semantics by optimizing multiple reward signals focusing on both detection accuracy and reasoning quality.

Result: Models trained with MuPHIRM achieve better harm detection performance and produce higher-quality reasoning chains on MuPHI, and they generalize better to out-of-distribution settings than both standard fine-tuning baselines and inference-time augmentation approaches.

Conclusion: Explicitly training VLMs with reasoning-focused, multi-perspective reward optimization on a carefully designed dataset like MuPHI leads to improved recognition of subtle, context-dependent multimodal harm and more robust reasoning, suggesting a promising path for safer and more generalizable multimodal systems.

Abstract: Understanding how harm emerges from interaction between otherwise benign image-text pairs requires intent-aware cross-modal reasoning beyond surface-level features. Existing vision-language models (VLMs) excel at literal reasoning over perceptual cues but often fail to derive harmful semantics that rely on implicit, context-dependent reasoning. To evaluate VLMs on compositional harm detection and reasoning, we introduce Multimodal Pragmatic Harm Interpretation (MuPHI), a dataset containing image-text pairs where harm is encoded in subtle multimodal cues. MuPHI spans diverse harm categories and includes annotated harm rationales for assessing VLM reasoning chains. To improve both detection and reasoning in VLMs, we propose MuPHIRM, a reasoning-augmented training framework which learns joint semantics by optimizing multi-perspective rewards. MuPHIRM improves both harm detection and reasoning quality of VLMs while demonstrating superior out-of-distribution robustness compared to both trained and inference-time baselines. Our findings suggest that reasoning-oriented reward optimization offers a promising direction towards building multimodal systems that generalize beyond benchmark-specific shortcuts.

</details>


### [238] [Formalizing Mathematics at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29955)
*Ahmad Rammal,Niket Patel,Fabian Gloeckle,Amaury Hayat,Julia Kempe,Remi Munos,Charles Arnal,Vivien Cabannes*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AutoformBot is a large-scale multi-agent system that autoformalizes textbook mathematics into Lean 4, producing Atlas, a massive machine-checked library, demonstrating that graduate-level math formalization is now feasible at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formalizing advanced mathematics in proof assistants is powerful but traditionally slow, labor-intensive, and requires rare expertise. Recent LLMs can help, but prior approaches have been limited in scale, robustness, and coverage. The paper seeks a way to systematically and economically convert large amounts of informal textbook math into fully verified formal code, enabling reliable, large libraries of machine-checked mathematics and supporting automated verification of both human and AI-generated research-level math.

Method: They design AutoformBot, a multi-agent system coordinating thousands of LLM instances. These agents interact with Lean 4 and supporting tools, using dependency-aware task scheduling to break textbooks into manageable formalization tasks, and collaborative version control to integrate contributions. Each agent translates informal prose into candidate Lean code, which is then checked and iteratively improved using feedback from the proof assistant and the multi-agent workflow. The system is applied across 26 open-access textbooks covering several core mathematical domains.

Result: Using AutoformBot, they autoformalize content from 26 textbooks to build Atlas, a Lean 4 library containing over 45,000 verified declarations and about 500k lines of code. This demonstrates that a largely automated pipeline can produce a large, coherent, machine-checked corpus of graduate-level mathematics across multiple fields.

Conclusion: AutoformBot plus Atlas show that large-scale autoformalization of graduate-level mathematics is now technically and economically viable. This creates a foundation for automated verification of complex mathematical arguments, whether written by humans or generated by AI, and suggests a path toward comprehensive, machine-checked mathematical knowledge bases.

Abstract: We present AutoformBot, a multi-agent system for building an Autoformalized Textbook Library At Scale (Atlas) in Lean 4. AutoformBot orchestrates thousands of LLM agents, equipped with formal verification tools, dependency-aware task scheduling, and collaborative version control, to translate informal textbook prose into machine-checked definitions and proofs. We apply our methods to a corpus of 26 open-access textbooks spanning analysis, algebra, topology, combinatorics, and probability, producing Atlas: a verified library of over 45,000 Lean 4 declarations and 500 thousand lines of code. We release two artifacts: (i) AutoformBot, the open-source multi-agent framework; and (ii) Atlas, the resulting formal library. Our results suggest that autoformalizing the core content of graduate-level mathematics at scale is now economically and technically feasible. This opens the door to the automated verification of both human- and machine-generated mathematics at a research level.

</details>


### [239] [Meta-Programming for Linear-time Temporal Answer Set Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29965)
*Susana Hahn,Amade Nems,Javier Romero,Torsten Schaub*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They introduce a unified, flexible meta-programming framework to implement and experiment with different temporal extensions of Answer Set Programming in a single declarative setting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing temporal extensions of Answer Set Programming (TEL, DEL, MEL) have different semantics, but state-of-the-art ASP systems are highly optimized and rigid, which makes it hard to quickly prototype, compare, and implement alternative temporal logics and semantic variations. A more flexible, declarative mechanism is needed to explore temporal logical designs without changing the underlying solver implementation.

Method: They extend standard ASP meta-programming in clingo by (1) augmenting clingo's theory grammar with formal type specifications and support for nesting of modalities, and (2) designing a transformation pipeline that preserves the semantics of nested temporal operators by preventing incorrect simplifications during grounding under stable-model semantics. On top of this generic infrastructure, they implement meta-encodings that capture the semantics of three temporal equilibrium logics: non-monotonic linear-time TEL, metric MEL, and dynamic DEL, including handling of interval constraints and Fischer-Ladner closure.

Result: The authors obtain a working meta-programming framework that can encode the semantics of several temporal logics (TEL, MEL, DEL) within ASP itself. They show detailed meta-encodings, give a full account of TEL semantics in the framework, and demonstrate how it supports MEL’s interval constraints and DEL’s Fischer-Ladner closure. This all comes packaged in a practical tool, metasp, that realizes the proposed workflow on top of clingo.

Conclusion: A unified, declarative meta-programming layer on top of clingo can flexibly realize and experiment with different temporal equilibrium logics without modifying the underlying ASP solver. By extending theory grammars with types and nesting plus a careful transformation pipeline, the framework maintains semantic correctness while enabling rapid prototyping. The metasp system operationalizes this idea and serves as a general platform for temporal ASP research and applications.

Abstract: The development of temporal extensions of Answer Set Programming (ASP) has led to the emergence of non-monotonic linear-time (TEL), dynamic (DEL), and metric (MEL) temporal equilibrium logics. However, the inherent rigidity of highly optimized ASP systems often hinders the rapid exploration and implementation of alternative logical designs. In this work, we propose a flexible meta-programming framework that operationalizes the semantics of varied temporal logics through a unified, declarative framework. Our approach extends standard ASP meta-programming by augmenting clingo's theory grammar with formal type specifications and nesting capabilities. To ensure semantic correctness, we introduce a transformation pipeline that protects nested modalities from stable-model-based simplifications during grounding. We demonstrate the extensibility of our framework by implementing meta-encodings for TEL, MEL, and DEL. We provide a comprehensive account of TEL and highlight the key features for managing the interval constraints of MEL and the Fischer-Ladner closure in DEL. Finally, we introduce the metasp system, a versatile tool that encapsulates this workflow.

</details>


### [240] [Compass: Navigating Global Marine Lead Data Integration through Expert-Guided LLM Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29966)
*Yiming Liu,Bin Lu,Meng Jin,Ziyuan Sang,Shuo Jiang,Lei Zhou,Xinbing Wang,Chenghu Zhou,Jing Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents Compass, an expert-guided LLM agent framework that extracts high-quality marine lead (Pb) data from large corpora of scientific papers without fine-tuning, substantially expanding global Pb databases.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Marine Pb and its isotopes are important tracers for understanding ocean circulation and human pollution, but collecting new in-situ measurements is expensive and existing historical data are locked in unstructured literature, forming data silos. Manual extraction is too labor-intensive and standard LLMs hallucinate or misinterpret domain-specific content, so a new, scalable and scientifically reliable data extraction approach is needed.

Method: The authors develop an expert-guided adaptation framework implemented as Compass, an LLM agent steered by a domain-informed Knowledge Tree co-designed with marine scientists. This structure decomposes complex extraction tasks into smaller, verifiable reasoning steps and imposes constraints that enforce scientific validity. Compass is deployed on a corpus of over 230,000 open-access papers related to marine Pb to automatically identify, extract, and validate Pb concentration and isotope records, with multiple layers of automated and expert validation to assess accuracy.

Result: Using Compass on the large paper corpus yields 3,751 Pb records that were not part of existing compilations, forming the largest integrated marine Pb database so far. The records notably improve spatial coverage in historically under-sampled regions such as the East China Sea and Southern Ocean. Expert manual checks show that Compass achieves 92% extraction accuracy, indicating high reliability for a non–fine-tuned LLM-based system.

Conclusion: Expert-guided LLM agents, when structured with domain-specific knowledge and robust validation, can overcome the limitations of general-purpose LLMs in high-stakes scientific contexts. Compass showcases that such agents can scalably and reliably mine legacy literature to construct large, high-quality domain databases, advancing data availability for marine Pb research and offering a transferable paradigm for data discovery across geosciences.

Abstract: Marine lead (Pb) and its isotopes are critical tracers for ocean circulation and anthropogenic pollution, yet in-situ observations remain costly and sparse. While vast historical records exist, they lie buried within the unstructured content of academic papers, creating "data silos" inaccessible to comprehensive analysis. Manual extraction is unscalable, while general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) lack the necessary domain-specific knowledge, leading to hallucinations and scientifically invalid outputs. To address this, we introduce an expert-guided adaptation approach that enables LLMs to perform rigorous scientific data extraction without fine-tuning. We operationalize this approach through Compass, an LLM agent framework enhanced by a Knowledge Tree co-designed with marine scientists, which decomposes complex tasks into verifiable steps, guiding the agent's reasoning to ensure scientific validity. Deploying Compass across a corpus of over 230,000 relevant open-access papers, we successfully extract 3,751 previously unincorporated Pb records. This effort establishes the largest integrated marine Pb database to date. Beyond standard metrics, Compass demonstrates superior reliability through multi-layered validation, achieving 92% accuracy as confirmed through expert manual verification. The newly integrated data expand coverage in previously under-sampled regions such as the East China Sea and the Southern Ocean, providing an enriched data foundation for future scientific discoveries. We release an interactive visualization platform to facilitate open scientific access. Our work demonstrates that expert-guided agents can effectively bridge the gap between general-purpose LLMs and high-stakes scientific domains, enabling scalable data discovery in geosciences.

</details>


### [241] [Accelerating Constrained Decoding with Token Space Compression](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.29986)
*Michael Sullivan,Alexander Koller*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents CFGzip, a method to compress the token search space in CFG-constrained decoding for LLMs, drastically reducing latency and making complex grammar-constrained generation practical at scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CFG-constrained decoding engines for LLMs suffer from very high overhead when dealing with complex context-free grammars because they must search over the entire token vocabulary at every decoding step. This makes structured, grammar-constrained generation impractical in precisely the challenging settings where it is needed most. The paper aims to remove this performance bottleneck.

Method: The authors propose CFGzip, an offline compression technique that reduces the token search space used by CFG decoding engines. Instead of considering the full vocabulary during decoding, CFGzip precomputes a compact representation (compressed search space) tailored to the CFG so that, at inference time, the grammar engine only needs to search over a much smaller, grammar-aware subset of tokens at each step. This integrates with existing SoTA CFG engines without changing model weights.

Result: Experiments show that using CFGzip with a state-of-the-art grammar engine leads to latency reductions of up to two orders of magnitude for CFG operations, and up to a 7.5x speedup in total constrained generation time for complex grammars. This demonstrates that CFGzip makes grammar-constrained decoding far more efficient in realistic scenarios.

Conclusion: CFGzip effectively addresses the scalability problem of CFG-constrained decoding in LLMs by compressing the per-step token search space. This makes it practical to use complex grammars for structured generation at scale, significantly reducing latency while maintaining the benefits of CFG-based control.

Abstract: To guarantee that an LLM's outputs conform to a specified structure, context-free grammar (CFG) decoding engines force the selection of next tokens that produce strings that conform to a given CFG. While current CFG-constrained decoding engines are highly optimized, the inherent costs arising from the massive per-step search space -- i.e. the entire token vocabulary -- result in intractably high overhead for more complex CFGs: precisely the situation where CFG engines are most useful. In this paper, we introduce CFGzip, an offline technique for compressing the token search space, which massively reduces CFG engine overhead. In experiments, we report latency reduction of up to two orders of magnitude when CFGzip is used with a SoTA grammar engine, yielding an up to 7.5x speedup in total constrained generation time: with CFGzip, constrained decoding is now feasible at scale for complex CFGs.

</details>


### [242] [Cookie-Bench: Continuous On-screen Key Interaction Evaluation for Web Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30000)
*Haoyue Yang,Zhangxiao Shen,Fan Ding,Hangting Lou,Yifeng Kou,Haoqing Yu,Jingyao Li,Zhengfan Wu,Siqi Bao,Jing Liu,Hua Wu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a new, automated, reference-free framework to evaluate LLM-generated front-end web apps, introducing both a benchmark (WebDev dataset) and a metacognitive evaluation agent that closely matches human judgments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating interactive, LLM-generated web front-ends is slow and expensive because current methods rely on human leaderboards or rigid automated checklists and test suites. These existing approaches fail to capture the holistic, reasoned assessment that human reviewers perform over a live interactive session, and they do not scale with rapid model development. The authors want a scalable, automated, yet human-like evaluation regime for web app generation.

Method: The authors introduce two main components. (1) A benchmark dataset, named WebDev (\dataname), comprising 1,000 tasks across 11 domains and 54 leaf categories, covering both static and interactive web tasks. The tasks are balanced by difficulty (three tiers) and output languages (three language groups), with prompts rewritten to resist trivial recall from public prompt sets. (2) An evaluation framework (\framename) inspired by Flavell's metacognitive monitoring. It decomposes evaluation into three stages: Static Perception (a first impression from passive observation of the UI), Agent-Driven Interaction (an autonomous agent explores the page, recording video, audio, and stepwise screenshots as evidence), and Dynamic Scoring (after evidence collection is complete, the system issues holistic judgments about functionality and aesthetics, along with structured attribution of failures). The framework is reference-free and does not rely on gold implementations or predefined test suites.

Result: On the WebDev benchmark, the proposed \framename evaluation aligns closely with expert human raters, indicating that it captures similar qualitative judgments. At the same time, its scores reveal considerable performance gaps (“headroom”) across 13 state-of-the-art LLMs on interactive web generation tasks, suggesting that current models are far from saturating the benchmark.

Conclusion: The paper demonstrates that a reference-free, autonomously driven, metacognitively structured evaluation framework can effectively approximate expert human judgments for LLM-generated front-end web applications. By releasing both the WebDev benchmark and the \framename evaluation pipeline, the authors provide a scalable and more human-like alternative to leaderboard-based or checklist-style evaluations, and they show that existing frontier LLMs still have substantial room for improvement on complex interactive web generation.

Abstract: Front-end web code has become a core product surface for every frontier LLM release, yet evaluating these interactive applications at development speed remains costly because human-judged leaderboards like Arena do not scale. Existing automated proxies typically lean on reference implementations, test suites, or rigid checklists, and tend to miss the reasoned synthesis a human reviewer performs over a live session. We articulate a new evaluation regime that is simultaneously reference-free, autonomously driven, and holistically reasoned, and instantiate it through two artifacts. \textbf{\dataname} is an 11-domain, 54-leaf, 1,000-query WebDev benchmark spanning both static-presentation and interactive-application tasks, balanced across three difficulty tiers and three target-language groups, with briefs rewritten to resist recall from circulated prompts. \textbf{\framename}, grounded in Flavell's metacognitive monitoring, separates evidence accumulation from judgment across three stages: Static Perception forms a first impression from passive observation; Agent-Driven Interaction explores the application autonomously while capturing continuous screen video, audio, and per-step screenshots; Dynamic Scoring issues holistic functionality and aesthetics verdicts with structured failure attribution only after the evidence chain is complete. On \dataname, \framename aligns closely with expert human ratings while surfacing substantial headroom across 13 frontier LLMs on interactive web generation. \noindenthttps://anonymous.4open.science/r/Cookie-3CE/

</details>


### [243] [KairosAgent: Agentic Time Series Forecasting with Fused Semantic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30002)
*Kun Feng,Ziwei Shan,Yuchen Fang,Yiyang Tan,Sihan Lu,Shuqi Gu,Lintao Ma,Xingyu Lu,Kan Ren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: KairosAgent is an agentic framework that combines an LLM-based reasoner with a time-series foundation model forecaster, using tools, trajectory data, and RL to improve zero-shot multimodal time-series forecasting.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cross-domain multimodal time series forecasting needs both strong numerical prediction and deep semantic reasoning across domains and modalities. Existing time-series foundation models lack future-oriented semantic reasoning, while LLM-based methods have poor numerical comprehension and quantitative accuracy. There is a need for a framework that can fully leverage strengths of both TSFMs and LLMs for accurate, interpretable, and efficient forecasting.

Method: The authors design KairosAgent, an agentic architecture with two main components: (1) an LLM-based reasoner that performs textual and semantic reasoning, dynamically calling analytical tools to strengthen numerical understanding; and (2) a TSFM-based forecaster that performs quantitative prediction. The LLM’s reasoning outputs are fused into the TSFM forecasting pipeline. They also construct a large-scale corpus of reasoning trajectories and propose a reinforcement learning from forecasting paradigm with multi-turn refinement and turn-level credit assignment to optimize the reasoning process for better forecasting performance.

Result: Empirical experiments show that KairosAgent delivers superior zero-shot performance on multimodal, cross-domain time series forecasting benchmarks compared with prior approaches, while effectively leveraging existing pretrained LLMs and TSFMs. The system improves both forecasting accuracy and interpretability of the prediction process.

Conclusion: KairosAgent demonstrates that an agentic framework that tightly couples LLM-based reasoning with TSFM-based numerical forecasting, enhanced by tool use and RL from forecasting feedback, can overcome limitations of standalone LLM or TSFM models. This offers a promising path toward practical, efficient, and interpretable time-series forecasting agents and highlights the value of integrating semantic reasoning with quantitative modeling.

Abstract: Cross-domain multimodal time series forecasting is a challenging task, requiring models to integrate precise numerical comprehension, cross-domain semantic understanding, and effective multimodal fusion. Existing approaches either build Time Series Foundation Models (TSFMs) from scratch or leverage pretrained Large Language Models (LLMs). However, TSFMs often overlook semantic understanding and lack the ability to perform future-oriented semantic reasoning, and LLMs struggle with numerical comprehension and accurate quantitative forecasting. To overcome these limitations, we propose KairosAgent, a novel agentic framework for multimodal time series forecasting, including an LLM-based reasoner and a TSFM-based forecaster. KairosAgent unifies textual reasoning and numerical forecasting by dynamically invoking analytical tools to enhance the numerical understanding and semantic reasoning capabilities of LLMs. The reasoning results are subsequently fused into the TSFM pipeline, enabling more accurate and reliable future predictions. To further improve the reasoning, we curate a large-scale corpus of high-quality trajectories, alongside a reinforcement learning from forecasting paradigm with multi-turn refinement and turn-level credit assignment. Experiments demonstrate that KairosAgent achieves superior zero-shot forecasting performance while maximizing the utility of pretrained LLMs and TSFMs, presenting a promising direction for efficient and interpretable time series agents. The project page is at https://foundation-model-research.github.io/KairosAgent .

</details>


### [244] [From GPS Points to Travel Patterns: Flexible and Semantic Trajectory Generation with LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30014)
*Silin Zhou,Chenhao Wang,Yuntao Wen,Shuo Shang,Lisi Chen,Panos Kalnis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes HTP, a hierarchical trajectory generation framework that first learns discrete travel-pattern tokens from raw GPS data and then uses an LLM to generate these patterns under various conditions, finally decoding them back to realistic trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban trajectory data are vital for modeling city dynamics and enabling smart-city applications, but privacy concerns limit access to large, high-quality real datasets. Existing synthetic trajectory generators usually operate directly on GPS points, struggle to explicitly model human travel patterns, and are constrained to fixed-length trajectories under a single type of condition. There is a need for a privacy-preserving trajectory generation method that can flexibly model rich, realistic movement patterns and support varied generation conditions.

Method: HTP uses a hierarchical, two-stage approach. First, a trajectory-specific residual quantization variational autoencoder (RQ-VAE) encodes fine-grained GPS trajectories into compact, discrete travel pattern tokens via a coarse-to-fine quantization scheme. These tokens summarize macro-level movement behaviors while preserving micro-level spatial irregularities like varying point densities due to traffic. Second, the authors augment the vocabulary of a large language model with these learned tokens to make trajectories compatible with text-style sequence modeling, and apply supervised fine-tuning so the LLM learns to generate sequences of travel-pattern tokens conditioned on different context variables. The generated token sequences are then decoded back into full GPS trajectories.

Result: On two real-world trajectory datasets, HTP achieves substantially better synthetic trajectory quality than the strongest existing baseline, with an average improvement of 29.78% across evaluation metrics. The experiments demonstrate that HTP more faithfully captures spatial and structural properties of real trajectories while supporting conditional and variable-length generation.

Conclusion: By hierarchically modeling trajectories as sequences of learned travel-pattern tokens and leveraging LLMs for conditional sequence generation, HTP overcomes key limitations of prior trajectory generators that work directly on GPS points. The approach yields more realistic, flexible, and high-quality synthetic trajectories, offering a stronger privacy-preserving alternative to sharing raw mobility data and a new way to integrate representation learning with LLM-based generative modeling for urban computing tasks.

Abstract: Urban trajectories play a crucial role in modeling urban dynamics and supporting various smart city applications. However, privacy concerns restrict access to large-scale and high-quality trajectory datasets. Trajectory generation provides a promising alternative by synthesizing realistic data to mitigate privacy risks. However, existing methods fail to explicitly capture travel patterns and can only generate fixed-length trajectories under a single condition. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{HTP}, which \textbf{H}ierarchically generates \textbf{T}ravel patterns first and then generates GPS \textbf{P}oints by using large language models (LLMs), rather than directly generating GPS points. We first design a trajectory-specific residual quantization variational autoencoder (RQ-VAE) that quantizes micro-level GPS trajectories into compact, macro-level travel pattern tokens in a coarse-to-fine manner. These tokens capture rich segment spatial irregularities, such as point density variations caused by traffic conditions. Then, we extend the LLM vocabulary with travel pattern tokens to align trajectory representations with the LLM input, and apply supervised fine-tuning (SFT) to align the LLM with the trajectory generation task, enabling generation of travel pattern sequences under various conditions. Extensive experiments on two real-world datasets show that HTP outperforms the strongest baseline by an average of 29.78\% in terms of generation quality. Our code is available at https://github.com/slzhou-xy/HTP.

</details>


### [245] [RAISE: RAG Design as an Architecture Search Problem](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30029)
*Zhen Chen,Yibing Liu,Weihao Xie,Yu Liang,Peilin Chen,Shiqi Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces RAISE, a standardized framework and benchmark for systematically searching and optimizing RAG system hyperparameters, showing that optimal configurations are highly task-dependent.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing RAG systems involves many interacting choices (query rewriting, chunking, retrieval depth, reranking, context compression, etc.) that are usually set by ad-hoc heuristics, making results hard to reproduce and compare. There is a need for a principled, systematic way to search over these design choices and to fairly evaluate optimization methods across tasks.

Method: The authors formulate the configuration of RAG pipelines as a RAG architecture (or hyperparameter) search problem. They build RAISE, which defines standardized search spaces and budgets for RAG design choices and implements 13 different search/optimization algorithms. They then run these methods across seven public text and multimodal datasets, using three random seeds, to compare how well the algorithms optimize RAG pipelines under controlled conditions.

Result: Experiments show that optimization performance varies significantly by task: algorithms that work very well on one dataset may perform poorly on others. Consequently, aggregate performance rankings of search methods do not reliably indicate a universally best strategy across tasks.

Conclusion: RAISE offers a common, reproducible benchmark and framework for studying and comparing RAG hyperparameter optimization methods. The findings emphasize the task-dependence of RAG optimization and warn against over-generalizing from aggregate rankings, encouraging more nuanced, task-aware evaluation of search strategies.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems expose numerous design choices spanning query rewriting, chunking, retrieval depth, reranking, and context compression. In practice, these choices are often configured through heuristics, hindering systematic evaluation and reproducibility across settings. We argue that this challenge is best formulated as RAG architecture search. To support controlled and reproducible study of this problem, we introduce the RAG Intelligence Search Engine (RAISE), a comprehensive framework and benchmark for RAG hyperparameter optimization, which evaluates optimization methods for RAG pipelines under standardized search spaces and budgets. RAISE implements 13 search algorithms and evaluates them across seven public text and multimodal datasets using three random seeds. Our experiments show that optimization performance is highly task-dependent: methods that perform strongly on one dataset may not generalize consistently across others, cautioning against interpreting aggregate rankings as evidence of universally superior strategies. RAISE provides a common experimental substrate for fair, reproducible, and systematic research on RAG hyperparameter optimization.

</details>


### [246] [Teaching Values to Machines: Simulating Human-Like Behavior in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30036)
*Asaf Yehudai,Naama Rozen,Ariel Gera*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper tests whether large language models can be prompted to adopt stable, human-like value structures, using psychology-based questionnaires at scale, and finds that value-induced LLMs resemble humans and are useful for simulating human behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs can mimic various personas, it is unclear if they can consistently exhibit behavior guided by coherent, human-like values. Psychological value theory offers a structured way to characterize human values, but it has not yet been systematically applied to understand or shape LLM behavior. The authors want to know if LLMs can be configured to follow realistic human value structures and whether this makes them better tools for modeling human decisions and social behavior.

Method: The authors prompt state-of-the-art LLMs with value-related instructions derived from established psychological value theory. They administer validated human psychological questionnaires, at large scale (over 5 million questions), to these value-prompted models. They then measure (1) how closely the inferred value structures of LLMs match those found in human populations, and (2) how well the relationship between values and behaviors in LLMs parallels that observed in humans. They also experiment with injecting empirical human value distributions into LLM populations to assess effects on population-level behavioral simulations.

Result: Value-prompted LLMs show strong alignment with human data in both their underlying value structures and in how those values relate to behavioral choices. When human value distributions are used to configure LLM populations, the resulting simulations better approximate aggregate human behavior. This suggests that simple prompting with psychologically grounded value constructs is enough to induce realistic, human-like value patterns in LLMs.

Conclusion: The study concludes that LLMs can be induced, via psychologically informed prompting, to express value systems that are coherent and similar to those of humans, and that the link between values and behavior in these models resembles that seen in human studies. Incorporating empirically measured human value distributions further improves the realism of population-level simulations. Therefore, value-induced LLMs can serve as powerful, psychologically grounded tools for simulating and studying human behavior at scale.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate a remarkable capacity to adopt different personas and roles; however, it remains unclear whether they can manifest behavior that adheres to a coherent, human-like value structure. In this work, we draw on established psychological value theory to induce human-like values in LLMs and assess their alignment with patterns observed in human studies. Using validated psychological questionnaires, we conduct large-scale experiments -- over 5 million questions -- to evaluate value structures and value-behavior relationships in leading LLMs and compare them to humans. Our findings reveal strong agreement between value-prompted LLMs and humans across both dimensions. Moreover, incorporating human value distributions enhances population-level simulations with value-induced LLMs. These findings highlight the potential of value-induced LLMs as effective, psychologically grounded tools for simulating human behavior.

</details>


### [247] [Domain-Specific Data Synthesis for LLMs via Minimal Sufficient Representation Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30039)
*Tong Ye,Hang Yu,Tengfei Ma,Xuhong Zhang,Jianguo Li,Peng Di,Peiyu Liu,Jianwei Yin,Wenhai Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces DOMINO, an inductive framework that synthesizes domain-specific training data for LLMs using only reference examples instead of natural-language domain descriptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs can be fine-tuned for specific domains, obtaining high-quality, domain-relevant data is difficult. Existing synthesis methods rely on explicit, well-articulated natural language descriptions of the target domain and careful prompt engineering. This is impractical when domain characteristics are subtle, implicit, or hard to explain, such as certain coding or style domains. The paper aims to solve the problem of generating useful synthetic data in such hard-to-describe domains.

Method: The authors propose DOMINO, a framework that defines a target domain using only a set of reference samples. DOMINO learns a minimal sufficient representation of the domain via prompt tuning combined with a contrastive disentanglement objective. This objective separates domain-level patterns from instance-specific noise, preventing overfitting to particular examples while preserving core domain characteristics. The learned domain representation is then used to condition an LLM to generate new synthetic data aligned with the domain. Theoretical analysis shows that this approach expands the support of the synthetic data distribution compared to the reference set alone.

Result: On challenging coding benchmarks where domain definitions are implicit rather than explicitly described, synthetic data generated with DOMINO is used to fine-tune instruction-tuned LLM backbones. This improves Pass@1 accuracy by up to 4.63% compared with strong baselines, indicating better code generation performance and demonstrating the effectiveness and robustness of the method.

Conclusion: DOMINO establishes an inductive, example-driven paradigm for domain-specific data synthesis that avoids manual prompt design and the need for natural-language domain specifications. By learning a disentangled domain representation from reference samples and using it to guide synthetic data generation, it enables practical, scalable domain adaptation and yields more diverse, domain-aligned training data with measurable performance gains.

Abstract: Large Language Models have demonstrated remarkable progress in general-purpose capabilities and can achieve strong performance in specific domains through fine-tuning on domain-specific data. However, acquiring high-quality data for target domains remains a significant challenge. Existing data synthesis approaches follow a deductive paradigm, heavily relying on explicit domain descriptions expressed in natural language and careful prompt engineering, limiting their applicability in real-world scenarios where domains are difficult to describe or formally articulate. In this work, we tackle the underexplored problem of domain-specific data synthesis through an inductive paradigm, where the target domain is defined only through a set of reference examples, particularly when domain characteristics are difficult to articulate in natural language. We propose a novel framework, DOMINO, that learns a minimal sufficient domain representation from reference samples and leverages it to guide the generation of domain-aligned synthetic data. DOMINO integrates prompt tuning with a contrastive disentanglement objective to separate domain-level patterns from sample-specific noise, mitigating overfitting while preserving core domain characteristics. Theoretically, we prove that DOMINO expands the support of the synthetic data distribution, ensuring greater diversity. Empirically, on challenging coding benchmarks where domain definitions are implicit, fine-tuning on data synthesized by DOMINO improves Pass@1 accuracy by up to 4.63\% over strong, instruction-tuned backbones, demonstrating its effectiveness and robustness. This work establishes a new paradigm for domain-specific data synthesis, enabling practical and scalable domain adaptation without manual prompt design or natural language domain specifications.

</details>


### [248] [Learning to Choose: An Empowerment-Guided Multi-Agent System with semantic communication for Adaptive Method Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30042)
*Geremy Loachamín-Suntaxi,Robert Lazar,Dimitrios G. Giovanis,Ioannis G. Kevrekidis,Eleni D. Koronaki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a multi-agent framework for automating scientific computing workflows that prevents semantic drift between agents using semantic checkpoints, improving reliability and learning performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In complex multi-agent scientific workflows, small mismatches between what agents intend to do and what they actually execute can accumulate into semantic drift, causing the final computation to deviate from the originally chosen strategy. This undermines evaluation, adaptation, and autonomous learning, especially in adaptive decision-making settings like contextual bandits. The authors are motivated by prior frameworks (ATHENA, empowerment) and aim to ensure that autonomous systems not only choose good actions but also faithfully carry them out and keep outcomes causally attributable to those choices.

Method: They design a multi-agent framework that integrates contextual bandits for adaptive decision-making with structured communication between specialized LLM agents. The framework introduces semantic checkpoints that explicitly record and verify intended strategies and actions at key stages, ensuring fidelity between plans and executed procedures. It incorporates grounded code generation, self-healing execution loops that detect and correct deviations, and an architecture inspired by empowerment that emphasizes preserving action-outcome relationships across agents.

Result: Through case studies on sensitivity analysis and uncertainty quantification workflows, they empirically show that semantic drift—when left unchecked—harms policy learning, slowing or destabilizing convergence and reducing robustness. In contrast, their framework with semantic checkpoints and structured communication improves convergence of the contextual bandit policies, enhances robustness, and better supports adaptation to new problem contexts.

Conclusion: The study concludes that for scientific multi-agent systems, it is not sufficient to have strong decision-making algorithms alone; these must be paired with explicit mechanisms that preserve semantic consistency and faithful information flow across the entire computational pipeline. Semantic checkpoints and structured inter-agent communication are proposed as a general design principle to maintain action-outcome fidelity, enabling more reliable autonomous learning and adaptation in scientific computing workflows.

Abstract: Automating scientific computing workflows requires more than generating executable code: autonomous systems must also select appropriate computational strategies, implement them faithfully, and ensure that the resulting outcomes remain causally attributable to the decisions that produced them. In multi-agent pipelines, this process is particularly fragile, as small inconsistencies between agent intentions and actions can lead to semantic drift, where the eventually executed procedure no longer reflects the originally selected strategy, thereby corrupting downstream evaluation and adaptation. In this work, motivated by the ATHENA framework (Toscano et al., 2025; Toscano et al., 2026) and the concept of empowerment (Yiu et al., 2025), we introduce a multi-agent framework that combines contextual bandits with structured inter-agent communication and, most importantly, semantic checkpoints that preserve action-outcome fidelity throughout the pipeline. The system integrates specialized large language model (LLM) agents, grounded code generation, and self-healing execution loops within an adaptive decision-making architecture. Interpreting the framework through the lens of empowerment, we show that reliable autonomous learning requires not only identifying high-quality actions, but also preserving the integrity of their propagation across agents. Using sensitivity analysis and uncertainty quantification workflows as representative case studies, we demonstrate that unchecked semantic drift degrades policy learning, whereas the proposed framework improves convergence, robustness, and adaptation to novel problem contexts. These results suggest a broader design principle for scientific multi-agent systems: adaptive decision-making must be coupled with explicit mechanisms that guarantee semantic consistency and reliable information flow across the computational pipeline.

</details>


### [249] [Robust and Generalizable Safety Steering for Text-to-Image Diffusion Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30049)
*Zihao Xue,Yan Wang,Zhen Bi,Long Ma,Zhonglong Zheng,Zeyu Yang,Bingyu Zhu,Longtao Huang,Jie Xiao,Jungang Lou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SafeDIG is a safety-steering framework for Diffusion Transformers that robustly reduces unsafe image generations across shifting risk domains while preserving image quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safety methods for text-to-image models mostly operate at the input prompt level or the final output, which is inadequate for Diffusion Transformers (DiTs) whose safety-relevant information emerges gradually and cross-modally. Safety controls learned at a single layer or for a specific risk category tend to be unstable and fail to generalize when the type of harmful content or domain shifts. There is a need for a fine-grained, internal control mechanism that is robust to such shifts and can disentangle transferable safety behavior from domain-specific representations.

Method: The paper introduces SafeDIG, which treats safety adaptation in DiTs as position-aware sparse feature transfer. First, Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) are trained at multiple, functionally distinct intervention positions inside the DiT. A robustness-aware pre-training routing strategy then selects intervention sites expected to remain stable under shifts between source and target risk domains. The encoder part of each SAE is frozen as a reusable sparse safety dictionary, while the decoder is adapted to the target model’s activation manifold, separating transferable safety features from domain-specific geometry. At inference time, SafeDIG performs two operations on unsafe activations: Blend, which steers them toward the learned safe manifolds, and Repel, which pushes them away from harmful sparse directions identified in the safety dictionary.

Result: On FLUX.1 Dev and Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large, SafeDIG reduces unsafe generations both in the target risk domain and overall, without degrading safety in the original (source) domain. It maintains or preserves image quality while achieving more consistent safety improvements compared to baselines, especially under domain shift conditions.

Conclusion: SafeDIG provides a robust, internal safety-steering framework for Diffusion Transformers by using sparse, position-aware feature transfer. By decoupling transferable safety directions from domain-specific activation structures and combining Blend/Repel interventions at well-chosen layers, it achieves better generalization of safety controls across changing risk domains while keeping image quality high.

Abstract: Diffusion Transformers have become a powerful backbone for text-to-image generation, but their layered and cross-modal generation process makes safety control fundamentally different from prompt-level filtering or output-level detection. Harmful semantics may be weakly expressed in text representations, progressively bound to visual latents, and finally entangled with rendering dynamics. As a result, safety steering at a fixed layer can be unstable, and a steering mechanism learned from known risks may not transfer reliably to a shifted target risk domain. We propose SafeDIG, a safety steering framework that formulates DiT safety adaptation as position-aware sparse feature transfer. SafeDIG first constructs Sparse Autoencoders over functionally distinct DiT intervention positions and uses robustness-aware pre-training routing to prioritize intervention sites that are expected to remain stable under source-target risk shift. It then separates transferable safety features from domain-specific activation geometry by freezing the SAE encoder as a reusable sparse safety dictionary and adapting only the decoder to the target-domain activation manifold. During inference, SafeDIG combines Blend and Repel operations to steer unsafe activations toward transferred safety manifolds or away from harmful sparse directions. Experiments on FLUX.1 Dev and Stable Diffusion 3.5 Large show that SafeDIG consistently reduces target-domain and overall unsafe generation rates while preserving source-domain safety and image quality.

</details>


### [250] [Conformal Certification of Reasoning Trace Prefixes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30085)
*Matt Y. Cheung,Ashok Veeraraghavan,Hanjie Chen,Guha Balakrishnan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces CROP, a method to statistically certify how much of a language model’s step-by-step reasoning can be safely kept before errors appear, improving downstream repair and evaluation of reasoning traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Language models often make reasoning errors partway through multi-step solutions, but still produce many correct intermediate steps. Existing uncertainty methods focus on whole answers, offering no principled way to keep the correct prefix of a reasoning trace while discarding the unreliable remainder. There is a need for a framework that can provide statistical guarantees on what fraction of a reasoning trace is error-free so that valid partial reasoning can be preserved and used safely.

Method: The authors propose CROP (Conformal Reasoning Output Prefixes), a verifier-agnostic calibration procedure based on conformal prediction. Given any step-level risk proxy (e.g., verifier scores at each reasoning step), CROP learns a calibrated threshold from data. At inference time, it scans each reasoning trace and returns the longest contiguous prefix whose per-step risk proxies all lie below this threshold, while routing the remaining suffix for review or repair. Under an exchangeability assumption, CROP guarantees that the marginal probability that the certified prefix contains an error is controlled at a user-specified level. They evaluate this approach across six datasets with process-level annotations.

Result: Empirically, CROP successfully controls the error rate of certified prefixes while retaining substantial portions of correct reasoning. The experiments show that common verifier metrics like AUROC are insufficient to characterize how useful a verifier is for prefix certification: verifiers with similar AUROC can lead to very different certified prefix lengths. Using CROP to withhold only the risky suffixes while preserving earlier steps improves the performance of downstream repair models, since they receive more accurate intermediate context and fewer misleading steps.

Conclusion: CROP establishes prefix certification as a practical and rigorous way to leverage process supervision and abstention for multi-step reasoning. By calibrating step-level risk proxies to guarantee clean prefixes, it enables better use of partial solutions, more meaningful evaluation of verifiers through certified prefix length, and improved downstream repair. The framework suggests a shift from certifying final answers to certifying safe portions of reasoning traces in order to manage uncertainty in language model reasoning.

Abstract: Language model reasoning traces are rarely all-or-nothing; they frequently contain valid intermediate steps before a critical error occurs. Existing uncertainty quantification methods typically certify final answers or entire responses, failing to provide statistical guarantees for the proportion of a sequential trace that can be safely retained. To address this, we introduce CROP (Conformal Reasoning Output Prefixes), a verifier-agnostic calibration procedure for clean-prefix certification. Given any step-level risk proxy, CROP selects a calibrated threshold and returns the longest contiguous prefix whose step risk proxies remain below it, routing the uncertified suffix for downstream review or repair. Assuming exchangeability, CROP rigorously controls the marginal probability that the returned prefix contains an annotated error. Across six process-labeled reasoning datasets, we demonstrate that standard step-level metrics such as AUROC do not fully capture prefix utility, suggesting verifiers should instead be evaluated by certified prefix length. Furthermore, CROP balances over- and under-withholding, improving downstream repair accuracy by preserving valid intermediate reasoning while discarding misleading suffixes. Ultimately, this work positions prefix certification as a rigorous, practical bridge between process supervision, abstention, and repair.

</details>


### [251] [Selective QA over Conflicting Multi-Source Personal Memory: A Diagnostic Testbed and Method Comparison](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30087)
*Tiancheng Yang,Matthias Schonlau,Ilia Sucholutsky*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a benchmark to evaluate how personal AI agents resolve conflicting or incomplete information in multi-source memories, focusing on selective question answering and abstention decisions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personal AI agents increasingly rely on persistent memories compiled from multiple, sometimes inconsistent data sources. Evaluating these agents is difficult because current benchmarks do not clearly separate errors caused by flawed evidence from those caused by poor conflict resolution. There is a need for a controlled benchmark that stresses selective question answering over conflicting personal memories and can distinguish these error sources.

Method: The authors frame the task as selective QA over conflicting multi-source personal memory, where systems must either answer questions based on noisy, conflicting evidence or abstain when evidence is insufficient. They construct a large benchmark with 18 question templates, 8 reasoning types, 480 personas, 4 random seeds, and 34,560 total instances. The benchmark includes controlled distortions to the sources and deterministic ground-truth labels. They then evaluate several baselines: models with no source access, single-source access, structured fusion methods trained for conflict resolution, and frontier large language models prompted to do the task.

Result: A trained fusion-based conflict resolver achieves 80.3% accuracy without abstention, outperforming prompt-only LLM baselines whose best performance is 70.0% accuracy. When allowing abstention, the fusion resolver reaches 85.3% selective accuracy at 78.3% coverage, while the best LLM baseline reaches 71.0% selective accuracy at 95.4% coverage. The analysis shows that different models excel on different reasoning types, indicating varied strengths and weaknesses across the evaluated approaches.

Conclusion: The benchmark effectively exposes and measures how different models handle conflicting and incomplete multi-source personal memories, separating evidence quality from conflict-resolution ability. Structured fusion methods currently outperform prompt-only LLMs on this task, especially when selective answering with abstention is allowed. By releasing data, code, cached outputs, and the data-generation process, the authors provide a reusable resource for advancing research on robust personal AI memory and conflict resolution.

Abstract: Emerging personal AI agents are moving toward persistent, multi-source memory. This creates an evaluation problem: systems must decide how to use conflicting or incomplete evidence; they cannot just retrieve facts from one clean history. Existing benchmarks rarely show whether an error came from the evidence given to a method or from the method's conflict-resolution step. We study this as selective QA over conflicting multi-source personal memory: systems answer based on conflicting, sometimes incomplete sources, or abstain when evidence is insufficient. We develop a benchmark containing 18 question templates across 8 reasoning types, 480 personas, 4 random seeds, and 34,560 instances, with controlled source distortions and deterministic ground truth. We evaluate the performance of baselines without access to any source, access to a single source, structured fusion methods, and frontier LLMs. The best trained fusion resolver reaches 80.3% accuracy, while the strongest prompt-only LLM baseline reaches 70.0%. With abstention, the same resolver reaches 85.3% selective accuracy at 78.3% coverage and the best LLM reaches 71.0% selective accuracy at 95.4% coverage. Different models have different strengths across reasoning types. We release the data, code, cached model outputs, and data-generating process for reuse.

</details>


### [252] [PokerSkill: LLMs Can Play Expert-Level Poker without Training or Solvers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30094)
