*Boning Li,Baoxiang Wang,Longbo Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PokerSkill is a training‑free, solver‑free framework that lets LLMs play strong poker by grounding them in an expert‑crafted, rule‑based skill library and constraining their actions to strategically reasonable options.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing top poker AIs rely on computationally expensive equilibrium solvers based on counterfactual regret minimization, taking millions of core-hours, while traditional rule-based agents are weak and LLMs play poorly when used directly despite rich poker knowledge. There is a need for a method that is cheaper, interpretable, and does not require game-specific training or solver access, yet still approaches high-level play in an imperfect-information setting.

Method: Design a human-expert-built, layered library of detailed rule-based poker skills that captures strategic principles. Use a deterministic context engine to analyze each game state and retrieve only relevant skill fragments. Present these filtered, structured options as an action-grounding interface to an LLM (e.g., GPT-5.5 XHigh, Claude Opus 4.6/4.7), constraining its possible actions to those implied by the skills, and have the LLM choose and execute among them without any additional training or solver queries.

Result: When evaluated against GTOWizard, a state-of-the-art GTO benchmark, the PokerSkill framework with GPT-5.5 XHigh, Claude Opus 4.6, and Claude Opus 4.7 achieves losses of −57±21, −80±29, and −87±64 mbb/hand, respectively. This reflects a 49–61% reduction in loss compared with default-prompt LLM baselines and surpasses the performance of a strong existing bot, Slumbot, despite using no training or solver access.

Conclusion: Combining interpretable, human-designed rule-based poker skills with LLM decision-making creates an effective, training-free and solver-free poker agent that competes with systems requiring massive computational resources. Neither rule-based skills nor LLMs alone are strong enough, but their integration closes much of the gap to GTO-level play, representing the first competitive LLM-based agent in a complex imperfect-information game without any game-specific training or solver queries.

Abstract: Poker is a landmark challenge for artificial intelligence. The dominant approach relies on equilibrium solvers built on counterfactual regret minimization, requiring millions of core-hours of training. Large Language Models (LLMs) possess extensive poker knowledge but perform far below solver-based agents when asked to play directly. Traditional rule-based poker agents are interpretable and training-free, but their strategic ceiling remains far below equilibrium play. We introduce \textbf{PokerSkill}, a training-free and solver-free framework that bridges this gap by using detailed rule-based poker skills as a structured action-grounding interface for LLMs. A deterministic context engine analyzes the current state and retrieves only the relevant fragments from a layered skill library, which is entirely designed by human poker experts, constraining the LLM's choice to reasonable actions. Against GTOWizard, a state-of-the-art GTO benchmark, GPT-5.5 XHigh with PokerSkill achieves $-57 \pm 21$ mbb/hand, Claude Opus 4.6 achieves $-80 \pm 29$ mbb/hand and Claude Opus 4.7 achieves $-87\pm 64$ mbb/hand, reducing losses by 49--61\% compared to default-prompt baselines and outperforming the strong bot Slumbot. Our key finding is that rule-based skills alone do not constitute a strong strategy, and LLMs alone cannot play well, but their combination yields an agent that requires neither training nor solver access yet competes with systems built on millions of core-hours of computation. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of an LLM achieving competitive performance in a complex imperfect-information game without game-specific training or solver queries. Code is available at https://github.com/lbn187/PokerSkill.

</details>


### [253] [VLA-Trace: Diagnosing Vision-Language-Action Models through Representation and Behavior Tracing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30117)
*Haoyuan Shi,Xiancong Ren,Yingji Zhang,Qinfan Zhang,Jiayu Hu,Haozhe Shan,Han Dong,Jinpeng Lu,Yinda Chen,Yi Zhang,Yong Dai,Xiaozhu Ju*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces VLA-Trace, a diagnostic framework to analyze how Vision-Language-Action models transform multimodal inputs into control, revealing their representation dynamics, causal control pathways, and behavioral properties.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language-Action models can act in embodied environments, but how they convert multimodal knowledge (vision, language) into actions is poorly understood. There is a need for systematic tools to trace internal representations, identify which modalities drive control, and connect these to observable behavior.

Method: The authors propose VLA-Trace, which creates a unified evidence chain from representations to behavior. It uses (1) cross-modal and checkpoint-drift centered kernel alignment (CKA) to track how internal representations evolve and align across modalities and over finetuning; (2) attention knockout interventions to causally test which modality-specific attention pathways are responsible for control; and (3) rollout-level behavioral probes to evaluate how well models ground perception in action, whether they rely on spurious shortcuts, and how accurately they follow semantic instructions.

Result: Applying VLA-Trace to two VLA models, π_{0.5} and OpenVLA, the authors find: (1) the models adapt their visual and language representations in qualitatively different ways during finetuning; (2) they use distinct multimodal routing strategies and different layers for action decoding; and (3) while both can generate visually grounded action trajectories, they struggle with fine-grained semantic instruction following.

Conclusion: The study shows that VLA-Trace can reveal important differences in how VLA models represent and use multimodal information for control. The authors argue that future work should focus on representation-preserving adaptation methods, designing and understanding causal VLA circuits, and improving models’ ability to perform compositional and fine-grained semantic control.

Abstract: Understanding how Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models transform multimodal knowledge into embodied control remains an open challenge. We present VLA-Trace, a progressive diagnostic framework that analyzes VLA models through a unified evidence chain from representation dynamics to causal control attribution and behavioral manifestation. It specifically combines cross-modal and checkpoint-drift centered kernel alignment (CKA) to trace representation evolution, attention knockout interventions to identify modality-specific control pathways, and rollout-level behavioral probes to examine grounding, shortcut dependence, and semantic following. Experiments on $π_{0.5}$ and OpenVLA reveal three key findings. First, the two models exhibit distinct modality-specific adaptation dynamics during VLA finetuning. Second, they rely on different multimodal routing strategies and layer-wise dependencies during action decoding. Third, although VLA policies excel at visually grounded trajectory generation, they remain limited in fine-grained semantic following. These findings highlight future directions for representation-preserving adaptation, causal VLA circuits, and compositional semantic control.

</details>


### [254] [Enhancing Multi-Agent Communication through Attention Steering with Context Relevance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30136)
*Hongxiang Zhang,Yuan Tian,Tianyi Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agent-Radar is a training-free context management method for LLM-based multi-agent systems that combats performance degradation from long conversation histories by dynamically focusing each agent on relevant context using temporal and spatial decay, outperforming prior methods and remaining robust as agents and rounds scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems solve complex tasks via collaborative reasoning but quickly accumulate very long conversation histories. As histories grow, important information becomes buried in irrelevant context, which harms performance. Existing context management approaches are insufficient at selectively surfacing the most relevant information as interactions scale in length and number of agents. The paper aims to address this context overload problem without requiring additional training.

Method: The authors introduce Agent-Radar, a training-free context management mechanism that operates during multi-agent interactions. It dynamically selects and weights context for each agent using a temporal and spatial decay mechanism: more recent and more semantically relevant messages are emphasized, while older or less related ones are down-weighted or omitted. This is integrated into the message passing of multi-agent LLM systems, steering each agent’s attention to the most pertinent subset of the conversation without modifying the underlying LLMs.

Result: Across five benchmarks for LLM-based multi-agent collaboration, Agent-Radar consistently outperforms state-of-the-art context management baselines, achieving improvements up to 7.64 absolute points. The method remains effective and robust as the number of agents increases and as the number of interaction rounds grows. Ablation studies indicate that the temporal and spatial decay components and other core design choices are key contributors to the performance gains and transfer well across different tasks and settings.

Conclusion: Agent-Radar provides an effective, training-free solution to the context overload problem in LLM-based multi-agent systems by dynamically steering agents toward the most relevant parts of long interaction histories. Its temporal and spatial decay mechanism improves performance, scales with more agents and longer dialogues, and generalizes across benchmarks, making it a practical context management strategy for complex multi-agent LLM applications.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems have demonstrated remarkable performance on complex tasks through collaborative reasoning. However, these systems tend to rapidly accumulate extremely long conversation histories during interaction. As conversations lengthen, relevant information is increasingly diluted by irrelevant context, leading to degraded performance. In this work, we present Agent-Radar, a training-free context management method that dynamically steers each agent's attention toward relevant context with a novel temporal and spatial decay mechanism. Our experiments demonstrate that Agent-Radar outperforms state-of-the-art methods across five different benchmarks, yielding gains of up to 7.64 absolute points. Furthermore, our analysis shows that Agent-Radar remains effective and robust as the number of agents and interaction rounds increases. Finally, the ablation study shows that core components in Agent-Radar are crucial to performance and generalizable in different settings.

</details>


### [255] [AgentSchool: An LLM-Powered Multi-Agent Simulation for Education](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30144)
*Yulei Ye,Wenhao Li,Zhong Wen,Yunshu Huang,Yichen Hu,Zifan Wei,Yige Wang,Xinyu Xie,Haoxuan Yang,Yanjun Huang,Ruijia Li,Hong Qian,Yu Song,Bo Jiang,Bingdong Li,Lijun Li,Bo Zhang,Pinlong Cai,Xingcheng Xu,Shuangye Chen,Xia Hu,Liang He,Aimin Zhou,Jingjing Qu,Jing Shao,Xiangfeng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentSchool is an LLM-based multi-agent educational simulator that models students as evolving cognitive states and teachers as adaptive planners, enabling safer, more realistic experimentation on learning processes and classroom dynamics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Validating AI in real classrooms is difficult because experiments are slow, ethically constrained, and affect real learners irreversibly. Existing LLM-based educational simulators typically reduce learning to static persona role-play and are optimized to mimic current institutions, which discourages exploring novel pedagogies and system-level reforms. There is a need for a richer, more principled simulation framework that captures how knowledge, skills, misconceptions, and social structures evolve over time in educational settings.

Method: The authors build AgentSchool, a multi-agent simulation environment powered by LLMs. Student agents have explicit, updatable internal structures: weighted subject knowledge graphs, libraries of thinking workflows, and modeled misconceptions; their learning is represented as state transitions in these structures. Teacher agents are designed to plan, scaffold, and reflect according to the Zone of Proximal Development (ZPD), adapting instruction to students’ current states. These agents operate in a configurable environment generator that can represent formal and informal learning contexts. A multi-scale simulator allows separation of interaction patterns, time-step granularity, and total simulation duration. The system is evaluated by comparing AgentSchool’s learning and social traces to a baseline simulator and to established educational and classroom social theories.

Result: Compared to a baseline, student agents in AgentSchool show more nuanced, differentiated patterns of knowledge mastery and misconception evolution. Teacher agents display adaptation behaviors that vary with the underlying LLM backbone and align with expectations from ZPD theory. At the classroom level, the simulator produces interaction and social network traces that qualitatively match known classroom social phenomena such as peripheral participation, clique formation, cohesion in response to aggressors, and the rise of opinion leaders.

Conclusion: AgentSchool demonstrates that modeling learning as cognitive state transitions in LLM-driven student and teacher agents leads to richer, more theory-consistent simulations than role-play-based approaches. The framework can serve both as an educational research tool and as a benchmark domain for advancing core AI capabilities in long-horizon memory, multi-agent coordination, and institutional decision-making under constraints, highlighting education as a meaningful testbed for future AI research.

Abstract: Despite the rapid deployment of LLMs into classrooms, validating educational AI remains uniquely intractable: interventions act on developing learners whose cognitive and social trajectories are irreversibly shaped, while real-world trials are slow, ethically constrained, and institutionally locked. LLM-based educational simulators have emerged as a potential remedy, but many still collapse learning into persona-conditioned role-play and, when optimized only to reproduce existing classrooms, can structurally penalize the institutional novelty that pedagogical reform requires. In this work, we introduce AgentSchool, an LLM-driven multi-agent simulator that models learning as state transition rather than prompted behavior. AgentSchool couples cognitively growable student agents -- equipped with weighted subject knowledge graphs, thinking-workflow pools, and explicit misconceptions -- with adaptive teacher agents that plan, scaffold, and reflect along the Zone of Proximal Development, embedded in a configurable scenery generator that situates instruction within both formal and informal learning fields, and a multi-scale simulator that decouples interaction scale, temporal granularity, and simulation duration. Experiments show that structured student agents produce more differentiated mastery and misconception traces than a baseline simulator, while teacher-agent comparisons show backbone-dependent patterns consistent with ZPD-informed adaptation. Further, AgentSchool generates plausible traces of peripheral participation, clique formation, aggressor-induced cohesion, and opinion-leader emergence consistent with classroom social theories. Beyond its role as an educational research instrument, AgentSchool frames education as a socially meaningful testbed for long-horizon memory, multi-agent coordination, and future institutional reasoning under organizational pressure.

</details>


### [256] [Anchorless Diversification for Parallel LLM Ideation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30150)
*Fares Nabil Ibrahim,Nafis Saami Azad,Raiyan Abdul Baten*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper evaluates inference-time strategies to diversify LLM-generated idea pools without relying on example anchors, finding simple anchorless methods can effectively balance diversity, quality, and compute cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for creative ideation, users need ways to generate broad, diverse idea pools that remain high-quality and cost-efficient. Many existing diversification methods rely on anchors (seed ideas or examples), which may not always be available, cheap, or desirable. The paper is motivated by the need to understand whether purely inference-time, anchorless controls can match or outperform anchor-based approaches for open-ended ideation.

Method: The authors run comparative experiments across three families of creative tasks. They evaluate multiple inference-time strategies for generating diversified candidate idea pools: (1) simple independent generations, (2) semantic direction stratification, which uses a single planning call to partition semantic space and then generate ideas along those directions, and (3) several anchored baselines (self-, peer-, and representative-anchor regeneration), which condition generation on observed seed ideas. They test these under two prompting regimes: neutral divergent instructions and population-referential divergent instructions that explicitly encourage ideas different from typical population responses. They then compare approaches on semantic diversity, quality proxies, and compute/token cost.

Result: Population-referential divergence emerges as a strong, low-cost baseline that increases semantic diversity without hurting quality proxies. Semantic direction stratification outperforms others overall: using one planning step to establish broad semantic directions for subsequent generations leads to the best trade-off between diversity, idea quality, and compute cost. While anchored regeneration can produce highly diverse final pools, its apparent advantage diminishes when full token usage across the pipeline is accounted for.

Conclusion: The study demonstrates that anchorless, inference-time methods—especially semantic direction stratification and population-referential divergent prompting—are practical and effective baselines for open-ended LLM ideation. These techniques can achieve or surpass the diversity of more complex anchor-based approaches while maintaining quality and cost-efficiency, making them attractive options for scalable creative applications using LLMs.

Abstract: LLMs are increasingly used to generate candidate-idea pools for creative tasks where broad exploration is valuable. Parallel inference can be attractive in this setting when it broadens the pool while retaining quality and cost efficiency. We study inference-time controls for candidate-pool diversification, asking whether anchorless methods can rival methods that depend on observed seed ideas. Across three creative task families, we compare independent generation and semantic direction stratification with self-, peer-, and representative-anchor baselines, under neutral and population-referential divergent instructions. Population-referential divergence is a strong low-cost baseline, increasing semantic diversity while preserving quality proxies. Semantic direction stratification is stronger: a single planning call organizes generations across broad semantic directions, yielding the best diversity--quality--compute frontier. Anchored regeneration can be strong in final-pool diversity, but its advantage shrinks under full-pipeline token accounting. These results establish practical anchorless baselines for open-ended LLM ideation.

</details>


### [257] [Temporal Stability and Few-Shot Prompting in Math Task Assessment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30151)
*Danielle S. Fox,Brenda L. Robles,Elizabeth DiPietro Brovey,Christian D. Schunn*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper investigates how stable and improvable different AI tools are, over time and with prompt engineering, when classifying the cognitive demand of math tasks using the Task Analysis Guide (TAG). It finds that version updates do not reliably improve performance, but few-shot prompting does.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI tools are increasingly used in education, educators and researchers need to know whether these tools are reliable over time and how much their performance can be improved through user-controlled strategies like prompt engineering, rather than waiting for opaque model updates. Specifically, the authors are motivated by the need to understand how well AI systems can classify the cognitive demand of mathematics tasks—a key component of instructional quality—and whether model version changes or prompting strategies are more effective in improving that ability.

Method: The authors conducted a longitudinal study using two AI tools: a general-purpose model (Gemini) and an education-oriented model (Coteach). Both were tasked with classifying mathematics tasks according to the Task Analysis Guide (TAG) levels of cognitive demand. The study comprised three phases: (1) baseline testing of each model’s classification accuracy; (2) retesting after model version updates to see how performance changed over time; and (3) testing again with few-shot prompting, in which the models were given two exemplar tasks for each TAG cognitive demand category before classification. Performance (accuracy) was compared across phases for each model.

Result: Model version updates alone did not consistently improve performance. Gemini’s accuracy on TAG classification remained constant at 58% across versions, while Coteach’s accuracy dropped from 75% to 50% after its update. In contrast, few-shot prompting yielded clear gains for both tools: Gemini’s accuracy rose from 58% to 67%, and Coteach’s performance rebounded from 50% back to 75%. Thus, tailored prompting produced more reliable and larger performance improvements than passive reliance on newer model versions.

Conclusion: The study concludes that prompt engineering, specifically few-shot prompting with well-chosen exemplars, can significantly and more reliably enhance AI tools’ performance on specialized educational tasks than simply relying on model version updates. Version changes may even degrade performance for some educationally relevant tasks. Therefore, educators and researchers should not assume that newer AI versions are inherently better for classroom or research use; instead, they should systematically evaluate tools over time and invest in developing effective prompts and usage protocols when integrating AI into educational practice.

Abstract: As AI tools become increasingly integrated into educational contexts, questions arise about both their stability over time and their responsiveness to prompt engineering techniques. This longitudinal study focused on different AI tools' ability to use the Task Analysis Guide (TAG; Stein \& Smith, 1998) to classify the cognitive demand of mathematics tasks. In particular, it examined whether this classification ability changed with (1) model version updates over time and (2) few-shot prompting using exemplar tasks. We tested a general-purpose AI tool (Gemini) and an education-specific AI tool (Coteach). The specific tools were selected because of their relatively high performance on relevant published benchmarks and prior task-specific tests. Models were tested at baseline, retested with model version updates, and then tested again using few-shot prompting (two exemplar tasks for each cognitive demand category). Results revealed that newer model versions alone produced mixed effects: Gemini's accuracy remained stable at 58\%, while Coteach's accuracy decreased from 75\% to 50\%. However, few-shot prompting improved both models' performance: Gemini increased to 67\% and Coteach recovered to 75\% accuracy. These findings demonstrate that prompt engineering techniques can have larger and more reliable effects than passive model improvements, and that version updates may not always improve performance on specialized educational tasks. The study has important implications for how educators and researchers should approach AI tool selection, evaluation, and implementation in educational contexts.

</details>


### [258] [Meta-Cognitive Memory Policy Optimization for Long-Horizon LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30159)
*Ziyan Liu,Zhezheng Hao,Yeqiu Chen,Hong Wang,Jingren Hou,Ruiyi Ding,Yongkang Yang,Wence Ji,Wei Xia,Feng Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes MMPO, a method to optimize memory policies for memory-augmented LLM agents by minimizing belief entropy, improving long-horizon reasoning and robustness over very long contexts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory-augmented LLM agents summarize interaction histories into compact memories, but these summaries are optimized only via trajectory-level RL rewards, which do not reveal where in the trajectory memory quality degrades. Recursive summarization can discard crucial task information and add semantic noise, leading to belief drift and failure on long-horizon tasks. The authors want a way to explicitly optimize for clear, accurate beliefs at intermediate steps, not just final task success.

Method: They define Belief Entropy, a self-supervised measure that estimates the model's epistemic uncertainty about the latent task state given its current memory. Using this signal, they propose Metacognitive Memory Policy Optimization (MMPO), which augments or replaces sparse outcome-based RL with dense, memory-specific supervision. MMPO explicitly penalizes summaries that induce high belief entropy, guiding the memory policy to produce clearer, less ambiguous intermediate summaries.

Result: Across diverse long-horizon tasks and very long interaction trajectories, MMPO leads to better performance than existing outcome-based training methods for memory policies. The method preserves strong task performance (97.1%) even when the context length is scaled to 1.75M tokens, indicating robustness to extreme long-horizon settings.

Conclusion: Optimizing memory in LLM agents should focus on the quality of intermediate beliefs rather than only on final task outcomes. Belief Entropy provides a useful self-supervised proxy for memory clarity, and MMPO leverages it to deliver more reliable long-horizon reasoning. This approach mitigates belief drift caused by lossy recursive summarization and scales effectively to very long contexts.

Abstract: Memory-augmented LLM agents tackle complex long-horizon tasks by recursively summarizing interaction trajectories into compact memory. However, existing approaches typically train these memory policies using outcome-based reinforcement learning, failing to localize where intermediate memory quality degrades. As interactions unfold, ambiguous recursive summaries progressively discard task-relevant information and introduce semantic noise. This exacerbates belief deviation, obscuring the agent's estimate of the latent task state and ultimately derailing long-horizon reasoning. We therefore argue that memory optimization should focus not merely on trajectory-level success, but on the clarity of the belief induced by intermediate summaries. To this end, we introduce Belief Entropy, a self-supervised proxy that probes how uncertain the model remains about the latent task state given its current memory. Based on this proxy, we propose Metacognitive Memory Policy Optimization (MMPO). Instead of relying only on sparse outcome-based signals, MMPO provides fine-grained, memory-specific supervision via explicitly penalizing summaries that induce high epistemic uncertainty. Experiments show that MMPO consistently outperforms existing methods on diverse long-horizon tasks, maintaining 97.1% performance even when scaled to 1.75M-token contexts.

</details>


### [259] [BioRefusalAudit: Auditing Biosecurity Refusal Depth Using General and Domain-Fine-Tuned Sparse Autoencoders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30162)
*Caleb DeLeeuw*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper examines how structurally reliable language-model safety refusals are, showing that small prompt and formatting changes can flip refusals and that internal activation patterns can reveal hidden failure modes beyond surface behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most biosecurity evaluations check whether language models output hazardous content, but pay less attention to whether refusals are robust or easily circumvented. The authors aim to understand if models’ safety behaviors are structurally sound across prompt variations and what internal mechanisms drive correct or incorrect refusals, especially in biosecurity-relevant domains.

Method: They empirically test five small language models on 75 biosecurity-related prompts, varying prompt framing, chat-template formatting, and output-length caps to see when models comply or refuse. They analyze over-refusal and under-refusal patterns, including responses to legally and culturally salient but biologically non-toxic Schedule I substances. They then introduce a divergence score D that compares surface refusal/compliance labels to internal sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature activations, computing this in detail on Gemma 2 2B-IT and Gemma 4 E2B-IT with specialized bio-domain SAEs trained on modest hardware.

Result: No architecture cleanly distinguished benign from hazardous prompts. Gemma 2 2B-IT almost never gave explicit refusals, instead hedging on nearly all hazard-adjacent queries. Gemma 4 E2B-IT’s refusal behavior depended heavily on chat-template formatting and collapsed under short output caps. Qwen 2.5 1.5B and Phi-3-mini strongly over-refused, labeling most benign biology as hazardous. Only Llama 3.2 1B showed a meaningful gradient across hazard tiers. Some models refused information about legally controlled but biologically non-toxic compounds at higher rates than genuinely dangerous biology, indicating that refusal aligned more with legality and cultural salience than real CBRN hazard. Using SAEs on Gemma models, they found that comply vs refuse responses were cleanly separable in activation space on Gemma 4, with a sizable, non-overlapping divergence score gap between the two classes, albeit on a narrow, in-sample dataset.

Conclusion: Model refusals are brittle, highly sensitive to formatting and length constraints, and often misaligned with true biological hazard, sometimes tracking legality or cultural salience instead. Behavioral evaluations alone miss important safety failure modes. Activation-level auditing via sparse autoencoders and divergence scoring shows promise for revealing and characterizing these failures, though current results are preliminary and limited to specific models, prompts, and within-sample calibration. This suggests that more systematic, architecture-aware interpretability tools are needed to reliably assess and improve model biosecurity behavior.

Abstract: Biosecurity evaluations of language models typically ask whether models produce hazardous output. This paper asks a complementary question: when a model refuses, is that refusal structurally sound, or does it disappear under modest changes to prompt framing, formatting, or output length? Across five architectures, no model cleanly discriminated benign from hazard. Gemma 2 2B-IT never genuinely refused across 75 prompts, hedging on every hazard-adjacent query. Gemma 4 E2B-IT refused 65/75 prompts with chat-template formatting and 0/75 without it. Both Gemma models collapsed to 0% under an 80-token cap. Qwen 2.5 1.5B and Phi-3-mini over-refused, flagging 83-87% of benign biology as hazardous. Llama 3.2 1B showed the only meaningful tier gradient (61-point spread). To probe what drives such over-refusal, we tested a panel of Schedule I but biologically non-toxic compounds (notably psilocybin cultivation, with FDA Breakthrough Therapy status). Some models refused these at rates exceeding genuinely hazardous biology, suggesting refusal tracks legality and cultural salience over CBRN hazard. To measure the internal side, we introduce a divergence score D comparing a model's surface response label to its internal sparse autoencoder (SAE) feature activations. Full D was computed on Gemma 2 2B-IT (Gemma Scope 1) and Gemma 4 E2B-IT (author-trained bio SAE). Two fine-tuned Gemma 2 domain SAEs were released. On Gemma 4, comply and refuse responses separated by a 0.647-point gap with zero overlap (n=75), though this is preliminary, with a narrow catalog, within-sample calibration, and Gemma-family-only SAE coverage. Built over one hackathon weekend on consumer hardware (GTX 1650 Ti Max-Q, plus Colab T4 for SAE training), this preliminary evidence suggests activation-level auditing may surface failure modes invisible to behavioral evaluation, with substantial variation across architectures.

</details>


### [260] [Modularizing Educational LLM-Agency for Fostering Responsible Learning Assistance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30187)
*Julius Gabelmann,Felix Jahn,Kevin Baum,Sophie van Rossum,Emely Wuenscher,Timo P. Gros,Verena Wolf*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a modular, agentic AI chatbot architecture for helping students solve exercises in a pedagogically responsible way, addressing risks of current monolithic LLM chatbots in education.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI chatbots become common in education, there is a risk that generic LLMs, which are not designed to follow pedagogical principles, may harm learning outcomes by undermining skills like transfer, critical thinking, and creativity. The authors want educational AI systems that use LLMs more responsibly and in alignment with educational science.

Method: Conceptual and architectural design: the authors identify desiderata for responsible LLM-based educational systems, analyze structural shortcomings of monolithic “out-of-the-box” LLM chatbots, and then design an agentic, modular architecture. They define distinct modules that correspond to different stages of exercise solving and embed pedagogical guidance into each module.

Result: The paper delivers a detailed proposal of an agentic, modular chatbot architecture for exercise support, specifying modules aligned with learning stages and showing how these allow more targeted integration of pedagogical strategies compared to monolithic LLM use.

Conclusion: A modular, agent-based chatbot architecture offers a more controllable, transparent, and pedagogically grounded way to deploy LLMs in education than monolithic chatbots. By structuring support into stage-specific modules, educators can better ensure responsible AI use that aligns with educational goals and mitigates negative impacts on students’ learning processes.

Abstract: The widespread adoption of AI chatbots in education will drastically change learning, making responsible deployment a critical concern. While large language models (LLMs) might have access to sources discussing insights from educational sciences, they are not particularly inclined to adhere to pedagogical concepts, risking negative effects on the learning process, such as a loss of transfer capabilities, critical thinking, or creativity. In this paper, we introduce an agentic AI chatbot architecture assisting students with exercise solving, specifically designed to contribute to more responsible AI use in education. We base our conceptual development on the identification of several desiderata for responsible LLM-based educational systems, argue for the structural shortcomings inherent in monolithic, out-of-the-box solutions, and instead suggest modularizing the agentic architecture. We propose specific modules for different stages of exercise solving, enabling incorporation of targeted pedagogical advice, guiding students through the learning process in a more controllable, transparent, and overseeable manner.

</details>


### [261] [Double-Edged Sword or Sharp Tool? Designing and Evaluating Triadic LLM-Teacher Collaboration for K-12 Writing at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30200)
*Canran Wang,Yuwen Yang,Zhen Wang,Ming Ma,Ding Yu,Chentai Wang,Keman Huang,Xiaoyong Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper builds and tests a triadic collaboration system among LLMs, teachers, and K‑12 students for writing, using a very large dataset, and shows it improves writing but with diminishing returns from excessive LLM-driven expansion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Integrating LLMs into K‑12 education can both help and harm learning: they can reduce teacher workload and provide rich feedback, but may also overwhelm or misguide students if not properly managed. There is a need for an effective collaboration mechanism that clarifies how LLMs and teachers should share responsibilities in supporting student writing and how this impacts learning outcomes at scale.

Method: The authors design a triadic collaboration system for K‑12 writing in which an LLM acts as a generative engine and the teacher as a pedagogical gatekeeper. They construct a multidimensional evaluation framework grounded in Systemic Functional Linguistics and develop a suggestion trajectory tracing pipeline to follow how feedback and revisions unfold. Using this infrastructure, they conduct a large-scale empirical study on 57,954 essays written by 10,195 students from 120 schools over two years.

Result: The study shows that the triadic system effectively improves student writing quality. A strategic division of labor emerges: the LLM provides generative, linguistically rich suggestions that help reduce teacher burnout, while teachers filter, adapt, and contextualize this feedback to ensure pedagogical quality. Both LLM and teacher involvement are important for skill gains, but the authors also observe a ceiling effect: beyond a certain point, further linguistic elaboration from the LLM leads to diminishing marginal improvements in writing quality.

Conclusion: An LLM-teacher-student triadic collaboration can successfully support K‑12 writing development when LLMs are used as generative assistants and teachers remain in control of pedagogical decisions. However, more LLM feedback is not always better; excessive linguistic expansion yields limited additional benefits. The authors argue for dynamically adaptive collaboration strategies, where the extent and nature of LLM support adjust to students’ evolving proficiency levels to balance effectiveness and efficiency.

Abstract: The double-edged sword of integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) requires an effective triadic collaboration mechanism among LLMs, teachers and students, especially for K-12 education. By developing a triadic collaboration system to support K-12 writing learning, a multidimensional evaluation framework grounded in Systemic Functional Linguistics and the suggestion trajectory tracing pipeline, this paper contributes a large-scale empirical dataset involving $57,954$ essays from $10,195$ students across $120$ schools over two years. Our findings confirm the efficacy of this system in improving writing quality through a strategic labor division: the LLM serves as a generative engine to mitigate teacher burnout, and the teacher acts as a pedagogical gatekeeper and bridge to guarantee feedback quality. While both LLM and teacher are critical for skill improvement, we uncover a ceiling effect where excessive linguistic expansion yields diminishing marginal utility. These suggest a dynamically adaptive LLM-teacher collaboration as student proficiency increases.

</details>


### [262] [When Should Models Change Their Minds? Contextual Belief Management in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30219)
*Haoming Xu,Weihong Xu,Zongrui Li,Mengru Wang,Yunzhi Yao,Chiyu Wu,Jin Shang,Yu Gong,Shumin Deng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces Contextual Belief Management (CBM) as the challenge of maintaining a correct belief state over long interactions, and proposes BeliefTrack, a benchmark to evaluate this, plus RL and representation-steering methods that substantially reduce belief-management failures in LLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current language models struggle with long-horizon interactions because they accumulate information without reliably deciding when to update, keep, or ignore context, leading to incorrect or inconsistent internal states. There is a need for a principled, measurable way to study and improve this belief-state management problem.

Method: 1) Formalize Contextual Belief Management (CBM) as maintaining a predicted belief state aligned with evidence and robust to irrelevant noise. 2) Build BeliefTrack, a closed-world benchmark with tasks (Rule Discovery and Circuit Diagnosis) where the belief space is finite and symbolic verifiers allow exact turn-level evaluation. 3) Define and measure three kinds of failures: Failed Stay, Failed Update, and Failed Isolation. 4) Evaluate multiple LLMs with and without explicit belief-tracking prompts, then apply reinforcement learning with belief-state-based rewards. 5) Probe latent belief dynamics in model representations and apply representation-level steering to correct them.

Result: Vanilla LLMs show substantial CBM failures across the defined failure types, and explicit belief-tracking prompting only slightly improves performance. Training with reinforcement learning using belief-state rewards cuts failure rates by about 70.9% on average. Additionally, representation-level steering of internal states reduces failure rates by about 46.1% across two evaluated tasks.

Conclusion: Contextual Belief Management is a critical, measurable aspect of long-horizon LLM performance. The BeliefTrack benchmark exposes systematic failures of existing models to maintain consistent, evidence-aligned belief states. Reinforcement learning with belief-state rewards and representation-level steering significantly mitigate these failures, suggesting promising directions for building more reliable long-context language agents.

Abstract: Long-horizon interactions require language models to manage accumulating information: when to update their state, when to preserve their state, and what to ignore. We study this challenge as \textbf{Contextual Belief Management (CBM)}: maintaining a predicted belief state aligned with formal evidence while isolating task-irrelevant noise. To make CBM measurable, we introduce BeliefTrack, a closed-world benchmark spanning Rule Discovery and Circuit Diagnosis, where a finite belief space and symbolic verifiers enable exact turn-level evaluation. BeliefTrack diagnoses three failures: Failed Stay, Failed Update, and Failed Isolation. Across multiple LLMs, vanilla models exhibit severe CBM failures, while explicit belief-tracking prompts provide limited gains. In contrast, reinforcement learning with belief-state rewards reduces failure rates by 70.9\% on average. Further probing reveals latent belief-state dynamics behind these failures, and representation-level steering reduces failure rates by 46.1\% across two tasks\footnote{Code is coming soon at https://github.com/zjunlp/CBM.

</details>


### [263] [mcp-proto-okn: Natural-language access to open scientific knowledge graphs through the Model Context Protocol](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30283)
*Peter W. Rose,Benjamin M. Good,Amanda M. Saravia-Butler,Charlotte A. Nelson,James P. Balhoff,Yaphet Kebede,Patricia L. Whetzel,Christopher Bizon,Andrew I. Su,Sergio E. Baranzini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper presents MCP Server Proto-OKN, a Python-based Model Context Protocol server that lets AI assistants interact with scientific knowledge graphs via natural language, simplifying cross-domain graph analysis for biomedical and scientific applications.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interacting with heterogeneous scientific and biomedical knowledge graphs typically requires expertise in graph query languages, ontologies, and infrastructure. This creates a barrier for domain scientists and for AI assistants that could help them. The authors aim to make it easier to discover, inspect, and query multiple knowledge graphs through natural language interfaces, leveraging the emerging Model Context Protocol ecosystem.

Method: They implement a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server called MCP Server Proto-OKN in Python using the FastMCP framework. The server exposes tools for graph routing, schema inspection, SPARQL query execution, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, and transcript generation. It integrates with AI assistants that speak MCP so the assistants can programmatically discover available graphs, understand their schemas, and issue SPARQL or higher-level queries. The system is packaged as an open-source GitHub repository with documentation, client configuration instructions, and example analysis transcripts.

Result: The result is a working MCP server that connects AI assistants to scientific knowledge graphs, supporting capabilities such as routing queries to appropriate graphs, inspecting graph schemas, executing SPARQL across one or more graphs, and generating analysis transcripts. The implementation is publicly released on GitHub with configuration examples, demonstrating that MCP can be used as a bridge between LLM-based assistants and Open Knowledge Network resources.

Conclusion: MCP Server Proto-OKN lowers the technical barrier for AI assistants and scientific users to perform cross-domain knowledge graph analysis. By wrapping common graph operations and SPARQL access behind an MCP server and providing documentation and examples, the work shows a practical path for integrating scientific knowledge graphs into natural-language-driven workflows in biomedicine and other scientific domains.

Abstract: MCP Server Proto-OKN (mcp-proto-okn) is a Python-based Model Context Protocol server that enables AI assistants to discover, inspect, query and integrate scientific knowledge graphs through natural language. The server provides graph routing, schema inspection, SPARQL execution, ontology expansion, multi-graph querying, and transcript generation, lowering the barrier to cross-domain knowledge graph analysis for biomedical and scientific users.
  mcp-proto-okn is implemented in Python using the FastMCP framework and is available at https://github.com/sbl-sdsc/mcp-proto-okn. Documentation, client configuration instructions, and example analysis transcripts are provided in the GitHub repository.

</details>


### [264] [ProjectionBench: Evaluating Scientific Hypothesis Generation in LLMs Under Progressive Information Disclosure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30284)
*A. J. Lew,Y. Cao,M. J. Buehler*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose a new benchmark to test how well LLMs can perform genuine scientific discovery and reasoning, from forming hypotheses with little information to drawing conclusions with full details.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM benchmarks focus mostly on retrieval and known-knowledge reasoning, not on the creative, uncertain reasoning required for real scientific discovery. There is a need to systematically assess how innovative and scientifically grounded LLMs are when tackling real research questions.

Method: They design a progressive benchmark framework based on recent scientific papers. The model is given only the topic and research question at first, and then more technical details in stages. At each stage, the model must generate hypotheses to answer the research question. The generated hypotheses are decomposed into atomic claims and automatically compared (via semantic similarity and F1) with the original paper’s conclusions, measuring divergence at each stage.

Result: Using this benchmark on 45 materials-science papers (bioactive, mechanical, nano), they evaluate GPT-5, GPT-5.4, Gemini 2.5 Pro, and Gemini 3.1 Pro Preview. The newer models (GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 Pro) outperform the previous versions. GPT-5.4 achieves about 0.7 F1 alignment with ground-truth conclusions even when given minimal context.

Conclusion: The framework offers a systematic way to evaluate both innovative and grounded scientific reasoning of LLMs and shows that state-of-the-art models already achieve relatively strong alignment with true scientific conclusions, even under limited information. This supports its use as a foundation for developing and benchmarking next-generation AI scientist/co-scientist systems.

Abstract: Scientific discovery is an inherently creative and uncertain process, requiring reasoning beyond the recall of known knowledge. While many benchmarks have been proposed to evaluate large language model (LLM) performance on deep research tasks via multi-hop retrieval, their innovative reasoning abilities essential for true scientific discovery remain largely untested. We introduce a benchmark framework for evaluating model performance in scientific discovery and reasoning, building up from a raw problem to the classical null hypothesis test. In our framework, models initially receive only the topic and research question from a recent paper, with technical details progressively revealed. At each stage of information disclosure, the model is tasked with generating hypotheses that address the research question, which is compared with the conclusions from the original paper and evaluated via automated semantic similarity of constituent atomic claims. This progressive evaluation of semantic divergence from ground-truth conclusions enables assessment of a model's innovativeness (under minimal information) to grounded reasoning capabilities (under full experimental details), both critical for using LLMs for scientific discovery purposes. Our framework provides a foundation for systematically evaluating scientific reasoning and discovery capabilities in LLMs, crucial for advancing the development of next-generation AI scientist/co-scientist systems. Specifically, here we evaluate GPT-5, GPT-5.4, Gemini 2.5 pro, and Gemini 3.1 pro preview across 45 papers spanning bioactive materials, mechanical materials, and nanomaterials. We find that GPT-5.4 and Gemini 3.1 pro outperform their previous generation counterparts as expected, and GPT-5.4 in particular maintains 0.7 F1 score alignment with ground truth conclusions even under minimal context.

</details>


### [265] [MIRA: Mid-training Rubric Anchoring for Source-Aware Data Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30288)
*Haowen Wang,Yaxin Du,Jian Yang,Jiajun Wu,Shukai Liu,Yuxuan Zhang,Pingjie Wang,Siheng Chen,Tuney Zheng,Ming Zhou,Xianglong Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MIRA is a source-aware, rubric-discovery-based filtering framework for mid-training data selection in LLMs that achieves baseline-beating performance on code tasks using half the training tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mid-training data selection for LLMs is challenging because it must operate at near-pretraining scale while targeting specific downstream capabilities and handling heterogeneous data sources with different formats and roles. Existing scalable model-based filters give weak, implicit quality measures, while semantic methods with explicit rubrics usually assume uniform data formats and fixed evaluation criteria, making them ill-suited for complex, mixed-source mid-training corpora.

Method: The authors introduce MIRA, a source-aware framework where rubric discovery is integrated into the data selection pipeline. For each group of data sources, MIRA first automatically discovers what semantic qualities and criteria (rubrics) should be evaluated. It then uses these discovered rubrics to generate judgments on data quality and relevance, which are distilled into smaller student scoring models. These student scorers can then efficiently score and filter the entire corpus, providing scalable, source-adaptive semantic selection.

Result: Applied to a code-oriented mid-training setup with 21 data sources organized into 5 source groups, MIRA outperforms existing selection baselines on nine code benchmarks. It also achieves performance comparable to training on the full unfiltered corpus while requiring only half as many training tokens, indicating substantially improved data efficiency.

Conclusion: Incorporating source-aware, self-anchored rubric discovery into the data selection process enables scalable and semantically informed filtering for mid-training. MIRA successfully bridges the gap between scalable model-based filtering and rigid rubric-based semantic selection, improving code-model performance and data efficiency in large-scale mid-training scenarios.

Abstract: Mid-training has become an important stage in modern LLM development, using large-scale curated mixtures to strengthen capabilities before final post-training. Its data selection problem is distinct: the data are optimized under a pretraining-style objective at near-pretraining scale, but are curated toward downstream capabilities and drawn from heterogeneous sources with different formats and training roles. As a result, effective selection requires both scalability and source-adaptive semantic criteria. Existing model-based methods scale well, but provide only implicit quality signals. Semantic selection methods offer stronger judgments, but usually assume fixed rubrics or standardized data formats. To address this mismatch, we propose MIRA, a source-aware filtering framework based on self-anchored rubric discovery. The key idea is to make rubric construction part of data selection: MIRA first discovers what should be evaluated for each source group, then distills those judgments into scalable student scorers for full-corpus filtering. On code-oriented mid-training with 21 sources and 5 source groups, MIRA outperforms selection baselines across nine code benchmarks and matches the full-corpus run while using only half the tokens.

</details>


### [266] [Demystifying Data Organization for Enhanced LLM Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30334)
*Yalun Dai,Yangyu Huang,Tongshen Yang,Yonghan Wang,Xin Zhang,Wenshan Wu,Qihao Zhao,Hao Li,Yuanyuan Gao,Kim-Hui Yap,Scarlett Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper studies how the *ordering and organization* of training data, not just which data is selected, affects the efficiency and performance of LLM training, and proposes practical data ordering strategies that improve stability and outcomes with almost no extra compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Although LLM training depends heavily on good data, most work focuses on *what* data to use (selection, filtering) rather than *how to organize it* over the course of training, especially since LLMs are usually trained for only a few epochs. Existing sample-level quality or usefulness scores are usually used just for selection, leaving open the question of whether reusing these scores to schedule and order data over time can yield better training dynamics and performance at low cost.

Method: The authors first reuse existing per-sample scores (e.g., quality, difficulty, utility) that were computed for data-efficiency or curriculum learning. Based on systematic exploration of how different orderings affect training, they distill four principles for data organization: Boundary Sharpening (focus learning near decision boundaries or capability frontiers), Cyclic Scheduling (cycle through different types or difficulty levels of data), Curriculum Continuity (avoid abrupt shifts in what the model sees), and Local Diversity (ensure diversity in short training windows). They then design two concrete data ordering algorithms, STR and SAW, that implement these principles and plug into standard LLM training pipelines without extra model forward passes. They evaluate these methods across multiple model sizes, datasets, and both pre-training and supervised fine-tuning (SFT) regimes.

Result: Across extensive experiments, the proposed guidelines and ordering methods consistently improve training stability and final performance relative to random shuffling or naive curricula, at the same data and compute budget. The improvements hold across different model scales and data sizes, and in both pre-training and SFT settings. The methods are shown to be robust, easy to integrate, and to leverage existing scoring signals without any substantial overhead.

Conclusion: Thoughtful organization and ordering of training data is a key but underused lever in LLM training. By reusing existing sample-level scores and following four general principles, one can design simple data schedules (such as STR and SAW) that yield more stable and effective learning, without additional compute. This suggests that future LLM training pipelines should treat data organization as a first-class component, alongside data selection and model architecture.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized various fields, yet their training efficiency is heavily reliant on effective data curation. While data selection has been widely studied, the strategic data organization for enhanced training remains an underexplored area, particularly since current LLMs are often trained for only one or a few epochs. This paper systematically explores the influence of data organization on LLM training by reusing pre-computed sample-level scores originally generated for data efficiency, thereby incurring minimal additional computational overhead. We identify and formalize four key guidelines for optimizing data organization: Boundary Sharpening, Cyclic Scheduling, Curriculum Continuity, and Local Diversity. Guided by them, we introduce two novel data ordering methods termed STR and SAW. Extensive experiments across different model scales and data sizes, encompassing both pre-training and SFT stages, validate the effectiveness of our summarized guidelines. They also demonstrate the robustness of our proposed data ordering methods in enhancing the stability and performance of LLM training. Github Link: https://github.com/microsoft/data-efficacy/

</details>


### [267] [Locally Coherent, Globally Incoherent: Bounding Compositional Incoherence in Multi-Component LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30335)
*Anany Kotawala*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper shows that combining outputs from multiple locally consistent LLM agents can produce globally inconsistent probabilistic beliefs, introduces a metric (eps*) to quantify this incoherence, analyzes when this happens, and proposes projection-based methods to repair it, showing that incoherence is common and practically harmful in betting-style evaluations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM applications increasingly use multi-agent or multi-component setups where different models handle different parts of a problem and their probabilistic outputs are combined, there is a risk that the combined beliefs violate basic probability rules even when each component is internally consistent. This incoherence can lead to arbitrage and regret in decision-making. The paper is motivated by the need to understand, measure, predict, and fix this kind of compositional incoherence in multi-component LLM systems.

Method: The authors formalise the notion of local vs global probabilistic coherence in multi-component systems and define the compositional residual eps* as the L2 distance from the composed probability vector to the nearest point in the joint coherent polytope, given explicit cross-component coupling constraints. They derive a product-structure dichotomy characterising cases where local coherence implies global coherence, and use a Rayleigh-quotient-based approximation to predict eps*. They introduce a hierarchical Boyle-Dykstra alternating projection algorithm to project incoherent compositions back into the coherent polytope, and design an anytime-valid e-process to monitor coherence sequentially. Empirically, they evaluate eps* and regret on ensembles of 4-LLM panels over many "cliques" of related questions and bets, and test intuitive mitigation strategies such as retrieval augmentation, partition-aware prompting, and using a separate aggregator LLM.

Result: They find that in a large-scale experiment over 1,876 ensemble cliques composed from a mid-tier panel of four LLMs (with an additional frontier-panel rerun), a substantial fraction (33–94% depending on relation class) of cliques have nonzero eps*, meaning global incoherence despite local coherence. This incoherence leads to significant exploitable regret: about +0.115 nats per bet under a proportional allocation betting rule on 1,770 resolved bets, though this drops to +0.006 nats when the bettors themselves enforce coherence. Their Rayleigh-quotient approximation predicts eps* within 7% on three of four evaluated relation classes, showing good predictive power. The hierarchical Boyle-Dykstra projection successfully repairs incoherent compositions deterministically, and their e-process supports real-time coherence monitoring. The three simple LLM-side mitigations all fail to eliminate incoherence and can even worsen it.

Conclusion: The paper concludes that multi-component LLM agents frequently exhibit global probabilistic incoherence even when each component is locally well-calibrated, and that this incoherence is large enough to be practically harmful in decision-making and betting contexts. Local coherence is not generally sufficient: only under specific product-structure conditions does it guarantee global coherence. The proposed eps* metric, theoretical characterisation, projection repair method, and sequential monitoring tools together provide a principled framework for diagnosing and mitigating incoherence. However, straightforward LLM-side fixes like better prompting or an aggregator model are insufficient, so system designers must either enforce explicit coherentisation (e.g., via projections) or work within architectures where the product-structure conditions hold.

Abstract: Multi-component LLM agents assemble probabilistic claims from components that each see only part of a joint problem; the composition can violate basic probability axioms even when every component is locally coherent. We formalise this locally coherent, globally incoherent failure via the compositional residual eps*, the L2 distance from the composed quote to the joint coherent polytope, computable at runtime from system output and the declared cross-component coupling constraints. A product-structure dichotomy characterises when local coherence suffices, and a Rayleigh-quotient prediction matches the observed residual within 7% on three of four relation classes. A hierarchical Boyle-Dykstra projection repairs the composition deterministically; an anytime-valid e-process gives sequential coherence monitoring. Across 1,876 ensemble cliques on a four-LLM mid-tier panel (frontier-panel rerun in Section 5.5), eps* > 0 on 33-94% of cliques, translating to +0.115 nats per bet of regret on 1,770 resolved bets under the proportional allocation rule (the gain collapses to +0.006 under bettors that themselves coherentise). Three intuitive LLM-side mitigations(retrieval, partition-aware prompting, aggregator-LLM) each fail or regress.

</details>


### [268] [Tiny but Trusted: Efficient Vision-Language Reasoning for Time-Series Anomaly Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30344)
*Xiaona Zhou,Muntasir Wahed,Tianjiao Yu,Constantin Brif,Ismini Lourentzou*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They build a new benchmark with natural-language anomaly explanations for time-series and fine-tune a VLM (VisAnomReasoner) on it to get big gains in anomaly detection and explanation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLMs do poorly on detecting abnormal patterns in sequential/time-series data, partly because public anomaly detection benchmarks lack natural-language rationales, making it hard to train models for grounded, interpretable anomaly reasoning.

Method: 1) Construct VisAnomBench from public time-series anomaly datasets, augmenting them with high-quality textual anomaly explanations automatically selected from multiple large VLMs using fine-grained, task-specific reward signals. 2) Fine-tune a vision-language model in a parameter-efficient way on this benchmark to create VisAnomReasoner. 3) Evaluate anomaly localization performance and generalization across benchmarks.

Result: On VisAnomBench, VisAnomReasoner localizes anomalies more accurately and outperforms all baselines, improving precision and F1 by at least 21.23 and 23.87 percentage points. On the separate TSB-AD-U benchmark, it also generalizes well, with precision and F1 gains of 9.57 and 13.39 percentage points over baselines.

Conclusion: Curating a benchmark with explicit, high-quality anomaly explanations and using it to fine-tune a VLM yields a parameter-efficient model (VisAnomReasoner) that substantially improves both accuracy and generalization for time-series anomaly detection, while providing more interpretable, grounded anomaly localization.

Abstract: Recent advances in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have achieved impressive performance across many tasks, yet prior studies report unsatisfactory performance when applying large language or multimodal models to finding abnormal patterns in sequential data. Public anomaly detection benchmarks typically provide interval annotations but not natural-language rationales, making it difficult to fine-tune VLMs to produce grounded, interpretable decisions. To address this gap, we construct VisAnomBench, a curated benchmark built from public time-series datasets and augmented with high-quality anomaly explanations selected from multiple large VLMs using fine-grained, task-specific rewards. Through fine-tuning on this benchmark, we develop VisAnomReasoner, a parameter-efficient VLM for time-series anomaly detection. Experimental results on VisAnomBench show that VisAnomReasoner achieves more accurate anomaly localization and consistently outperforms all baselines, with improvements of at least 21.23 and 23.87 percentage points in precision and F1, respectively. Additional experiments on the TSB-AD-U benchmark demonstrate strong cross-benchmark generalization, with VisAnomReasoner improving precision and F1 by 9.57 and 13.39 percentage points, respectively.

</details>


### [269] [SchGen: PCB Schematic Generation with Semantic-Grounded Code Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30345)
*Qinpei Luo,Ruichun Ma,Xinyu Zhang,Lili Qiu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: They propose SchGen, an LLM-based system that generates PCB schematics directly from natural-language descriptions, enabled by a new schematic code representation and a large paired dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PCB schematics define most electronic hardware but are still designed manually and require significant expertise. Although generative AI has progressed in IC design, there is a gap in applying it to PCB schematic creation from natural language, partly because existing schematic formats are not suited to LLM generation and there is no large paired dataset.

Method: They design a new semantically grounded code representation for schematics that focuses on editing primitives, relative component placement, and pin-name-based wiring instead of geometry-heavy, tool-specific syntax. They then build a large-scale dataset that pairs natural-language prompts with schematics in this representation using a human-agent collaborative pipeline that converts open-source hardware designs. They train an LLM, SchGen, on this data and compare it against other representations and larger general-purpose LLMs.

Result: SchGen achieves substantially higher wire connectivity accuracy and functional correctness in generated PCB schematics than models using alternative representations or larger general-purpose LLMs, demonstrating that their representation and dataset are effective for this task.

Conclusion: Careful design of a schematic representation that aligns with LLM strengths, combined with a large paired dataset, enables accurate natural-language-to-PCB-schematic generation. Representation choice is crucial for applying generative models to complex hardware design workflows.

Abstract: Printed circuit board (PCB) schematic design defines nearly all electronic hardware, but it remains manual and expertise-intensive. While generative AI has advanced digital and analog IC design, PCB schematic generation from natural-language intent is largely unexplored. This paper presents SchGen, the first large language model that generates editable PCB schematics from natural-language requests. The key challenge lies in the lack of an LLM-suited representation and a large-scale dataset. Current schematic formats are dominated by verbose, tool-specific syntax and geometry-heavy descriptions, making them difficult to generate reliably. We introduce a semantically grounded code representation that encodes schematic editing primitives with relative placement and pin-name-based wiring, transforming a geometry-driven generation problem into a semantics-driven matching task amenable to LLMs. We further construct a large-scale dataset of PCB schematics paired with user prompts via a human-agent collaborative pipeline that converts open-source hardware designs into our representation. Experiments show that SchGen significantly outperforms alternative representations and even larger general-purpose LLMs on wire connectivity accuracy and functional correctness. Our results highlight the critical role of representation design in enabling generative models for complex hardware design tasks.

</details>


### [270] [Physics Is All You Need? A Case Study in Physicist-Supervised AI Development of Scientific Software](https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.30353)
*Nhat-Minh Nguyen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Case study of a physicist supervising an AI coding agent to build a physics module, analyzing when the agent succeeds or fails and what supervision practices are needed for trustworthy results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Clarify the role of AI coding agents in scientific work (tool, co-author, or researcher) by analyzing how well they can autonomously develop a complex physics code under human supervision, and what kinds of failures occur beyond what automated tests can catch.

Method: Single-subject (N=1) longitudinal case study over 12 work days and 57 interaction sessions between a physicist and Claude Code (Sonnet and Opus). The interactions and supervision events were logged, classified by intervention level, and the technical development of a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module in JAX was analyzed, including failure modes relative to oracle tests and supervision strategies.

Result: Out of 15 supervision events, the AI agent autonomously resolved ten using oracle tests, two were resolved using the physicist's domain knowledge, and three remained unresolved by the agent because they evaded oracle detection. In these failures, the agent repeatedly made local coefficient adjustments inside a fundamentally inadequate code architecture, failed to reconsider an early branch choice without explicit conceptual guidance, and introduced a numerically calibrated but theoretically meaningless correction that still passed all tests.

Conclusion: The trustworthiness of the AI agent’s scientific output depended more on supervision design than on raw model capability. Robust supervision required diverse testing beyond a single calibration point, cross-session changelogs, and explicit norms against ad hoc numerical fixes. Current agents lacked the ability to propose alternative model architectures or to distinguish between merely predictive fits and theoretically correct explanations, and these missing capabilities are unlikely to be solved by scale alone.

Abstract: Are AI agents tools, co-authors, or researchers? We present a quantified case study ($N=1$): a physicist supervising an AI coding agent (Claude Code, Sonnet and Opus models) over 12 work days and 57 sessions to build CLAX-PT, a differentiable one-loop perturbation theory module in JAX. We documented and classified 15 supervision events by intervention level.
  The agent resolved ten autonomously by iterating against oracle tests. Two more by the physicist's domain knowledge. The three it could not -- all evaded oracle detection -- share a common property: the agent treated symptom reduction as root-cause resolution. It spent 33 of the 57 sessions adjusting coefficients within a code architecture that could not represent the target physics, and could not re-evaluate its CLASS-PT branch choice even when prompted to reconsider; only an injected physics concept (anisotropic BAO damping) triggered the redesign. Separately, the agent committed a calibrated correction that passed all oracle tests but corresponded to no quantity in the theory, predicting wrong values at any other cosmology.
  The fudge factor was caught and replaced within the same session. Three supervision practices proved critical for catching what oracle tests missed: testing at diverse parameter points beyond the fiducial calibration; shared changelogs that surfaced stalled exploration across sessions; and an explicit rule against unphysical numerical patches. In this case, supervision design, not model capability, determined whether the agent's output was trustworthy. Closing the gap would require agents that propose architectural alternatives rather than optimize within a given structure, and distinguish predictive adequacy from explanatory correctness -- capabilities not exhibited here, not obviously addressed by scaling alone. [Abridged.]

</details>
